The painting’s subject, the raising of the Virgin Mary’s body into heaven, relates to the function of the church as the funerary monument of a wealthy noblewoman, Doña Maria de Silva; the iconography of the entire commission focuses on death and salvation.
persona1_adapted: The Assumption of the Virgin was the main painting in El Greco’s first big job. He made it for an altarpiece in a convent church in Toledo, Spain. The painting shows the Virgin Mary’s body rising up to heaven. This subject connects to the church’s role as a burial place for a rich noblewoman named Doña Maria de Silva. The whole set of artworks in the church focuses on death and salvation.

CuratorialText: El Greco used broad, free brushwork, flickering hues, rich color harmonies, and bold figural arrangements to arouse devotional fervor in the viewer and impart a deep sense of faith. The composition is divided into two zones: the earthly sphere of the apostles and the celestial realm of the angels. El Greco masterfully compressed many figures into a tall and narrow format: In the earthly zone, the apostles’ faces and gestures express a range of emotions through their dynamic, agitated gestures and poses. They are so individually considered that it seems El Greco studied actual studio models. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm, graceful, and direct in their focus toward the looming figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.
persona1_adapted: El Greco used broad, quick brushstrokes and bright, shifting colors to create a strong feeling of faith and devotion in the viewer. The painting is split into two parts: the earthly world of the apostles below and the heavenly world of the angels above. El Greco packed many figures into a tall, narrow space. In the earthly part, the apostles show a wide range of emotions through their lively, restless poses and gestures. Each apostle looks so unique that it seems El Greco studied real people as models. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm and graceful. They all focus directly on the Virgin Mary as she rises up into their realm.

CuratorialText: Katsushika Hokusai’s much celebrated series, Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (Fugaku sanjûrokkei), was begun in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This tour-de-force series established the popularity of landscape prints, which continues to this day. Perhaps most striking about the series is Hokusai’s copious use of the newly affordable Berlin blue pigment, featured in many of the compositions in the color for the sky and water. Mount Fuji is the protagonist in each scene, viewed from afar or up close, during various weather conditions and seasons, and from all directions.
persona1_adapted: Katsushika Hokusai’s famous series, *Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji*, began in 1830. The artist was 70 years old at the time. This amazing series helped make landscape prints popular, and they are still loved today. One of the most striking things about the series is Hokusai’s use of a new blue pigment called Berlin blue. It had just become affordable, and he used it a lot in the sky and water of many prints. Mount Fuji is the main subject in every scene. You see it from far away or up close, in different weather and seasons, and from all directions.

CuratorialText: The most famous image from the set is the “Great Wave” (Kanagawa oki nami ura), in which a diminutive Mount Fuji can be seen in the distance under the crest of a giant wave. The three impressions of Hokusai’s Great Wave in the Art Institute are all later impressions than the first state of the design.
persona1_adapted: The most famous picture from the series is the "Great Wave." In it, you can see a tiny Mount Fuji in the distance, under the top of a huge wave. The Art Institute has three copies of Hokusai's Great Wave. All of them were printed later than the very first version of the design.

CuratorialText: Pablo Picasso made The Old Guitarist while working in Barcelona. In the paintings of his Blue Period (1901-04), the artist restricted himself to a cold, monochromatic blue palette, flattened forms, and emotional, psychological themes of human misery and alienation related to the work of such artists as Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin. The elongated, angular figure of the blind musician also relates to Picasso’s interest in Spanish art and, in particular, the great 16th-century artist El Greco. The image reﬂects the twenty-two-year-old Picasso’s personal struggle and sympathy for the plight of the downtrodden; he knew what it was like to be poor, having been nearly penniless during all of 1902.
persona1_adapted:
Pablo Picasso painted *The Old Guitarist* while he was living in Barcelona. During his Blue Period (1901–1904), he used only cool shades of blue. He also made his figures look flat and stretched. His paintings focused on sad, lonely feelings and human suffering. These themes were similar to the work of artists like Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin.

The blind musician in the painting has a long, angular body. This style shows Picasso’s interest in Spanish art, especially the work of a famous 16th-century artist named El Greco.

The painting also reflects Picasso’s own life. He was only twenty-two years old at the time. He knew what it was like to be poor—he had almost no money during all of 1902. The image shows his personal struggle and his sympathy for people who were suffering.

CuratorialText: Illustrator Norman Rockwell was one of the most popular artists of the 20th century, beloved in particular for the hundreds of cover images he created for the Saturday Evening Post. The Dugout is the largest of several studies for one such cover, published on September 4, 1948. It was inspired by a springtime doubleheader that he attended during which the hometown Boston Braves swept the visiting Chicago Cubs. The artist created an image contrasting dejected Cubs players and elated Braves fans.
persona1_adapted: Illustrator Norman Rockwell was one of the most popular artists of the 1900s. People especially loved the hundreds of cover images he made for the *Saturday Evening Post* magazine. *The Dugout* is the largest of several studies for one of those covers. The cover was published on September 4, 1948. Rockwell got the idea from a spring baseball doubleheader he watched. In that game, the hometown Boston Braves beat the visiting Chicago Cubs in both games. The artist created an image that shows a contrast: sad Cubs players and happy Braves fans.

CuratorialText: Rockwell staged the entire scene, which enabled him to take photographs for later reference. He recruited several family members of Braves players to act as fans. In the dugout, he positioned (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The figure of the forlorn batboy, however, was not a member of the Cubs; Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to don the Cubs uniform and pose.
persona1_adapted: Rockwell set up the whole scene so he could take photos to use later. He asked several family members of Braves players to pretend to be fans. In the dugout, he placed (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. But the sad-looking batboy was not a real Cubs player. Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to put on a Cubs uniform and pose for the picture.

CuratorialText: By the time the issue was published, the Cubs had fallen to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped bolster their growing reputation as the “loveable losers,” and it became one of the most recognized sports images of the 20th century.
persona1_adapted: By the time the magazine came out, the Cubs had dropped to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped build their image as the “loveable losers.” It became one of the most famous sports pictures of the 1900s.

CuratorialText: The monumental stacks that Claude Monet depicted in his series Stacks of Wheat rose fifteen to twenty feet and stood just outside the artist’s farmhouse at Giverny. Through 1890 and 1891, he worked on this series both in the field, painting simultaneously at several easels, and in the studio, refining pictorial harmonies. In May 1891, Monet hung fifteen of these canvases next to each other in one small room in the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. An unprecedented critical and financial success, the exhibition marked a breakthrough in Monet’s career, as well as in the history of French art. In this view, and in nearly all of the autumn views in the series, the conical tops of the stacks break the horizon and push into the sky. But in most of the winter views, which constitute the core of the series, the stacks seem wrapped by bands of hill and field, as if bedded down for the season. For Monet, the stack was a resonant symbol of sustenance and survival. He followed this group with further series depicting poplars, the facade of Rouen Cathedral, and, later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute has the largest group of Monet’s Stacks of Wheat in the world.
persona1_adapted:
The huge stacks of wheat that Claude Monet painted in his *Stacks of Wheat* series were 15 to 20 feet tall. They stood right outside his farmhouse in Giverny, France. Between 1890 and 1891, Monet worked on this series both outside and inside his studio. Outside, he painted at several easels at the same time. Inside, he worked on making the colors and shapes fit together perfectly.

In May 1891, Monet hung fifteen of these paintings side by side in one small room at the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. The show was a huge success, both with critics and with buyers. It was a major turning point in Monet’s career and in French art history.

In this painting, and in almost all the autumn views in the series, the pointed tops of the stacks break the line of the horizon and rise into the sky. But in most of the winter views—which are the heart of the series—the stacks seem wrapped by bands of hill and field. They look like they are tucked in for the season.

For Monet, the stack was a powerful symbol of food and survival. After this series, he painted more groups of works showing poplars, the front of Rouen Cathedral, and later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute of Chicago has the largest collection of Monet’s *Stacks of Wheat* in the world.

CuratorialText: In 1886 Vincent van Gogh left his native Holland and settled in Paris, where his beloved brother Theo was a dealer in paintings. Van Gogh was apparently inspired by the energy of the city and by his introduction to Impressionism, and he created at least twenty-four self-portraits during his two-year stay in the French capital.
persona1_adapted: In 1886, Vincent van Gogh left his home country of Holland and moved to Paris. His brother Theo, whom he loved very much, worked there as an art dealer. The energy of the city and his first look at Impressionism—a style of painting that focuses on light and color—inspired van Gogh. During his two years in Paris, he painted at least twenty-four portraits of himself.

CuratorialText: This early example is modest in size and was painted on prepared artist’s board rather than canvas. Its densely dabbed brushwork, which became a hallmark of Van Gogh’s style, reﬂects the artist’s response to Georges Seurat’s revolutionary pointillist technique in A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884. What was for Seurat a method based on the cool objectivity of science became in Van Gogh’s hands an intense emotional language. The surface of the painting dances with particles of color—intense greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Dominating this dazzling array of staccato dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and the intensity of their gaze. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to Theo. “However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” From Paris, Van Gogh traveled to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for only five years.
persona1_adapted:
This early painting is small. Van Gogh painted it on a prepared artist’s board, not on canvas. He used thick, dabbed brushstrokes. This style later became a key feature of his work. It was inspired by Georges Seurat’s revolutionary pointillist technique. Pointillism uses tiny dots of color to create a picture. Seurat’s method was based on science and objectivity. But Van Gogh turned it into a way to express strong emotions.

The painting’s surface seems to dance with particles of color. You can see intense greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Among these bright dots and dashes, the artist’s deep green eyes stand out. Their gaze is intense. Van Gogh once wrote to his brother Theo, “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals. However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.”

After Paris, Van Gogh moved to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. When he died in 1890, he had been actively making art for only five years.

CuratorialText: Van Gogh’s career as a painter was actually very brief. From Paris, he traveled to the southern town of Arles for ﬁ ft een months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for just ﬁve years.
persona1_adapted: Van Gogh’s career as a painter was actually very short. From Paris, he moved to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. When he died in 1890, he had been actively making art for only five years.

CuratorialText: In American Gothic, Grant Wood directly evoked images of an earlier generation by featuring a farmer and his daughter posed stiffly and dressed as if they were, as the artist put it, “tintypes from my old family album.” They stand outside of their home, built in an 1880s style known as Carpenter Gothic. Wood had seen a similar farmhouse during a visit to Eldon, Iowa. When it was exhibited at the Art Institute in 1930, the painting became an instant sensation, its ambiguity prompting viewers to speculate about the figures and their story. Many understood the work to be a satirical comment on midwesterners out of step with a modernizing world. Yet Wood intended it to convey a positive image of rural American values, offering a vision of reassurance at the beginning of the Great Depression.
persona1_adapted:
In *American Gothic*, Grant Wood painted a farmer and his daughter standing stiffly outside their home. They are dressed in old-fashioned clothes, like what Wood called “tintypes from my old family album.” (Tintypes were early photographs from the 1800s.) Their house was built in a style called Carpenter Gothic, which was popular in the 1880s. Wood first saw a similar farmhouse during a trip to Eldon, Iowa.

When the painting was shown at the Art Institute in 1930, it became an instant hit. People were curious about the two figures and their story. Many thought the painting was making fun of midwesterners who seemed out of touch with a changing world. But Wood actually wanted to show a positive view of rural American values. He hoped to offer comfort and reassurance at the start of the Great Depression.

CuratorialText: Little did Claude Monet know that the water garden he created three years after buying his Giverny, France, property in 1890 would become his primary inspiration over the next two and a half decades. These paintings, numbering around 250, mark Monet’s artistic journey from more straightforward depictions of the pond spanned by the wooden Japanese bridge to the monumental and near-abstract series on the water lily theme he made in preparation for his murals at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris between 1914 and 1926.
persona1_adapted: When Claude Monet bought his home in Giverny, France, in 1890, he didn’t know that the water garden he built three years later would become his main source of inspiration for the next 25 years. He painted about 250 works of this garden. At first, his paintings showed the pond and the wooden Japanese bridge in a clear, realistic way. Over time, his style changed. Later works became large and almost abstract. These paintings focused on water lilies. Monet made them between 1914 and 1926 as studies for the huge murals now at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris.

CuratorialText: Water Lilies, one of a group of paintings on the subject made between 1903 and 1908, comes at the midpoint of Monet’s developing style and spatial experimentations. The nearly square format underscores his move away from painting the conventional zones of land, sky, and water to focus solely on the water’s surface. Clusters of water lilies at bottom left and top right frame a watery path, while the water’s surface reflects trees and clouds. Although the dreamlike quality of floating forms might seem to be a natural development for the artist, he considered these works “an obsession,” a sentiment borne out by technical examinations on the Art Institute’s canvas, revealing many significant changes made to the painting in progress.
persona1_adapted:
**Water Lilies** is part of a series of paintings Monet created between 1903 and 1908. This work marks a middle point in his style and his experiments with space.

The painting is almost square. This shape shows Monet moving away from traditional scenes of land, sky, and water. Instead, he focuses only on the water's surface.

Clusters of water lilies sit at the bottom left and top right. They frame a watery path. The water's surface reflects trees and clouds.

The floating forms have a dreamlike quality. This might seem like a natural step for the artist. But Monet called these works "an obsession." Technical studies of this painting at the Art Institute support that. They show many major changes made while the work was in progress.

CuratorialText: Pierre-Auguste Renoir considered the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou (a village along the Seine River) the perfect spot to enjoy nature and good company away from the urban distractions of Paris. His painting Two Sisters (On the Terrace), which he exhibited at the Impressionist exhibition of 1882, is among the last of his paintings set there.
persona1_adapted: Pierre-Auguste Renoir thought the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou was the perfect place to enjoy nature and good company. Chatou is a village along the Seine River, far from the busy city of Paris. His painting *Two Sisters (On the Terrace)* is one of the last paintings he made there. He showed this painting at the Impressionist exhibition in 1882.

CuratorialText: Sometimes, as in his most famous work on this subject, Luncheon of the Boating Party (1882; Phillips Collection, Washington, DC), Renoir used his close friends as models. For Two Sisters, however, despite the title of the painting, he paid unrelated models to create this intimate and perfectly balanced pairing of youth and nature. The older girl, wearing the blue flannel of a female boater’s outfit, sports a luxurious red hat, but it is her younger companion who captivates. Her oversize headdress composed of a floral bouquet is echoed in the brightly colored skeins of yarn in the basket that she gently touches. As was typical for the artist, Renoir painted the faces of his models with controlled and precise brushwork creating a porcelain-like surface that contrasts brilliantly with the diaphanous, impressionist backdrop of river, boats, and blossoming trees just beyond the railing of the terrace.
persona1_adapted:
In one of his most famous paintings, *Luncheon of the Boating Party* (1882), Renoir used his close friends as models. But for *Two Sisters*, he paid strangers to pose. Even though the title suggests a family bond, the models were not related.

The painting shows two girls sitting close together. The older girl wears a blue flannel boating outfit and a bright red hat. But it is the younger girl who really catches your eye. She has a large headdress made of flowers. The colorful yarn in the basket beside her matches the flowers. She gently touches the yarn.

Renoir painted the girls’ faces with careful, smooth brushstrokes. Their skin looks like porcelain. This style contrasts with the soft, blurry background. Behind them, you can see a river, boats, and blooming trees just beyond the terrace railing. This mix of sharp detail and dreamy scenery is typical of Renoir’s work.

CuratorialText: Willem de Kooning was a central figure in Abstract Expressionism, an art movement that espoused the painterly actions of the artist as a sign of his or her emotions. De Kooning completed Excavation in June 1950, just in time for it to be exhibited in the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale. His largest painting up to that date, the work exemplifies the Dutch-born innovator’s style, with its expressive brushwork and distinctive organization of space into sliding planes with open contours. According to the artist, the point of departure for the painting was an image of women working in a rice field in Bitter Rice, a 1949 Neorealist film by Italian director Giuseppe de Santis. The mobile structure of hooked calligraphic lines defines anatomical parts—bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to dance across the painted surface, revealing the particular tension between abstraction and figuration that is inherent in de Kooning’s work. The original white pigment has yellowed over the years, diluting somewhat the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that punctuate the composition. Aptly titled, the painting reflects de Kooning’s technically masterful painting process: an intensive building up of the surface and scraping down of its paint layers, often for months, until the desired effect was achieved.
persona1_adapted:
Willem de Kooning was a key artist in Abstract Expressionism. This art movement focused on the artist's physical actions as a way to show their emotions. De Kooning finished *Excavation* in June 1950. He completed it just in time for the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale, a major art exhibition. It was his largest painting up to that point. The work shows his style, with bold brushstrokes and a unique way of arranging space. He used sliding shapes with open outlines.

The artist said the painting was inspired by a scene from the 1949 film *Bitter Rice*. The movie shows women working in a rice field. In the painting, hooked, calligraphy-like lines create body parts. You can see bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws. These forms seem to dance across the canvas. This mix of abstract shapes and real objects shows the tension in de Kooning’s work. Over time, the white paint has turned slightly yellow. This change softens the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink in the painting.

The title *Excavation* fits the work well. It describes de Kooning’s process. He built up layers of paint and then scraped them down. He often did this for months until he got the effect he wanted.

CuratorialText: White Crucifixion is the first in Marc Chagall’s series of compositions that feature Jesus as a Jewish martyr and dramatically call attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany at the hands of the National Socialist Party. Chagall stressed Jesus’s religious identity by depicting him and the biblical figures above him in traditional Jewish garments. The surrounding images show the devastation of pogroms, violent attacks against Jewish communities often organized or sanctioned by local governments. Combining the Crucifixion with contemporary events, Chagall’s painting links the martyred Jesus with the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe and implicitly compares the Nazis with Jesus’s tormentors.
persona1_adapted:
**White Crucifixion** is the first painting in a series by Marc Chagall. In these works, he shows Jesus as a Jewish martyr. The paintings draw strong attention to the suffering of Jewish people in 1930s Germany. At that time, the National Socialist Party (the Nazis) was persecuting them.

Chagall made sure to show Jesus’s Jewish identity. He painted Jesus and the biblical figures above him wearing traditional Jewish clothing. The scenes around them show the destruction caused by pogroms. Pogroms were violent attacks on Jewish communities. Local governments often organized or allowed these attacks.

By mixing the Crucifixion with current events, Chagall connects the suffering of Jesus to the suffering of Jewish people across Europe. The painting also suggests that the Nazis are like the people who tortured Jesus.

CuratorialText: The Child’s Bath is a tender portrayal of familial closeness, a subject that Mary Cassatt explored throughout her career. The caregiver’s cheek brushing the child’s shoulder, her encircling embrace, and the child’s pudgy hand on her knee suggest an emotional bond between the two. Captivated by a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris in 1890, Cassatt set out to produce a series of color prints influenced by Japanese aesthetics. She then continued her investigation across media, culminating in this bold composition, with its dramatically flattened picture plane, decorative patterning, and bright palette.
persona1_adapted:
**The Child’s Bath** shows a quiet moment between a caregiver and a child. Mary Cassatt often painted this kind of family closeness.  

The caregiver’s cheek touches the child’s shoulder. Her arms wrap around the child. The child’s chubby hand rests on her knee. These details show a strong emotional bond between them.  

In 1890, Cassatt saw a big show of Japanese prints in Paris. She was fascinated by them. She decided to make her own series of color prints using Japanese ideas.  

Later, she kept exploring those ideas in other types of art. This painting is the result. It has a flat, shallow space. It uses bold patterns and bright colors.

CuratorialText: René Magritte painted Time Transfixed at a moment when he was attempting to elicit “poetic secrets” through his works. With both philosophy and psychology in mind, the artist challenged himself to juxtapose two images in such a way that they would suggest in the viewer’s mind a third un-pictured thing. Here he connected two highly disparate objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most iconic works. Although the fireplace is domestic and the train is industrial, they are linked by the smoke. Magritte understood that viewers would connect the locomotive’s billowing exhaust with the plumes of smoke that travel up chimneys, uncovering the painting’s poetic secret.
persona1_adapted: René Magritte painted *Time Transfixed* at a time when he was trying to reveal “poetic secrets” through his art. He thought about both philosophy and psychology. Magritte challenged himself to put two images together in a way that would make viewers imagine a third thing that wasn’t actually painted. In this work, he connected two very different objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most famous paintings. Even though the fireplace is a home object and the train is an industrial one, they are linked by smoke. Magritte knew that viewers would connect the train’s puffing exhaust with the smoke that rises from chimneys. That connection is the painting’s poetic secret.

CuratorialText: One of the most avant-garde aspects of the Impressionists was their choice of subject matter, which frequently included scenes derived from modern, industrial Paris, from iron bridges to exhibition halls to train sheds. The train station at Saint-Lazare would have been a familiar, meaningful sight to Claude Monet in the 1870s. The terminal linked Paris to Normandy, where the artist developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the point of departure for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition, in 1877, probably displaying them in the same gallery.
persona1_adapted:
One of the most daring choices the Impressionists made was what they painted. They often showed scenes from modern, industrial Paris. This included iron bridges, exhibition halls, and train sheds.

In the 1870s, the train station at Saint-Lazare was a familiar and important place for Claude Monet. The station connected Paris to Normandy. There, in the 1860s, Monet learned to paint outdoors. The station was also the starting point for towns and villages west and north of Paris. The Impressionists often visited these places.

Monet finished eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition in 1877. He probably showed them all in the same room.

CuratorialText: Monet chose to focus his attention here on the glass-and-iron train shed, where he found an appealing combination of artiﬁ cial and natural effects—the rising steam of locomotives trapped within the structure and the daylight penetrating large, glazed sections of the roof, for instance. Monet’s depictions of the station inaugurated what was to become for him an established pattern of painting a speciﬁc motif repeatedly in order to capture subtle and temporal atmospheric changes, as in his famous series of stacks of wheat. But the Saint-Lazare paintings also represented his last attempt to capture urban life: from this point on in his career, Monet largely devoted himself to landscapes.
persona1_adapted: Monet focused on the glass-and-iron train shed. He liked how it mixed man-made and natural effects. For example, steam from trains rose up inside the shed, while sunlight came through the large glass sections of the roof. These paintings of the Saint-Lazare train station started a pattern for Monet. He began painting the same subject over and over to show small changes in light and weather over time. He did the same thing later in his famous series of haystacks. However, the Saint-Lazare paintings were Monet’s last attempt to capture city life. After this, he mostly painted landscapes.

CuratorialText: Salvador Dalí, Surrealism’s most publicized practitioner, created monstrous visions of a world turned inside out, which he made even more compelling through his extraordinary technical skills. When the Art Institute acquired Inventions of the Monsters in 1943, the artist wrote his congratulations and explained.
persona1_adapted: Salvador Dalí was the most famous artist of Surrealism. Surrealism is an art style that uses dreamlike and strange images. Dalí created scary visions of a world turned upside down. His amazing painting skills made these visions even more powerful. In 1943, the Art Institute bought his painting *Inventions of the Monsters*. Dalí wrote to congratulate them and explained his work.

CuratorialText: “According to Nostradamus the apparition of monsters presages the outbreak of war. The canvas was painted in the Semmering mountains near Vienna a few months before the Anschluss [the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany] and has a prophetic character. Horse women equal maternal river monsters. Flaming giraffe equals masculine apocalyptic monster. Cat angel equals divine heterosexual monster. Hourglass equals metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí equal sentimental monster. The little blue dog is not a true monster.”
persona1_adapted:
Here is the adapted text for an 8th-grade reader, following your guidelines.

---

According to Nostradamus, seeing monsters means a war is coming. Dalí painted this picture in the Semmering mountains near Vienna. He finished it just a few months before the Anschluss—the 1938 union of Austria and Germany. So the painting has a warning, or prophetic, quality.

In the painting, different things stand for different types of monsters. Horse women are like river monsters that are mothers. A flaming giraffe is a male monster that brings destruction. A cat angel is a divine monster that is heterosexual. An hourglass is a monster of time and fate. Gala and Dalí themselves are a monster of emotion. The little blue dog is the only one that is not a real monster.

CuratorialText: Inventions of the Monsters has an ominous mood. It is rife with threats of danger, from the menacing fire in the distance to the sibylline figure in the foreground with an hourglass and a butterfly, both symbols of the inevitability of death. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his native Catalonia embroiled in the Spanish Civil War, the artist surely felt great anxiety over a world without a safe haven, a world that indeed had allowed for the invention of monsters.
persona1_adapted: The painting *Inventions of the Monsters* feels dark and threatening. Danger appears everywhere—from the scary fire in the distance to the mysterious figure in the front. This figure holds an hourglass and a butterfly, both symbols that death is unavoidable. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. At the time, Dalí’s home region of Catalonia was caught in the Spanish Civil War. The artist likely felt deep worry about a world without a safe place—a world that had allowed monsters to be created.

CuratorialText: Shiva, one of the most important Hindu divinities, is here depicted as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), an iconic image in Indian art. Shiva’s cosmic dance sets in motion the rhythm of life and death; it pervades the universe, as symbolized by the ring of fire that is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of the god’s hair. One pair of his arms balances the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life while another performs symbolic gestures: the raised right hand means “fear not,” and the left hand (gajahasta) pointing down toward his raised left foot signifies release from the ignorance that hinders realization of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right leg planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), stamping out ignorance. The tiny figure of the personified river goddess, Ganga, is caught up in his matted, flowing locks. Shiva was believed to break the fall of the great Ganges River as it descends from the Himalayas by standing beneath the waters, which divide over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze comes from the Chola period in the south of India. Icons such as this were carried in procession during religious ceremonies.
persona1_adapted:
Shiva is one of the most important gods in Hinduism. Here, he is shown as the Lord of the Dance, or Nataraja. This is a famous image in Indian art.

Shiva’s cosmic dance sets the rhythm of life and death. His dance fills the universe, shown by the ring of fire around him. The ring is filled with his long, snake-like hair.

One pair of his arms holds the flame of destruction and a small drum called a damaru. The drum beats the rhythm of life. His other arms make symbolic gestures. His raised right hand means “fear not.” His left hand points down toward his raised left foot. This gesture means freedom from ignorance, which blocks people from understanding the ultimate truth.

Shiva stands perfectly balanced. His right leg is planted on a demon named Apasmara, who represents darkness and ignorance. By standing on the demon, Shiva stamps out ignorance.

A small figure of the river goddess Ganga is caught in Shiva’s matted, flowing hair. According to belief, Shiva broke the fall of the great Ganges River as it came down from the Himalayas. He stood under the waters, and the river split over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India.

This classic bronze statue comes from the Chola period in southern India. Statues like this were carried in processions during religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Albert Bierstadt is best remembered for his paintings of the American West, but during his early career, the ambitious artist also created New England landscapes, particularly of the White Mountains, as seen here. When this painting was first exhibited in 1863, critics declared it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised the artist’s deft contrast of “light and shade,” which brought a heightened realism to the image. Bierstadt’s affinity for the White Mountains mirrored a growing interest in the region as one of America’s premier tourist attractions. Photographs of the area from the 19th century suggest that Bierstadt utilized landscape elements from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, in this imaginary composition.
persona1_adapted:
Albert Bierstadt is famous for his paintings of the American West. But early in his career, he also painted landscapes of New England, especially the White Mountains. You can see one of those paintings here.

When this painting was first shown in 1863, critics called it Bierstadt’s “best work.” They praised how he used light and shadow to make the scene look very real. Bierstadt’s love for the White Mountains matched a growing interest in the area as a top tourist spot in America.

Photos from the 1800s suggest that Bierstadt used real features from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, to create this imaginary scene.

CuratorialText: In this large-scale canvas, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of varying ages appear to go about daily life within a colorful, stylized outdoor environment. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting combines observed detail with artistic fantasy to create a dreamlike scene whose narrative resists simple interpretation. The work’s deliberately provocative title enhances this effect, leaving us to wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to the viewer.
persona1_adapted: In this large painting, women of different ages seem to go about their daily lives in a colorful, stylized outdoor setting. Paul Gauguin sent this work, along with several others, back to France in 1897. Like much of his art, this painting mixes real details with imagined scenes. The result is a dreamlike picture whose story is not easy to explain. The title is deliberately provocative—meaning it is meant to make you think or question. It leaves us wondering if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to us, the viewers.

CuratorialText: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of travel. Whether people traveled alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern modes of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture how and why we move through the world.
persona1_adapted: People have traveled for many different reasons throughout history. The ways they traveled have also changed a lot. Some people walked or rode animals. Others traveled in caravans or on ships. Today, we use planes, trains, and cars. Art can show us both how and why people move around the world.

CuratorialText: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a Venetian merchant who spent about seventeen years in China—from around 1274 to 1292—during the reign of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (reigned 1260–94), founder of the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies conquered the Chinese capital at Hangzhou with minimal destruction. Sometime after this, Marco Polo visited the city and its West Lake.
persona1_adapted: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a merchant from Venice, Italy. He spent about 17 years in China, from around 1274 to 1292. This was during the rule of Khubilai Khan, a Mongol emperor who founded the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies took over the Chinese capital at Hangzhou. They did very little damage to the city. Sometime after that, Marco Polo visited Hangzhou and its West Lake.

CuratorialText: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. From ancient times, the emperors of China maintained a network of roads and waterways that unified their vast realm and facilitated the movement of people and goods. Owing to this imperial association, images of ordinary travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers connoted the benevolent rule of an enlightened sovereign and evoked an era of peace and prosperity. Rather than documenting the hardships or actual circumstances of any particular journey, images of anonymous characters traveling or working together capture some of the noteworthy sights and scenes a traveler might encounter along the way. Simultaneously, they promote the fundamental ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.
persona1_adapted:
Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. Long ago, China's emperors built a network of roads and waterways. These routes connected their huge empire and made it easier for people and goods to move around.

Because the emperor was linked to these roads, pictures of ordinary people traveling on them suggested something important. They showed that a wise ruler was in charge. These images also brought to mind a time of peace and success.

The paintings don't focus on the hard parts of a journey or show a real trip. Instead, they show unnamed people traveling or working together. These scenes capture interesting sights a traveler might see along the way. At the same time, they promote the basic ideas of a peaceful society and a well-run country.

CuratorialText: One of the most enduring motivations for travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a site of spiritual significance. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to walk in the footsteps of Christ or visited more local destinations connected to the lives or relics of the saints. In Islam, pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the principal duties of all believers. Other holy sites were also visited on a regular basis. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims would sometimes bring gifts, called votive offerings, with them as a donation to the destination site.
persona1_adapted:
One of the biggest reasons people have traveled throughout history is their religious faith. Almost every major religion—including Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism—has a tradition of pilgrimage. A pilgrimage is a journey to a place that is spiritually important.

For example, Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to follow in the footsteps of Jesus. Others visited local sites connected to the lives or remains of saints. In Islam, the pilgrimage to Mecca, called the hajj, is one of the main duties for all believers. People also visited other holy places on a regular basis.

Over many centuries, hundreds of pilgrims from China, Japan, and Korea braved many dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Sometimes, pilgrims brought gifts called votive offerings. These were donations left at the destination site.

CuratorialText: Trade was another common motivation for travel and occurred in many forms, but the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road, the overland trade routes that linked the prosperous Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. On the Silk Road, foreign merchants joined Buddhist missionaries, diplomatic envoys, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants in a wealthy, worldly environment that offered a ready market for exotic imports, including silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.
persona1_adapted:
People also traveled for trade. Trade happened in many ways. The most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road. This was a network of land routes that connected the powerful Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia.

On the Silk Road, foreign merchants traveled alongside Buddhist missionaries, government messengers, translators, craftspeople, entertainers, and other skilled newcomers. They all moved through a wealthy, international world. This world had a strong demand for rare goods from faraway places. These included items made of silver and gold, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.

CuratorialText: In the twentieth century, travel offered a key means for diplomacy and cultural exchange. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), Theodore Roosevelt’s daughter, embarked on a tour of Asia accompanying William Howard Taft’s goodwill mission in 1905. This voyage is immortalized in a series of photographs but also through diplomatic gifts that dignitaries, such as the emperor of Japan, bestowed on Roosevelt.
persona1_adapted: In the 1900s, travel was an important way for countries to build friendships and share culture. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), the daughter of President Theodore Roosevelt, joined a goodwill trip to Asia in 1905. The trip was led by William Howard Taft. We remember this journey through photos and through special gifts. Leaders, like the emperor of Japan, gave these gifts to Roosevelt as a sign of friendship between nations.

CuratorialText: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long been traversing the globe to conduct research and to explore foreign lands. In the early twentieth century, leisure travel among Europeans and North Americans also became increasingly common. Fascinated by the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to expand their horizons by venturing there.
persona1_adapted: For a long time, adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have traveled around the world to study and explore new places. In the early 1900s, more people from Europe and North America started traveling for fun. They were curious about the cultures of Asia and the Middle East, so they began to visit those regions.

CuratorialText: Artists who traveled played a pivotal role in documenting and popularizing their visions of the distant places they visited. Their interactions with other artists and the things they experienced on their journeys contributed to the dispersal of different artistic styles and motifs.
persona1_adapted: Artists who traveled played a key role in recording and sharing their views of faraway places. They met other artists and saw new things on their trips. This helped spread different art styles and patterns.

CuratorialText: While travel has mostly positive connotations in popular culture today, it’s important to remember the darker sides of travel as well. Not all travel is undertaken by choice: people can be forced to travel as a result of political exile, as well as due to war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, and colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be dangerous, and this was particularly true in ages past. The physical act of traveling exposed people to potential injuries and diseases, the natural elements were unpredictable and threatened modes of transportation, and there was an inherent unpredictability to being on the road far from home. The recognition of these dangers shaped stories and cultural beliefs.
persona1_adapted:
Today, people often think of travel as a good thing. But travel also has a darker side. Not everyone travels by choice. Some people are forced to leave home because of war, famine, disease, money problems, natural disasters, or colonialism. Others are sent away as political exiles.

Even when travel is voluntary, it can be dangerous. This was especially true in the past. Traveling put people at risk of injury or sickness. The weather could be unpredictable and damage ships, wagons, or other ways of getting around. Being far from home also meant facing the unknown. These dangers shaped the stories people told and the beliefs they held.

CuratorialText: Objects themselves have rich histories and often undertook journeys of their own, whether through trade, gift giving, or collecting. For conservators, the journey of an item is embedded within its material presence; marks, scars, repairs, and other interventions describe the actions of others who have contributed to its present state. The process of conservation involves the synthesis of material evidence and its placement in time, not just to understand an item’s past but to envision its future preservation. Anthropologists have described the trajectories of material items in terms of their lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such notions are intuitively familiar to conservators, whose actions are fundamentally additive to an item’s journey, both materially and through time.
persona1_adapted:
Objects have their own rich histories. They often traveled from place to place through trade, gifts, or collecting. For conservators—people who care for and preserve objects—an item’s journey is written into its physical condition. Marks, scars, repairs, and other changes show what others have done to it over time.

Conservation work combines physical clues with a sense of time. It’s not just about understanding an object’s past. It’s also about planning for its future care. Anthropologists describe the paths of objects as their lives, biographies, or journeys. These ideas feel natural to conservators. Their actions add to an object’s story, both in material and in time.

CuratorialText: Individual travelers carried things with them that were essential for the journey itself. Clothing and equipment suitable to traversing long distances sometimes set them apart from local residents, and travelers carried personal effects and accessories, such as small books, drinking flasks, and protective amulets, to make the journey easier.
persona1_adapted: Travelers brought along items they needed for the trip. Their clothes and gear for long journeys sometimes made them look different from local people. They also carried personal belongings like small books, drinking flasks, and protective charms to make traveling easier.

CuratorialText: Travelers brought objects acquired on their journeys home with them as well. Holy objects acquired by pilgrims allowed them to bring the blessings of their pilgrimage back from their destination. Images made during travel—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey for future reflection. Souvenirs purchased during a trip also offered travelers a way of commemorating a journey and gave them something tangible on which to reflect. The advent of commercial global travel networks in many cultures and contexts was accompanied by a flood of photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other means of recording scientific and sentimental experiences abroad. Perhaps the most recognizable artifact of travel over the past 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from abroad, accruing the additional prestige of a foreign stamp and postmark.
persona1_adapted:
Travelers also brought home objects they found on their journeys. Pilgrims collected holy items. These items let them bring the blessings of their trip back from their destination.

Images made during travel—like sketches, paintings, or photos—captured the experience. Travelers could look back on them later. Souvenirs bought on a trip also helped people remember their journey. They gave travelers something real to hold and reflect on.

When global travel networks became common, a flood of photos, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other items followed. People used these to record both scientific discoveries and personal experiences abroad.

The most familiar travel item from the last 150 years is the picture postcard. People collected them as keepsakes or mailed them to family and friends from other countries. A foreign stamp and postmark added extra prestige to the card.

CuratorialText: Most travel we think of involves physically moving between places, but virtual travel (colloquially, “armchair travel”) that takes place in the mind spans time and cultures and is facilitated by books and works of art. Through focused contemplation of a travel account, a map, a cherished souvenir, or images of far-off places, memory and imagination combine to transport someone mentally and to simulate the experience of travel.
persona1_adapted: Most travel means moving your body from one place to another. But there is another kind of travel: virtual travel, sometimes called "armchair travel." This kind of travel happens in your mind. It can take you across time and between cultures. Books and works of art help make this possible. When you look closely at a travel story, a map, a special souvenir, or pictures of faraway places, your memory and imagination work together. They can carry you away in your thoughts and make you feel like you have really traveled.

CuratorialText: While it may not be the first thing we think of when looking at a work of art, environmental factors have shaped everything about it, from how it was made, to how it has been perceived and appreciated, to how it has been preserved for today’s audiences. Dive into the complex and interconnected relationships between art and the environment—from images of the natural world to recycling ancient jades—to see our collections in a whole new way.
persona1_adapted: When you look at a work of art, you might not think about the environment first. But the environment has shaped almost everything about it. It affected how the art was made. It changed how people saw and valued it. It also helped decide how the art was saved for us today. Explore the deep connections between art and the environment. You’ll see images of nature and even ancient jades that were reused. This will help you look at our collections in a whole new way.

CuratorialText: While environmental conditions differ from place to place, contact with nature has been a universal human experience. It’s therefore not surprising that natural motifs—plants, animals, water, the sky—adorn works of art across all cultures and are a consistent source of inspiration and meaning.
persona1_adapted: People everywhere experience nature, even though the environment looks different from place to place. That's why artists from all cultures use natural images—like plants, animals, water, and the sky—in their work. These motifs are a steady source of ideas and meaning.

CuratorialText: Depictions of landscapes can serve as valuable documentation of specific places, but they can also evoke strong emotions. Images of the natural world can remind us of the beauty and power of nature, evoking feelings of tranquility, wonder, awe, and contemplation.
persona1_adapted: Landscape paintings can show us what a place really looks like. They can also make us feel strong emotions. Pictures of nature can remind us of its beauty and power. These images might make us feel calm, amazed, or thoughtful.

CuratorialText: From the earliest Japanese poetry anthology, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of the Japanese landscape has been celebrated in literature and art. At first, landscapes were not an independent subject but were incorporated into the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, seasonal subjects (shikinami-e), and paintings of famous scenic or sacred sites (meisho-e). Beginning around the thirteenth century, possibly through the influence of the introduction of new modes of ink painting from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Landscapes of Japan were most often, but not exclusively, painted in the full color mode, which effectively expressed the seasonal changes that are inextricably linked with natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually chosen for varied and dramatic landscapes of China, which most Japanese artists understood through their study of imported paintings, and later, of painting manuals as well. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle tones of ink, landscapes by Japanese artists are imbued with a sense of intimate understanding and appreciation of the transient beauty and spiritual significance of the natural world.
persona1_adapted: From the earliest Japanese poetry book, made in the 700s, to today, people have celebrated the beauty of Japan’s landscapes in both art and writing. At first, landscapes were not the main subject. They appeared in the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, in scenes of the four seasons (called *shikinami-e*), and in paintings of famous or sacred places (called *meisho-e*). Around the 1200s, landscape became its own subject in Japanese painting. This may have happened because new ink painting styles came from China and Korea. Most Japanese landscapes were painted in full color. This style showed the changing seasons, which are closely tied to nature in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually used for dramatic Chinese landscapes. Most Japanese artists learned about these landscapes by studying imported paintings and later, painting manuals. Whether painted in rich colors or soft black ink, Japanese landscapes show a deep understanding and love for nature’s short-lived beauty and spiritual meaning.

CuratorialText: Travel to misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the sixteenth century on, accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi (bamboo flute), in this exploration of nature as an inspiration for Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.
persona1_adapted: Explore nature through the eyes of great Japanese artists. Starting in the 1500s, they painted misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches. As you view their work, listen to the sounds of the shakuhachi, a bamboo flute. This journey is guided by master musician Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens. Together, they show how nature inspires Japanese art and music.

CuratorialText: For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings not only provided aesthetic delight, but a landscape setting was often vested with meaning, which could range from providing a clue about the season of the painting to conveying more complex meanings. Seeing an image of figures in a remote landscape often referred to the key cultural and political concept of eremitism. The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian notion that government service was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was faced with serving a dynasty founded by usurping foreign rulers then, following the tradition of eremitism, a true gentleman refused government office. Instead, he became a mountain recluse, or at least assumed the demeanor of a hermit or rustic fisherman, to protest the corruption of moral order. Vested with meaning as a symbol of ethical reclusion, landscape motifs had poignant significance.
persona1_adapted:
For Chinese scholar-officials, looking at landscape paintings was more than just a beautiful experience. The setting of a landscape often carried a hidden meaning. It could tell you the season of the painting, or it could express deeper ideas.

For example, when a painting showed people in a faraway, wild landscape, it often referred to a key idea called **eremitism**. Eremitism is the practice of withdrawing from society to live alone, usually as a form of protest.

The scholar class, known as the **literati**, believed in a Confucian idea: serving the government was a high moral duty. But there were exceptions. If the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was asked to serve a dynasty founded by foreign rulers who had taken power unfairly, then a true gentleman would refuse the job. Following the tradition of eremitism, he would become a mountain recluse. Or at least, he would act like a hermit or a simple fisherman. This was his way of protesting the breakdown of moral order.

Because of this, landscape motifs—like mountains, rivers, or fishermen—were filled with meaning. They stood for ethical reclusion, or the choice to live apart from a corrupt world.

CuratorialText: Careful observation of nature did not only translate to depictions of real places. Artists used their knowledge of the natural world to create imagined landscapes as well.
persona1_adapted: Artists didn't just paint real places they saw. They also used what they knew about nature to create imaginary landscapes.

CuratorialText: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes embracing the category of “scholar’s rocks,” alternatively called “spirit stones.”  Scholar’s rocks consist of wondrously shaped and textured rocks, often of limestone, that are collected for placement in a designed garden or are brought indoors for display in a scholar’s study. The size can range from twelve feet or more to tiny enough to be enclosed in a fist. Philosophic and religious texts as well as poetry have a long history of singling out mountains and rocks for special consideration. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) notes, “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” At the same time, Daoists pictured mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in elixirs for infinite life. Placement of evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn a mundane space into a paradise, or at least into an environment conducive to cultivating personal longevity or to the pursuit of eternal life.
persona1_adapted:
Chinese art includes something called "scholar's rocks," also known as "spirit stones." These are special rocks, often made of limestone, with amazing shapes and textures. People collected them to place in gardens or inside a scholar's study. The rocks can be huge—over twelve feet tall—or small enough to fit in your hand.

For a long time, Chinese philosophy, religion, and poetry have focused on mountains and rocks. A saying from the time of Confucius (551–479 BCE) says, "The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains." Daoists saw mountains as safe places or paradises for immortal beings. They also used minerals in special drinks meant to give eternal life.

Putting these interesting rocks in gardens or homes turned ordinary spaces into paradise. Or at least, it created a place that helped people think about living a long life or even achieving immortality.

CuratorialText: In Chinese tradition, the educated class delighted in rocks as tangible embodiments of the powers of creation and cosmic transformation, and they sometimes rhapsodized about them as eternally faithful “friends.” Gazing upon them could encourage imaginary travel deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all engaged in collecting rocks, and painters often studied the rocks they collected, treating them as both muse and model for their landscape paintings.
persona1_adapted: In Chinese tradition, educated people loved rocks. They saw them as real examples of nature's creative power and change. Sometimes they called rocks their forever loyal "friends." Looking at rocks could make them feel like they were traveling deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all collected rocks. Painters often studied the rocks they owned. They used these rocks as both inspiration and a model for their landscape paintings.

CuratorialText: From intimate courtyards to monumental temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia at the confluence of the Tigris and the Euphrates in present-day Iraq. The very word “paradise” is derived from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, referred to as pardis. This type of enclosed garden became an integral part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries and also often included pavilions to provide shade and relief from the sun. According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (ca. 1050–256 BCE), consisted of enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often characterized by carefully positioned rocks and pools intended to recreate microcosms of nature at large. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design and incorporated rolling hills and languid ponds to underline harmony between humans and their surroundings. In the arid landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden plans depended on a series of interconnected pools and axial watercourses.
persona1_adapted:
Asia has played a major role in shaping how people design gardens and landscapes. These range from small private courtyards to huge temple gardens, tombs, and pleasure gardens.

The earliest known garden might be the Garden of Eden from the Bible. It may have been located in West Asia, where the Tigris and Euphrates rivers meet in present-day Iraq.

The word "paradise" comes from ancient Iran. There, people built walled gardens and hunting parks called *pardis*. These enclosed gardens became a key part of Persian architecture for many centuries. They often included pavilions—small buildings that offered shade and relief from the sun.

In China, the earliest gardens date back to the Zhou period (around 1050–256 BCE). Written records say these were enclosed hunting grounds for the royal family. Chinese gardens often featured carefully placed rocks and pools. These elements were meant to create a small version of nature.

Japanese gardens followed a more natural style. They included rolling hills and calm ponds. This design highlighted the harmony between people and their surroundings.

In the dry landscapes of West and South Asia, a common garden plan used a series of connected pools and straight water channels.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, art has served as a reflection of and response to environmental change. Sometimes this change is deliberately brought about through human beings adapting to and altering their surrounding environment. Cultural landscapes—including the ways that humans have built on, altered, and imagined natural environments—play a significant role in how we understand the world we inhabit.
persona1_adapted: Throughout history, art has shown how people react to changes in the environment. Sometimes, humans cause these changes on purpose as they adapt to and reshape the world around them. Cultural landscapes—the ways people have built on, changed, and pictured nature—help us understand the world we live in.

CuratorialText: Works of art can bear witness to the environmental changes that have taken place throughout recorded history. Depictions of animals from regions where those animals no longer exist, for example, can tell us about migrations or extinctions.
persona1_adapted: Works of art can show us how the environment has changed over time. For example, paintings of animals from places where those animals no longer live can tell us about their movements or extinction.

CuratorialText: Humans have always had an impact on the environments where they live, but the scale of that impact dramatically increased with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and advances in transportation sprung up to create a range of consumer goods and ways to travel with new ease and speed. Largely coal powered, these machines spewed pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.
persona1_adapted: People have always affected the places where they live. But the size of that impact grew a lot with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and new ways to travel appeared. They made many consumer goods and let people move faster and easier. These machines mostly ran on coal. They released pollution that artists like Whistler tried to show in their paintings.

CuratorialText: Similarly, the infusion of Western technology into Japan from the beginning of the Meiji era (1868–1912) spurred widespread visual documentation of the new developments. Railroad transportation, telegraphic communication, gas-fueled lighting, steam-powered river and ocean vessels, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings were crammed into the Japanese landscape over two decades.
persona1_adapted: When Western technology entered Japan at the start of the Meiji era (1868–1912), people began making many pictures and records of these new changes. Railroads, telegraphs, gas lights, and steam-powered boats appeared across Japan. New materials and designs for bridges and buildings also arrived. All of these developments filled the Japanese landscape in just twenty years.

CuratorialText: Contemporary artists are increasingly exploring the impact of climate change on the natural world and human communities, whether their goal is to raise awareness about the urgent need to address climate change, to highlight the social and political issues that are bound up in environmental degradation, or to envision alternative futures and inspire hope and action.
persona1_adapted: Many artists today focus on how climate change affects nature and people. Some want to show why we need to act fast. Others point out the social and political problems linked to environmental damage. Still others imagine different futures to inspire hope and action.

CuratorialText: The creation of art itself impacts and is impacted by the environment. Stones and precious metals need to be mined. Wood needs to be harvested. Clay is extracted from the earth. Plants need to be gathered. Many works of art make use of animal products, such as parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different production techniques require burning fuel sources for melting, firing, and glazing. The gathering of raw materials and their transformation into art objects is not without impact on the environment and has at times come at the expense of the exploitation of natural resources and people.
persona1_adapted: Making art affects the environment, and the environment affects art. To create art, people must mine stones and precious metals. They harvest wood. They dig clay from the earth. They gather plants. Many artworks use animal products like parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Some art-making methods need fuel for melting, firing, and glazing. Getting raw materials and turning them into art has an impact on nature. Sometimes, this has led to the overuse of natural resources and the mistreatment of people.

CuratorialText: While recycling may seem like a very recent phenomenon, materials have long been reused in the creation of works of art, whether due to the scarcity of materials or to the value placed on an existing object. For example, since jade was such a prestigious medium and a valuable commodity in ancient China, broken or out-of-fashion articles were not discarded but were instead recycled to make new objects. This appears to be especially true in the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly due to the scarcity of resources at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) objects were being transformed into decorated jewelry and other items that held more interest and meaning to the Shang people. All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a cong, which had lost its ritual significance by Shang times. It is noteworthy that the recycled cong used here represent different Stone Age cultures dispersed across north and south China.
persona1_adapted:
Recycling might seem like a new idea, but people have reused materials to make art for a very long time. Sometimes they did this because materials were hard to find. Other times, they reused objects because those objects were already valuable.

For example, in ancient China, jade was a precious material. When jade items broke or went out of style, people did not throw them away. Instead, they recycled the jade to make new objects. This happened a lot during the late Shang dynasty (around 1250–1050 BCE).

Archaeologists working at the Shang capital, Anyang, found something interesting. They think that because resources were scarce and demand was high, people took objects from the Stone Age (about 5000–1700 BCE) and turned them into decorated jewelry and other items. These new objects had more meaning for the Shang people.

The three pendants shown here were all made from a special type of Neolithic square tube called a *cong*. By Shang times, the *cong* had lost its original ritual meaning. So people cut it up to make these pendants. It is worth noting that the recycled *cong* used here came from different Stone Age cultures across north and south China.

CuratorialText: Environmental conditions can significantly impact the preservation of works of art. Factors such as temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the chemical and physical properties of materials in different ways, causing damage and degradation to objects over time. On a larger scale, major environmental changes such as temperature shifts and more destructive weather patterns can threaten heritage sites around the world. Conservators play a pivotal role in safeguarding the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research initiatives at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution acknowledges climate change as the existential threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.
persona1_adapted: The environment around us can affect how well artworks last. Things like temperature, humidity, light, and pollution can change the materials in art over time. These changes can cause damage and wear. On a bigger scale, shifts in weather and more extreme storms can put historic sites around the world at risk. Conservators are the people who help protect the objects in a museum’s collection. Read about how the National Museum of Asian Art works to conserve and study its art. The Smithsonian Institution says climate change is the biggest threat we face today. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s efforts to help the environment.

CuratorialText: A color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration, red has undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs our blood, forcing us to take notice. From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, learn about various ways that people have made and understood red through examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.
persona1_adapted: Red is a color full of life. It stands for passion, good luck, and celebration. Red has a strong energy that is hard to ignore. It catches your eye and makes you feel alert. People have made red from many different sources. These include shiny stones, reddish soil, insects, and minerals. In this exhibit, you will see examples from the museum’s collections. They show how people have created and understood red in many ways.

CuratorialText: Several metal compounds (that include iron, mercury, or lead) form commonly used historic colored pigments. These compounds may be found, mined, or produced. Iron compounds in earth pigments can have different colors. Red iron earths are readily found or can be manufactured and have been discovered in many applications, including pottery and paints.
persona1_adapted: Several metal compounds—like those containing iron, mercury, or lead—were often used to make colored pigments in the past. People could find these compounds in nature, dig them up, or create them. Iron compounds found in earth pigments can come in different colors. Red iron earths are easy to find or make, and they have been used in many things, such as pottery and paints.

CuratorialText: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are made from grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Synthetic versions have long been produced and are referred to as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, incorporated in lacquers, and adorns paintings and documents.
persona1_adapted: Mercury-based pigments come from grinding a red mineral called cinnabar. People have made synthetic, or man-made, versions of this pigment for a long time. These are called vermilion. Vermilion is a deep red color. Even though it is toxic, or poisonous, people used it in burial ceremonies. They also mixed it into lacquers, which are clear coatings that protect surfaces. And it appears in paintings and documents.

CuratorialText: While red lead has a naturally forming mineral equivalent (minimum), it was manufactured far more commonly for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions favorable to produce the desired colored material and minimizing the formation of others.
persona1_adapted: Red lead is a pigment that people usually make in a lab, even though it can also be found as a natural mineral. To make it, lead compounds are heated in a way that creates the right color and avoids making unwanted colors.

CuratorialText: Red copper oxide or cuprite is found as a degradation product on bronzes and forms by the reaction of the copper from the bronze and the atmosphere.
persona1_adapted: Cuprite is a red mineral that forms on bronze objects. It happens when copper from the bronze reacts with the air.

CuratorialText: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.
persona1_adapted: Plants and animals can also give us reds for art. People can take color from madder root or safflower. They clean it and get it ready to use in things like fabric or prints. How the dye or lake is made can change the final color. Scale insects, like lac and cochineal, are another source. People collected these bugs, took out the red color, and got it ready to use as a pigment (a coloring material) or dye. When possible, finding out what colorants were used in artworks can tell us about old trade routes. That's because these insects only lived in certain areas.

CuratorialText:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Red also occurred naturally in the form of precious and semi-precious stones, such as rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Prized for their color and rarity, they were valuable gifts and were often worn by elites or used to adorn luxury items.
persona1_adapted:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution.

Red also appeared in nature as precious and semi-precious stones. These included rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. People valued these stones for their color and rarity. They made valuable gifts. Elites often wore them or used them to decorate luxury items.

CuratorialText: Rubrication, the practice of writing portions of a text in red ink, helped visually underscore certain information and became particularly common in European manuscripts after the seventh century.
