A key example is the large, multi-part painting in the center of this gallery. It was made for nuns in Perugia. Today, it is called the Colonna Altarpiece, named after a later owner. This is the first time the whole set of paintings has been shown together since it was taken apart around 1663. The individual pieces were scattered after that.

Other important projects include a huge altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello. It was later destroyed in an earthquake. Only fragments and early drawings survive. There is also a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia. Here, you can see studies and the painting that formed its base.

CuratorialText: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece reveal the workshop practices that Raphael had absorbed during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Dazzling studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of media—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with a virtuosic command of technique. Drawings such as these likely served as his calling card and undoubtedly impressed his patrons.
persona1_adapted: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece show how Raphael learned workshop methods from his training and work with Perugino. These bright studies on paper also reveal how he used different materials—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with great skill. Drawings like these probably acted as his calling card and surely impressed his clients.

CuratorialText: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular murals there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery convey the electrifying novelty and arresting sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, promptly and seamlessly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.
persona1_adapted: Raphael might have moved to Florence because other painters couldn't stop talking about the huge mural designs by Leonardo and Michelangelo. These designs were called "cartoons," which are full-scale drawings used to plan large paintings. The artworks in this gallery show how exciting and powerful those unfinished projects were. While living in Florence from 1504 to 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo's sculptures and Leonardo's paintings and drawings. He quickly and smoothly borrowed parts of their styles and methods for his own work.

CuratorialText: Raphael also learned from his peers’ revolutionary approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s theories about “sketching quickly,” in the fervor of artistic inspiration, transformed Raphael’s methods. Asserting that the effort to finish drawings and puzzle out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet scrawling the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more artisanal way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.
persona1_adapted: Raphael also learned from other artists his age. They had new, bold ways of creating compositions. Leonardo’s ideas about “sketching quickly” changed how Raphael worked. Leonardo believed that spending too much time on details could kill fresh ideas. He said artists should work in “rough sketches.” This is like a poet writing a messy first draft of a poem, with crossed-out words. Before moving to Florence, Raphael made art in a more careful, step-by-step way. He drew one figure in detail at a time, just as his teacher Perugino had taught him. But after learning from Leonardo, Raphael started sketching freely. This helped him create more natural compositions. His art became full of movement and strong emotion.

CuratorialText: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art infused Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—reveal Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually animated a pyramidal composition (favored by Leonardo), which could seem static, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.
persona1_adapted:
Seeing the art of Leonardo and Michelangelo changed how Raphael worked. His paintings gained a stronger sense of space, bigger-looking figures, and more powerful emotions. This came from a huge amount of experimenting on paper.

Raphael made many drawings in a series. He started with quick sketches. Then he studied how to arrange figures using small clay or wax models. These steps show how Raphael became more careful and organized in his design process.

For example, he worked step by step to bring life to a pyramid-shaped composition. (Leonardo liked this shape.) A pyramid can look still or boring. But Raphael made it feel active by building a lively give-and-take of gestures and looks between the figures.

CuratorialText: In 1507 the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most significant work before his move to Rome, an altarpiece of Christ’s entombment for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia. The preliminary drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and demonstrate how he constructed a dramatic composition with a climactic narrative. Through arduous study, aided by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist evolved a static arrangement of figures into an emotionally arresting action scene. Raphael had absorbed Leonardo’s principle that the physical gestures of the body express the “motions” of the mind and soul.
persona1_adapted:
In 1507, the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most important work before moving to Rome. It was an altarpiece—a large painting placed above an altar in a church. This one showed the burial of Christ. He made it for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia.

His early sketches show he was a master storyteller. They reveal how he built a dramatic scene with a powerful, climactic moment. Through hard work and his knowledge of ancient Roman statues, Raphael turned a still group of figures into an emotionally gripping action scene. He had learned from Leonardo da Vinci that the body’s movements show the “motions” of the mind and soul.

CuratorialText: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a display of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary embraces the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Disseminated widely, the imagery inspired numerous medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and thoroughly transformed it.
persona1_adapted: For centuries, people have admired Raphael’s paintings of Mary and the baby Jesus. These works show a gentle, loving bond. Around the year 1300, Byzantine religious art from the Eastern Orthodox Church featured a type of Mary called the Virgin Eleousa. “Eleousa” comes from a Greek word meaning “compassionate.” In these images, Mary holds the Christ Child close as he touches his cheek to hers. This image spread widely and inspired many artists from the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael used this model as a starting point, but he changed it in his own way.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of vitality and innocent playfulness, especially while maintaining a superior command of anatomical realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book displayed here records funerary expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related complications in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of gynecology and obstetrics was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant mortality rates astronomically high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.
persona1_adapted:
Raphael was great at showing energy and innocent playfulness in his art. He also had a strong understanding of how the human body really looks. This is surprising when you think about what life was like for mothers and babies in his time.

The rare account book on display here lists funeral costs for Raphael’s mother. She died from childbirth problems in 1491. It also lists costs for two of his siblings who died as babies.

Back then, people knew very little about women’s health and childbirth. Pregnancy and giving birth were very dangerous. Many babies did not survive.

These facts help explain why paintings of the Madonna and Child were so popular. Renaissance artists wanted to make religious subjects feel more human. So tender images of mother and child became common in art.

CuratorialText: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely thwarted in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most feasible source of patronage among the city’s wealthy merchants, who commissioned portraits and tender devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his collaborators followed his example.
persona1_adapted: Raphael had big dreams, but he struggled to succeed in Florence's tough art market. His best chance for work came from the city's rich merchants. They paid him to paint portraits and gentle religious scenes, mostly of Mary and the baby Jesus. Raphael kept making these kinds of paintings for private buyers even after he moved to Rome in 1508. Many of his assistants copied his style.

CuratorialText: Raphael sought to bring the lauded ethereal physical beauty of the Madonna into radiant being. He was not immune to the convention, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, aristocratic lady with dainty facial features and golden hair, but he also strove for long-established Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as mirrors of her unique spiritual perfection. He infused his representations with a humanity and psychological presence revealed through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings display an arresting command of light, color, space, and geometry, and his renderings of infants are more naturalistic in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his peers.
persona1_adapted: Raphael wanted to show the Virgin Mary’s famous, heavenly beauty in his paintings. He followed a popular style that poets in his circle also used. This style favored elegant, noble women with delicate features and golden hair. But Raphael also aimed for older Christian ideals. Church writings, sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry had long praised Mary’s beauty and grace. They saw these as signs of her perfect spiritual nature. Raphael added a human touch to his images. He showed Mary’s feelings through her gestures and reactions. His paintings also show a strong understanding of light, color, space, and shape. And his baby Jesus figures look more natural in their size, poses, and expressions than those by many other artists of his time.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s portraits communicate a profound empathy and reflect years of exercising his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, concerted contemplation of the sitter. The regal pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a revolutionary model for the likeness of a luxuriously dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, torso erect, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the cultivated manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets. Raphael was an intimate friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of comportment and grace that prized sprezzatura, a studied nonchalance or insouciance.
persona1_adapted:
Raphael’s portraits show deep understanding of people’s feelings. They also reflect years of practice in drawing. This helped him create a close, thoughtful look at the person he painted.

The royal pose of Leonardo’s *Mona Lisa*, started in 1503, offered a new way to paint a richly dressed person. The pose shows a three-quarter view, with the head turned left, the body upright, and hands resting gracefully at the waist.

The elegant poses in Raphael’s work also suggest a desire for the polished manners of Italian Renaissance courts. They reflect the ideals of beauty praised by famous poets.

Raphael was a close friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529). Castiglione wrote a book about fashionable behavior at court. It promoted a model of grace and good manners that valued *sprezzatura*—a kind of studied effortlessness or casual coolness.

CuratorialText: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
persona1_adapted: During the Renaissance, many portraits of women were painted when they got engaged or married. Back then, marriage was often about money and family power, not romantic love. The women wore fancy clothes and jewelry to impress their future husband’s family while families discussed dowries and property. Poets and artists also often praised women’s looks. This helped create a culture where young women could become *innamorate*—meaning a man’s platonic love interest or actual mistress—among the wealthy and powerful men of the time.

CuratorialText: At some point in 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite court artist of Popes Julius II (r. 1503–13) and Leo X (r. 1513–21). An intriguing question is how exactly the twenty-five-year-old Raphael managed to supersede an earlier generation of painters working in the Vatican Palace and take control of the fresco decoration of the most important of the rooms, the Stanza della Segnatura. Surely artistic genius and personal savvy were key factors, but research shows that networks of political influence between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also paved the way, including support from his friend and countryman Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.
persona1_adapted:
In 1508, a young artist named Raphael arrived in Rome. He soon became the favorite court artist for two popes: Julius II and Leo X. Raphael was only twenty-five years old at the time. So how did he take over the most important painting job in the Vatican Palace? He was chosen to paint the frescoes in a room called the Stanza della Segnatura. This was a big deal because older, more experienced painters had been working there first.

Raphael’s artistic talent and personal charm certainly helped. But research shows that politics also played a role. Powerful people in Rome and in Raphael’s hometown of Urbino helped him get the job. One key supporter was his friend Donato Bramante, who was the pope’s favorite architect.

CuratorialText: Gathered here are Raphael’s studies for those Vatican frescoes: the School of Athens, with its gathering of philosophers, and the Disputa, representing Roman Catholic theology. These works show an artist in full command of the expressive potential of different drawing media. Rapidly sketched pen-and-ink sheets teem with the energy of ideas in the making, while studies in black chalk appear monumental despite their small size. In the next gallery, a digital video conveys the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.
persona1_adapted: Here are Raphael’s study drawings for his famous Vatican frescoes. One is called the *School of Athens*. It shows a group of philosophers. The other is the *Disputa*. It represents Roman Catholic theology. These drawings show an artist who fully controls the expressive power of different drawing materials. Quick pen-and-ink sketches are full of the energy of ideas being formed. Studies in black chalk look grand and powerful, even though they are small. In the next gallery, a digital video shows the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.

CuratorialText: By about 1510 Raphael was collecting accolades as the most visible and prolific painter at the papal court in Rome; meanwhile, shut away in the Sistine Chapel, the secretive Michelangelo labored to paint his ceiling frescoes, which would deeply influence the younger artist, who snuck in to see them. Drawings here relate to projects that occupied Raphael as he concluded his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began the second, in the Stanza di Eliodoro, which communicate with greater monumentality and dramatic force. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X ascended to the throne, some learned imagery vanished in favor of blunter papal messaging.
persona1_adapted:
By around 1510, Raphael was gaining fame as the most visible and productive painter at the pope’s court in Rome. At the same time, the secretive Michelangelo was working alone in the Sistine Chapel, painting his famous ceiling. These frescoes—paintings done on wet plaster—would deeply influence Raphael, who snuck in to see them.

Some drawings here show projects Raphael worked on as he finished his first set of frescoes in the Vatican Palace. He then began a second set in a room called the Stanza di Eliodoro. These later works feel bigger and more dramatic. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X became pope, some of the more complex, learned images were replaced with simpler messages that directly supported the pope’s power.

CuratorialText: Other drawings and prints illustrate Raphael’s collaboration with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a Bolognese printmaker and close friend. In their fruitful partnership, beginning in 1510, they created engravings of tremendous technical skill and artistic power. This medium enabled the reproduction of imagery in multiples and ensured a long afterlife for Raphael’s compositions. The prints were copied by artists well into the late 1800s and repurposed in other media, such as the maiolica plates in a nearby case.
persona1_adapted: Other drawings and prints show how Raphael worked with Marcantonio Raimondi (around 1480–1534). Raimondi was a printmaker from Bologna and a close friend of Raphael. Their partnership began in 1510. Together, they made engravings—a type of print made by carving into a metal plate. These works showed great skill and artistic power. Engraving allowed them to make many copies of the same image. This helped Raphael’s designs live on long after he made them. Artists copied these prints well into the late 1800s. They also reused the designs in other art forms, like the painted ceramic plates in a nearby case.

CuratorialText: In the next gallery is a digital video showing Raphael’s monumental fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The display provides a sense of scale and context for the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, the frescoes were painted by Raphael and his workshop in the nearly square rooms now known as the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, and in the gargantuan oblong Sala di Costantino (completed by his assistants after his death in 1520).
persona1_adapted:
In the next room, you'll find a digital video. It shows Raphael's huge wall paintings, called frescoes, in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. This video helps you see how big these paintings are and how they fit into the rooms. It also gives you a better idea of the many drawings in this exhibition that relate to them.

Between 1508 and 1524, Raphael and his team painted these frescoes. They worked in rooms that are almost square: the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio. They also painted in the very large, rectangular Sala di Costantino. Raphael died in 1520, so his assistants finished that room.

CuratorialText: The durable but difficult fresco technique requires expertise, the use of full-scale drawings (cartoons), and a willingness to anticipate challenges. Water-based colors applied to moist surface plaster (intonaco) dry and carbonate in a chemical process, permanently bonding the colors to the wall. Executed primarily by Raphael between 1508 and 1512, the frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura are the most famous and include the School of Athens, the Disputa, the personifications of Justice, and the Parnassus. In the following three rooms Raphael worked with a greater involvement of collaborators who followed his designs closely.
persona1_adapted:
Painting a fresco is a difficult but long-lasting art method. It takes skill, full-size drawings called cartoons, and careful planning. The artist uses water-based paint on wet plaster. As the plaster dries, a chemical reaction bonds the paint to the wall forever.

Raphael painted most of the frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura between 1508 and 1512. These are his most famous works. They include the School of Athens, the Disputa, the personifications of Justice, and the Parnassus. In the next three rooms, Raphael worked more with assistants. These helpers followed his designs closely.

CuratorialText: The paintings here represent the breathtaking visual eloquence of Raphael’s later years. New harmonies of color are transformed by a dark chiaroscuro reminiscent of Leonardo. Sculptural forms seem to project from dense compositions. Charged gazes, poses, and gestures communicate a sense of impending drama.
persona1_adapted: These paintings show the stunning visual power of Raphael’s later work. He used new color combinations, changed by a dark contrast of light and shadow that reminds us of Leonardo. Figures look like they are sculpted, pushing out from the crowded scenes. Intense looks, poses, and gestures tell us that something dramatic is about to happen.

CuratorialText: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X rained project after project upon Raphael. He produced few paintings independently; using his drawings as a guide, skilled assistants painted most of the frescoes in the Vatican’s Stanza dell’Incendio. He was often disparaged for this practice by rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop completed an astonishing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Executive help freed the artist to concentrate his creative energies on inventing new designs and exploring new forms. The involvement of workshop assistants in paintings and in certain types of drawings has inevitably led to debates about authorship. Labels in this exhibition attempt to indicate instances of their likely intervention, and a nearby display allows comparison of studies by Raphael and his fellow artists in an effort to characterize their individual styles.
persona1_adapted: Between 1514 and 1520, Pope Leo X gave Raphael one project after another. Raphael made very few paintings by himself. Instead, he drew the designs, and his skilled assistants painted most of the frescoes (wall paintings) in a room called the Stanza dell’Incendio in the Vatican. His rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo, often criticized him for this. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop finished an amazing number of large projects in his last six years. Having helpers freed Raphael to focus his creative energy on coming up with new designs and trying new forms. Because assistants worked on paintings and some drawings, experts often disagree about who made what. Labels in this exhibition show where assistants likely helped. A nearby display lets you compare studies by Raphael and his assistants to see how their styles differ.

CuratorialText: The imperious Pope Leo X charged Raphael with the daunting task of designing a set of monumental tapestries to hang in the Sistine Chapel during special occasions—a project that put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo and his ceiling frescoes above. Starting in 1515, Raphael began drawing small preliminary studies. Aided by workshop assistants, he then painted in color gouaches on paper the enormous full-scale cartoons that would guide the weavers. Woven in Brussels using rich materials, the stratospherically expensive tapestries contributed to the bankruptcy of Leo’s papacy. With their inventive compositions, striking color harmonies, and monumental scale, they were soon the envy of monarchs across Europe. Kings commissioned second editions woven from Raphael’s cartoons, and the set owned by King Philip II of Spain (1527–1598) is represented here by three stunning examples.
persona1_adapted:
Pope Leo X gave Raphael a huge task. He asked Raphael to design a set of large tapestries for the Sistine Chapel. These tapestries would hang there during special events. This project put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo, who had painted the famous ceiling frescoes above.

Raphael started in 1515. He drew small study sketches first. Then, with help from his workshop assistants, he painted full-size color guides on paper. These guides, called cartoons, showed the weavers exactly what to make.

The tapestries were woven in Brussels using rich materials. They cost an enormous amount of money. In fact, they were so expensive that they helped cause Pope Leo's papacy to go bankrupt.

The tapestries had creative designs, striking colors, and a huge scale. Soon, kings all across Europe wanted them. Many rulers ordered their own copies made from Raphael's cartoons. The set owned by King Philip II of Spain is shown here with three stunning examples.

CuratorialText: As the tapestry designs progressed, the Raphael team largely took over the fresco work in the Vatican Palace, endeavoring to complete the Stanza dell’Incendio and assisting Raphael as he began to conceive decorations for the final space. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) acted as his studio foreman and went on to enjoy a brilliant independent career.
persona1_adapted: As the tapestry designs moved forward, Raphael’s team took over most of the fresco painting in the Vatican Palace. They worked to finish the Stanza dell’Incendio, a room with wall paintings. They also helped Raphael plan decorations for the last room. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) was Raphael’s studio manager. He later had a very successful career on his own.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s last and largest altarpiece is the enormous Transfiguration, commissioned by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (the future Pope Clement VII) around 1517. Spurred by competition with artistic rivals, Raphael produced a work markedly more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. Although its execution was interrupted by his untimely death in 1520, the Transfiguration became Raphael’s artistic testament. The main event is Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, during which he transformed before the stunned apostles Peter, James, and John to reveal his divinity.
persona1_adapted:
Raphael’s last and largest altarpiece is the huge *Transfiguration*. An altarpiece is a painting placed behind an altar in a church. Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (who later became Pope Clement VII) asked Raphael to paint it around 1517.

Raphael wanted to outdo his artistic rivals. So he made this work much more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. He died suddenly in 1520 before finishing it. Even so, the *Transfiguration* became Raphael’s final artistic statement.

The main scene shows Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor. During this event, Christ changed form to reveal his divine nature. This happened in front of the amazed apostles Peter, James, and John.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s studies for the Transfiguration, displayed in the niche at left, represent the culmination of his career as a draftsman. They are reunited here to show the range of his design process. Raphael used red chalk primarily when drawing nude models from life. He reserved black chalk for the full-scale studies of apostles’ heads and hands, a type of drawing called an “auxiliary cartoon” because the design starts from a dotted chalk outline transferred from another sheet.
persona1_adapted: Raphael’s drawings for the Transfiguration are on display in the niche on the left. These works show the peak of his skill as a designer. They are brought together here to reveal how he created his art. Raphael mostly used red chalk when drawing nude models from real life. He used black chalk for large studies of the apostles’ heads and hands. This type of drawing is called an “auxiliary cartoon.” The name comes from the design process: the artist starts with a dotted chalk outline copied from another sheet.

CuratorialText: Raphael rarely interrupted his projects for Popes Julius II and Leo X to make room for other commissions. The notable exception was his powerful friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met in about 1510. Born in Siena, Chigi became the richest banker in Europe, and his legendary wealth financed the popes’ cultural and military enterprises. Raphael’s work for Chigi included fresco paintings, as well as designs for his remodeled stables and the multimedia ensembles of his two chapels.
persona1_adapted: Raphael rarely stopped working for Popes Julius II and Leo X to take on other jobs. But he made an important exception for his powerful friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met around 1510. Chigi was born in Siena and became the richest banker in Europe. His huge fortune helped pay for the popes’ art projects and military efforts. For Chigi, Raphael created fresco paintings—art made by painting on wet plaster—and also designed his remodeled stables and the artwork for his two chapels.

CuratorialText: Displayed here are a variety of Raphael’s preparatory drawings for Chigi’s projects. In chalk studies of nude figures, Michelangelo’s language of contorted poses and powerful muscularity is transformed with an effortless grace and the sensuality of living flesh. The drawings also allude to the legacy of Raphael’s approach to the human figure, which led to the style known as Mannerism after his death. Male and female forms here anticipate prototypical Mannerist poses—as with Raphael’s primeval personification of an earthquake in a Sistine Chapel tapestry design, which spawned the figural vocabulary that his former assistant Giulio Romano would exploit to spectacular effect in his later frescoes.
persona1_adapted: Here are a variety of Raphael’s early drawings for Chigi’s projects. In chalk studies of nude figures, Raphael took Michelangelo’s twisted poses and strong muscles and gave them a graceful, lifelike feel. The drawings also show how Raphael’s style of drawing the human body led to a new style called Mannerism after his death. Male and female figures here show early Mannerist poses. For example, Raphael’s drawing of a person representing an earthquake—for a tapestry in the Sistine Chapel—created a set of poses that his former assistant Giulio Romano later used in his own dramatic wall paintings.

CuratorialText: Rome and its ancient monuments transformed Raphael’s art, and his work likewise changed the face of Rome. Following his arrival in the Eternal City in 1508, he threw himself into archaeological studies and made drawings of monuments that demonstrate a nearly scientific rigor. Pope Leo X tasked Raphael with creating an enormous drawing of ancient Rome, “making those members that are entirely ruined and are completely invisible correspond with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter to Leo, written on Raphael’s behalf, attests to the artist’s deep archaeological knowledge and technical skill, including a highly original method for using a magnetic compass in conducting surveys of ruins.
persona1_adapted:
Rome and its ancient buildings changed how Raphael made art. In the same way, his art changed how Rome looked. After he arrived in the city in 1508, he started studying old ruins. He made drawings of monuments that were very careful and exact—almost like a scientist.

Pope Leo X asked Raphael to create a huge drawing of ancient Rome. The pope wanted him to “connect the parts that are completely ruined and invisible with the parts that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter written for Raphael to the pope shows how much he knew about archaeology and how skilled he was. It also describes a clever new way he used a magnetic compass to measure ruins.

CuratorialText: Around 1503 Pope Julius II had charged Bramante, Raphael’s mentor and close friend from Urbino, to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica that would convey the majesty of the papacy in the aesthetic language of ancient Rome. Drawings and prints here illuminate Raphael’s career as an architect after he took over this massive project upon Bramante’s death. In 1517 Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini, designed by Bramante and now destroyed but pictured in two works here, where he lived out his final years in Rome as the “prince of painters.”
persona1_adapted: Around 1503, Pope Julius II asked Bramante to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica. Bramante was Raphael’s mentor and close friend from Urbino. The pope wanted the building to show the power of the papacy using the style of ancient Rome. The drawings and prints here show how Raphael became an architect after Bramante died and took over this huge project. In 1517, Raphael bought a house called the Palazzo Caprini. Bramante had designed it, but it is now destroyed. Two works here show what it looked like. Raphael lived there for his final years in Rome, known as the “prince of painters.”

CuratorialText: The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display. The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
persona1_adapted:
The Met got its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy. Calligraphy is the art of beautiful handwriting. Together, these works form one of the most complete collections in the world. The collection covers fifteen hundred years of art and includes many different types, techniques, and styles. It has become a key resource for studying Chinese painting and calligraphy.

This exhibition shows a rich selection of works from the collection. They are arranged mostly in order of when they were made. The last room tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018) and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). Fong was the head of the museum’s Asian Art department from 1971 to 2000. As a young calligraphy prodigy—a child with amazing talent—in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display focuses on a group of fans that Li painted and wrote on. These fans were meant to pass on the classic models of the tradition from teacher to student. They were brought to the United States in 1949 and have been treasured by the Fong family ever since. Now, these fans are shown to the public for the first time.

CuratorialText: The hand-held fan was an unexpected muse for some of the most innovative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became hugely popular across many levels of society during this period, serving as functional and fashionable objects of adornment and communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only featured this feminine accessory in their work but also adopted it as an experimental format for their art. Fanmania investigates why avant-garde artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and appropriation. Artists were attracted to the semicircular form for myriad reasons, including fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in formal and technical innovation. Displaying more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition features painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia as well as artworks that depict women wielding fans to explore the phenomenon of “fanmania.”
persona1_adapted:
In 19th-century Europe, some of the most creative artists found inspiration in an unexpected object: the hand-held fan. Fans became very popular across all levels of society during this time. They were both useful and fashionable—people used them to cool off, to show off their style, and even to communicate without words.

Well-known French Impressionists like Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro did more than just paint fans in their pictures. They also used fans as a new, experimental surface for their own art. The exhibition *Fanmania* explores why these forward-thinking artists were drawn to fans. It also looks at themes like gender roles, dating and romance, shopping and spending, and borrowing ideas from other cultures.

Artists liked the fan's half-circle shape for many reasons. Some were fascinated by fans from Asia and Spain. Others saw a chance to sell their work to a wider audience. Still others were interested in trying new techniques and forms.

This multimedia exhibition brings together more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection. You'll see painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia, as well as artworks that show women holding fans. Together, they help explain the craze known as "fanmania."

CuratorialText: During the nineteenth century, handheld fans achieved unprecedented popularity throughout European society. A newfound artistic appreciation for these objects also emerged, fueled by cultural crazes for all things Japanese and Spanish as well as by exhibitions and publications devoted to fan making and its history. “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans,” wrote the author Octave Uzanne in 1882. For the fourth Impressionist exhibition of 1879, Edgar Degas envisioned an entire gallery devoted to fans. Though the fan-shaped paintings his colleagues submitted were ultimately dispersed throughout the show, they nevertheless elicited strong responses from the public, including bewilderment, as illustrated below. Today, these works continue to raise questions—mainly, why did ambitious European artists adopt this accessory as a format for their work? Bringing together artworks from across The Met, Fanmania explores the reasons artists were drawn to this semicircular form, including its commercial potential, its fashionability, and the opportunities it offered for formal and technical innovation. Although avant-garde fans were primarily designed for display rather than use, they retain many of the associations of functional fans. Themes of gender, courtship, appropriation, and experimentation unfold in this examination of fans as objects of adornment, decoration, and communication in the nineteenth century.
persona1_adapted:
During the 1800s, handheld fans became extremely popular across Europe. People also started to see these fans as art. This new interest was sparked by crazes for Japanese and Spanish culture, as well as by shows and books about fan-making and its history. In 1882, writer Octave Uzanne said, "Almost all the great artists of the century have helped decorate the best fans."

For the fourth Impressionist exhibition in 1879, artist Edgar Degas planned an entire room filled with fans. The fan-shaped paintings his fellow artists made were spread throughout the show, but they still got strong reactions from the public. Some people were confused, as shown in the picture below. Today, these artworks still raise questions—mainly, why did serious European artists choose this accessory as a format for their work?

Bringing together artworks from across The Met, *Fanmania* explores why artists liked this half-circle shape. Reasons include its potential to sell well, its fashion appeal, and the chance it gave for new techniques and styles. Although these avant-garde (experimental) fans were mostly made for display, not for use, they still carry many of the meanings of real fans. Themes of gender, romance, borrowing from other cultures, and trying new things come to light in this look at fans as objects of decoration, beauty, and communication in the 1800s.

CuratorialText: Handheld folding fans are multimedia objects that involve many types of artistry and artisanal skill. This show focuses primarily on the role of painters and printmakers in the production of the fan’s semicircular leaf. In Europe, prior to 1700, most of these designs were unsigned. In the nineteenth century, major artists grew increasingly interested in making fans and began signing their work, as did preeminent fan-making houses, which evolved out of earlier guilds. Some of the precedents for these practices are on display here.
persona1_adapted: Handheld folding fans are objects that combine different kinds of art and craft skills. This exhibition looks mainly at the painters and printmakers who created the fan's semicircular leaf. In Europe, before 1700, most fan designs were not signed. In the 1800s, famous artists became more interested in making fans and started signing their work. So did the top fan-making companies, which grew out of earlier guilds. Some examples of these earlier practices are shown here.

CuratorialText: Historically, European fans have been most closely associated with the eighteenth century, when their use became widespread. The examples here show the various roles fans played, including as tools for flirtation, amusement, education, propaganda, observation, and masquerade. Fan leaves communicated through their subjects and style, while users brought them to life through their gestures. Women were not only the consumers of fans but also among their producers, as fan making was considered an art form appropriate for female practitioners, whether amateur or professional.
persona1_adapted: Fans in Europe are most linked to the 1700s, when many people started using them. The fans on display here show how they served many purposes. People used them to flirt, have fun, learn, spread ideas, watch others, and hide their identity at masquerades. The painted scenes on fans sent messages through their images and style. The people holding them brought those scenes to life with their hand movements. Women bought and used fans, but they also helped make them. Fan making was seen as a proper art form for women, whether they were beginners or experts.

CuratorialText: Handheld fans found perhaps their most prominent public display in the opera house. The atmosphere of spectatorship and performance—present both onstage and among the audience—heightened attention to fans in the hands of female theatergoers. The emphasis on seeing and being seen even gave rise to fan designs that incorporated opera glasses, or lorgnettes (from the French lorgner, “to ogle”), and small mirrors. A lorgnette fan is on view in the case nearby. Fan makers also developed a genre of fans devoted to the opera. These fans incorporated scores, lyrics, and homages to celebrated composers.
persona1_adapted: Handheld fans became very popular at the opera house. People went to the opera to watch the show—and to watch each other. This made fans a key part of how women in the audience looked and were seen. Some fans even had small binoculars called lorgnettes (from a French word meaning "to ogle") or tiny mirrors attached to them. You can see a lorgnette fan in the case nearby. Fan makers also created special fans just for the opera. These fans featured music scores, song lyrics, and tributes to famous composers.

CuratorialText: As theatergoing grew more popular later in the century, Impressionist artists seized on the novel subject of spectators in the theater loge. The fashionability and ubiquity of fans at the opera had become fully apparent by the early 1880s, as evidenced by the cartoon below.
persona1_adapted: As more people went to the theater in the late 1800s, Impressionist artists became interested in a new subject: audience members sitting in theater boxes. By the early 1880s, it was clear that fans were both fashionable and everywhere at the opera. The cartoon below shows this.

CuratorialText: Movement is an essential aspect of the handheld fan. The nineteenth-century critic Charles Blanc wrote that the fan is “above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness.” The expressive qualities of a fan’s motions are among the ways it can convey meaning and reveal the user’s personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall (or be deliberately dropped) at a particularly opportune moment before a potential suitor.
persona1_adapted:
Movement is a key part of how a handheld fan works. In the 1800s, the critic Charles Blanc wrote that a fan is "above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness." In simpler terms, a fan is a tool for making graceful motions, all while pretending to just stir the air for a breeze.

The way a fan moves can express a lot. It can show the user's personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall—or be dropped on purpose—at just the right moment in front of someone the user likes.

CuratorialText: Artists in the late nineteenth century understood the fan as a moving object and attempted to capture its dynamism in myriad ways. The fleeting and ephemeral nature of its movement surely made it an appealing subject for the Impressionists. Fan designs frequently incorporate metallic paints, gold leaf, feathers, spangles, and sequins, the visual impacts of which are heightened when in motion.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1800s, artists saw the fan as a moving object. They tried to show its energy in many different ways. The fan’s quick, short-lived motion made it a perfect subject for the Impressionists. These artists often used shiny materials like metallic paints, gold leaf, feathers, and sequins in their fan designs. The visual effects of these materials become stronger when the fan is in motion.

CuratorialText: The term “Japonisme” was first used by French critics in the early 1870s to describe the passion for Japanese art and culture they observed among their contemporaries. Japanese goods became increasingly available in Europe after the United States forced Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54. The 1867 and 1878 Universal Exhibitions in Paris were pivotal events in Europeans’ exposure to the arts of Japan. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected such works, including fans, acquiring them from newly established specialist dealers and shops. By the late 1880s, Japanese fans were imported in the millions to France, where they were used as women’s accessories and decoration.
persona1_adapted: The term "Japonisme" was first used by French critics in the early 1870s. It described the strong interest in Japanese art and culture that many people had at that time. Japanese goods became easier to find in Europe after the United States forced Japan to open its ports to world trade in 1853–54. Two big world fairs in Paris, in 1867 and 1878, helped Europeans see Japanese art for the first time. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected Japanese items, like fans. They bought them from new shops and dealers that specialized in Japanese goods. By the late 1880s, millions of Japanese fans were imported to France. People used them as fashion accessories for women and as home decoration.

CuratorialText: European artists focused on the aesthetic value of these objects and borrowed from them liberally, often with little understanding of their original contexts. Today, some would call this cultural appropriation, though subtleties of interpretation varied. Certainly, the history of Japonisme is fraught, but the admiration for Japanese fans freed European artists to imagine new techniques and strategies for their art.
persona1_adapted: European artists admired the beauty of these objects and used them freely in their own work. Often, they did not fully understand the original meaning or history behind them. Today, some people would call this cultural appropriation—taking elements from another culture without proper respect. The story of Japonisme is complicated, but the love for Japanese fans inspired European artists to try new methods and ideas in their art.

CuratorialText: In the nineteenth century, Spain became a center of fan production at the same time that the country’s tourism industry emerged and its culture was increasingly exported throughout Europe. Reporting from his travels to the Iberian Peninsula in the early 1840s, the French writer Théophile Gautier claimed, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan’s prominent role in Spanish flamenco dance is likely another reason it became inextricably linked with Spain in the nineteenth-century imagination, and remains so today. In Paris at mid-century, Spanish culture was in vogue, a phenomenon known as Hispagnolisme. Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, exerted considerable influence on fashion and taste. Spanish dance troupes and musicians performed often in the capital, and avant-garde artists, such as Edouard Manet, took them up as subjects. For some artists who made fans in this period, it was Spain that they had foremost in mind. Manet and Degas both chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.
persona1_adapted:
In the 1800s, Spain became a major place for making fans. At the same time, tourism in Spain was growing, and Spanish culture was spreading across Europe. In the early 1840s, a French writer named Théophile Gautier traveled to Spain. He wrote, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” 

Fans also played a big role in Spanish flamenco dance. This is another reason why fans became strongly linked to Spain in people’s minds during the 1800s—and they still are today. 

Around the middle of the 1800s, Spanish culture became very popular in Paris. This trend was called Hispagnolisme (a love for Spanish things). The Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, had a big influence on fashion and style. Spanish dance groups and musicians often performed in Paris. Avant-garde artists—those who tried new, bold ideas—like Edouard Manet, painted these Spanish performers as subjects. 

For some artists who made fans during this time, Spain was their main inspiration. Both Manet and Degas chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.

CuratorialText: The theme of the bourgeois woman with her fan in a domestic setting proliferated in art of the last quarter of the nineteenth century. In his 1882 book The Fan, the French writer Octave Uzanne described the role of fans to his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether at home or on ventures out, the handheld fan served as a companion, trusted protector, and elegant accoutrement.
persona1_adapted: The image of a middle-class woman holding a fan at home became very common in art during the late 1800s. In his 1882 book *The Fan*, French writer Octave Uzanne explained the fan's role to his female readers. He wrote, "In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante." Whether at home or out on trips, the handheld fan acted as a companion, a trusted protector, and a stylish accessory.

CuratorialText: As fashion accessories, fans signaled facets of their user’s identity through their materials and stylistic form as well as in the way they were handled. “One wave of the Fan is sufficient by itself to distinguish between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a plebeian,” remarked one female observer cited by Uzanne. A Japanese uchiwa fan in the hands of the Impressionist Berthe Morisot or a plumed fan held by one of Mary Cassatt’s sitters displayed their alignment with current taste and confirmed their social status.
persona1_adapted: Fashion fans were more than just accessories. They showed parts of a person's identity through their materials, style, and how they were used. One woman, quoted by a writer named Uzanne, said, "One wave of the fan is enough to tell a princess from a countess, or a marchioness from a commoner." For example, when the Impressionist artist Berthe Morisot held a Japanese uchiwa fan, or when one of Mary Cassatt's subjects held a feathered fan, it showed they followed current fashion. It also confirmed their place in society.

CuratorialText: Fans were among the first souvenirs, used since the seventeenth century to commemorate events and to remember visits to important sites. As travel increased in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, fans became popular as small, practical, and artistic objects that could serve as mementos. The Universal Exhibitions of the second half of the nineteenth century played a crucial role in promoting the French fan industry and exposing the European public to fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were produced for each of these events, providing visitors with information and maps while also recording the remarkable buildings erected specifically for these occasions.
persona1_adapted:
Fans were some of the first souvenirs. People used them as early as the 1600s to remember special events and visits to important places. As travel became more common in the 1700s and 1800s, fans grew popular. They were small, useful, and artistic objects that could serve as keepsakes.

The Universal Exhibitions in the late 1800s helped boost the French fan industry. They also introduced European audiences to fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were made for each of these events. They gave visitors information and maps, and they also showed off the amazing new buildings built just for those exhibitions.

CuratorialText: This monumental work was one of a series of carved architectural supports designed for the exterior courtyard of a Yoruba palace. It was commissioned by a king from the most renowned master sculptor in the history of Yoruba art, Olowe of Ise. The two-tiered composition embodies a formal dynamism that established Olowe's exceptional artistic reputation. Its principal personage, an equestrian warrior, is depicted frontally above a female caryatid with arms raised in three-quarter view. Through this contrast in their alignment, the figures at once reflect distinct attitudes while relating to each other fluidly. The compressed style of the upper half also contrasts with the greater degree of openness below. At the base two smaller figures radiate outward at oblique angles. In addition to the inventive dynamism of their overall design, works by Olowe are noted for the attention given to surface detail. The mounted warrior holds a spear and pistol in either hand, and his vest, saddle, and horse's headgear are articulated through deeply carved linear motifs. While innovative in its formal interpretation, the subject of this work is a classic emblem of regional leadership. In Yoruba art such equestrian figures identify their patrons with martial conquest achieved through physical might.
persona1_adapted:
This large carved post was part of a set made for the courtyard of a Yoruba palace. A king ordered it from the most famous master carver in Yoruba art history, Olowe of Ise.

The carving has two levels. Its design shows a lively sense of movement, which made Olowe famous. The main figure is a warrior on a horse, shown facing forward. Below him is a female figure with her arms raised, shown at a slight angle. This contrast in their poses shows different attitudes, yet they still connect smoothly.

The top half of the carving is tightly packed, while the bottom half is more open. At the base, two smaller figures lean outward at angles. Besides the creative energy of the overall design, Olowe's works are known for their careful surface details. The mounted warrior holds a spear in one hand and a pistol in the other. His vest, saddle, and the horse's headgear are decorated with deep, carved lines.

Even though the style is new and inventive, the subject is a classic symbol of local leadership. In Yoruba art, these horse-and-rider figures link their owners to military victory won through physical strength.

CuratorialText: This saltcellar is both an extraordinary example of skilled workmanship and an artifact that epitomizes a singularly important convergence of cultures. In the second half of the fifteenth century, Portuguese explorers and traders were impressed by the considerable talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. As a result, they were inspired to commission works of this kind for their patrons, which ingeniously combine both European and African aesthetics and forms. During this period, salt and pepper were costly commodities and elaborate receptacles were appropriate for their storage in princely homes. This work contains imagery relating to early Temne and Bullom worldviews. The four snakes, associated with mystical wealth, appear to confront four growling dogs. According to regional traditions, dogs are considered spiritually astute animals able to see spirits and ghosts that are invisible to humans. This depiction of the dogs, with teeth bared, hair bristling, and ears laid back, may relate to that ability. However, the level of animation in this scene could also derive from chivalric hunting scenes in European woodcuts, which were furnished to local African artists by their European patrons.
persona1_adapted:
This saltcellar shows amazing skill in carving. It also marks an important meeting of cultures.

In the late 1400s, Portuguese explorers and traders saw how talented West African ivory carvers were. They were impressed. So they asked these artists to make special objects for their wealthy patrons back in Europe. These works mixed European and African styles and shapes in clever ways.

Back then, salt and pepper were expensive. Rich families kept them in fancy containers like this one.

The carvings on this saltcellar come from early Temne and Bullom beliefs. Four snakes face four growling dogs. Snakes were linked to spiritual wealth. Dogs were seen as animals that could see spirits and ghosts that people cannot see. The dogs here show their teeth, bristle their fur, and flatten their ears. That may show their special sight.

But the lively scene might also come from European hunting pictures. Those pictures were given to African artists by their European customers.

CuratorialText: By the early nineteenth century, Chokwe chiefs in the savanna of present-day Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola had become active trading partners with merchants from Europe and the New World. Profiting from commerce in ivory, rubber, wax, and African slaves, they emerged as important regional rulers whose prestige and power was reflected in the art they commissioned for their courts at this time. This figure depicts a Chokwe chief, or mwanangana, and epitomizes the balance of power and refinement that is characteristic of Chokwe court art. His large, spreading hands, muscular shoulders, and aggressive posture epitomize the strength and vigor of a ruler. A bulging, domelike forehead and large eyes suggest mental acuity and keen eyesight. The sweeping curves and swelling volumes of the distinctive headdress, rendered here in wood, depict an actual Chokwe crown made of cloth-covered basketry. Horns of the tiny duiker antelope, which bear associations with hunting and the dangerous, mysterious practices of medicine and sorcery, are placed on the sides of the headdress. The chief holds a lamellophone, or "thumb piano," an instrument whose music often accompanies recitations of oral histories and the praise songs of important individuals. Instrument in hand, the chief is cast as a repository and guardian of historical and cultural knowledge. While depicting a nineteenth-century Chokwe chief, this sculpture also evokes the culture hero Cibinda Ilunga, a legendary hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. Cibinda Ilunga is said to have introduced new principles of governance to the Chokwe peoples, and figures such as this one honor the productive union of these two cultures.
persona1_adapted:
By the early 1800s, Chokwe chiefs in the grasslands of what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola were trading regularly with merchants from Europe and the Americas. They made money by selling ivory, rubber, wax, and enslaved people. These chiefs became powerful regional rulers, and their wealth and status showed in the art they ordered for their courts.

This sculpture shows a Chokwe chief, also called a *mwanangana*. It captures the mix of power and elegance found in Chokwe court art. The chief’s large, open hands, strong shoulders, and bold pose show the strength and energy of a leader. A big, dome-shaped forehead and large eyes suggest intelligence and sharp vision.

The tall, curved headdress with its rounded shapes is made of wood. It copies a real Chokwe crown made from cloth-covered basketry. On the sides of the headdress are horns from a tiny duiker antelope. These horns are linked to hunting and to the dangerous, mysterious practices of medicine and sorcery.

The chief holds a lamellophone, also called a “thumb piano.” Music from this instrument often goes with spoken histories and songs that praise important people. By holding the instrument, the chief is shown as a keeper and protector of history and culture.

Although this sculpture shows a 1800s Chokwe chief, it also brings to mind a legendary figure named Cibinda Ilunga. He was a hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. According to tradition, Cibinda Ilunga taught the Chokwe people new ways of ruling. Works like this one honor the blending of these two cultures.

CuratorialText: The Dogon live in the elevated rocky heights of Mali's Bandiagara Escarpment. They rely on agriculture and manage to wrest subsistence crops from poor soil in an area that receives little rain. This monumental figure is one of the largest Dogon sculptures known. The region's dry climate has preserved Dogon works far longer than is usual for African wood sculpture, and radiocarbon testing of this work suggests that it may date as far back as the fifteenth century. Dogon sculpture is primarily concerned with the spirits responsible for the fertility of both land and people. These include a family's real and mythical ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. While the exact functions and meanings of individual works often remain obscure, scholars agree that Dogon sculptures were created for shrines. The most distinctive subject rendered by Dogon sculptures is that of a single figure standing with raised arms. This posture has usually been interpreted as a gesture of prayer—an effort to link earth and heavens—and it has been suggested that it may represent an appeal for rain. This masterpiece of Dogon figurative art depicts a man with a well-modeled body in a naturalistic stance. The artist has carefully rendered the figure's musculature, anatomical detail, and body ornament. His stylized beard identifies him as an elder and an individual whose age and experience entitle him to participate in the most important religious, political, and social affairs of Dogon society. The figure wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that indicate his status, as well as a belt and neck pendants resembling leather talismans that also suggest his spiritual importance.
persona1_adapted:
The Dogon people live high up on the rocky cliffs of Mali’s Bandiagara Escarpment. They farm the land to grow food, even though the soil is poor and rain is scarce. This large statue is one of the biggest Dogon sculptures ever found. The dry climate in the region has helped preserve Dogon wooden artworks much longer than usual for African wood sculptures. Tests on this piece suggest it may be from as early as the 1400s.

Dogon sculpture focuses on spirits that help the land and people be fertile. These spirits include a family’s real and mythical ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. We don’t always know the exact purpose of each sculpture, but experts agree that Dogon statues were made for shrines.

The most common subject in Dogon sculpture is a single figure standing with raised arms. This pose is usually seen as a gesture of prayer—a way to connect earth and sky. Some scholars think it may be a plea for rain.

This masterpiece shows a man with a well-shaped body in a natural pose. The artist carefully carved the figure’s muscles, body details, and ornaments. His styled beard marks him as an elder—someone whose age and experience allow him to take part in the most important religious, political, and social events of Dogon society. The figure wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that show his status. He also has a belt and neck pendants that look like leather charms, suggesting his spiritual importance.

CuratorialText: When works from Equatorial Africa in this refined style began to enter Western consciousness in the early twentieth century, they were a great enigma to art critics. Many speculated about the sources of their exotic aesthetic and even proposed possible Asian influence, though the art form was in fact indigenous to southern Gabon. Such masks were worn by virtuosic male performers of a stilt dance called "mukudj," which involved towering impressively while executing complex choreography and astonishing feats of acrobatics. The creator of a "mukudj" mask would attempt to capture the likeness of the most beautiful woman in his community. The subject of this particular idealized and stylized portrait was embellished in classic nineteenth-century fashion with a coiffure composed of a central lobe and two lateral tresses and with cicatrization motifs on the forehead and temples. Kaolin taken from riverbeds, which was associated with healing and with a spiritual, ancestral realm of existence, was applied to the surface of the face. By using this material, the artist both celebrated the beauty of a mortal woman and transformed her into a transcendent being.
persona1_adapted:
When artworks from Equatorial Africa first became known in the West around 1900, art critics found them very puzzling. They guessed that the style might have come from Asia. But in fact, these masks were made by artists in southern Gabon, a country in Africa.

Male dancers wore these masks during a stilt dance called *mukudj*. The dancers were very skilled. They balanced on tall stilts and performed difficult moves and amazing acrobatic tricks.

The mask maker tried to create a portrait of the most beautiful woman in his community. This particular mask shows an idealized, or perfect, version of a woman. It follows a classic 1800s style. The hair has a central part with two side sections. There are also scar patterns, called cicatrization, on the forehead and temples.

The artist covered the face with kaolin, a white clay from riverbeds. People believed kaolin had healing powers and was connected to the spirit world of ancestors. By using this special material, the artist honored the beauty of a real woman and also turned her into a spiritual, otherworldly being.

CuratorialText: A lü me was typically commissioned by a man of standing in honor of a favorite wife. The subject’s distinguishing traits, including her facial features, cicatrices, and braided coiffure, would be captured by the artist through direct observation. The unveiling of a completed figure was celebrated with lavish feasting sponsored by its patron. Named for their subjects and stored in special baskets, such portraits were subsequently displayed on special occasions. This example was carved by celebrated early 20th century carver, Zlan of Belewale, and his wife, Sonzlanwan. Zlan’s calling was revealed to him as an adolescent through the miraculous appearance of his signature tool, an adze, falling from the branches of an oil palm. The sculptor honed his skills on soft tuber roots and soapstone before his mastery of wood earned him the name Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), meaning "creator" or "god." As the most sought-after Dan artist of the first half of the twentieth century, Zlan mentored scores of apprentices. Many of these trainees were sent by regional chiefs to acquire his expertise for their own communities. Through his efforts, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a sculptor—became an important carving center. Zlan’s works, including masks, ladles, and figurative portraits, have been collected globally. Defying the convention that woodcarving was a man’s vocation, Sonzlanwon (literally, "a snail, if god agrees") collaborated with her husband on many of his greatest works. She accompanied him on commissions for important patrons—the intense concentration required to execute these was such that they often worked side by side in seclusion. Sonzlanwon is credited with blocking out Zlan’s works’ initial form, using wood felled by his apprentices. Following his refinement of the contours, she handled final detailing, including the braided raffia-palm coiffures added to figures and ladles. As Zlan aged, it is believed that Sonzlanwon’s role in their collaboration expanded. Documentation of her contributions derives from interviews conducted by German anthropologist Hans Himmelheber shortly before Zlan’s death, as well as from remembrances of surviving relatives and acquaintances.
persona1_adapted:
In the past, a man of high status would often commission a *lü me*—a carved portrait figure—to honor his favorite wife. The artist would study the woman closely to capture her unique features, like her face, scars, and braided hairstyle. When the figure was finished, the man who paid for it threw a big feast to celebrate.

These portraits were named after the women they showed and were kept in special baskets. They were only brought out for important occasions.

This particular figure was carved by Zlan of Belewale, a famous artist from the early 1900s, and his wife, Sonzlanwan. When Zlan was a teenager, he discovered his life’s work in a strange way: his special tool, an adze (a type of carving axe), appeared to him by falling from the branches of an oil palm tree. He first practiced carving on soft yams and soapstone. Once he mastered wood, he earned the name Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), which means "creator" or "god."

Zlan became the most sought-after artist among the Dan people in the first half of the 1900s. He taught many apprentices, some sent by local chiefs who wanted their own communities to learn his skills. Thanks to Zlan, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a carver—became an important place for making sculptures.

Zlan’s works, including masks, ladles, and portrait figures, are now collected all over the world.

Even though woodcarving was usually seen as a man’s job, Sonzlanwon (whose name means "a snail, if god agrees") worked with her husband on many of his best pieces. She went with him to work for important clients. The work required so much focus that they often worked alone together. Sonzlanwon is credited with shaping the first rough form of Zlan’s carvings, using wood cut by his apprentices. After Zlan refined the shape, she added the final details, including the braided raffia-palm hairstyles on figures and ladles.

As Zlan got older, people believe Sonzlanwon took on a bigger role in their partnership. What we know about her work comes from interviews by German anthropologist Hans Himmelheber, done shortly before Zlan’s death, as well as from the memories of surviving family and friends.

CuratorialText: Central African power figures are among the ubiquitous genres identified with African art. Conceived to house specific mystical forces, they were collaborative creations of Kongo sculptors and ritual specialists. This example belongs to the most ambitious class of that tradition, attributed to the atelier of a master active along the coast of Congo and Angola at the end of the nineteenth century and identified with Mangaaka, the preeminent force of jurisprudence. That power was represented as a presiding authority and enforcing lord or king. Its crowning element is the distinctive mpu headdress worn by chiefs or priests. The figure's posture and gesture, leaning forward with hands placed akimbo on the hips, is the aggressive attitude of one who challenges fearlessly. Traces of a missing beard-a sign of seniority-survive in the form of nails along the contours of the chin. There are also vestiges of an abdominal cavity for medicinal matter that originally attracted the figure's defining force. The various metals embedded in the figure's expansive torso attest to its central role as witness and enforcer of affairs critical to its community. They document vows sealed, treaties signed, and efforts to eradicate evil. Ultimately, this work inspired reflection on the consequences of transgressing established codes of social conduct.
persona1_adapted:
In Central Africa, power figures are a well-known type of art. People made them to hold special spiritual forces. Carvers and ritual experts from the Kongo culture worked together to create them.

This figure is one of the most important examples of that tradition. It was made by a master artist who worked along the coast of Congo and Angola in the late 1800s. The figure represents Mangaaka, a powerful force of justice. Mangaaka was shown as a ruler or king who enforced the law.

The figure wears a special hat called an *mpu*, which chiefs or priests wore. Its pose—leaning forward with hands on its hips—shows an aggressive, fearless attitude. There are traces of a missing beard, a sign of old age and wisdom, marked by nails along the chin. The figure also has a hollow space in its belly that once held medicine, which gave it its power.

The many metal pieces stuck into the figure's chest show that it played a key role in important community events. They mark promises made, treaties signed, and efforts to stop evil. In the end, this work made people think about what happens when they break the rules of society.

CuratorialText: Throughout the twentieth century, members of Poro, a Senufo initiation association, wore small, finely carved face masks as insignia. The masks, known as kpeliye'e, feature delicate oval faces with geometric projections at the sides. Raised and incised scarification patterns ornament their smooth, glossy surfaces. Considered feminine, the masks honor deceased Senufo elders with their grace and beauty. They provide a complement to the aggressive Senufo helmet masks also sponsored by fraternal organizations in the region. The feathers and animal horns attached to this example are unusual, and may have reflected its owner's power to counteract negative forces in the community.
persona1_adapted: Throughout the 1900s, members of Poro—a Senufo group for teaching traditions—wore small, carefully carved face masks as badges of membership. These masks, called *kpeliye'e*, have delicate oval faces with geometric shapes sticking out at the sides. Raised and carved scar patterns decorate their smooth, shiny surfaces. Seen as feminine, the masks honor older Senufo leaders who have passed away, using their grace and beauty. They balance the more aggressive Senufo helmet masks, which are also sponsored by men's groups in the area. The feathers and animal horns attached to this mask are unusual. They may have shown the owner's power to fight against bad forces in the community.

CuratorialText: This cross was created in the northern Highlands, near the Red Sea, the birthplace of Ethiopia's earliest kingdom and of Christianity in Africa. A small corpus of Ethiopian Christian works from before the seventeenth century has survived. This exceptional creation, in which a highly unusual interplay of materials affords rich tonal contrasts, is a tour de force. The solidity of the carved-wood structure is permeated by finely applied metal inlays. The complexity of this work, with each side elaborately distinct, is unrivaled. It has been attributed to Ezra (ca. 1460-1522), a member of the Stephanite monastic order. The Stephanites came to be recognized as the foremost carvers of crosses and illuminators of manuscripts. During a period of the order's exile in Egypt, Ezra is reputed to have enhanced his training as a craftsman and learned the technique of marquetry. It has been suggested that he may have produced this work at the Gunda Gunde monastery, renowned for its manuscripts. In the Ethiopian church, the wooden cross is perceived as having been sanctified by Christ's blood, which conferred upon it infinite power to heal and to bless. Foliate and organic interlace designs, branching from the foot of the cross, are interpreted as symbolizing the Tree of Paradise, and thus a vibrant life-giving force. Commissioned by Ethiopian royalty, such works were presented to important monasteries to be carried in liturgical processions.
persona1_adapted:
This cross was made in the northern Highlands, near the Red Sea. That area is where Ethiopia’s first kingdom began, and where Christianity first came to Africa. Very few Ethiopian Christian artworks from before the 1600s still exist today.
