This piece is special. It uses a rare mix of materials that creates strong contrasts in color and texture. The wood carving is solid, but it is covered with finely placed metal inlays. Each side of the cross is very different from the other, making it one of a kind.

Experts believe it was made by Ezra (around 1460–1522). He was a member of the Stephanite monastic order. The Stephanites were known as the best carvers of crosses and painters of manuscripts. While the order was living in exile in Egypt, Ezra is said to have improved his skills as a craftsman. He also learned a technique called marquetry, which uses pieces of wood or metal to create patterns.

Some think Ezra may have made this cross at the Gunda Gunde monastery, which was famous for its manuscripts.

In the Ethiopian church, a wooden cross is seen as holy because of Christ’s blood. This gives the cross great power to heal and bless. The designs on the cross include leaves and twisting patterns that grow from the base. These are meant to represent the Tree of Paradise, a symbol of life and energy.

Ethiopian royalty ordered crosses like this one. They gave them to important monasteries to be carried in religious processions.

CuratorialText: This illuminated manuscript of the Four Gospels was created at a monastic center in northern Ethiopia. Twenty full-page paintings depict scenes from the life of Christ and four portraits of the evangelists introduce the respective Gospel texts. The New Testament was translated from Greek into Geez, the classical language of Ethiopia, in the sixth century. Both this text and its pictorial format draw upon Byzantine prototypes, which were transformed into a local idiom of expression. Stylistically consistent, the paintings reflect the hands of two distinct artists. The color scheme consists of red, yellow, green, and blue. A stylized uniformity is reflected in the abbreviated definition of facial features and the bold linear articulation of the human form in black and red. Figures' heads are depicted frontally, their bodies often in profile. Bodies are treated as columnar masses encased in textiles composed of striated fields juxtaposed against one another. This work is evidence of sub-Saharan Africa's historically complex interrelationships with Arabia, Egypt, and the eastern Mediterranean. Early complex societies in the Highlands developed primarily through local processes beginning in the late second millennium B.C.E. Those societies attracted trade from across the Red Sea, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean, which spurred migrations, religious exchanges, and greater concentrations of wealth. By the early first millennium C.E., the Kingdom of Aksum emerged as a powerful civilization that, at its height, extended from Ethiopia's Highlands in the South, to the Eastern Desert in the North, to southern Arabia in the East. Emperor Ezana (r. ca. 320s-360 C.E.) was the first monarch to convert to Christianity, and adopted it as the state religion. Subsequent kingdoms, including the Zagwe Dynasty (ca. 1137-1270 C.E.) and the Ethiopian Empire (1270-1974 C.E.) continued to embrace Christianity. Monasteries were founded as centers of learning responsible for disseminating knowledge and consolidating the power and influence of the ruling elite. In the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the text of the Gospels was considered the most important holy writing; the miniatures at the beginning of this manuscript were intended to be viewed during liturgical processions. Such works were frequently presented to churches by distinguished patrons; they reflected both the prestige of royal benefactors and the erudition of the monastic scriptoria in which they were created. Recent research suggests that a member of Ethiopia's ruling elite may have commissioned this manuscript at Dabra Hayg Estifanos monastery for presentation to his or her favored church or monastery. Brief notations indicate that the church in question was dedicated to the Archangel Michael.
persona1_adapted:
This illuminated manuscript—a handwritten book with painted decorations—contains the Four Gospels. It was made at a monastery in northern Ethiopia.

Twenty full-page paintings show scenes from the life of Christ. Four portraits of the evangelists (the writers of the Gospels) introduce each Gospel text. The New Testament was translated from Greek into Geez, the classical language of Ethiopia, in the sixth century.

Both the text and the pictures were based on Byzantine (Eastern Roman Empire) models. But Ethiopian artists changed them into a local style. The paintings are consistent in style, but they show the work of two different artists. The colors used are red, yellow, green, and blue.

The faces have simple, repeated features. The human form is outlined boldly in black and red. Figures’ heads face forward, but their bodies are often shown from the side. Bodies look like columns wrapped in cloth made of striped patterns placed next to each other.

This work shows how sub-Saharan Africa had complex ties with Arabia, Egypt, and the eastern Mediterranean. Early societies in the Ethiopian Highlands developed mostly on their own, starting in the late second millennium B.C.E. These societies attracted trade from across the Red Sea, the Mediterranean, and the Indian Ocean. This trade led to migrations, religious exchanges, and greater wealth.

By the early first millennium C.E., the Kingdom of Aksum became a powerful civilization. At its height, it stretched from Ethiopia’s Highlands in the south to the Eastern Desert in the north, and to southern Arabia in the east. Emperor Ezana (ruled around 320s–360 C.E.) was the first king to convert to Christianity. He made it the state religion.

Later kingdoms, including the Zagwe Dynasty (about 1137–1270 C.E.) and the Ethiopian Empire (1270–1974 C.E.), continued to embrace Christianity. Monasteries were founded as centers of learning. They spread knowledge and helped the ruling elite keep power and influence.

In the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the Gospels were considered the most important holy writings. The paintings at the beginning of this manuscript were meant to be viewed during church processions. Such works were often given to churches by wealthy patrons. They showed both the prestige of royal donors and the learning of the monks who made them.

Recent research suggests that a member of Ethiopia’s ruling elite may have ordered this manuscript at Dabra Hayg Estifanos monastery. It was likely given to his or her favorite church or monastery. Brief notes indicate that church was dedicated to the Archangel Michael.

CuratorialText: Phillips pursued a long and successful career over fifty years, taking to the road as an itinerant artist painting portraits of clusters of friends, relatives, and neighbors in New York State and New England. His stock in trade was the plain likeness, devoid of shadows or modeling. Although he depended on repetitious formulas to work efficiently as he traveled the countryside, he was nevertheless able to individualize his sitters imaginatively. In "Mrs. Mayer and Daughter," Phillips’s masterful design abilities are evident in the cleanly contained forms of the mother and child, enriched by the use of brilliant, saturated color and careful detail.
persona1_adapted: Phillips had a long and successful career that lasted over fifty years. He traveled as an artist who painted groups of friends, relatives, and neighbors in New York State and New England. His specialty was simple, realistic portraits without shadows or shading. To work quickly while moving from place to place, he used repeated patterns. But he still made each person look unique and interesting. In the painting *Mrs. Mayer and Daughter*, you can see Phillips’s strong design skills. The shapes of the mother and child are clean and clear. Bright, rich colors and careful details make the painting even more striking.

CuratorialText: Madame Pierre Gautreau (the Louisiana-born Virginie Amélie Avegno; 1859–1915) was known in Paris for her artful appearance. Sargent hoped to enhance his reputation by painting and exhibiting her portrait. Working without a commission but with his sitter’s complicity, he emphasized her daring personal style, showing the right strap of her gown slipping from her shoulder. At the Salon of 1884, the portrait received more ridicule than praise. Sargent repainted the shoulder strap and kept the work for over thirty years. When, eventually, he sold it to the Metropolitan, he commented, “I suppose it is the best thing I have done,” but asked that the Museum disguise the sitter’s name.
persona1_adapted: Madame Pierre Gautreau was known in Paris for her stylish appearance. She was born in Louisiana as Virginie Amélie Avegno. Sargent hoped to boost his own reputation by painting and showing her portrait. He worked without being hired, but with her permission. In the painting, he highlighted her bold personal style. He showed the right strap of her dress slipping off her shoulder. At the Salon of 1884, the portrait got more mockery than praise. Sargent painted the shoulder strap again and kept the work for over thirty years. When he finally sold it to the Metropolitan Museum, he said, “I suppose it is the best thing I have done.” But he asked the museum to hide the sitter’s name.

CuratorialText: Alexander, who lived in Paris during the 1890s, achieved international success with his figure paintings of gracefully posed women in elegant interiors. In this striking composition, the model’s provocative facial expression and supple curves speak to the contemporary French taste for sensuous images of women. The bold composition also suggests the undulating linear rhythms of the Art Nouveau style, examples of which were featured at the Paris Exposition. Alexander, known as the "the painter of the flowing line," was celebrated at home and abroad for these works of sinuous grace and lyricism.
persona1_adapted: Alexander lived in Paris during the 1890s. He became famous worldwide for his paintings of women. In these works, the women pose gracefully in elegant rooms. In this striking painting, the model’s bold facial expression and smooth curves show what French people at the time found beautiful and appealing in images of women. The strong composition also hints at the flowing, wavy lines of the Art Nouveau style. Examples of this style were shown at the Paris Exposition. Alexander was known as "the painter of the flowing line." People at home and abroad celebrated him for these graceful, musical works.

CuratorialText: In early 1859, Bierstadt accompanied a government survey expedition, headed by Frederick W. Lander, to the Nebraska Territory. By summer, the party had reached the Wind River Range of the Rocky Mountains in present-day Wyoming. Painted in New York after Bierstadt’s return, this work advertised the landscape as a frontier destined to be claimed by White settlers, according to the doctrine of Manifest Destiny. This belief that Americans were the divinely ordained "masters" of the continent systematically ignored—with dire consequences—the presence of Indigenous populations, such as the Shoshone peoples depicted in the foreground.
persona1_adapted: In early 1859, Bierstadt joined a government survey trip led by Frederick W. Lander to the Nebraska Territory. By summer, the group reached the Wind River Range of the Rocky Mountains in present-day Wyoming. Bierstadt painted this scene back in New York after his return. The painting showed the landscape as a frontier meant to be taken over by White settlers. This idea came from a belief called Manifest Destiny. Manifest Destiny was the belief that Americans were the chosen "masters" of the continent. This belief ignored the fact that Indigenous peoples, like the Shoshone people shown in the front of the painting, already lived there. Ignoring them had serious and harmful results.

CuratorialText: In the summer of 1845, Bingham returned to his St. Louis home from a winter stay in central Missouri, bringing with him several paintings and sketches. This was one of those works that he later sent to New York’s American Art-Union, a subscription-based organization that promoted American art nationally through exhibitions and the distribution of popular prints. Titled by the artist "French Trader & Half breed Son", the Art-Union changed it to the more generic and less controversial "Fur Traders Descending the Missouri". Bingham, who began his career as a portraitist, produced this distinctive genre painting with little precedent in his oeuvre. The tranquil scene, with its luminous atmosphere, idealized the American frontier for the benefit of an Eastern audience.
persona1_adapted:
In the summer of 1845, Bingham came back to his home in St. Louis after spending the winter in central Missouri. He brought several paintings and sketches with him. This was one of those works. He later sent it to New York’s American Art-Union. That group was a subscription-based organization. It promoted American art across the country by holding exhibitions and selling popular prints.

Bingham titled the painting "French Trader & Half breed Son." The Art-Union changed the name to "Fur Traders Descending the Missouri." This new title was more general and less likely to cause disagreement. Bingham started his career painting portraits. He made this painting, which shows a scene from everyday life, with little similar work before it in his career. The calm scene, with its bright and glowing light, made the American frontier look ideal. It was meant to please viewers from the eastern part of the country.

CuratorialText: Fascinated by the winding Connecticut River, Cole made the U-shaped bend known as the “Oxbow” the subject of this painting. Beyond the tempestuous storm clouds and blasted tree lies a patchwork of farmland, sunlight illuminating private properties full of crops, hay bales, and sheep. Cole’s composition emphasizes the encroachment of Euro-American settlement into a supposed untamed “wilderness.” At the lower center, the artist depicted himself pausing from a sketch and gazing at the viewer. “I think that [Cole] is calling out the fact that our way of developing—our way of improving the land or making it profitable—was pushing nature out, Indigenous people out.” Xiye Bastida, climate activist, Audioguide 4026
persona1_adapted:
Thomas Cole was fascinated by the winding Connecticut River. He made the U-shaped bend, called the "Oxbow," the main subject of this painting. Storm clouds and a broken tree fill the left side. On the right, a patchwork of farmland spreads out. Sunlight shines on private properties with crops, hay bales, and sheep.

Cole’s composition—the way he arranged the painting—shows how Euro-American settlers were moving into land that people thought was "wilderness." At the bottom center, Cole painted himself taking a break from sketching. He looks straight at the viewer.

Climate activist Xiye Bastida says, "I think that [Cole] is calling out the fact that our way of developing—our way of improving the land or making it profitable—was pushing nature out, Indigenous people out."

CuratorialText: Returning to Philadelphia from Europe in 1870, Eakins began a series of representations of the sport of sculling, a subject with which he is uniquely identified. This is the first major work in that series of paintings and watercolors, and the one that launched his professional career. It is believed to commemorate the victory of Max Schmitt (1843–1900), a school friend, attorney, and skilled amateur rower, in an important race on the Schuylkill River in October 1870. Also an avid rower, Eakins depicted himself pulling the oars of a scull in the middle distance.
persona1_adapted:
When Thomas Eakins came back to Philadelphia from Europe in 1870, he started painting a sport called sculling. Sculling is a type of rowing where each person uses two oars. This painting is the first major work in that series. It helped launch his career as an artist.

The painting is believed to celebrate a victory by Max Schmitt (1843–1900). Schmitt was a school friend of Eakins, a lawyer, and a very skilled amateur rower. He won an important race on the Schuylkill River in October 1870.

Eakins was also a big fan of rowing. In the painting, he shows himself rowing a scull in the middle distance.

CuratorialText: A man and his dog sit perched on the shore of Narragansett Bay, Rhode Island, sunlight at their backs as they witness darkening clouds and a thin bolt of lightning warning of the tempest to come. Heade vividly captures the anticipation—in the words of a critic of his day, the “ominous hush”—that prevailed under a blackening sky and the eerily illuminated terrain. This is one of the earliest works in a small series of coastal storm subjects painted by Heade, based on a sketch of an approaching storm he witnessed from Prudence Island around 1858.
persona1_adapted: A man and his dog sit on the shore of Narragansett Bay in Rhode Island. The sun is at their backs as they watch dark clouds gather. A thin bolt of lightning warns of the coming storm. Heade captures the feeling of waiting—what one critic called the “ominous hush.” This quiet tension fills the scene under the blackening sky and strangely lit land. This is one of Heade’s earliest paintings of coastal storms. He based it on a sketch of a storm he saw from Prudence Island around 1858.

CuratorialText: Threatened by sharks and a waterspout, a Black man faces his demise aboard a damaged boat. The painting represents the culmination of Homer’s interest in depicting conflict between humans and nature. Sugarcane, the Caribbean commodity central to the economy of empire, and transatlantic slavery are linked by the titular Gulf Stream current. Homer interwove geopolitical themes into an epic saga that foregrounds human struggle—personified by a stoic survivor—against the relentless power of nature and history.
persona1_adapted: A Black man is stranded on a damaged boat. He faces danger from sharks and a waterspout, a tornado over water. This painting is the peak of Homer's work on the struggle between humans and nature. The title, *The Gulf Stream*, connects sugarcane—a key crop in the Caribbean that fueled empires—to the history of transatlantic slavery. Homer wove big political ideas into a dramatic story. It focuses on human struggle, shown through a calm survivor fighting against the unstoppable forces of nature and history.

CuratorialText: Cassatt and her family spent the summer of 1880 at Marly-le-Roi, outside of Paris, where the artist focused on depicting gardens and domestic life. Here, she has portrayed her older sister, Lydia, fashionably if incongruously overdressed and insulated by a walled garden landscape. She is absorbed in the sort of old-fashioned textile handwork that was increasingly prized by the affluent in these years of mechanized labor. Although Cassatt’s experiments with plein-air (outdoor) painting are few, in this dynamic composition she effectively captured the dazzling sunlight associated with her fellow French Impressionists.
persona1_adapted:
In the summer of 1880, Cassatt and her family stayed at Marly-le-Roi, a town near Paris. During this time, the artist focused on painting gardens and everyday home life. In this painting, she shows her older sister, Lydia. Lydia is dressed in fancy clothes that seem a bit out of place for the setting. She is surrounded by a walled garden that feels private and separate from the outside world.

Lydia is busy doing old-fashioned handwork with fabric. In those years, when machines did much of the labor, wealthy people valued this kind of handmade work more and more.

Cassatt did not often paint outdoors, a style called "plein-air." But in this lively scene, she successfully captured the bright, dazzling sunlight that other French Impressionist artists were known for.

CuratorialText: Spencer portrayed the unvarnished reality of male-female relationships in this painting, which chronicles the travails of early married life. Here, the unseen young wife is not in full command of her kitchen, having sent her young husband out to procure the groceries—a task for which he is evidently not qualified. Using her own husband as a model, Spencer depicted the man stumbling down the street and attempting to hold on to the items in his overflowing basket, attracting gazes from at least one onlooker and suffering a public humiliation. Spencer depicted in humorous terms a real social anxiety of the time: that of setting up a household and running it efficiently.
persona1_adapted:
In this painting, Spencer shows the real, unpolished side of relationships between men and women. It tells the story of the struggles of early married life. The young wife, who we cannot see, is not fully in control of her kitchen. She sent her husband out to buy groceries—a job he is clearly not good at.

Spencer used her own husband as a model. She painted the man stumbling down the street, trying to hold on to the items in his overflowing basket. At least one person watches him, and he suffers public embarrassment.

Spencer used humor to show a real worry of the time: the challenge of setting up a home and running it well.

CuratorialText: This sculpture is one of the most spectacular examples of ceramic figures created by Tolita-Tumaco artists. It depicts an elderly person, recognizable by facial wrinkles, baggy skin beneath the eyes, the few remaining teeth, and chin stubble. Its flattened head reveals cranial modification during childhood, a cultural practice that could have signaled ethnic identity or rank in antiquity. The gender of this effigy is not clear. Marked nipples, as in this figure’s chest, were more common in female than male representations in Tolita-Tumaco and Jama-Coaque ceramic traditions of coastal Ecuador. This statue, however, lacks the long skirts that are typically seen in female images. The artist(s) would have taken several days to complete the sculpture due to the complexity of the body and the weight of the moist clay. This figure was crafted using the gray clay paste that characterizes Tolita-Tumaco ceramics. The torso was shaped most likely using clay slabs that were gradually added until completing the final form. The limbs and the head were made separately and attached to the torso before drying and firing. A one-side mold was used for the face while the wrinkles and other details were incised when the clay paste was still soft. Today’s monochromatic and matte appearance of these sculptures is the result of wear. Apparently, many of these objects had a smooth and polished finish and were decorated with post-fired paints (see Valdez 1992:136; MMA 1991.436.7a, b), in a similar fashion to Jama-Coaque sculptures (MMA 1980.34.38a, MMA 1980.34.39). A careful examination of the lower back shows that this figure may have been seated on a stool (compare with MMA 1991.436.7a, b). Elaborate stone or wood seats were important symbols of authority in the ancient Americas, from Ecuador to Mesoamerica, including the Amazonian and the Caribbean areas (McEwan and Looper 2021). The relatively high rank or importance of this individual is also suggested by the collar and bracelets modeled in clay. Adornments, probably made of gold, originally hung from holes on its nose, ears, and nipples (see MMA 1979.206.1204 and 1979.206.1136). Tolita-Tumaco potters are known for their realistic renderings of humans, such as the examples in the collections of Casa de Alabado in Quito (Cruz Cevallos 1992:225) and Museo Chileno de Arte Precolombino in Santiago (MCHAP 0348). Some of their figures are depicted leaning forward, adopting an animated pose, with flexed legs, arched torsos, and faces staring ahead. Elders were a popular theme, and several examples can be found in the collections of the Museo del Banco Central de Ecuador in Quito (No. 12-53-69, LT-2-16-69, LT-3-11-70) and in Guayaquil (GA-1-2268-82, GA-5-1631-80). A common interpretation is that these elder figures were representations of religious specialists, or ancient "shamans" who had the responsibility to mediate relations between people and other beings in nature. The marked spine in the back may be revealing of more than a very lean body, as it bears a resemblance to iguanas (compare with a Bahia example of iguanas: MMA 1988.117.7). With dry and wrinkled skin, the comparison of Tolita-Tumaco revered elders with those reptiles could have been a way to evoke the human-animal connection that is a key element in shamanistic rituals. Tolita-Tumaco was a civilization that flourished on the tropical Pacific coast of northern Ecuador and southern Colombia. In a landscape shaped by the rivers, with swampy mangroves, and hills covered in dense forests, these communities settled in large villages organized around plazas and several massive earthen mounds, known as tolas, that were the residences and burials of the elite. Remains of these human and animal sculptures have been found in settlements and cemeteries, but their original use remains unknown. The Tolita-Tumaco tradition started around 600 BCE, but its distinctive style matured between 200 BCE and 400 CE. During these centuries, these communities produced one of the most refined metalwork traditions in the ancient Americas and established relationships with other communities on the Pacific coast, including those in Northern Peru.
persona1_adapted:
This sculpture is a stunning example of ceramic art made by the Tolita-Tumaco people. It shows an elderly person. You can tell by the wrinkles on the face, the baggy skin under the eyes, the few teeth left, and the stubble on the chin.

The head is flat. This happened because the person’s skull was shaped during childhood. In ancient times, this practice may have shown a person’s ethnic group or social rank.

It is not clear if this figure is male or female. The chest has marked nipples. In Tolita-Tumaco and Jama-Coaque art from coastal Ecuador, this feature was more common in female figures. But this statue does not have the long skirts usually seen on female images.

The artist or artists would have spent several days making this sculpture. The body was complex, and the wet clay was heavy. The figure was made from gray clay paste, which is typical of Tolita-Tumaco ceramics. The torso was built using clay slabs, added one by one until the shape was complete. The arms, legs, and head were made separately and then attached before the clay dried and was fired in a kiln. A one-sided mold was used for the face. The wrinkles and other details were carved into the soft clay.

Today, the sculpture looks plain and matte. But this is because of age and wear. Originally, many of these objects had a smooth, polished finish. They were also decorated with paint that was added after firing. A close look at the lower back shows that this figure may have been sitting on a stool. In the ancient Americas, from Ecuador to Mesoamerica, and even in the Amazon and Caribbean, fancy stone or wood seats were important symbols of power.

This person was likely of high rank or importance. We can tell because the figure wears a collar and bracelets made of clay. There are also holes in the nose, ears, and nipples. These holes probably held ornaments made of gold.

Tolita-Tumaco potters were known for making realistic human figures. Some of their sculptures show people leaning forward, with bent legs, curved torsos, and faces looking straight ahead. Elders were a popular subject. Many examples can be found in museums in Ecuador and Chile.

A common idea is that these elder figures represent religious specialists, or ancient "shamans." Shamans were people who helped connect humans with other beings in nature. The figure’s spine is very visible on the back. This might not just show a thin body. It also looks like the spine of an iguana. Comparing respected elders to reptiles may have been a way to show the human-animal connection that is important in shamanistic rituals.

The Tolita-Tumaco civilization thrived on the tropical Pacific coast of northern Ecuador and southern Colombia. The landscape had rivers, swampy mangroves, and hills covered in dense forests. People lived in large villages organized around plazas and huge earthen mounds called "tolas." These mounds were homes and burial places for the elite. Remains of human and animal sculptures have been found in settlements and cemeteries, but we do not know exactly how they were used.

The Tolita-Tumaco tradition began around 600 BCE. Its unique style became fully developed between 200 BCE and 400 CE. During these centuries, these communities created some of the finest metalwork in the ancient Americas. They also built relationships with other groups along the Pacific coast, including those in Northern Peru.

CuratorialText: This mask, made of hammered sheet gold alloy and covered in red pigment, once adorned the body of a deceased ruler on Peru’s north coast. Powerful dynasties arose in this region between the eighth and the fourteenth centuries CE and amassed great riches in gold and silver before they were conquered by the Inca Empire in the late fifteenth century. The lords of these dynasties were the patrons of vast workshops where finely crafted ornaments and ceremonial vessels were created (see 1991.419.62; 66.196.27). At death, the lords were buried deep in monumental mud-brick platform mounds along with large numbers of objects of precious metal, shell, and cloth. In addition to beakers, disks, and other ornaments, the burials included large masks made of sheet gold. As many as five masks were placed into one burial: one attached to the head of the textile-wrapped body, and the other four stacked at the feet of the deceased. This mask was made of an alloy of 74 percent gold, 20 percent silver, and 6 percent copper which was then hammered into a sheet and shaped into the form of a face. Interestingly, cinnabar, a red mineral pigment, covers much of the cheeks and forehead of this mask, obscuring the gold surface. The cinnabar may emulate the patterning of face paint worn by individuals of importance: according to early colonial accounts a courtier was charged with the task of maintaining such paints. Faint traces of the textile wrappings that once enveloped the mummy bundle can be detected in impressions on the red paint. Masks in museum collections are often missing such surface embellishment (see 1979.206.556), as the pigment would have been removed in modern times to highlight the golden substrate. The eyes of this mask have thin, skewer-like projections emerging from the pupils, perhaps suggesting powerful or even piercing vision. In other masks these projections include small beads of amber and emerald, which have led scholars to interpret the projections as tears. On the ears and ear ornaments can be seen restored examples of the silver-surfaced overlays (now corroded green) that would have also been originally present attached to the mask. Further surface additions include danglers on the U-shaped nose ornament. Such spangles would have caught the light of the bright sun and conveyed a sense of movement and life as a mummy bundle was conveyed to its final resting place. Masks such as these, with their characteristic ovoid eyes terminating in a point, are associated with the Lambayeque culture, named for a region near the modern city of Chiclayo on Peru’s north coast. This polity, also known as Sicán, erected great monumental centers such as Batán Grande, Chornancap, and other sites. Recent excavations at Chornancap by Carlos Wester and his team have revealed that such masks were part of the burial regalia of high-status women as well as men. According to myths on the north coast, gold was particularly associated with male rulers, silver with noble women, and copper with commoners. A mask from Chornancap, found in the tomb of an importance priestess, was made of silver. The pointed ovoid eyes on this mask and others, sometimes referred to as winged eyes, have been identified as defining features of a being known as the Sicán Deity. According to Izumi Shimada and colleagues, an individual interred with such a mask would have been thought to take on aspects of the Sicán Deity’s power, and would have been transformed into a venerated ancestor upon death.
persona1_adapted:
This mask once covered the body of a dead ruler on Peru’s north coast. It is made from hammered gold alloy and covered in red pigment. Between the 700s and 1300s CE, powerful dynasties ruled this area. They gathered great wealth in gold and silver before the Inca Empire conquered them in the late 1400s.

These rulers paid for large workshops. There, skilled workers made fine ornaments and ceremonial vessels. When a ruler died, he was buried deep inside huge mud-brick mounds. These tombs held many precious objects made of metal, shell, and cloth.

Along with cups, disks, and other items, the burials included large masks made of sheet gold. Up to five masks could be placed in one burial. One mask was attached to the head of the wrapped body. The other four were stacked at the feet of the dead person.

This mask is made of an alloy—a mix of metals—that is 74 percent gold, 20 percent silver, and 6 percent copper. It was hammered into a thin sheet and shaped into a face. Interestingly, a red mineral pigment called cinnabar covers much of the cheeks and forehead. This hides the gold surface underneath.

The cinnabar may copy the face paint worn by important people. According to early colonial records, a court official was in charge of keeping up such paints. Faint marks on the red paint show where cloth wrappings once covered the mummy bundle. Many masks in museums today are missing this surface decoration. Modern cleaners often removed the pigment to show off the gold.

The eyes of this mask have thin, stick-like shapes coming out of the pupils. These may suggest powerful or piercing vision. On other masks, these shapes have small beads of amber and emerald. Scholars think these might represent tears.

On the ears and ear ornaments, you can see restored silver overlays. These have now turned green from corrosion. They would have been attached to the mask originally. There are also danglers on the U-shaped nose ornament. These spangles would have caught the sunlight and made the mummy bundle seem alive and moving as it was carried to its final resting place.

Masks like this one, with their pointed oval eyes, are linked to the Lambayeque culture. This culture is named for a region near the modern city of Chiclayo on Peru’s north coast. It is also known as Sicán. The Sicán people built large ceremonial centers at places like Batán Grande and Chornancap.

Recent digs at Chornancap by Carlos Wester and his team show that such masks were part of the burial gear for high-status women as well as men. According to north coast myths, gold was linked to male rulers, silver to noble women, and copper to common people. A mask from Chornancap, found in the tomb of an important priestess, was made of silver.

The pointed oval eyes on this mask are sometimes called "winged eyes." Scholars see them as a key feature of a being known as the Sicán Deity. According to researcher Izumi Shimada and colleagues, a person buried with such a mask was thought to take on some of the Sicán Deity’s power. Upon death, that person would be transformed into a venerated ancestor.

CuratorialText: The Olmec, whose heartland was located in present-day Mexico from 1200-400 BCE, excelled in creating fine greenstone sculptures. The almost flesh-like quality of the nose and parted lips belie the hardness of the stone from which this mask was made. Although it has been rendered in a naturalistic style, the face itself is not fully human. Rather, it is an idealized composite that alludes to the supernatural: the almond-shaped eyes, slightly downturned mouth, and wide, prominent nose are traits commonly found in depictions of Olmec otherworldly beings. One particular entity, the Olmec Maize God, is further evoked through the defined cleft at the center of the upper forehead, an element that represents the earth from which maize—the principal crop of many Mesoamerican peoples including the Olmec—sprouts and grows. The mask additionally features light incisions on both cheeks and etched scallop-like motifs on the upper rim. These shallow markings were possibly made through repeated scratching. The use of drilling is also evident in the mask, particularly at the corners of the mouth and eyes. Olmec artists may have carefully placed the drill marks as guidelines for the sculpture, to determine the placement of facial features and to demarcate the depth of the carving. Many of these holes may have been preserved in the finished sculpture for aesthetic effect. From the middle of the 11th century BCE to the Spanish Conquest in the 16th century, green and blue colored stones (broadly called greenstone, or “chalchihuitl” in Nahuatl) were esteemed across Mesoamerica for their exceptional luster and translucency. Greenstone, moreover, was thought to be water-retentive, capable of emitting vapor that bolstered the growth and sustenance of surrounding vegetation. The color of the stones was further tied to that of water and maize sprouts, closely linking greenstone to notions of fertility, abundance, and life-giving properties. The Olmec especially valued the bluish color of this jadeite mask. Jadeite, a rare variety of greenstone, occurs naturally in very few places around the world. The material for this mask likely originated from the Motagua River valley in present-day Guatemala, the only known source of jadeite in ancient Mesoamerica. The eroded corner on the mask’s proper right suggests that it was previously exposed to water, perhaps as a river cobble. Some of the earliest jadeite obtained from the region may have been stream-tumbled cobbles or boulders, rather than quarried stone. So special was the jadeite from which this mask was made that the artists seemed to have removed as little of it as possible from the reverse side. The shallowness of the circular, concave depression on the back of the mask may also be due to the toughness of the stone. Jadeite is an extremely dense rock with a relative hardness value equivalent to or even greater than that of steel. Artists in Mesoamerica generally used a combination of percussion and cutting—using lithic implements such as flint blades—to approximate the size of the jadeite, after which they grinded the surface of the stone with other coarse rocks for many hours to achieve the desired shape. Despite the concave depression, this mask was not made to be worn on the face, at least not by the living. Unlike the earlobes, which feature small holes, neither the eyes nor the mouth have been perforated for sight or breath. Holes along the edges of the mask, however, hint at its ritual usage. Masks appear in depictions of rulers or performers and are represented as belt ornaments, pectorals, or headdress components. The perforations additionally suggest that the mask may have been affixed to a textile, as part of a funerary bundle.
persona1_adapted:
The Olmec people lived in what is now Mexico from 1200 to 400 BCE. They were skilled at making fine sculptures from greenstone, a type of hard, green-colored stone. This mask is carved from jadeite, a rare kind of greenstone.

The nose and parted lips look almost like real human skin, even though the stone is very hard. The face is carved in a naturalistic style, meaning it looks lifelike. But it is not a portrait of a real person. Instead, it is an idealized, or perfect, face that suggests something supernatural. The almond-shaped eyes, slightly downturned mouth, and wide, prominent nose are features often seen in Olmec images of otherworldly beings.

One such being is the Olmec Maize God. The mask shows this god through a cleft, or split, at the top of the forehead. This cleft represents the earth from which maize (corn) grows. Maize was the main crop for many Mesoamerican peoples, including the Olmec.

The mask also has light scratches on both cheeks and scallop-like patterns on the upper edge. These marks were probably made by repeated scratching. Drilling is also visible, especially at the corners of the mouth and eyes. Olmec artists may have used these drill marks as guides to place the facial features and to show how deep to carve. Many of these holes were likely kept in the finished mask for decoration.

From around 1050 BCE to the Spanish Conquest in the 1500s, green and blue stones (called greenstone, or “chalchihuitl” in Nahuatl) were highly valued across Mesoamerica. People prized them for their shine and see-through quality. Greenstone was also thought to hold water and release vapor that helped plants grow. The color of the stones was linked to water and young corn plants, connecting greenstone to ideas of fertility, abundance, and life.

The Olmec especially valued the bluish color of this jadeite mask. Jadeite is a rare type of greenstone found in very few places worldwide. The stone for this mask likely came from the Motagua River valley in present-day Guatemala, the only known source of jadeite in ancient Mesoamerica. The eroded corner on the mask’s right side suggests it was once exposed to water, perhaps as a river cobble. Some of the earliest jadeite may have been smooth stones from streams, not quarried rock.

The jadeite in this mask was so special that the artists removed as little as possible from the back. The shallow, circular hollow on the back may also be due to the stone’s toughness. Jadeite is extremely dense and as hard as, or harder than, steel. Mesoamerican artists usually used tools like flint blades to cut and shape the jadeite. Then they ground the surface with other rough rocks for many hours to get the desired shape.

Despite the hollow on the back, this mask was not made to be worn on the face—at least not by a living person. Unlike the earlobes, which have small holes, the eyes and mouth are not pierced for sight or breath. But holes along the edges of the mask hint at its use in rituals. Masks appear in images of rulers or performers, worn as belt ornaments, chest pieces, or parts of headdresses. The holes also suggest the mask may have been attached to cloth as part of a funeral bundle.

CuratorialText: The ceramic vessels of highland Tiwanaku are sturdy and clean lined, with a no-nonsense approach to shape that sets them apart from the sculptural ceramic traditions of the coastal regions to the north. Surface color too is restrained, with a pre-dominance of earth tones in a limited range of hue. This flaring-side vessel is a typical, but distinctive, Tiwanaku type. It has a large feline head on one side and a stumpy, erect tail on the other. The head is surrounded by a large flange on which remnants of a design appear. Winged profile felines are on each side of the vessel. These big-footed cats abundantly fill the space allotted to them. Their eyes are round and divided in half with one half white and the other black, a stylization that was widely used on feline images. Vessels such as these usually represent either large cats or llamas. A number are modeled on the shape of the animal itself, with the animal's body forming the vessel container. The insides of both modeled and abstract vessels are often found blackened and sooty. This has lead to the conclusion that they were censers in Precolumbian times. When in use, smoke would have come from the open mouth of the feline head of this example, which does have a soot-blackened interior.
persona1_adapted:
The ceramic pots from highland Tiwanaku are sturdy and simple in shape. They look different from the more sculptural pottery found along the northern coast. The colors are also restrained, mostly using earthy tones.

This pot flares outward at the top. It is a typical but special Tiwanaku style. On one side is a large cat head. On the other side is a short, upright tail. A wide rim surrounds the head, and traces of a design remain on it.

Winged cats in profile appear on each side of the pot. These big-footed cats fill the space completely. Their eyes are round and split in half—one half white, the other black. This style was common for cat images.

Pots like this usually show either big cats or llamas. Some are shaped like the animal itself, with the animal’s body forming the container. The insides of both shaped and abstract pots are often blackened with soot. This suggests they were used as incense burners in Precolumbian times. When in use, smoke would have come out of the open mouth of the cat head. The inside of this pot is also blackened by soot.

CuratorialText:
A master Maya potter modeled a vignette with three characters onto the body and lids of this ceremonial vessel. The main form of the vessel is composed of two identical cylindrical chambers connected by a central hollow bridge, each of which rests on two squarish supports. This style of ceramic, recognizable by a very fine reddish or gray paste fired with a reddish-black clay slip, is known from what is now northern Guatemala and Southern Campeche, Mexico. Artists who specialized in this highly polished blackware style in the 5th-6th centuries achieved great feats in gouged and incised designs, often on very thin walls or lids. Such a fine vessel would have been used on very special feasting or ritual occasion, perhaps later buried in the tomb of its owner by his or her descendants.

In this scene, a large supernatural bird known as the Principal Bird Deity (PBD) perches on one lid (which is actually a false lid, fused with the chamber), and faces a kneeling humanlike figure. A spherical chamber of a whistle is hidden inside the head of the bird, visible through an Rx radiograph image, and sounds when water is poured into the opposite chamber. The bird’s large beak and caruncle protrude from its open mouth, which connects to the interior chamber of the vessel. From the beak hangs a stylized double-headed serpent. The bird’s glittering eyes are square and marked with an L-shaped pupil, a convention that Maya artists used to depict deities with supernatural vision. The spray of feathers of the serpent-wings are modeled and incised, as are the scaley legs and talons. The bird is decked out in jade jewels: a diadem, circular earflares, and a large necklace with a central pendant.

A kneeling figure on the other removable lid presents the bird with a bundle in devotional tribute. He wears a loincloth, a feathered, jeweled headdress, nose ornaments, ear ornaments, and a necklace. He stares at the bird in reverence, stoic in his encounter with this resplendent being. The young man’s submissive position may be a trap laid to bring down the portentous bird. Despite his impending defeat, this majestic creature was revered as an avian manifestation (or a spiritual companion, way) of the god Itzamnaaj, the old celestial god. A feline or simian creature, also wearing a necklace, bridges the two chambers of the vessel and seems to climb up the side towards the two figures on top.

This vessel may be part of a series of three double-chambered blackware vessels featuring different scenes involving the PBD; one was excavated from a tomb at Calakmul, Mexico. Religious narratives from the late first millennium BCE until the colonial period feature this creature, which has a large hooked raptor beak grabbing a serpent and animate wings in the form of stylized serpents. Artists depicted the PBD descending from the sky, perching on trees, receiving offerings from humanlike figures, or even being shot by a blowgun.
persona1_adapted:
A master Maya potter shaped a scene with three characters onto the body and lids of this special vessel. The main part of the vessel has two identical round chambers connected by a hollow bridge in the middle. Each chamber rests on two square supports. This style of pottery uses a very fine reddish or gray clay, fired with a reddish-black slip (a liquid clay coating). It comes from what is now northern Guatemala and southern Campeche, Mexico. Artists who made this highly polished blackware in the 5th-6th centuries were skilled at carving and scratching designs, often on very thin walls or lids. Such a fine vessel would have been used for very special feasts or rituals. Later, it might have been buried in the tomb of its owner by their descendants.

In this scene, a large supernatural bird called the Principal Bird Deity (PBD) sits on one lid (which is actually a false lid, fused to the chamber). It faces a kneeling humanlike figure. Inside the bird's head is a small whistle chamber, visible in an X-ray image. The whistle sounds when water is poured into the opposite chamber. The bird's large beak and fleshy bump (caruncle) stick out from its open mouth, which connects to the inside of the vessel. A stylized double-headed serpent hangs from the beak. The bird's shiny eyes are square, with an L-shaped pupil. Maya artists used this shape to show that a being had supernatural vision. The feathers of the serpent-wings are carved and incised (scratched into the clay), as are the scaly legs and claws. The bird is decorated with jade jewels: a crown, round ear ornaments, and a large necklace with a central pendant.

A kneeling figure on the other removable lid offers the bird a bundle as a gift of devotion. He wears a loincloth, a feathered and jeweled headdress, nose ornaments, ear ornaments, and a necklace. He stares at the bird with deep respect, staying still and serious in this meeting with the magnificent creature. The young man's kneeling position might actually be a trap meant to bring down the powerful bird. Even though the bird will be defeated, it was still honored as a bird form (or a spiritual companion, called a *way*) of the god Itzamnaaj, the old sky god. A feline or monkey-like creature, also wearing a necklace, connects the two chambers of the vessel. It seems to climb up the side toward the two figures on top.

This vessel may be part of a set of three double-chambered blackware vessels showing different scenes with the PBD. One was dug up from a tomb at Calakmul, Mexico. Religious stories from the late first millennium BCE until the colonial period feature this creature. It has a large hooked raptor beak grabbing a serpent, and wings made of stylized serpents. Artists showed the PBD coming down from the sky, sitting on trees, receiving offerings from humanlike figures, or even being shot with a blowgun.

CuratorialText: Ceramic drums with central, bulging sounding chambers were made in southern Peru at the turn of the first millennium. Among the most elaborately finished are those of Nasca style. They were surfaced with the many rich colors commonly used on Nasca ceramic vessels. A favored form was one in which a fat-bodied figure was worked into the shape of the instrument, the rotund body spreading out equally on all sides and the legs drawn up in the front. The figure is depicted atop the wide mouth of the drum, over which a skin would have been stretched. The image is symbolically complex; a snake emerges from under the figure's chin and a killer whale outlines each eye. The killer whales are in profile and show the "two-tone" color differentiation normally given them in Nasca depictions. A headband is wound around the head and tied to form a hornlike projection on the forehead. In back, the figure's hair is shown as serpents with long tongues.
persona1_adapted:
In southern Peru, around the year 1000 CE, artists made ceramic drums with big, bulging middle sections. The most detailed ones were made in the Nasca style. These drums were covered with many bright colors, just like other Nasca pottery.

A popular design showed a figure with a fat body shaped into the drum itself. The round body spread out evenly on all sides, and the legs were pulled up in front. The figure sits on top of the drum's wide opening, where a skin would have been stretched to make the drumhead.

The image has deep meaning. A snake comes out from under the figure's chin. Around each eye, there is a killer whale. These whales are shown from the side, with the two-tone coloring that Nasca artists often used. A headband wraps around the figure's head and ties into a horn-like shape on the forehead. On the back, the figure's hair is shown as snakes with long tongues.

CuratorialText: The three-dimensional figures of the Tolita-Tumaco area are among the most striking of Precolumbian gold objects. The present example is particularly distinguished by its fancy nose ornament, evidence that some form of headdress was once present, and by the position of the hands, which originally held objects now missing, perhaps an atlatl and darts. The feet too have been lost. Made of many pieces of high-quality sheet gold, the figure may have been clothed for special occasions. The Tolita-Tumaco style area crosses the Colombia-Ecuador border along the humid Pacific coast.
persona1_adapted: The three-dimensional figures from the Tolita-Tumaco region are some of the most impressive gold objects from ancient America. This figure stands out because of its fancy nose ornament. It also shows signs that it once wore some kind of headdress. The position of its hands suggests it originally held objects that are now missing—possibly an atlatl (a tool for throwing spears) and darts. The feet are also lost. The figure was made from many pieces of high-quality sheet gold. It may have been dressed in clothing for special events. The Tolita-Tumaco style area stretches across the border between Colombia and Ecuador, along the humid Pacific coast.

CuratorialText: This cylindrical drinking cup is the magnum opus of the Maya vase painter known as the Metropolitan Master. It contains one of the finest extant deity portraits from the Classic Maya corpus. The young rain god, named Chahk, poses mid-stride, lifting off his left foot and extending the right leg in front of him, gracefully pointing his toes. The underside of each leg is marked with a scale pattern, evoking a shimmering and wet aquatic creature. He wears a complex loincloth that shows the typical knotted cotton of his costuming, and the rear panel of the loincloth contorts as if the artist was referencing a fish tail. The necklace of Chahk is quite unique: the collar consists of pendant extruded eyeballs and the pectoral is in the shape of an upside-down water jar marked with the hieroglyph for darkness, with what looks like a small serpent emerging from its mouth. His other jewels on the ankles and wrists may be jade or another precious material, and his headdress is a wild tangle of watery vegetation. The typical shell earrings accent the barbel extending from the nostril and projecting off of the god’s chin. His humanlike quality is emphasized by the artists’ brushstrokes in the distinguished profile of the face, the point of the elbow, the ankle joints, and finger and toenails. In his right hand, he grasps the wooden handle of a shining stone axe, and his left hand holds an animate stone. The rain god actively engages with a giant agnathus creature, likely the representation of a witz, the spirit of a mountain, as his leg crosses in front of the upper lip while his left arm passes behind. It is as if the rain personified needs to partake in a ritual combative dance with an animate mountain to set the actions presented here in motion. The mountain monster has a feathered eyelid present on crocodilians in Maya art, a jagged tooth, and liquid or vegetation spewing forth and spilling on the ground line at Chahk’s feet. The head of the mountain is covered with the grape bunch markings that signify that it is a stony place. Most significant about the zoomorphic mountain is the character reclining on top of it: the supernatural baby jaguar. The face of the jaguar baby character is clearly supernatural and contrasts sharply with Chahk’s more human-like visage. The square eye is a marker of godliness in Maya art, and the overbite with shark-like tooth also denotes that this reclining personage is a peer of Chahk and the sun god, both of which are shown with this protruding tooth at times. The ears, hands, feet, and tail are masterfully painted to portray those of a jaguar. In fact, all signs point to this creature as an infant jaguar, known from other portrayals of the same reclining infant, and the hieroglyphic logogram found in some royal names. The baby jaguar has a similar knotted hairstyle and vegetal headdress as Chahk and flails about as if fitfully searching for stability. Almost touching the baby jaguar is a frightening creature of the night, with a skeletal head marked with bone sutures and two extruded eyeballs, insect-like carapace, distended belly of a corpse, and spindly legs with knobby knees. This is likely a death god, a denizen of the Maya underworld who plays a role in this myth of the birth of the baby jaguar. He dances too, raising off his left foot and extending the right. His outstretched arms hold tense hands that seem to reach for the baby jaguar or the hieroglyphic caption hovering above him. The death god wears an elaborate backrack composed of textiles, bone elements, and extruded eyeballs. The death god has two creepy companions. Floating above the ground is a firefly. He appears as a skeletal cyclops, the central eye in the form of akbal, the hieroglyph for darkness. Three extruded eyeballs crown his head, and the artist delicately rendered his insect-like hind legs and abdomen. He holds a cigar or torch, a Maya artistic convention for signifying a creature of the night: the light of a cigar being smoked in the darkness mimics the natural flickering of mating bioluminescence of lightning bugs. Below the firefly is a mischievous dog with a spotted tail and ears. He pants in the rear of the dancing death god and raises up on his hind legs as if begging for a morsel of food or playing. The text comprising the eight glyph blocks that float above the baby jaguar is opaque in meaning. The owner is named as a "k’uhul chatan winik," a royal title in use during the Classic Period at certain places. The overarching theme of this vessel is the necessary interaction of life-giving rains and rotting death, the contrasts needed to produce life, represented in this case by the baby jaguar god. The death god and his companions are present as organic remains decay and when rains fall, life begins at the site of the fertilized space. All of this occurs on the top of a mythological mountain at the center of the Maya world. The wash used by the artist on the lower portion of the vessel perhaps represents the steamy breath that emerges from caves in order to create clouds and produce rain. Chahk in all his glory emerges from and interacts with the cave as he celebrates the birth of the baby jaguar. The death god’s pose seems to show that the baby jaguar was snatched from the clutches of death itself.
persona1_adapted:
This cylindrical drinking cup is the best work of a Maya vase painter known as the Metropolitan Master. It shows one of the finest portraits of a god from the Classic Maya period.

The young rain god, named Chahk, is caught mid-step. He lifts his left foot and stretches his right leg forward, with his toes gracefully pointed. The underside of each leg has a scale pattern. This makes him look like a shiny, wet water creature.

He wears a complex loincloth made of knotted cotton, which was typical for his costume. The back panel of the loincloth twists as if the artist was hinting at a fish tail. Chahk's necklace is very unusual. The collar is made of hanging eyeballs. The chest piece is shaped like an upside-down water jar. It is marked with the hieroglyph for darkness, and a small snake seems to come out of its mouth.

His other jewelry on the ankles and wrists might be jade or another precious material. His headdress is a wild mess of water plants. Typical shell earrings accent the barbel—a fleshy growth—that sticks out from his nostril and chin.

The artist's brushstrokes make Chahk look very human. You can see this in the clear profile of his face, the point of his elbow, his ankle joints, and his fingernails and toenails. In his right hand, he grips the wooden handle of a shiny stone axe. In his left hand, he holds a stone that seems alive.

The rain god is actively fighting with a giant, jawless creature. This creature likely represents a *witz*, the spirit of a mountain. Chahk's leg crosses in front of the creature's upper lip, while his left arm passes behind it. It is as if the rain god must perform a ritual combat dance with a living mountain to start the actions shown here.

The mountain monster has a feathered eyelid, which is common for crocodile-like creatures in Maya art. It also has a jagged tooth. Liquid or plants spray out from it and spill onto the ground at Chahk's feet. The mountain's head is covered with grape-like markings, which show that it is a rocky place.

The most important part of this animal-shaped mountain is the character lying on top of it: the supernatural baby jaguar. The baby jaguar's face is clearly that of a god. It looks very different from Chahk's more human face. The square eye is a sign of godliness in Maya art. The overbite with a shark-like tooth also shows that this lying figure is an equal of Chahk and the sun god, who are sometimes shown with this same tooth. The ears, hands, feet, and tail are masterfully painted to look like those of a jaguar.

In fact, everything points to this creature being a baby jaguar. We know this from other paintings of the same lying infant and from the hieroglyphic logogram found in some royal names. The baby jaguar has a knotted hairstyle and a plant headdress similar to Chahk's. He flails around as if he is restlessly searching for stability.

Almost touching the baby jaguar is a frightening creature of the night. It has a skeletal head marked with bone sutures and two bulging eyeballs. It has an insect-like shell, a swollen belly like a corpse, and spindly legs with knobby knees. This is likely a death god, a being from the Maya underworld who plays a role in the myth of the baby jaguar's birth.

He dances too, lifting his left foot and extending his right. His outstretched arms hold tense hands that seem to reach for the baby jaguar or the hieroglyphic caption floating above him. The death god wears an elaborate backrack made of textiles, bone pieces, and bulging eyeballs.

The death god has two creepy companions. Floating above the ground is a firefly. He looks like a skeleton with one eye. The central eye is in the form of *akbal*, the hieroglyph for darkness. Three bulging eyeballs crown his head. The artist carefully drew his insect-like hind legs and abdomen. He holds a cigar or torch. This is a Maya art convention for showing a creature of the night: the light of a cigar being smoked in the darkness mimics the natural flickering of the mating glow of lightning bugs.

Below the firefly is a mischievous dog with a spotted tail and ears. He pants behind the dancing death god and rises up on his hind legs as if begging for a bit of food or playing.

The text made up of eight glyph blocks floating above the baby jaguar is hard to understand. The owner is named as a *k'uhul chatan winik*, a royal title used during the Classic Period in certain places.

The main theme of this vessel is the necessary interaction between life-giving rain and rotting death. These contrasts are needed to produce life, represented here by the baby jaguar god. The death god and his companions are present because when organic matter decays and rains fall, life begins in that fertilized space. All of this happens on top of a mythological mountain at the center of the Maya world.

The wash that the artist used on the lower part of the vessel might represent the steamy breath that comes out of caves to create clouds and produce rain. Chahk in all his glory emerges from and interacts with the cave as he celebrates the birth of the baby jaguar. The death god's pose seems to show that the baby jaguar was snatched from the clutches of death itself.

CuratorialText: Paired male and female figures are common subjects in the ceramics of the western Mexico peoples of Jalisco, Colima, and Nayarit. Named for the states in which the works have been discovered in quantity in deeply buried, multichambered shaft tombs, the ceramic sculptures show aspects of daily and ritual life, from ballgame representations to feasting ceremonies to deceptively simple family scenes. While subject matter was relatively consistent throughout the entire region, style distinctions existed. The figures here are in Nayarit's Ixtlán del Río style, which is characterized by thin, pliant limbs, and stout, cylindrical torsos. Gender differences center on attire and activity, but both sexes wear numerous ornaments on ears and nose. Here the male figure wears a conical hat, a shoulder cloth, and a loincloth. He holds a drum in his right hand and raised drumstick in the other. His companion wears a shoulder cloth and skirt, and her right arm is draped around his shoulder.
persona1_adapted:
In western Mexico, artists from the regions of Jalisco, Colima, and Nayarit often made ceramic sculptures of male and female pairs. These sculptures were found in deep, underground tombs with multiple rooms, called shaft tombs. The ceramics show scenes from daily life and special ceremonies. Some scenes show ballgames, feasting, or simple family moments.

Although the subjects were similar across the region, each area had its own style. These two figures are from the Ixtlán del Río style in Nayarit. This style is known for thin, flexible-looking limbs and short, round bodies. The differences between male and female figures are shown through their clothes and what they are doing. Both wear many ear and nose ornaments.

The male figure wears a cone-shaped hat, a cloth over his shoulder, and a loincloth. He holds a drum in his right hand and a raised drumstick in the other. His female partner wears a shoulder cloth and a skirt. Her right arm is wrapped around his shoulder.

CuratorialText: This Wari bottle seamlessly merges the body of an anthropomorphized feline with that of a flask-like vessel. Combining in-the-round, low-relief, and painted elements, this ceramic is both visually and technically intricate. The depth of its earthy tones and its balance of volumes brilliantly illustrate the ability of Wari artists to fuse Andean mastery of color and sculpture in the art of pottery. Standing upright with its arms against its body, the feline’s posture de-emphasizes its animality. The figure stares defiantly at the viewer with its eyes wide-open, a threatening stance amplified by its upright ears, gaping fanged mouth, and dotted eyebrows. The animal’s paws are partially humanized, depicted with digits rather than claws and topped with a grey wristband. The clawed fingers grab a fantastical creature, commonly known as the "humped animal" in scholarship (Menzel 1964; Knobloch 1983), on each side (one side is damaged, perhaps from prolonged exposure to salts in the ground). The two humped animals were painted in brick red with a yellow outline on raised roundels. This color scheme is reversed on the two figures on the circumference of the bottle, whose elongated bodies fit closely the curvature of the vessel’s chamber. Geometric designs, such as dotted circles and rhombuses with appendages, complement the painted decoration of the vessel’s body. A row of orange, brick red, and grey chevrons crown the bottle’s spout, a feature common to Wari ceramics. The highly burnished orange slip (a suspension of clay mixed with water, often with added pigments) on this bottle provides a smooth and glossy background that accentuates the raised roundels and feline’s legs on the ceramic. The white highlights stand out on this surface, in particular the eyes, fangs, and claws of the multiple figures—lethal attributes that suggest that this object abounds with dangerous wildlife. By bringing together naturalistically-rendered animal features, hybrid supernatural figures, and abstract geometric motifs, this bottle invokes a complex microcosm of bodies and forms, which the viewer must examine from a distance as well as up-close. The imagery unfolds as the viewer handles, turns, and angles the vessel. This bottle comes from a looted elite burial from the Ingenio Valley in the Nasca drainage on the south coast of present-day Peru (Tello 1917; Menzel 1968). From around 600 to 1000 CE, the Wari ran an empire that expanded throughout most of the Central Andes, albeit with varying degrees of presence and control. Wari culture was born out of the interactions between the Huarpa, a local group of the central highlands in present-day Ayacucho, and the Nasca people on the South Coast. Its empire bridged multiple regions, cultures, and artistic practices. Indeed, this bottle features techniques and motifs inherited from the Nasca repertoire, from its polychromy to motifs such as the humped animal and the ventrally extended creature, but they are combined with elements taken from northern traditions, such as the modeled feline. An almost identical bottle was excavated at the site of Wilkawain, in the northern highlands of Peru (Paredes Olvera 2016: fig. 16). Archaeologists believe that it was imported there, possibly from the Wari heartland. Whether the bottles from Wilkawain and the Ingenio Valley were both made in the same workshop and then exported to different regions remains unknown. While other Wari bottles have a similarly flattened body, this model differs in that it has to be oriented sideways in order for the viewer to face the feline, adapting the form of the container to the figure. Although it is possible that this bottle was never used and only destined to be deposited in a burial, it could have once contained liquid to be poured on the ground or ingested, perhaps maize- or red peppercorn- beer (Sayre et al. 2012). The vessel’s small size suggests that it was meant for personal devotional practice. As one held this bottle featuring multiple powerful beings in one’s hands, the viewing experience would have evolved, especially as the bottle was tilted upside-down to pour liquid from the bottle’s spout. Would this physical manipulation have prompted in the user sensations of fear and respect for these entities, or instead a sense of control over them?
persona1_adapted:
This Wari bottle blends the body of a cat-like creature with the shape of a flask. The cat is part human, and it stands upright with its arms at its sides. This pose makes the animal seem less wild.

The bottle uses three types of decoration: three-dimensional shapes, low-relief (slightly raised) designs, and painted details. The earthy colors and balanced shapes show how skilled Wari artists were at combining color and sculpture in pottery.

The cat stares directly at the viewer with wide eyes. Its upright ears, open mouth with fangs, and dotted eyebrows make it look threatening. Its paws are partly human, with fingers instead of claws, and each paw wears a grey wristband. The clawed fingers grab a strange creature on each side. Scholars call this creature the "humped animal." One side is damaged, possibly from long contact with salts in the ground.

The two humped animals are painted brick red with yellow outlines on raised roundels (circular raised areas). The colors are swapped on two other figures around the bottle. These figures have long bodies that follow the curve of the bottle. Geometric designs, like dotted circles and rhombuses with extra parts, add to the painted decoration. A row of orange, brick red, and grey chevrons (V-shaped patterns) sits around the bottle’s spout. This is a common feature of Wari ceramics.

The bottle has a highly polished orange slip. A slip is a mix of clay and water, often with added color. This smooth, shiny background makes the raised roundels and the cat’s legs stand out. White highlights on the eyes, fangs, and claws catch the eye. These deadly features suggest the bottle is full of dangerous animals.

By mixing realistic animal features, hybrid supernatural figures, and abstract geometric patterns, this bottle creates a complex world of bodies and forms. The viewer must look at it from far away and up close. The images reveal themselves as the viewer handles, turns, and angles the bottle.

This bottle comes from a looted elite burial in the Ingenio Valley, in the Nasca region on the south coast of present-day Peru. From around 600 to 1000 CE, the Wari ran an empire that spread across most of the Central Andes, though their control varied. Wari culture grew from interactions between the Huarpa people (from the central highlands in present-day Ayacucho) and the Nasca people (from the South Coast). The empire connected many regions, cultures, and art styles.

This bottle uses techniques and designs from the Nasca tradition, such as its many colors and motifs like the humped animal. But it also combines elements from northern traditions, like the modeled cat. An almost identical bottle was found at the site of Wilkawain, in the northern highlands of Peru. Archaeologists think it was brought there, possibly from the Wari heartland. Whether both bottles were made in the same workshop and then sent to different regions is unknown.

Other Wari bottles have a similar flat body, but this one is different. It must be turned sideways for the viewer to face the cat. This adapts the bottle’s shape to the figure. Although the bottle may have never been used and was only meant for a burial, it could have once held liquid to be poured on the ground or drunk. That liquid might have been maize beer or red peppercorn beer. The bottle’s small size suggests it was used for personal devotion. As someone held this bottle with its many powerful beings, the viewing experience would change, especially when tilting it upside-down to pour liquid from the spout. Would this physical handling have made the user feel fear and respect for these beings, or instead a sense of control over them?

CuratorialText: The imagery on this plate represents the earliest known depiction of a well-known episode from the story of Bahram Gur or Bahram V, the Sasanian king (r. 420 –438). The story seems to have been popular for centuries—both in Sasanian times and under the Islamic rule that followed—but was only recorded in the Shahnamah, or Book of Kings, by Firdausi in the early eleventh century A.D., centuries after this plate was created. The Shahnamah recounts how Bahram Gur, challenged to feats of archery by his favorite musician, Azadeh, changed a male gazelle to a female and a female to a male and pinned the hind leg of an animal to its ear. This plate directly refers to the story of Bahram Gur because the horns of the male have been shot off with an arrow, transforming his appearance into that of a female, while the two arrows embedded in the head of the female make her appear to have horns like those of a male gazelle. This plate was likely hammered into shape and then decorated to highlight its varied surface contours and colors, making its human and animal forms stand out in relief. It was partially gilded using a mixture of mercury and gold, which could be painted onto the surface. Large numbers of gold and silver vessels made during Sasanian rule in Iran and Mesopotamia have survived, demonstrating an ancient Near Eastern tradition that spread westward to the Hellenistic and Roman world, where vessels made of precious metals also became symbols of power and prestige. These objects, presumably official state products, were presented as royal gifts to persons of importance within Sasanian Iran and beyond its borders.
persona1_adapted:
The images on this plate show the earliest known scene from a famous story about Bahram Gur (also called Bahram V), a king who ruled from 420 to 438. The story was popular for hundreds of years, both during Bahram’s time and later under Islamic rule. But it was not written down until the early 1000s A.D., in a book called the *Shahnamah* (or *Book of Kings*) by the poet Firdausi. That is centuries after this plate was made.

The *Shahnamah* tells how Bahram Gur’s favorite musician, Azadeh, challenged him to do amazing archery tricks. He shot a male gazelle so that it looked like a female, and a female so that it looked like a male. He also pinned a gazelle’s back leg to its ear. This plate shows that story directly. The male gazelle’s horns have been shot off with an arrow, making it look like a female. The female gazelle has two arrows in its head, which make it appear to have horns like a male.

The plate was probably hammered into shape first. Then it was decorated to make the surface patterns and colors stand out. The human and animal figures rise up in relief (raised from the background). Part of the plate was covered with a mix of mercury and gold, which could be painted on.

Many gold and silver vessels from the Sasanian period in Iran and Mesopotamia have survived. They show an old tradition from the ancient Near East that spread west to the Greek and Roman worlds. There, precious metal vessels also became symbols of power and status. These objects were likely official royal products. They were given as gifts to important people inside and outside the Sasanian empire.

CuratorialText: This solid-cast sculpture is one of a pair of nearly identical images of a hero or a demon wearing the upturned boots associated with highland regions, his power enhanced by the mighty horns of the ibex on his head and the body and wings of a bird of prey draped around his shoulders. It was created at the time the first cities emerged in ancient Sumer. A new world view conceived of human figures in realistic terms, through accurate proportions and highly modeled forms with distinctive features. The blending of human and animal forms to visualize the supernatural world and perhaps to express shamanistic beliefs, however, is more characteristic of the contemporary arts of Proto-Elamite Iran, where a remarkable tradition of metalworking developed during this period.
persona1_adapted:
This solid-cast metal sculpture is one of two nearly identical figures. It shows a hero or a demon. The figure wears upturned boots, which were common in highland regions. Its power is shown by the large horns of an ibex on its head. Around its shoulders are the body and wings of a bird of prey.

This artwork was made when the first cities appeared in ancient Sumer. At that time, people had a new way of seeing the world. They began to show human figures in a realistic style. Artists used correct body proportions and highly detailed forms with unique features.

However, mixing human and animal forms to show the supernatural world is more typical of the art from Proto-Elamite Iran. This style may have expressed shamanistic beliefs. During this period, a remarkable tradition of metalworking developed in that region.

CuratorialText: This fragmentary piece takes the form of the face of a roaring lion. It was found in a storeroom at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. During the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C., the royal complexes at Nimrud were sacked and burned, and the lion was likely blackened through exposure to fire at this time. The flat back suggests that this piece may have originally been attached to a piece of wooden furniture such as the arm of a chair.
