The right cheek, eyebrows, and incised muzzle are detailed with incised dots and the eyes are deeply carved for now-lost semiprecious stone or colored glass inlays. A pattern of painted white triangles outlined with raised ribs is preserved on the right side at the back of the face, perhaps to represent a mane. Scenes of the Assyrian king hunting lions are depicted on royal reliefs from the Assyrian palaces. Because it shares the same subject matter, this piece has been attributed to an Assyrian style. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.
persona1_adapted:
This broken piece is shaped like the face of a roaring lion. It was found in a storage room at Fort Shalmaneser. That fort was a royal building in the ancient city of Nimrud. The Assyrians likely used it to store treasure and goods taken during their military campaigns.

When the Assyrian Empire was finally defeated at the end of the 600s B.C., the royal buildings at Nimrud were attacked and burned. The lion was probably darkened by fire at that time.

The back of the piece is flat. This suggests it was once attached to a wooden piece of furniture, like the arm of a chair. The right cheek, eyebrows, and muzzle (the nose and mouth area) are decorated with small carved dots. The eyes are deeply carved. They once held shiny stones or colored glass, but those are now missing.

On the right side, at the back of the face, there is a pattern of white triangles painted with raised lines around them. This may have been meant to show a mane.

Scenes of Assyrian kings hunting lions appear on stone carvings from Assyrian palaces. Because this piece shows the same subject, experts say it was made in the Assyrian style.

The Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II built the palaces and storerooms at Nimrud. These buildings held thousands of carved ivory pieces. Most of the ivories were used as decorations on furniture or as small precious objects, like boxes.

Some ivories were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian stone carvings on the palace walls. But most of the ivories show images and styles from North Syria and the Phoenician city-states.

Phoenician-style ivories use images from Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing Egyptian-style crowns. They also use detailed carving methods like openwork (cut-out designs) and colored glass inlay.

North Syrian-style ivories tend to show thicker, stronger figures in more active poses. They are carved as solid pieces with fewer extra decorations.

However, some pieces do not fit neatly into any of these three styles.

Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as tribute (gifts from weaker states) or as booty (goods taken from conquered enemies). Some may have been made in workshops at Nimrud.

The ivory tusks used to make these objects almost certainly came from African elephants. These elephants were brought from lands south of Egypt. However, elephants did live in several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the 700s B.C.

CuratorialText: Of the many legacies left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is paramount. At the end of the fourth millennium B.C., written language developed in the region, first as pictographs and then evolving into abstract forms called cuneiform. The pictographs, like the ones on this tablet, are called proto-cuneiform and were drawn in the clay with a pointed implement. Circular impressions alongside the pictographs represented numerical symbols. Cuneiform (meaning wedge-shaped) script was written by pressing a reed pen or stylus with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. Clay, when dried to a somewhat hardened state, made a fine surface for writing, and when fired the records written on it became permanent. Early writing was used primarily as a means of recording and storing economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple, although the absence of verbs in early texts makes them difficult to interpret with certainty. In addition to the writing that appears on this tablet, the imagery of the cylinder seal, which was incompletely impressed on both faces and the edges of the tablet before it was inscribed, also records information. This seal apparently has not survived. The seal impression depicts a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash and hunting or herding boars in a reed marsh. He is the so-called priest-king, a male figure who can be identified by his dress and pose. Here he appears in his role as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.
persona1_adapted:
Of the many important things left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is the most important. At the end of the fourth millennium B.C., written language developed in the region. It first appeared as pictographs—simple pictures that stand for words or ideas. Over time, these pictures evolved into abstract forms called cuneiform.

The pictographs on this tablet are called proto-cuneiform. They were drawn into the clay with a pointed tool. Small circular marks next to the pictographs stood for numbers.

Cuneiform means "wedge-shaped." This script was made by pressing a reed pen (called a stylus) with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. When clay dried to a somewhat hard state, it made a good surface for writing. When fired, the records became permanent.

Early writing was mainly used to record and store economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple. However, early texts have no verbs, so it is hard to know for sure what they mean.

Besides the writing on this tablet, the image from a cylinder seal also records information. The seal was pressed onto both sides and the edges of the tablet before the writing was added. This seal has not survived. The seal impression shows a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash. He is hunting or herding boars in a reed marsh. This figure is called the priest-king. He can be identified by his dress and pose. Here he appears as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.

CuratorialText:
In ancient Mesopotamia, a cylinder-shaped seal could be rolled on a variety of objects made of clay. When seals were impressed on tablets or tablet cases the seal impressions served to identify the authority responsible for what was written in the documents, much as a signature does today. When seals were impressed on sealings — lumps of clay that were used to secure doors and the lids of storage jars— the seal impressions served to identify their owner and protect against unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals have survived because they were made of durable materials, particularly stone, but also metal and fired clay. Perforated through the middle like a bead, seals were also believed to have apotropaic, or protective, functions and were worn as jewelry or pinned on garments.

The modern impression of a seal is shown here so that the entire design can be seen. The scene is composed of two basic groupings that form an overall continuous design unified by the stylized landscape setting. In one group, two tall trees flank a hunter grasping an ibex. Above the man a cuneiform inscription gives the name of the seal owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession, cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other and flanking three trees. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when the iconography used by the seal engraver expanded to include a variety of new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient motif of the hunt is given a new setting in a clearly defined landscape. If there was a relationship between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer understood.

Sacred and secular ideas, fundamental to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples, were visualized in the miniature images carved on the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which the forms were cut into the stone, to create the raised impression. The challenge of the seal carver was to create a design that would maintain its balance and clarity when rolled out only half its length on a small surface or twice its length on a larger surface. On the left and right edges of the impression one can see how the rolled out design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art of the ancient Near East, which survives only in a fragmentary state, cylinder seals are in the unique position of appearing almost exactly as they would have looked to the ancient people who used them.
persona1_adapted:
In ancient Mesopotamia, people used special cylinder-shaped seals. These seals could be rolled onto objects made of clay. When rolled on clay tablets or tablet cases, the seal acted like a signature. It showed who was responsible for the document's writing.

Seals were also used on clay lumps called sealings. These lumps secured doors and storage jar lids. The seal impression showed who owned the item and helped prevent unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals survive today because they were made from strong materials. Most were stone, but some were metal or fired clay. Seals had a hole through the middle, like a bead. People believed they had protective powers. They wore them as jewelry or pinned them on their clothes.

The modern impression shown here lets you see the whole design. The scene has two main groups that form one continuous picture. A stylized landscape connects them. In one group, two tall trees stand on either side of a hunter. The hunter is grabbing an ibex, a type of wild goat. Above the man, there is a cuneiform inscription. It gives the seal owner's name, Balu-ili, who was a court official. It also says his job: cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains. They face each other, with three trees between them.

This seal was made during the Akkadian period. At that time, seal engravers started using new subjects in their designs. These included myths, themes, and stories. Here, the old idea of a hunt is shown in a clear landscape. If there was a connection between this image and the written text, we no longer know what it means.

Sacred and everyday ideas were important to ancient Mesopotamian people. These ideas were shown in the tiny images carved on seals. The carver used a technique called intaglio. This means they cut the design into the stone. When rolled, the cut parts created a raised impression. The seal carver had a challenge: to make a design that looked balanced and clear. It had to work when rolled only halfway on a small surface, or twice its length on a larger one. On the left and right edges of the impression, you can see the design starting to repeat. Unlike much ancient Near Eastern art, which survives only in broken pieces, cylinder seals look almost exactly as they did to the people who used them long ago.

CuratorialText: This type of figurine known as an eye idol, made of stone and having incised eyes, has been excavated at Tell Brak, where thousands were found in a building now called the Eye Temple. They were probably dedicated there as offerings. Many are incised with multiple sets of eyes, others with jewelry, and still others with representations of "children"—smaller eyes and body carved on the body of the larger idol. Wide eyes demonstrate attentiveness to the gods in much of Mesopotamian art.
persona1_adapted: This type of figurine is called an eye idol. It is made of stone, and its eyes are carved into the surface. These idols were dug up at a place called Tell Brak. Thousands of them were found in a building now known as the Eye Temple. People probably left them there as offerings. Many idols have several sets of eyes carved on them. Others show jewelry. Some even have small eyes and bodies carved onto the larger idol. These small figures may represent children. In much of Mesopotamian art, wide eyes show that a person is paying close attention to the gods.

CuratorialText: Some of the most elaborate representations of females in the art of the ancient Near East are images of divine and cult figures whose association with certain aspects of life made them essential to the welfare of humanity. Fertility, procreation, and the growth of crops and livestock were among the basic concepts identified with female divinities. Representations of nude females in clay, stone, and metal are the simplest and most obvious expression of these concepts, and such figures appear throughout antiquity in many regions. This striking example in clay from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran is hollow and probably served as a cult vessel as well as a sacred image.
persona1_adapted:
In the art of the ancient Near East, some of the most detailed images of women show goddesses and religious figures. These figures were linked to important parts of life, so people believed they were essential for human well-being. Basic ideas like fertility, having children, and the growth of crops and animals were connected to female gods.

Simple statues of naked women—made from clay, stone, and metal—are the clearest way to show these ideas. Such statues appear in many regions throughout ancient times. This striking example is made of clay and comes from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran. It is hollow and probably served both as a sacred image and as a container used in religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Distinctive clay female figures like this one were produced at sites belonging to the Halaf culture. This example displays a strong stylization with an emphasis on the sexual features. She sits with her large thighs extended, supporting her breasts with her arms; neither hands nor feet are shown. Her head is missing; in other figures of this type when the head is intact, it is elongated into a large noselike projection but otherwise is featureless. Remains of paint may represent jewelry. The meaning of such representations is unknown but may be connected with fertility. The stylized depiction of the nude female form remained an artistic convention in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean for several millennia.
persona1_adapted:
This clay female figure comes from the Halaf culture. People made figures like this one at several of their settlements.

The figure is highly stylized. This means the artist simplified the body and focused on certain features. In this case, the sexual features stand out the most.

She sits with her large thighs spread out. Her arms support her breasts. She has no hands or feet.

Her head is missing. On other figures of this type that still have their heads, the head is long and shaped like a large nose. It has no other features like eyes or a mouth.

Traces of paint may show that she wore jewelry.

We do not know what these figures meant to the people who made them. They may have been connected to fertility, or the ability to have children.

This stylized way of showing the nude female body became a common artistic tradition. It lasted for several thousand years in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean region.

CuratorialText: Furniture inlaid with carved ivory plaques was highly prized by the Assyrian kings. During the ninth to seventh centuries B.C., vast quantities of luxury goods, often embellished with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles, accumulated in Assyrian palaces, much of it as booty or tribute. This object belongs to a group of plaques depicting animals and stylized plants. They were made by master carvers in a delicate openwork technique characteristic of Phoenician ivory carving. However, the style and subjects depicted have close parallels on stone relief sculptures from Tell Halaf, in northern Syria, and a debate exists over which tradition produced these fine panels.
persona1_adapted:
Assyrian kings really valued furniture decorated with carved ivory plaques. From the 800s to the 600s B.C., huge amounts of luxury goods piled up in Assyrian palaces. These items were often decorated with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles. Much of it came as war prizes or tribute payments.

This object is part of a group of plaques showing animals and stylized plants. Master carvers made them using a delicate openwork technique. This technique is a style where the background is cut away, leaving a lace-like design. It was typical of Phoenician ivory carving.

But the style and subjects shown are very similar to stone relief sculptures from Tell Halaf in northern Syria. So experts debate which tradition actually produced these fine panels.

CuratorialText: This statuette group, carved in the round, was found with five other statuettes with similar imagery. The six statuettes were excavated in and just below two arched niches built into the wall of a room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. Those found just below the niches could have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during the the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C. Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory plinths and exhibited in the arched niches, perhaps arranged in a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as tribute to the Assyrian king. A frontally facing male, striding to the right with his lower body in profile, grasps the horns of an oryx (a species of desert-dwelling antelope) that strides behind him. The male figure’s eyes, necklace, and armlet were carved to receive colored glass or semiprecious stone inlays. He wears a short kilt belted with a long sash, embroidered with decoration including two uraei (mythical, fire-spitting serpents), zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey, whose fur is rendered with short incisions, sits erect on his left shoulder and grasps his short curly hair. Although his right arm does not survive, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.
persona1_adapted:
This small group of carved figures was found with five others that look similar. All six were discovered in and below two arched spaces built into a wall at Fort Shalmaneser. This was a royal building in the ancient city of Nimrud. The Assyrians probably used it to store treasure and gifts they collected during military campaigns.

The figures found below the niches may have fallen when the palaces were destroyed. This happened when Assyria was finally defeated at the end of the 600s B.C. Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory bases and displayed in the arched niches. They might have been arranged like a parade of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as gifts to the Assyrian king.

The carving shows a man facing forward, but his lower body is turned to the right. He is walking and holding the horns of an oryx—a type of antelope that lives in deserts. The oryx walks behind him. The man's eyes, necklace, and armband were carved to hold colored glass or semiprecious stone inlays, though these are now missing.

He wears a short kilt held by a long sash. The sash is decorated with patterns, including two uraei (mythical snakes that spit fire), zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey sits upright on his left shoulder and holds his short curly hair. The monkey's fur is shown with small cut lines. The man's right arm is missing, but it probably once held a patterned leopard skin draped over his shoulder.

The Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II built the palaces and storerooms at Nimrud. They held thousands of carved ivory pieces. Most of these ivories were used as decorations on furniture or as small precious objects, like boxes. Some were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian stone reliefs that lined the walls of the Northwest Palace. But most ivories show styles and images from North Syria and the Phoenician city-states.

Phoenician-style ivories use images from Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing Egyptian crowns. They also use detailed carving methods like openwork (cutting through the ivory) and colored glass inlay. North Syrian-style ivories usually show stockier figures in more active poses. They are carved as solid plaques with fewer extra decorations. However, some pieces do not fit neatly into any of these three styles.

Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states or as booty from conquered enemies. Some may have been made in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks used to make these objects almost certainly came from African elephants. These elephants were imported from lands south of Egypt. However, elephants did live in several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the 700s B.C.

CuratorialText:
This bronze monumental statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues of the third century preserved today. It is a reminder that many of the disembodied portrait heads displayed in these galleries were once attached to freestanding sculptures, whose original appearances were undoubtedly quite different. Portraits of the emperor served the highly important function in imperial propaganda of presenting the ruler to the public and of projecting the personality with which he wished to be perceived. Leadership and military strength are evoked in the heroic nudity of the figure and in the pose, which recalls the famous statue of Alexander the Great with the Lance by Lysippos. In contrast to the idealized body, the portrait head represents the emperor with brutish realism. Trebonianus likely would have cradled a parazonium, or short sword, in his left arm, and held a spear in his upraised right hand.
The statue has undergone several campaigns of restoration since its discovery in the early nineteenth century and was examined in great detail as part of the conservation treatment in preparation for its display here. Visual examination inside and out, combined with x-radiography, made possible the clear identification of ancient and restored areas, which are indicated in the accompanying illustration. At least three-quarters of the statue is ancient. Despite the discrepancy in scale, the head belongs to the body. Although the mantle draped over his left shoulder is a modern restoration, cast edges beneath it confirm that the statue had a similar embellishment in antiquity. The left foot with its elaborately decorated open-fronted boot appears to be ancient but may not belong.
persona1_adapted:
This bronze statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues from the 200s AD that still exists today. It reminds us that many of the headless portraits in this gallery were once attached to full-body statues. Those statues probably looked very different when they were whole.

Portraits of the emperor were a key part of Roman propaganda. They showed the ruler to the public and helped create the image he wanted people to see. This statue shows leadership and military strength. The hero’s nudity and the pose remind us of a famous statue of Alexander the Great holding a lance, made by the sculptor Lysippos.

The body looks perfect and heroic, but the head is different. It shows the emperor with a rough, brutal realism. Trebonianus likely held a short sword called a parazonium in his left arm. His right hand was raised to hold a spear.

Since it was found in the early 1800s, the statue has been repaired several times. Before it was placed here, experts studied it very carefully. They looked inside and out and used X-rays to tell which parts were original and which were added later. The illustration nearby shows these differences.

At least three-quarters of the statue is ancient. Even though the head is a different size, it belongs to this body. The cloth draped over the left shoulder is a modern addition, but cast edges underneath show the statue had a similar decoration in ancient times. The left foot, with its fancy open-fronted boot, appears to be ancient but may not be original to this statue.

CuratorialText: Eirene, the daughter of Zeus and Themis, was one of the three Horai (Seasons), maidens closely associated with the fertility of the earth and the nurturing of children. The original bronze was erected in the Agora (marketplace) of Athens between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. Rarely can an ancient monument be dated so exactly. We know from literary sources that the cult of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374, and six recently found Panathenaic amphorae dated to 360/359 show an image of the statue. The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. and reported that it was by the sculptor Kephisodotos. Eirene was represented as a beautiful young woman wearing a peplos and himation (cloak), holding a scepter in her right hand, and carrying the young child Ploutos (the personification of wealth) and a cornucopia on her left arm. The figure brings to mind images of Demeter, the major goddess of agricultural plenty and the mother of Ploutos.
persona1_adapted:
**Eirene and the Child Ploutos**

Eirene was the daughter of Zeus and Themis. She was one of the three Horai, or Seasons. These maidens were linked to the earth's fertility and the care of children.

The original bronze statue was set up in the Agora (marketplace) of Athens. This happened between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. It is rare for an ancient monument to be dated so exactly. We know from ancient writings that the worship of Eirene came to Athens in 375/374. Also, six recently found Panathenaic amphorae (special jars) from 360/359 show an image of the statue.

The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. He reported that the sculptor Kephisodotos made it.

Eirene is shown as a beautiful young woman. She wears a peplos and himation (a cloak). In her right hand, she holds a scepter. On her left arm, she carries the young child Ploutos (the personification of wealth) and a cornucopia (a horn of plenty). The figure reminds us of images of Demeter, the main goddess of farming and plenty, and the mother of Ploutos.

CuratorialText: Many private summer villas were located along the coast near Naples. One of the most sumptuous must have been the villa built by Agrippa, friend of the emperor Augustus and husband of his daughter, Julia. It stood overlooking the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase. The villa was partially excavated between 1903 and 1905 after its accidental discovery during work on a railway. Wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms and the Archaeological Museum at Naples received the rest. Agrippa died in 12 B.C. and his son, Agrippa Postumus became the villa's proprietor in 11 B.C. as inscriptions found there indicate. The frescoes must have been painted during renovations begun at the time. Painted by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest existing examples of Roman wall painting. The so-called Black Room was one of a sequence of bedrooms facing south toward the downward slope of the mountain and the sea. The source of light was a wide doorway giving onto a terrace or promenade. This ambiguous and sophisticated decoration is a masterpiece of the so-called third style of Roman wall painting, which flourished during the reign of Augustus. The theme is a playful rendition of architectural motif. A low red dado serves as the base from which a skeleton of thin white columns appears to rise against a black background. There almost weightless columns support pavilions, candelabra, tripods, and a narrow cornice that runs around the room. They were embellished with jewel-like decorations. On the back wall tiny swans, the bird of Apollo, patron god of Augustus, perch improbably on threadlike spirals, and yellow panels with Egyptianizing motifs must have brought to mind the recent annexation of Egypt after the death of Cleopatra in 30 B.C. This architectural scheme creates almost no sense of depth or volume. The black walls behind appear at once to be flat and to dissolve into limitless space. Tiny landscape vignettes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Burnished to a high polish, these walls must have appeared magical indeed when illuminated by lamps at night.
persona1_adapted:
Many wealthy Romans built summer homes along the coast near Naples. One of the most luxurious must have been the villa of Agrippa. He was a close friend of Emperor Augustus and married to Augustus’s daughter, Julia. The villa stood on a hill overlooking the Bay of Naples, near the modern town of Boscotrecase.

Workers accidentally discovered the villa in 1903 while building a railway. They dug it up between 1903 and 1905. They removed wall paintings that still survived in four bedrooms. The Metropolitan Museum in New York bought sections from three rooms. The rest went to the Archaeological Museum in Naples.

Agrippa died in 12 B.C. His son, Agrippa Postumus, became the owner in 11 B.C. We know this from inscriptions found at the villa. The wall paintings were probably made during renovations that started at that time. Artists working for the emperor’s household painted them. These paintings are among the finest examples of Roman wall painting that still exist.

The so-called Black Room was one of the bedrooms. It faced south, toward the mountain slope and the sea. Light came through a wide doorway that opened onto a terrace or walkway. This room’s decoration is complex and clever. It is a masterpiece of the “third style” of Roman wall painting. That style was popular during the reign of Augustus.

The theme is a playful version of an architectural design. A low red base (called a dado) runs along the bottom. Thin white columns seem to rise from it against a black background. These nearly weightless columns hold up small pavilions, candelabras, tripods, and a narrow shelf that goes around the room. They are decorated with jewel-like details.

On the back wall, tiny swans perch on thin spiral lines. Swans were the bird of Apollo, the god who protected Augustus. Yellow panels with Egyptian-style designs reminded viewers that Egypt had recently been added to the Roman Empire after Cleopatra’s death in 30 B.C.

This design creates almost no sense of depth or space. The black walls seem both flat and like they dissolve into endless space. Tiny landscape scenes float like islands in the middle of the blackness. The walls were polished to a high shine. At night, lit by lamps, they must have looked magical.

CuratorialText:
From the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)
Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda This fresco from the Imperial villa at Boscotrecase depicts two consecutive events from the myth of Perseus and Andromeda. Perseus is about to rescue Andromeda from the ketos, a snaky sea monster painted in a brilliant blue-green palette. The creature raises his head with gigantic open jaws and frightful teeth toward Andromeda, who stands with outstretched arms in the center of the panel. One hand appears to be chained to the crag; the other elegantly placed on the rocks. Perseus flies in from the left with his lyre in one hand, winged shoes on his feet, and a windblown cloak over his shoulder. In the upper right portion of the fresco, he is greeted by Andromeda's grateful father, a scene that alludes to the myth's happy endingâ€“the marriage of hero and princess. The fortunes of love and the ever-present sea are the themes that link this fresco and that of Polyphemus and Galatea (20.192.17) from the same cubiculum at Boscotrecase. The translucent blue-green background of both frescoes also unifies the disparate episodes combined in each painting, and must have brought a sense of coolness to the room.
persona1_adapted:
**From the Villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)**

**Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda**

This wall painting comes from a grand Roman country house. It shows two parts of the story of Perseus and Andromeda.

In the first part, Perseus is about to save Andromeda from a sea monster called a *ketos*. The monster is painted in bright blue-green colors. It lifts its huge head and opens its jaws wide, showing scary teeth. Andromeda stands in the middle of the scene with her arms stretched out. One hand is chained to a rock. The other rests gracefully on the stones.

Perseus flies in from the left. He carries a lyre, a stringed instrument like a small harp. He wears winged shoes and a cloak that blows in the wind.

In the upper right part of the painting, Perseus is greeted by Andromeda’s grateful father. This hints at the happy ending of the story: the hero and the princess get married.

The themes of love and the sea connect this painting to another one from the same room. That one shows the story of Polyphemus and Galatea. The clear blue-green background in both paintings also ties the different scenes together. It likely helped make the room feel cool.

CuratorialText: This noble image of a woman brings to mind the philosopher Aristotle's description of commonly held beliefs about the dead: "In addition to believing that those who have ended this life are blessed and happy, we also think that to say anything false or slanderous against them is impious, from our feeling that it is directed against those who have already become our betters and superiors" (Of the Soul, quoted in Plutarch, A Letter to Apollonius 27). Larger than life and seated on a thronelike chair, this figure assumes almost heroic proportions.
persona1_adapted: This statue of a woman is large and sits on a throne-like chair. It reminds us of what the philosopher Aristotle said about how people often think of the dead. He wrote: "We believe that those who have died are blessed and happy. We also think it is wrong to say anything false or mean about them. This is because we feel they have become better and greater than us." The statue is bigger than life and looks almost like a hero.

CuratorialText: A stylized octopus flanked by fish covers each side of this stirrup jar, named for the shape of its handles. Mycenaean pottery often reflects Minoan-inspired themes and techniques that originated on the island of Crete. Animated marine motifs adopted from Minoan art began to appear on Mycenaean vessels in about 1500 BCE and were initially rendered in a very naturalistic manner. Here, the symmetrical composition and abstract depiction of the sea creatures are characteristically Mycenaean. Such jars were commonly used to transport liquids.
persona1_adapted: A stylized octopus with fish on either side decorates this stirrup jar. The jar gets its name from the shape of its handles. Mycenaean pottery often shows themes and techniques that came from Minoan art on the island of Crete. Around 1500 BCE, Mycenaean artists began adding lively sea creatures inspired by Minoan art. At first, these creatures looked very realistic. In this jar, the balanced design and abstract style of the sea animals are typical of Mycenaean art. People commonly used jars like this to carry liquids.

CuratorialText:
On the shoulder, women dancing between musicians playing flute and lyre
On the body, wedding procession. This is the earliest and most complete known representation of an Attic wedding. The bridal couple with the best man behind them sit in a cart drawn by two donkeys. A mule cart with four guests follows. Other members of the procession are on foot. The woman in the lead holds two torches, indicating that the scene takes place at night. The procession heads toward the bridegroom's house where a woman, probably the mother of the groom, awaits. The architecture of the house is carefully indicated; the white columns of the porch may be painted wood.
persona1_adapted:
**On the shoulder:** Women dance between musicians playing the flute and lyre.

**On the body:** A wedding procession. This is the earliest and most complete known picture of an Attic wedding. The bride and groom sit in a cart pulled by two donkeys. The best man stands behind them. A mule cart with four guests follows. Other people in the procession walk. The woman in front holds two torches. This tells us the scene takes place at night. The procession heads toward the groom's house. A woman, probably the groom's mother, waits there. The house's architecture is shown in detail. The white columns of the porch may be made of painted wood.

CuratorialText: This highly ornate and extremely well-preserved Roman marble sarcophagus came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort and was formerly displayed in their country seat, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus records that it was installed there in 1733. In contrast to the rough and unsightly back, the sides and front of the sarcophagus are decorated with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief. The central figure is that of the god Dionysos seated on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures who represent the four Seasons (from left to right, Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). The figures are unusual in that the Seasons are usually portrayed as women, but here they are shown as sturdy youths. Around these five central figures are placed other Bacchic figures and cultic objects, all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two other groups of large figures, similarly intermingled with lesser ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is portrayed reclining on the ground; she is accompanied by a satyr and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure, probably to be identified with the personification of a river-god, reclines in front of two winged youths, perhaps representing two additional Seasons. The sarcophagus is an exquisite example of Roman funerary art, displaying all the virtuosity of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person would have been able to commission and purchase such a sarcophagus, and it was probably made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself. The subjects - the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons - are unlikely, however, to have had any special significance for the deceased, particularly as it is clear that the design was copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, although the treatment and carving of the figures is quite different. On the Badminton sarcophagus the figures are carved in high relief and so endow the crowded scene with multiple areas of light and shade, allowing the eye to wander effortlessly from one figure to another. One must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gilding, making the whole composition a visual tour de force. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus lacks its lid, the fact that it was found in the early eighteenth century and soon thereafter installed in Badminton Hall means that it has been preserved almost intact and only a few of the minor extremities are now missing.
persona1_adapted:
This highly decorated and well-preserved Roman marble coffin came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort. It was once displayed at their country home, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the rough, unfinished back of the coffin records that it was installed there in 1733.

Unlike the plain back, the sides and front of the coffin are covered with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief. High relief means the figures stand out sharply from the background. The central figure is the god Dionysos, seated on a panther. But he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures. These figures represent the four Seasons: Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall, shown from left to right. The figures are unusual because the Seasons are usually shown as women, but here they are shown as strong young men.

Around these five central figures are other Bacchic figures and cultic objects, all carved at a smaller scale. Bacchic refers to followers of Dionysos, the god of wine and celebration. On the rounded ends of the coffin are two other groups of large figures, mixed with smaller ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is shown reclining on the ground. She is accompanied by a satyr (a half-man, half-goat creature) and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure reclines in front of two winged youths. This bearded figure is probably meant to be a river-god. The two winged youths may represent two additional Seasons.

This coffin is an excellent example of Roman funerary art. It shows the great skill of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean. It was probably shipped to Rome, where it was carved. Only a very wealthy and powerful person could have ordered and bought such a coffin. It was likely made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself.

The subjects—the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons—probably did not have any special meaning for the person buried inside. This is because the design was clearly copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another coffin, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons. However, the treatment and carving of the figures are quite different.

On this coffin, the figures are carved in high relief. This gives the crowded scene many areas of light and shade. It allows the eye to move easily from one figure to another. You must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gold leaf. This would have made the whole composition a visual masterpiece.

Very few Roman coffins of this quality have survived. Although this coffin lacks its lid, the fact that it was found in the early 1700s and soon installed at Badminton House means it has been preserved almost intact. Only a few small parts, like fingers or toes, are now missing.

CuratorialText:
On the neck, obverse, battle of centaurs and Lapiths; reverse, youths and women
Around the body, Amazonomachy (battle between Greeks and Amazons). The ancient Greeks almost never depicted contemporary or historical events in art. Thus, while literary works of the fifth century B.C. make clear that the Greeks understood the magnitude of their victory in the Persian Wars, there was no concern among artists to illustrate major events or personalities. Instead, their preference was for grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern adversaries, notably Amazons. The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland lay far to the east and north. The most celebrated Amazonomachies in Athens during the first half of the fifth century were large-scale wall paintings that decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). Their influence was considerable and underlies the representation here and on the adjacent krater, 06.286.86.
persona1_adapted:
On the neck of the vase, one side shows a battle between centaurs and Lapiths. The other side shows young men and women. Around the body of the vase, there is an Amazonomachy—a battle between Greeks and Amazons.

The ancient Greeks almost never showed real events or historical people in their art. Even though writers in the 400s B.C. made it clear that the Greeks knew how important their victory in the Persian Wars was, artists did not try to show those major events or famous people. Instead, they preferred to paint grand, mythical battles between Greeks and enemies from the east, especially the Amazons.

The Amazons were a legendary race of warrior women. Their homeland was far to the east and north. In Athens, during the first half of the 400s B.C., the most famous Amazonomachies were large wall paintings. These paintings decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). These works had a big influence. That influence shows in the scene on this vase and on the nearby krater, number 06.286.86.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has about 230 still life paintings. A still life is a type of art that shows objects arranged together. Flemish painters started making these in the 1500s.

These paintings often include things like shells, books, food, drinks, plates, skulls, or dead animals. You might also see flowers, insects, or even a person in the background. The name "still life" means that life seems to pause for a moment.

Soon, artists began to focus on different subjects. For example, Rachel Ruysch mostly painted flowers. Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to paint food and eating tools. Another type is called a vanitas still life. It often shows a skull. This reminds viewers that life and material things do not last forever.

CuratorialText: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king is the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I has canals and roads constructed in the Netherlands and invests in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his sights firmly set on the economy, William I pays less attention to other affairs of state. Discontent in the Southern Netherlands grows. The population rises in revolt, and in 1831, the secession of Belgium becomes a reality. The king resists, but attempts to regain the territory come to nothing. After years of struggle, William I recognizes independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he abdicates the throne a year later, and his son, William II, takes over.
persona1_adapted:
**Orange above!** In 1815, the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time. This happened when King William I (1772-1843) was crowned. He was the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier.

King William I built canals and roads across the Netherlands. He also invested in trade, shipping, and industry. But he focused so much on the economy that he paid less attention to other matters of state. This caused growing unhappiness in the Southern Netherlands.

The people there rose up in revolt. In 1831, Belgium broke away from the Netherlands. The king tried to stop this, but his attempts to win back the territory failed. After years of struggle, William I finally recognized Belgium as an independent country in 1839.

Disappointed, he gave up the throne a year later. His son, William II, became king in his place.

CuratorialText: The extensive costume collection of the Rijksmuseum, the oldest in the Netherlands, tells a wealth of stories. From women's and men's to children's clothing: these pieces reveal stories about the wearer and their taste, the methods of the makers, and local and global influences. Many garments are custom-made for a special occasion, while others showcase the technological advancements of ready-to-wear fashion. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 17th century to Yves Saint Laurent: the collection features highlights of Dutch and international fashion. The collection is a remarkable reflection of fashion developments throughout the centuries.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has the oldest costume collection in the Netherlands. These clothes tell many stories. They include outfits for women, men, and children. Each piece reveals details about the person who wore it and their style. The clothes also show how makers worked and what influenced them, both locally and globally.

Many garments were made for special events. Others show how technology changed ready-to-wear fashion. The collection covers fashion from the 1600s to modern designers like Yves Saint Laurent. It includes Dutch and international fashion highlights. Overall, the collection reflects how fashion has changed through the centuries.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1800s, a new art style appeared. Artists created it because they were tired of always copying old styles from past centuries. They wanted something fresh and new. This style was called Art Nouveau. It used natural shapes like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The art focused on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and uneven, or asymmetrical, designs. Art Nouveau started in cities like Paris, Brussels, and Munich. It then spread across Western Europe and the United States. This movement lasted from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914. During that time, Art Nouveau showed up in all kinds of art, from paintings to furniture.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona1_adapted:
Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. He trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter. He also became a famous painter. Goltzius moved to Haarlem with his teacher, Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert. There, with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he brought a style called Mannerism to the Netherlands. This style came from the artist Bartholomäus Spranger. You can spot Mannerism by its muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in busy, detailed scenes.

During a trip to Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by Italian masters. When he returned to the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind. He chose a more classicist style, which used balanced, natural proportions. His technically complex engravings were admired all over Europe. Goltzius's work gives us a clear picture of how Dutch painting changed around 1600.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first apprenticed with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and subsequently continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt allowed himself to be unconstrained by rules and experimented freely with daring compositions, paint handling, and rendering of textures. He enhanced the drama in his depictions with strong light-dark contrasts. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, The Night Watch. Instead of a static portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are depicted in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and approximately 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.
persona1_adapted: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided as a young boy that he wanted to be a painter. He first trained with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden. Then he continued his studies with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt did not follow strict rules. He experimented freely with bold compositions, how he handled paint, and how he showed textures. He made his scenes more dramatic by using strong contrasts between light and dark. His early works are painted very carefully. Later, his brushstrokes became looser and more free. He often received important jobs from clients. His most famous and largest painting is *The Night Watch*. Instead of a still, formal portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment. In this scene, all the figures are shown in action. Besides this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum owns 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and about 1,300 prints by Rembrandt.

CuratorialText: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, little is known about him. It is clear, however, that the native of Delft spent his days as an art dealer, father of a large family, and, naturally, a painter. His early work includes a Biblical and a mythological scene. Other early works reveal influences from the Utrecht Caravaggists. Vermeer primarily paints interiors and tronies (portrait-like studies of people). His most famous works are the intimate genre pieces, snapshots of everyday life: a lady reading a letter, a woman engaged in a household chore. All the scenes share a serene calm, which sometimes even feels somewhat mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his exceptional use of light, powerful colors, and incredible precision. During his lifetime, he rose to a certain fame and commanded high prices for his paintings. Afterward, Vermeer fell into obscurity and was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.
persona1_adapted:
Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is famous around the world today. But we know very little about him. We do know he lived in Delft, a city in the Netherlands. He worked as an art dealer and was the father of a large family. He was also a painter.

His early paintings show scenes from the Bible and from old myths. Other early works show the influence of a group called the Utrecht Caravaggists. These artists used strong light and shadow.

Vermeer mostly painted indoor scenes and "tronies." Tronies are paintings that look like portraits but are studies of a type of person, not a specific individual.

His most famous works are quiet scenes from everyday life. For example, a lady reading a letter or a woman doing housework. These paintings share a calm, peaceful feeling. Sometimes they even feel a bit mysterious.

People admire Vermeer for his amazing use of light, his strong colors, and his great attention to detail. During his life, he became fairly well known and sold his paintings for high prices. After he died, people forgot about him. He was not rediscovered until the mid-1800s.

CuratorialText: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower still lifes are very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises: all almost indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work bears witness to a vast knowledge of nature. Ruysch acquires this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, teaches as a professor. She also studies his collection of prepared flowers and insects. She learns the tricks of the trade from still-life painter Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch becomes the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she works as court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate, a particularly honorable position. He is by no means the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes are collected by enthusiasts far beyond the national borders.
persona1_adapted: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) was one of the most successful artists of her time. She painted flower still lifes—pictures of flowers that don't move. Her work is very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises look almost like the real thing. Her paintings show she knew a lot about nature. Ruysch learned this at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden. Her father, Frederik Ruysch, was a professor there. She also studied his collection of dried flowers and insects. She learned painting tricks from still-life artist Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch became the first woman to join the Hague artists' society, Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she worked as a court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate. This was a very honorable job. He was not the only one who admired her work. Fans collected Ruysch's flower still lifes far beyond the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into the artistic family of painter Johann Georg Schwartze and grew into a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. Trained in her father's studio and after art education in Munich and Paris, she develops into a true talent. When her father dies prematurely, the 23-year-old Schwartze becomes the new breadwinner of the family. Her style is powerful, with rapid brushstrokes and large areas of color, which make her portraits full of character yet flattering. Schwartze's work is well received, and she becomes increasingly famous both at home and abroad. Around the year 1900, Schwartze is the most successful portraitist in the Netherlands, and she portrays members of the Royal Family. She works quickly and creates hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint during her career, of which the Rijksmuseum holds a number of artist portraits in its collection.
persona1_adapted:
Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into an artistic family. Her father, Johann Georg Schwartze, was a painter. She grew up to become a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. She trained in her father's studio and later studied art in Munich and Paris. Her skill grew quickly.

When her father died young, Schwartze was only 23 years old. She became the main earner for her family. Her painting style is bold. She used fast brushstrokes and large areas of color. This made her portraits full of personality, but still flattering.

People liked Schwartze's work. She became more and more famous in the Netherlands and other countries. Around the year 1900, she was the most successful portrait painter in the Netherlands. She painted members of the Royal Family. She worked fast and made hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint. The Rijksmuseum now holds several of her portraits of artists.

CuratorialText: Anyone walking through Amsterdam towards the end of the 19th century has a good chance of encountering George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). For days on end, the painter roams through his beloved Amsterdam with a sketchbook. He calls himself 'the painter of the people' and primarily draws and paints 'ordinary' city dwellers. Breitner also uses his camera to capture life. He subsequently applies his typical photographic style, in which figures partially fall outside the frame, to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges, is not understood by everyone. Journalists are critical of his work, but enthusiasts love it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.
persona1_adapted:
If you were walking through Amsterdam in the late 1800s, you might have run into George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). He spent days wandering through his favorite city with a sketchbook. Breitner called himself "the painter of the people." He liked to draw and paint everyday city folks.

Breitner also used a camera to capture life around him. He then brought that photographic style into his paintings. In his work, people often seem cut off at the edges, as if they are stepping out of the frame. He also used thick brushstrokes and smudges.

Not everyone understood his style. Journalists criticized his work, but fans loved it. Today, Breitner is still famous as one of the top Impressionist painters of his time.

CuratorialText: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a cousin by marriage and a renowned painter. In his early years, Van Gogh primarily drew and painted many peasant scenes in dark colors. After a brief period of study in Antwerp, Van Gogh left for Paris, where he was influenced by Impressionism and Pointillism. He developed a style characterized by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not prevent him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.
persona1_adapted: He drew a lot and got his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a famous painter who was also a relative by marriage. In his early years, Van Gogh mostly drew and painted scenes of peasants using dark colors. After a short time studying in Antwerp, he moved to Paris. There, he was influenced by two art styles: Impressionism and Pointillism. Impressionism focused on capturing light and everyday moments. Pointillism used small dots of color to build a picture. Van Gogh developed his own style with short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors placed side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues and was hospitalized at times. But this did not stop him from painting. Only after his death in 1890 did the public see his work, and Van Gogh became famous worldwide.

CuratorialText: Born into a Utrecht family of silversmiths, the career path of Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) was determined early on. Around 1590, Van Vianen must have completed his master's examination, as he worked independently from that moment on and joined the blacksmiths' guild. His imaginative objects tell a story through lobe-like ornaments and forms. In 1614, Van Vianen received a commission from the Amsterdam guild to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had passed away shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. In this jug, the ornament plays the leading role for the first time.
persona1_adapted:
Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) was born into a family of silversmiths in Utrecht, Netherlands. His career path was set from an early age. Around 1590, he likely passed his master's exam. After that, he worked on his own and joined the blacksmiths' guild.

Van Vianen created imaginative objects. These objects tell a story through their lobe-like shapes and decorations. In 1614, the Amsterdam guild asked him to make a jug. It was in memory of his brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had recently died. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. For the first time, the ornament on this jug took the leading role.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona1_adapted:
**Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617)**

Hendrick Goltzius was born in Germany. He trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter. Later, he became a famous painter too.

Goltzius moved to Haarlem with his teacher, Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert. There, he worked with two other artists, Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem. Together, they brought a new style called Mannerism to the Netherlands. This style is easy to spot. It shows muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in complex scenes.

In 1590-91, Goltzius traveled through Italy. He was inspired by the work of Italian masters. When he returned to the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind. He chose a more classicist style instead. This style used balanced, natural proportions.

Goltzius’s engravings were very detailed and technically difficult to make. People all over Europe admired them. His work gives us a clear picture of how Dutch painting changed around 1600.

CuratorialText: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.
persona1_adapted:
In 2012, visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (born 1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee. It is located deep in the rainforest of Suriname. The people there live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But even though the village is very remote, the modern world has left its mark. For example, the community has mobile phones and plastic household items.

Sassen created a photo series in Pikin Slee. Her pictures capture the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions—meaning the images focus on shapes, patterns, and light rather than showing things exactly as they are. Most of the photos are in black and white. But they also include colorful splashes, which is a style Sassen used in her earlier work.

In 2013, the Pikin Slee series became part of the Rijksmuseum’s collection. It adds a modern touch to the museum’s photos of Suriname, which before were mostly historical.

CuratorialText: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.
persona1_adapted: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman. He became the leader of the Dutch Revolt and was honored as the 'father of the fatherland'. When he was 11 years old, William inherited the French territory of Orange. This gave him the title 'Prince of Orange'. His life was full of change and an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, chose William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht. These were the most important Dutch provinces at the time. But the two men clashed. Philip wanted an empire with strong central control and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, however, believed in freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This conflict cost him his life on July 10, 1584, when he was shot.

CuratorialText: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.
persona1_adapted: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris? Not much is known about her. But we do know that around 1906, she worked as a model for Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. We know this from photographs Maris took of her in his studio. In Maris’s archive, there are documents where he calls her “Isabella.” He also gave that name to some other paintings he made of her. Still, we can’t be sure that her real name was Isabella. Maris might have made up the name. Or she might have used a false name, which was common for models back then.

CuratorialText: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands. She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.
persona1_adapted: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) helped make the House of Orange more powerful. She also raised the Netherlands' status around the world. She acted as a trusted advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. (A stadtholder was a leader of the Dutch Republic.) When he got sick, she took over some of his duties. She helped guide the country toward the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband died, her son also passed away. This started a period called the first stadtholderless era, when there was no leader from the House of Orange. To protect her family's future, Amalia used her influence both in the Netherlands and abroad. She used art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and objects from Asia was meant to compete with other royal courts in Europe. Through this, she helped make the Netherlands—led by the House of Orange—a major center for art and trade in Europe.

CuratorialText: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.
persona1_adapted: Between 1650 and 1672, the Netherlands had no Stadtholder (a leader like a governor). Instead, the most powerful citizens ran the country. Johan de Witt became the most important politician as Grand Pensionary of Holland. He cleverly played England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Still, in 1672, these countries, along with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attacked the Netherlands. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversaw the military as a deputy of the States General (the government assembly). As foreign troops gained ground, more people demanded the return of a Stadtholder. Cornelis was arrested on suspicion of plotting against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visited him in prison, an angry mob dragged the brothers out and killed them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.

CuratorialText: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.
persona1_adapted:
At the start of the 1800s, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland. Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte chose them for these roles. The royal couple had three sons together. One of them became the future Emperor Napoleon III. But their marriage was not a happy one.

Queen Hortense often appeared at parties and official events. She also visited local businesses. Because of this, she became more and more popular with the people. But her husband did not like her popularity. He forced her to stay out of the spotlight.

After Napoleon was defeated, Hortense moved to Switzerland without her husband. She lived at her impressive estate called Arenenberg. Hortense was a talented musician and composer. She organized cultural gatherings there that people talked about for a long time afterward.

CuratorialText: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after a construction period of nearly ten years. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers depicted himself, just as the master builders did in the decoration of cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over the years, many original decorations in the museum have disappeared. During the last renovation, however, Cuypers' work was restored to its former glory. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only showpiece. In 1889, 'his' Central Station in Amsterdam was unveiled. In addition to these famous projects, Cuypers focused primarily on churches and monuments.
persona1_adapted: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum opened its doors after nearly ten years of construction. The building was designed by Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a well-known member of the Catholic Church. On the outside of the museum, Cuypers included a picture of himself.
