He did this the same way master builders added their own images to cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over time, many of the museum’s original decorations were lost. But during the last renovation, Cuypers’ work was restored to its original beauty. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers’ only famous project. In 1889, his Central Station in Amsterdam opened. Besides these well-known buildings, Cuypers mainly designed churches and monuments.

CuratorialText: Willem Drees (1886-1988) made a career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which would later become the PvdA. The politician climbed a long ladder of various positions: municipal councilor, alderman, Member of Parliament. In the first cabinet elected after the war, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. With his Drees Emergency Act, a provision for fixed benefits, he helped the elderly out of financial distress. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. It was his task to effectively handle the reconstruction of the Netherlands. Drees proved to be the right man in the right place. With a sense of solidarity, the Netherlands was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, until 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was laid. To this day, the statesman is appreciated for his efforts for the Netherlands.
persona1_adapted:
Willem Drees (1886-1988) worked in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party. This party later became the PvdA. Drees held many different jobs in politics. He was a city councilor, an alderman, and a member of Parliament.

After World War II, Drees became the Minister of Social Affairs. He created the Drees Emergency Act. This law gave fixed payments to elderly people who were struggling financially. It helped them out of poverty.

Drees was popular. In 1948, he became Prime Minister. His main job was to lead the rebuilding of the Netherlands after the war. He was the right person for this task. With a sense of togetherness, the country was rebuilt.

Under Drees's leadership, from 1948 to 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was built. This system provides support for people in need. To this day, people appreciate Drees for his work for the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 16th century, there was a preference in Italy for Mannerism, a style full of complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke radically with this art form. His figures are actually very realistic. Wrinkled faces, unkempt hair, dirty feet: gods and saints are depicted as very ordinary people. Caravaggio has a preference for strong light-dark contrasts, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear structure. His method spreads rapidly throughout Europe. Especially in Utrecht, around 1620, many followers, known as Caravaggists, can be found. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen travel to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio's lighting effects.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1500s, Italian artists liked a style called Mannerism. This style used complex compositions and figures in twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian artist Caravaggio broke away from this style. His figures look very realistic. He showed gods and saints with wrinkled faces, messy hair, and dirty feet—like everyday people. Caravaggio loved strong contrasts between light and dark. He used dramatic effects and clear, simple compositions. His style spread quickly across Europe. Around 1620, many followers appeared in Utrecht, a city in the Netherlands. These artists were called Caravaggists. Artists like Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen traveled to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. Even Rembrandt, who never visited Italy, was inspired by Caravaggio's use of light and shadow.

CuratorialText: The 15th and 16th centuries are broadly regarded as the period of the Renaissance, a term that literally means 'rebirth'. In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish due to a new appreciation of the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal languages ​​such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and man, using perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe, thanks to the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.
persona1_adapted:
The 1400s and 1500s are known as the Renaissance. This word means "rebirth." During this time, art, literature, and science grew quickly in Europe. People began to admire the old cultures of Greece and Rome again.

Artists and architects looked for perfect shapes and sizes in their work. They studied Roman buildings and used features like round arches, columns, and triangular tops called pediments. In painting and sculpture, artists tried to show nature and people in a realistic way. They used perspective, a technique that makes objects look far away or close up. They also studied the human body to make their figures more accurate.

Besides religious stories, artists started painting myths and everyday life. The Renaissance began in Italy and spread across Europe. This happened partly because of new printing methods that appeared around 1450.

CuratorialText: The period between the First and Second World Wars is dominated by Art Deco, an international art movement in which tradition and industrial progress go hand in hand. Emerging from Art Nouveau, Art Deco also draws inspiration from stylized plant and floral motifs as well as linear, geometric designs. Additionally, artists look to folk art and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America, a source of inspiration that is further fueled by archaeological finds. The term Art Deco is derived from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.
persona1_adapted: The time between World War I and World War II was shaped by Art Deco. This was an international art movement. It combined tradition with modern industry. Art Deco grew out of an earlier style called Art Nouveau. It used stylized plants and flowers. It also used straight lines and geometric shapes. Artists looked to folk art for ideas. They also looked at art from Africa, East Asia, and Central America. New archaeological finds made these sources even more popular. The name "Art Deco" comes from a 1925 world's fair in Paris. Its full name was the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.

CuratorialText: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. It is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and would continue until shortly after 1500. Church walls became airier thanks to tall pointed arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass; the vaults rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed, and on the exterior, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses with pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance with elegant poses, calligraphic drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also seeped into other art forms, for example, painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses numerous ecclesiastical objects in this style, such as painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.
persona1_adapted:
Around 1150, churches in and near Paris started to get much taller. The most famous example is Notre-Dame. This marked the beginning of Gothic architecture. The style quickly spread across Europe and lasted until just after 1500.

Church walls became lighter and airier. Tall, pointed arch windows were filled with colorful stained glass. The ceilings rested on thin columns and had striking ribbed patterns. On the outside, the stone walls were supported by slender flying buttresses—arched supports that helped hold up the building's weight. These buttresses often had pointed tops called pinnacles.

Gothic style also influenced sculptors. They gave human, animal, and plant figures a stylized look. The figures had elegant poses, flowing drapery, and graceful gestures.

Gothic shapes and ideas also appeared in other art forms. These included paintings, illuminated manuscripts (hand-decorated books), metalwork, and furniture.

The Rijksmuseum has many church objects in this style. Examples include painted altars, silver monstrances (holders for the holy bread), wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

CuratorialText: The term Impressionism, which means snapshot, was initially used in the early 1870s as a negative label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot depict reality as seen through the eyes of the artist. The French Impressionists are inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School and the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They work with bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They choose their themes from modern life. Due to the sketchy approach, it sometimes appears as if their paintings were created quickly and spontaneously. This often leads critics to say that the artworks are unfinished. From the late 1880s onwards, appreciation for French Impressionism also grows in the Netherlands.
persona1_adapted: The term Impressionism, which means "snapshot," was first used in the early 1870s. It was a negative label for the work of a group of painters in Paris. Artists like Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot showed reality as seen through the artist's own eyes. The French Impressionists were inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School. They also looked at the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. These painters used bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They chose their subjects from modern life. Because their style looked sketchy, their paintings often seemed quick and spontaneous. This led critics to say the artworks looked unfinished. Starting in the late 1880s, people in the Netherlands began to appreciate French Impressionism more.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona1_adapted: In the late 1800s, a new art style appeared. Artists created it because they were tired of always copying old styles from earlier centuries. They wanted something fresh and different. This new style was called Art Nouveau. It used natural shapes like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The art focused on smooth, flowing lines, simple geometric shapes, and designs that were not perfectly balanced. Art Nouveau started in cities like Paris, Brussels, and Munich. It quickly spread across Western Europe and the United States. This style lasted from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914. During that time, Art Nouveau showed up in painting, sculpture, furniture, and many other types of art.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.
persona1_adapted:
Rembrandt was not just a great painter. He was also a highly skilled draftsman and etcher. An etcher is an artist who creates prints by drawing on a metal plate with acid. The Rijksmuseum has about 1,300 prints made by Rembrandt. Some of these prints exist in multiple copies or different versions.

His etchings show many different subjects. You can find scenes from the Bible and old myths. There are also everyday life scenes, landscapes, and portraits of himself and others.

In these works, you can clearly see how Rembrandt’s style and technique changed over time. His drawings look loose and free. He used strong contrasts between light and dark, which makes his work stand out. He also chose to show the most emotional moments in his scenes. This was unusual for artists of his time. It makes his work feel human and easy to relate to.

Because of all this, Rembrandt had a big influence on other artists during his life and for many years after.

CuratorialText: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.
persona1_adapted:
Augustus the Strong was the Elector of Saxony. He supported an alchemist named Johann Friedrich Böttger. In 1708, Böttger became the first person in Europe to make porcelain. Soon after, the first European porcelain factory opened in Meissen.

This factory helped Augustus the Strong with a big dream. He wanted to build a Japanese Palace. There, he planned to show off his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain.

Since the late 1500s, porcelain—often called "white gold"—had been brought from Asia. Wealthy people in European courts and high society collected it. So when Europe finally started making its own porcelain, it was a huge moment in the history of European decorative arts.

Today, the Rijksmuseum has one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain. It includes early pieces from the factory, which came from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.
persona1_adapted: The Rijksmuseum has collected Asian ceramics since it opened in 1885. Most of these pieces are Chinese (about 2,300) and Japanese (about 600). Another 50 come from other Asian countries. The Dutch have been bringing porcelain from Asia since the Dutch East India Company (VOC) started in 1602. By the 1600s, thin porcelain with blue Chinese designs became very popular. As a result, Chinese and Japanese porcelain became a big part of Dutch homes. Since 1952, the museum has also held the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK). This collection includes tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.
persona1_adapted: Starting in 1610, about 100,000 pieces of Chinese porcelain were shipped to the Netherlands every year. To compete with this popular Chinese pottery, Dutch potters started making their own products better. Their earthenware became thinner, whiter, and shinier. They painted blue designs on a white tin glaze. This blue-and-white look was strongly linked to Asian porcelain. This type of pottery was called "plateel" or "faience." But in the 1600s and 1700s, people began to call it "Hollants porceleyn," which means "Dutch porcelain." After 1650, the city of Delft became the main center for making this earthenware. That's why it later became known as "Delft earthenware." Around the same time, trade in Chinese porcelain almost stopped because of a civil war in China (1644–1684). This gave Delft earthenware a chance to grow and become very popular among rich people in Europe.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a collection of small bronze sculptures from the Renaissance, mostly from Italy. Some of these were made in the late 1400s in the university city of Padua. They are small, useful objects inspired by ancient Greek and Roman art. Examples include candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with figures from mythology.

Many small workshops made these luxury items in large numbers. But artists also created unique bronze sculptures and objects. A good example is the large candlestick designed by Verrocchio in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence.

Making bronze objects in the style of ancient Greece and Rome also became popular in other Italian cities, like Rome and Venice. In the 1500s and 1600s, these small statues ended up in the studies of scholars, the art cabinets of wealthy people, and the collections of princes and nobles.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.
persona1_adapted:
At first, artists who painted the sea mostly focused on big historical events that happened on the water. They wanted future generations to remember heroic moments. One artist, Willem van de Velde I, worked like a war reporter. He painted famous Dutch naval battles from what he saw with his own eyes.

His son, also named Willem, was a master at painting calm seas—or stormy ones. In the 1700s and 1800s, artists like Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen chose to show ships in danger in a dramatic way. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close.

Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and the father-and-son team Schotel, Dutch art became almost the same as seascapes in other countries. For the artists of the Hague School, the way light hit the clouds and water was very important. They also paid more attention to the hard lives of fishermen.

CuratorialText: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.
persona1_adapted: In the 1600s, members of the civic guard—called marksmen—were often painted in group portraits. Back then, powerful cities were in charge of their own safety. The militia helped keep order and security. Every adult man up to age 60 was expected to join, but he had to buy his own weapons. These group portraits were expensive because each marksman paid for his own picture. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 1500s and are quite still and formal. Over the 1600s, painters chose looser, more lively arrangements. Two famous examples are Rembrandt's *Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst's *Banquet of the Marksmen*. Group portraits weren't just for marksmen's groups—other city, regional, and national organizations, as well as individuals, also ordered them.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has about 230 still life paintings. A still life is a type of art that shows objects arranged together. Flemish painters started making these in the 1500s.

These paintings often show things like shells, books, food, drinks, dishes, skulls, or dead animals. They can also include flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. In a still life, everything is frozen in time.

Soon, artists began to focus on different subjects. For example, Rachel Ruysch mostly painted flowers. Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda liked to paint food, cups, and plates.

Another type is the vanitas still life. It often shows a skull. This reminds viewers that life and material things do not last forever.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a huge collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to today. These more than 1,700 portraits do more than just look nice. They give the past a face. They show what people looked like: their clothes, jewelry, and hairstyles. Sometimes they even show what rooms looked like back then.

Each century focuses on different things. In the Renaissance, portraits became more realistic. But artists from the 1900s and 2000s moved away from that realism. Over time, governments, groups, and people have paid for portraits for many reasons. They might show someone's job or social rank. They could mark a wedding or remember someone after death.

The Rijksmuseum collection also has many self-portraits by famous painters like Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.
persona1_adapted:
**Genre Paintings: Art of Everyday Life**

Genre paintings show scenes from everyday life. You might see a busy kitchen, a cozy living room, a crowded inn, or a lively market. These paintings capture people doing normal things: children playing, a family gathered together, or a merchant selling his goods.

The first genre paintings appeared in Flanders (now part of Belgium) in the 1500s. But in the Netherlands, this type of art didn't become popular until about 100 years later. Famous Dutch painters like Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster created well-known genre scenes.

These artists often hid a playful joke or a serious lesson in their paintings. The name "genre paintings" wasn't used until the 1800s, when this style was at its most popular. The Rijksmuseum also has works by 1800s painters like Jozef Israëls, who is known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.

CuratorialText: A history piece depicts a memorable historical story, which often originates from a written source. This can be a story from the Bible or mythology, but also events from history. Multiple figures are often visible in these narrative pieces. For instance, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert depicted a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman captured The Battle of Waterloo , the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, painters also chose Roman scenes as a metaphor for the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, the Batavian revolt against the Romans, depicted by Ferdinand Bol in the painting Claudius Civilis, refers to the struggle against the Spaniards.
persona1_adapted:
A history painting tells a story from the past. The story often comes from a written source, like the Bible, mythology, or real historical events. These paintings usually show many people in action.

For example, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert painted a scene from the conquest of America. In 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman captured The Battle of Waterloo. This is the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum.

In the 1600s, Dutch painters also used Roman scenes to comment on their own time. They used these stories as a metaphor, or a symbol, for the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, Ferdinand Bol painted the Batavian revolt against the Romans. That story refers to the Dutch struggle against the Spanish.

CuratorialText: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” By uttering these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno declared his country, Indonesia, independent on August 17, 1945. Former colonizer the Netherlands refuses to recognize that independence and attempts to reoccupy Indonesia. In defense of its independence, Indonesia wages war against the Netherlands on its own soil. From the beginning, Indonesia seeks and receives support from the international community. Under international pressure, the Netherlands is forced to recognize Indonesia's independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia becomes a member state of the United Nations, and the international confirmation of independence becomes a reality.
persona1_adapted: On August 17, 1945, a leader named Sukarno said, “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” With these words, he announced that Indonesia was now a free country. But the Netherlands, which had ruled Indonesia before, did not accept this. The Netherlands tried to take back control of Indonesia. To protect its freedom, Indonesia fought a war against the Netherlands on its own land. From the start, Indonesia asked for and got help from other countries around the world. Because of this international pressure, the Netherlands had to accept Indonesia’s independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia joined the United Nations. This made its independence official and recognized by the world.

CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.
persona1_adapted:
Water played a key role in the Netherlands becoming a trading nation. The economy grew because raw materials came in through rivers and oceans. Think of bulk goods like grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula.

The Dutch used these materials to make products such as beer, cloth (a type of woolen fabric), fine salt (used for preserving herring), weapons, and ships. Many of these goods were then shipped out again, often by water.

Cities were important links in this international trade. In the 1400s and 1500s, Antwerp was the biggest port city in the Netherlands. After the Spanish recaptured it during the Dutch Revolt, Holland—especially Amsterdam—took over as the main trading hub.

International trade led to the rise of a financial market, created many jobs, and caused cities to grow quickly.

CuratorialText: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.
persona1_adapted:
**What is money actually worth?**

Why does a small piece of metal worth a few cents suddenly have a much higher value just because it has "2 euro" stamped on it? The person or group that has the right to make coins gets to choose how they look, how much precious metal they contain, and what they are worth.

For centuries, gold and silver coins spread around the world through trade. Coins that had little gold or silver mostly stayed in local areas. That's because when they traveled to other countries, people cared most about the metal they were made of.

For a long time, the Amsterdam Exchange Bank was a key center for trading precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large amount of the silver that Spain took from the Americas ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 1700s, huge silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled all over the world—for example, to Asia with the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

CuratorialText: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.
persona1_adapted: William of Orange is called the 'Father of the Fatherland' because he led the fight for Dutch independence. The House of Orange got its princely title from a small French city-state called Orange, located on the Rhône River. Even though it was small, the family used its wealth and influence to become one of the great royal houses of Europe. The wives of the stadtholders (the leaders of the Dutch Republic) played an important role in building these connections. However, by the end of the 1700s, the House of Orange seemed finished. Stadtholder William V fled to England, and the Batavian Republic was declared. But in 1813, his oldest son returned and became King William I. From then on, the Netherlands became a 'constitutional monarchy,' meaning the king or queen shares power with a parliament. Since that time, a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always been on the throne.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.
persona1_adapted:
**Who owned this object before it came to the museum?**  
The lives, relationships, and backgrounds of the owners tell us something about why the object matters.  

**Provenance** research—the study of an object’s history of ownership—reveals stories about owners before World War II. It reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if an object was not stolen, its history still gives us important clues about its past.  

The Rijksmuseum’s collection mostly tells the stories of collectors from the years 1933 to 1945. Because of this, research into the collection cannot show a complete picture of all persecuted owners. Many everyday objects never ended up in museums, or are harder to identify.  

Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively took part in Dutch culture and history. Today, objects in museums remain silent witnesses to this past.

CuratorialText: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.
persona1_adapted:
Before Europeans arrived, people had already been trading across the Indian Ocean for hundreds of years. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) took over these trade routes by force. They used places like the Moluccas and Sri Lanka to get spices. Batavia (now Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope became major centers for sugar, coffee, and rice.

In the Atlantic region, Dutch traders also sold sugar and coffee. This led to the creation of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, every trading post used enslaved workers and took part in the slave trade. Even a craftsman in the Netherlands could make money from colonial trade. For example, they might supply goods for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. Transporting products to Europe and processing them also brought in money. In this way, colonial trade helped grow the Dutch economy.

CuratorialText: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.
persona1_adapted: The Netherlands was a colonial power for over 350 years. In the 1600s, it set up trading posts and colonies around the world. These included places like Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. The Dutch often settled through trade agreements, but they also used military force. The Rijksmuseum has many art and everyday objects that came to the Netherlands during and after this time. Some were taken as war booty, while others were bought or traded. It is important to be honest about how this collection was formed. We also need to think about the Netherlands' role in its colonial past. Through research and work with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to share a full and fair picture of this shared history.

CuratorialText: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.
persona1_adapted:
For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, thrives, and hums with activity. People from all over the world have shaped the city into what it is today. The first people to live in Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen. They settled along the River Amstel. 

In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city built on peatlands. The city changed quickly when the VOC and WIC trading companies were founded. These companies traded goods across the globe. During the 1600s, Amsterdam became a world city with over 200,000 people. Almost every Amsterdammer was involved in colonial trade, either directly or indirectly. This included rope makers, mapmakers, notaries, and candy makers. 

To handle the growing population, city leaders made a plan to expand Amsterdam. This was the start of the famous canal belt.

CuratorialText: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.
persona1_adapted:
The tulip originally came from Turkey. It was brought to Europe in the late 1500s. The flower grew very well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coast. 

In the 1600s, traders of tulips and other rare flowers started making "Florilegia." These were books with detailed pictures that showed what they had for sale. People who loved unusual flowers could also hire painters or artists. 

Flower still lifes became a popular type of art. Real flowers wilt and die, but paintings last much longer. Sometimes, these flower paintings cost less than the real flowers shown in them.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.
persona1_adapted:
Throughout history, the role of pets has changed a lot. At first, they were useful working animals. Later, they became beloved companions. Between the years 500 and 1500, noblemen started keeping dogs just for their company. In paintings from the 1600s, dogs often appear in this role.

For a long time, cats were mainly used to catch mice. They also had a bad reputation because, in the Middle Ages, people linked them to witchcraft. It wasn't until the Victorian era (the 1800s) that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful purebred cat, like a British Shorthair or a Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy people.

From the 1800s onward, dogs and cats—and later birds and fish—became everyday pets for people of all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which made her famous. Wealthy families often hired her to paint their beloved pets.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.
persona1_adapted:
**Sculpture at the Rijksmuseum**

Sculpture comes in all shapes and sizes. It can be made from many different materials. This online catalog shows you the Rijksmuseum's large and varied collection of European sculpture. It includes every European sculpture in the museum's art collection.

The last printed catalog of sculpture came out in 1973. That book is now outdated. Art historians have learned a lot since then. The museum has also added many new sculptures. So it was time for a thorough update.

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier versions: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began collecting sculpture on a regular basis only in the late 1800s. Before that, the museum added very few sculptures. One early example is a marble bust of Andries de Graeff. The Dutch government bought it in 1817. It was made by the artist Artus Quellinus.

At first, the museum focused on buying sculptures that were important to the Netherlands. That changed in the 1900s. Two people helped make this change happen. Adriaan Pit was museum director from 1898 to 1917. Jaap Leeuwenberg was curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969. He also put together the 1973 catalog. They worked to buy sculptures from other countries.

Today, about half of the collection comes from outside the Netherlands. This international focus grew even more in 1952. That year, the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of Italian and German sculptures. These works had belonged to Fritz Mannheimer. They were recovered from Germany after World War II. This addition helped the museum keep buying international works.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.
persona1_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum has a world-class collection of Japanese art on paper. This includes woodblock prints, illustrated books, and drawings. Among the nearly 4,000 prints, over 800 are special, privately published surimono. This online catalog shows 203 surimono. They were given to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by a collector named JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The information in this catalog comes from Matthi Forrer's 2013 book *Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam*.

**Poetry and Image Together**

Surimono (which means "printed things") are a very delicate type of Japanese printmaking. They are fascinating because they mix poetry with pictures. They also work like a visual puzzle. This puzzle gives viewers a special look into the smart and literary world of late 1700s and early 1800s Edo (now called Tokyo). Famous artists like Katsushika Hokusai, Utagawa Kunisada, Toyota Hokkei, and Yashima Gakutei made images for poems written by members of poetry clubs.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister, holding a fan.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Maria Trip (1619–1683). She was the sister of Jacobus Trip. Later, she married Balthasar Coymans. In the painting, she stands and faces left. The portrait shows her from the waist up. Her left arm rests on a banister, and she holds a fan.

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.
persona1_adapted: He sits on a cloud resting on a round base, with his wings spread wide. He holds his right index finger to his lips and smiles mischievously. With his left hand, he reaches for an arrow in a quiver lying beside him. His left leg is pulled up, and his right leg stretches out diagonally in front of him, hanging over the round base. Next to him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver, filled with arrows, is decorated with carved grooves, a wreath of laurel leaves, and a pattern of curved ridges. The pedestal is a short column. Its base has a carved profile and is decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves. The shaft has curved grooves that end in leaf shapes, then turns into a smooth upper section with a carved edge. On top is a cover plate decorated with a pattern of egg-shaped and dart-like carvings. This plate can be turned using two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: Indoor room with a mother removing lice from her child's hair, known as 'Mother's Duty'. Interior with mother and child sitting in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair on the right; on the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor and looks at the open window in the room behind. Above the door is a small painting of a landscape.
persona1_adapted: A mother sits with her child in a cozy indoor room. She is carefully removing lice from the child's hair. This scene is called "Mother's Duty." They are sitting in front of a box bed on the right side of the room. A high chair stands nearby on the right. On the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor. The dog looks toward an open window in the room behind them. Above the door hangs a small painting of a landscape.

CuratorialText: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.
persona1_adapted: This portrait shows Alida Assink from head to toe, wearing her finest clothes. That is unusual. The painter, Kruseman, usually painted full-length portraits only for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when her guardian, a wealthy landowner, asked for this portrait. The countryside scene includes a hunting dog and a garden vase. This style was inspired by English portraits, which Kruseman admired.

CuratorialText: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona1_adapted: Aert Schouman was an artist from Dordrecht. He was not a scientist, but he cared a lot about painting birds that looked real. His sharp observation skills show in how this red-billed toucan holds the branch. It uses two toes, not three like other birds. The background is painted lightly in see-through greens and blues. It reminds us of the bird’s home in Guyana and North Brazil.

CuratorialText: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.
persona1_adapted: A still life painting shows roses. You can also see a butterfly and two snails.

CuratorialText: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.
persona1_adapted: Here the Duke of Wellington learns that Prussian troops are coming to help him. Wellington led the Anglo-Dutch army. He is the main figure in this group portrait of the key people at the Battle of Waterloo. In the front left, the Dutch Crown Prince lies wounded on a stretcher. He later became King William II. The painting was first meant for Wellington. But it stayed in the Netherlands because King William I bought it for his son.

CuratorialText: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, strove for greater independence and advocated freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.
persona1_adapted: William of Orange became the main leader of the Dutch Revolt. He was one of the noblemen who fought against King Phillip II's Spanish rule of the Netherlands. William defended his own interests, pushed for more independence, and supported freedom of religion. In 1580, Phillip II placed a bounty on his head. Four years later, William was assassinated.

CuratorialText: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.
persona1_adapted: This still life painting asks you to look closely at every detail. It makes you wonder at the richness of nature: the colorful flowers, the rare shells, and the beautiful insects. But at the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit has the first rotten spots. Caterpillars and snails have eaten away at leaves. Ants drag a dead fly. So the painting is also about how nothing lasts forever.

CuratorialText: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows animals from around the world. You can see two squirrel monkeys from Central America. There are also two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia. A grey parrot from Africa sits nearby. At the lower left, a purple-naped lory from Indonesia is on a chain. The artist, Hondecoeter, painted these creatures along with other beautiful birds. He made this painting for Het Loo, the palace of King William III. It hung above the door of the king's private room.

CuratorialText: This is a panoramic, sun-drenched river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with sketchily drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers halt at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This idyllic scene does not unfold in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.
persona1_adapted: This is a wide, sunlit river scene. The distance looks a bit hazy, and the clouds are drawn with quick, loose strokes. Two Dutch officers stop by the river. They water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This peaceful scene does not take place in Italy. It is set along the Waal River, between Nijmegen and Cleves. The artist, Cuyp, made drawings of this landscape and brought them back to his studio in Dordrecht.

CuratorialText: Ice entertainment near a village. Winter village scene with many figures skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.
persona1_adapted:
**Winter Fun on the Ice**

People are skating, playing, and bowling on a frozen river near a village. On the left side, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, you can see a drawbridge and a windmill.

CuratorialText: This dolls’ house is exceptionally realistic. All the contents have been made of authentic materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.
persona1_adapted: This dolls’ house looks very real. Every item inside is made from true materials, like real wood and metal. The sizes of everything are exactly right. The fancy cabinet is made of tortoiseshell with pewter (a type of metal) decorations. A French cabinetmaker built it while he worked in Amsterdam for a few years. Petronella Oortman owned this house. She was married to Johannes Brandt, a merchant from Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to meticulous and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.
persona1_adapted: This scene is painted on a copper panel. The smooth surface allowed for very fine and careful detail. Van Kessel created dozens of these small copper paintings of insects and other animals. He would then arrange them into a larger scene. These pieces may have also been used as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.

CuratorialText: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

On June 18, 1648, a banquet was held at Amsterdam's crossbowmen's guild. The event celebrated the signing of the Treaty of Münster. This treaty ended the war with Spain. The captains of the city's guard shake hands as a sign of peace. They pass around a drinking horn. A poem on the drum announces the joy of Amsterdam's armed militia. Their weapons can now be put to rest.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
persona1_adapted:
**Fishing for Souls: A Painting About Religious Rivalry**

This painting is an allegory, which means it uses symbols to tell a bigger story. It shows the jealousy between different religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621). That was a time when the Dutch Republic and Spain paused their war.

In the scene, large groups of people stand on both sides of a river. Several rowboats float on the water. A rainbow appears on the horizon.

On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters. These include the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry. Also shown are Frederick V of the Palatinate, King James I of England, and the young King Louis XIII of France with his mother, Maria de' Medici.

In the boat at the front, Protestant fishermen sit. Their nets are labeled with three words: Fides (faith), Spes (hope), and Charitas (charity).

On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters. These include Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is carried by cardinals. In a small Catholic boat, priests fish under the leadership of a bishop.

CuratorialText: Wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is seated full-length on a raised platform under a tree. On the left, a thistle; on the right, ivy. On the right in the background, elegant couples are walking in a garden with a fountain at a country house.
persona1_adapted: This is a wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen. They got married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple sits on a raised platform under a tree. On the left side of the painting, there is a thistle plant. On the right side, there is ivy. In the background on the right, well-dressed couples walk through a garden. The garden has a fountain and is part of a country house.

CuratorialText: Figure of painted porcelain. The figure depicts a parrot (macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. The tail and the tips of the wings are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries lies beneath the right leg. The figure is unmarked.
persona1_adapted: This figure is made of painted porcelain. It shows a parrot, specifically a macaw, sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head, and a white body. Its tail and wing tips are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries sits under its right leg. The figure has no maker's mark.

CuratorialText: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
persona1_adapted: This is a formal kimono called a *furisode*. It was worn by a young unmarried woman. The kimono has long sleeves. It is decorated all over with over two hundred flying cranes. The fabric is black silk crepe, known as *chirimen*. The design uses a special dyeing technique called *yuzen*. The colors are white, red, and grey. The artist also added details with gold foil. The inside lining is red silk. The kimono has a single bamboo family crest, or *mon*.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers. A bouquet of flowers in a metal vase standing in a niche. The bouquet contains roses, irises, poppies, a wheat stalk, tulips, and other flowers. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl among the flowers and over the niche. Pendant of SK-A-337.
persona1_adapted:
**Still Life with Flowers**

This painting shows a bouquet of flowers in a metal vase. The vase sits inside a small, arched space called a niche. The bouquet includes roses, irises, poppies, a stalk of wheat, tulips, and other flowers. Crawling among the flowers and over the niche are a snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle. This painting is a companion piece to another artwork, SK-A-337.

CuratorialText: Mary Magdalene sitting under a tree with the ointment jar on her lap. The saint is seated in a landscape with mountains and rugged rock formations.
persona1_adapted: Mary Magdalene sits under a tree. She holds an ointment jar on her lap. The saint rests in a landscape with mountains and rough rock formations.

CuratorialText: Portrait of the twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans standing full-length on a checkered tile floor in an undefined space. He is dressed in an expensive black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters, wears a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.
persona1_adapted: This is a full-length portrait of twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans. He stands on a checkered tile floor in a plain, undefined space. Marten wears an expensive black suit. It has a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters. He also wears a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.

CuratorialText: Portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit standing full-length on a tiled floor in an otherwise undefined space. She is dressed in an expensive black dress, lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil. She wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan of black ostrich feathers.
persona1_adapted: Oopjen Coppit was 23 years old when this portrait was painted. She stands full-length on a tiled floor, but the space around her is not clearly defined. She wears an expensive black dress with a lace collar and lace cuffs. A black veil covers her head. She also wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan made of black ostrich feathers.

CuratorialText: Still life with a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver holder on which stands a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters and a roll.
persona1_adapted: A still life painting shows a golden ceremonial cup, a silver stand holding a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters, and a roll.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, in Turkish costume. Full-length, seated on a divan. Her head resting on her right hand. On the divan lie a book and a basket containing a mirror, comb, and bonnet. On the floor are an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife. She is wearing a Turkish costume. She sits on a divan, which is a long, low sofa. Her head rests on her right hand. A book lies on the divan next to her. There is also a basket holding a mirror, a comb, and a bonnet. An Oriental rug covers the floor. A small vase with carnations sits on the floor as well.

CuratorialText: A cheerful violinist holding a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he holds up a birch flask filled with wine; in his left hand, a violin with a bow. On his head, a feathered hat.
persona1_adapted: A cheerful violinist holds a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window. The window is covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he lifts a birch flask filled with wine. In his left hand, he holds a violin with a bow. On his head, he wears a feathered hat.

CuratorialText: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the foreground, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
persona1_adapted:
**View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil**

On the left, a hill shows a group of people near the ruins of a church. In the front of the painting, you can see tropical plants, fruits, and animals. These animals include an armadillo, a monkey, a sloth, an anteater, and a lizard.

CuratorialText: Winter landscape. View of a wide, snow-covered lane leading past a forest. In the foreground, three boys with sleds. Further back, a wagon drawn by two horses stands on the road in front of a farm.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a winter landscape. A wide, snow-covered lane runs through the scene. It leads past a forest. In the front, three boys stand with sleds. Further back, a wagon pulled by two horses waits on the road in front of a farm.

CuratorialText: Italian landscape with draftsman. A road through the landscape with a bridge over a mountain stream. On the road, a man with a mule. In the foreground, some seated figures.
persona1_adapted:
**Italian Landscape with a Draftsman**

This painting shows an Italian countryside scene. A road winds through the landscape and crosses a bridge over a mountain stream. A man walks on the road with his mule. In the front of the picture, a few people are sitting down.

CuratorialText: Still life with turkey pie. Table with a white damask tablecloth on which stand pewter plates with a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and olives (?). Also a pewter pitcher, roemer, knife, nuts, bread rolls, a nautilus cup, and a Wan Li bowl with fruit. In the background stands a large pie on which a turkey has been placed.
persona1_adapted:
**Still Life with Turkey Pie**

This painting shows a table covered with a white linen cloth. On the table are pewter plates holding a pie, oysters, lemon, salt, pepper, and what might be olives. There is also a pewter pitcher, a wine glass called a *roemer*, a knife, nuts, bread rolls, a cup made from a nautilus shell, and a bowl from China filled with fruit. In the back of the scene, a large pie sits with a turkey placed on top.

CuratorialText: The stone bridge. Landscape with a stone bridge over a river or canal. On the water are two small boats with figures. Various people are walking on the road, a wagon on the left. In the middle, a hay barn between trees, a church tower in the distance on the right.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a stone bridge crossing a river or canal. Two small boats with people float on the water. Several people walk along the road. A wagon sits on the left side. In the middle, a hay barn stands between trees. A church tower rises in the distance on the right.

CuratorialText: A stone bust of Flora in an ornamental cartouche surrounded by a festoon of various types of flowers, including tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.
persona1_adapted: A stone carving shows the goddess Flora inside a fancy frame. The frame is shaped like a shield. Around it hangs a garland of different flowers, such as tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.

CuratorialText: The overturned bouquet. A cat, playing with a mousetrap, knocks over a vase containing a bouquet of flowers. In the bouquet: tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Flying among the flowers are butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails.
persona1_adapted: A cat plays with a mousetrap. It knocks over a vase that holds a bouquet of flowers. The bouquet includes tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails fly or move among the flowers.

CuratorialText: The Fall of Man. Adam and Eve standing before the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Surrounding the figures are various animals: monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, Adam and Eve are warned by God not to eat from the tree.
persona1_adapted: Adam and Eve stand in front of the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Around them are many animals: a monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, God warns Adam and Eve not to eat fruit from the tree.

CuratorialText: View of an orchard with plum blossoms, seen through the branches of a plum tree in the foreground; picnicking visitors in the background; part of the entrance gate on the left in the foreground; against a bright red sky.
persona1_adapted: You look at an orchard full of plum blossoms. You see it through the branches of a plum tree in the front. In the background, visitors are having a picnic. Part of the entrance gate is on the left, also in the front. The sky is bright red.

CuratorialText: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. She is dressed in a pink gown, known as reform clothing, and has her hair simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp is partially visible on the table on the left, placed next to a small vase of roses on a small table covered with a floral tablecloth. Flowers in a large vase in the background are visually incorporated into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.
persona1_adapted: The artist used a technique called pointillism, which means they painted with tiny dots of color. The woman sits on the right side of the painting, facing us while reading a book by the light of a kerosene lamp. She wears a pink dress, a style called "reform clothing" that was meant to be more comfortable than typical dresses of the time. Her hair is simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp sits on a small table on the left, next to a vase of roses. The table is covered with a floral tablecloth. In the background, flowers in a large vase blend into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.

CuratorialText: Kitten game. A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. There is also an ashtray with a cigar on the table.
