persona1_adapted: A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. An ashtray with a cigar also sits on the table.

CuratorialText: Interior in which a family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas. In the center, a girl holding a John the Baptist doll and a bucket of candy in her arms. On the left, a crying boy who has received a rod in his shoe. Next to the mother with outstretched arms stands a laughing boy with a club. On the floor is a basket of cakes and rolls; by the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman.
persona1_adapted: A family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas inside their home. In the center, a girl holds a John the Baptist doll and a bucket of candy. On the left, a boy cries because he found a rod in his shoe instead of a gift. Next to the mother, who has her arms stretched out, a laughing boy stands with a club. On the floor, there is a basket of cakes and rolls. By the fireplace, several young people sing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman are watching.

CuratorialText: Jeremiah mourning over the destruction of Jerusalem. The old prophet sits on the rocky slope of a mountain, his head resting on his left hand. On a stone beside him lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books, sanctuaries from the destroyed temple. Below to the left, the burning buildings of the city and soldiers can be seen.
persona1_adapted: Jeremiah mourns the destruction of Jerusalem. The old prophet sits on a rocky mountain slope. His head rests on his left hand. Beside him on a stone lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books. These objects were saved from the destroyed temple. Below to the left, you can see the city's burning buildings and soldiers.

CuratorialText: Still life with fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl. On a stone table, a silver plate with oysters, fruit, a porcelain bowl, an inverted roemer, a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. Also snails and insects.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a still life—a type of artwork that focuses on objects that don't move. Here, the artist arranged oysters, fruit, and a porcelain bowl on a stone table. A silver plate holds the oysters and fruit. Next to them sits a porcelain bowl, an upside-down wine glass called a roemer, and a glowing wick. A bunch of grapes rests on a wooden box. Look closely, and you can also see snails and insects.

CuratorialText: Painting titled 'View at the Geestbrug'. River landscape with the Trekvliet near Rijswijk, with De Binckhorst Castle in the distance on the left and the Laakmolen on the right. A woman with children stands on the road to the farm, and boats are sailing on the water.
persona1_adapted: This painting is called *View at the Geestbrug*. It shows a river scene near Rijswijk. On the left, you can see De Binckhorst Castle in the distance. On the right is the Laakmolen, a windmill. A woman and her children stand on the road to a farm. Boats sail on the water.

CuratorialText: The well-stocked kitchen, with Jesus with Martha and Mary in the background. In the kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken, the other is skewering a chicken. To the left, others are sitting by the hearth. In the foreground, all kinds of game and poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. Among these are also various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.
persona1_adapted: In a well-stocked kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken, and the other is skewering one. To the left, other people sit by the hearth. In the background, you can see Jesus with Martha and Mary. In the front, many kinds of game, poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. There are also different glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers and fruit. On a plinth stands a stone vase with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the plinth is a glass, and lying are a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose. On the wall hangs a painting of a woman with a flower basket handing a child a flower wreath.
persona1_adapted: A stone vase sits on a base, filled with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the base, there is a glass. A pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose lie nearby. On the wall hangs a painting of a woman with a flower basket. She is handing a flower wreath to a child.

CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.
persona1_adapted: Six warships sit on a wide, calm sea. Crews are hoisting the sails and lifting the anchors. The back of the ship on the right, in the front, shows Amsterdam's coat of arms. Two standing lions hold it up. This scene likely shows part of a war fleet getting ready to leave. A small boat with important people—probably from the Admiralty—sails past the ships. Trumpets play and cannons fire in salute. A little farther away, workers are still loading the last supplies and crew members. The artist paid close attention to every detail. The ropes, sails, and cannons are shown with amazing accuracy.

CuratorialText: Still life with a hammered bronze vase containing a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the tabletop lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.
persona1_adapted: A still life shows a hammered bronze vase. The vase holds a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the table next to the vase are a dead frog, a bunch of berries, and a caterpillar.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (about 1445–1516). He was an architect. His father was Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. In the painting, his bust faces to the right. There is a landscape behind him. A compass and a pen rest on the ledge at the bottom. This piece is part of a two-panel artwork called a diptych. It goes with another painting, SK-C-1367.

CuratorialText: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), presumably depicted as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.
persona1_adapted: An old woman sits in a chair reading a large book that lies open on her lap. She is probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck, who died in 1640. The artist likely shows her as the prophetess Anna, a figure from the Bible.

CuratorialText: Allegorical musical group. On the left, a man in Oriental clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head looks on. In the foreground lie musical instruments (lute, violin) and stacks of books. On the wall is a painting of Lot's Flight from Sodom.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a group of people making music. The scene has a hidden, or "allegorical," meaning. On the left, a man in a turban and flowing clothes plays a viola da gamba (an old string instrument like a cello). Next to him, a young man stands with a harp. A young woman sits by a small table. She has an open songbook on her lap and is singing. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head watches. In the front of the painting, you can see musical instruments (a lute and a violin) and stacks of books. On the wall hangs a painting of a Bible story: Lot's Flight from Sodom.

CuratorialText: Venus and Adonis. The half-naked Venus and Cupid try to prevent Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves in the lower right. Adonis's quiver hangs from the tree. Adonis has two hunting dogs with him.
persona1_adapted:
**Venus and Adonis**

In this painting, the half-dressed goddess Venus and her son Cupid try to stop Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves sit in the lower right corner. Adonis's arrow holder, called a quiver, hangs from a tree. Two hunting dogs stand beside him.

CuratorialText: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with slack sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with furled sails. Pendant to SK-A-1848.
persona1_adapted:
**The Cannon Shot**

A warship sits calmly on the water with its sails hanging loose. Suddenly, it fires a cannon. Two longboats float on either side of the ship. In the distance, you can see another warship with its sails rolled up. This painting is a companion piece to another artwork called SK-A-1848.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
persona1_adapted: When people hear the word "Sufi," they often picture Turkish whirling dervishes. These dancers spin gracefully in a mystical ceremony. But outside of Islamic mysticism, most people don't know much about the beliefs and practices behind these ceremonies. They also don't know much about the communities that follow them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
persona1_adapted:
This display explores Sufi life and art through objects from Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. Sufism is a mystical movement that first appeared in the Islamic world during the 700s AD. It is a path to a personal experience with God.

The objects on display come from the British Museum. They are Islamic artifacts with Sufi meanings. Some objects, like alms bowls, are clearly linked to dervishes. Others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings, and wall tiles.

Sufis come from all walks of life. They cross boundaries of class, ethnicity, and region. The display explores themes like mystical love, poverty, and asceticism. It also shows how Sufis influenced the wider societies they lived in.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.
persona1_adapted:
**Hawaiʻi: A Kingdom Crossing Oceans** is a bright celebration of the art and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together amazing objects. You can see feathered cloaks worn by chiefs, finely carved statues of gods, powerful weapons made with shark teeth, and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across huge oceans. Their journey would change the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. This exhibition honors over 200 years since that unlucky royal visit. It shows Hawaiʻi's history and culture by exploring the deep and lasting bond between the Hawaiian and British nations.

The exhibition was built with years of work alongside Native Hawaiian artists, experts, and scholars. It puts indigenous knowledge at the center. This helps shine new light on special objects and amazing stories. Created together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the show brings together rare loans from other countries and the British Museum's own collections—one of the largest outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
persona1_adapted:
Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist, and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006). His work focuses on the idea of revolution and how it connects to today's society.

Finlay became known for his poetry in the late 1950s. But in the 1960s, he started making prints and three-dimensional artworks. He used materials like stone, metal, neon, and glass. He worked with many different craftspeople to create these pieces.

Finlay was also a dedicated gardener. He built a conceptual garden at his remote home, located on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh. The garden included many of his artworks. He named it Little Sparta, after the ancient Greek city that was a military rival to Athens. This name shows his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.
persona1_adapted: In the 1980s, Finlay became more focused on the French Revolution in his work. He was interested in revolutions and sudden changes. He also explored how good ideas could quickly turn into violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display were made between 1984 and 1992. They are part of Finlay's work on the French Revolution. These prints come from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Professor Stephen Bann. He is an art historian who has written a lot about Finlay and exchanged letters with the artist for many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
persona1_adapted: Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) worked as an artist from the 1760s to the 1830s. During that time, he lived through some of the most dramatic events in French history. Wille studied under Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805). He built his career by copying the everyday life scenes that made Greuze famous at the art shows of the 1760s. Wille’s work showed a taste for fine details, which fit the style that people liked during the old French monarchy, known as the *ancien régime*.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.
persona1_adapted:
Johann Georg Wille supported the French Revolution. He even joined the republican army. He spent his time recording the famous people of his day. But in the end, the Revolution was more of a problem than a chance for him.

As a young man, Wille tried many different art styles. Later in life, he found it hard to change to the simpler, more serious taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had been poor for many years.

During his life and after, Wille was better known for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been collecting his work since the 1800s. Its collection recently doubled in size thanks to a gift from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020).

This gift included some of Wille's finest finished drawings. One of the most powerful is a portrait that claims to show Charlotte Corday, the "Angel of Assassination." It was made in her prison cell just days or even hours before her execution.

This celebration of Clark's gift shows Wille's world through his drawings and those of his friends. It reveals him as a talented artist whose works give a fascinating look into French society before, during, and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.
persona1_adapted:
The Asante Ewer was made in England between 1340 and 1405. Later, it traveled to West Africa. In the 1880s, someone photographed it in a courtyard of the royal palace in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). That palace belonged to the Asantehene, the king of the Asante people.

During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896, British forces took the ewer as loot. They then sold it to the British Museum. A second jug, shown in the same photo and also taken in 1896, was given to a British army regiment. This display brings these two jugs back together.

A new research project is studying the Asante Ewer. It is one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting. The project is helping to trace how it got to Ghana and what it meant to people in West Africa.

In the photo, the ewer sits in the center of the palace courtyard. It appears to be inside a shrine, surrounded by the roots of sacred trees. This suggests it was an object of great cultural or ritual value.

It is remarkable that the ewer survived the long journey from Europe to West Africa and back. Over time, it has had very different lives: first a luxury English jug, then a sacred West African vessel, and finally colonial loot taken by British troops.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.
persona1_adapted: This display is part of a three-year research project called *The Age of Copper, Ivory and Gold*. The project was supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation. It looks at the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. The display was created through close work with partners at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, the York Army Museum, and many others across Ghana, the US, and the UK.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
persona1_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a major center for new art ideas. During this time, drawings in the Netherlands changed a lot. They started as rough sketches to plan other artworks. Later, they became finished pieces of art that people collected and admired.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition shows about 120 drawings by artists like Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder, and Hendrick Goltzius. It follows how drawing developed in the Low Countries (today’s Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands) before the year 1600.

The British Museum has a very large and high-quality collection of these drawings. This gives us a rare chance to tell a full story about drawing in this region.

Early Netherlandish drawings are very rare. Artists made them as working tools in their workshops, not as pieces to keep. This was different from Italy or German-speaking areas. There, people started collecting drawings in the 1500s, so more works from that time survived.

Drawings were key to making art in many forms, such as tapestries, paintings, stained glass, and prints. The drawings that still exist today show us how creative Netherlandish artists were and how they worked together.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition is based on a research project. It was supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation. The show highlights new discoveries made by combining the skills of curators, conservators, and scientists.

For example, new research has been done on drawings once linked only to the artist Rogier van der Weyden and his students. This research shows that the drawings actually came directly from Rogier's workshop. They were likely drawn by his pupils as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.
persona1_adapted: See early designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.
persona1_adapted:
A new memorial in St James’s Park will honor Queen Elizabeth II and her lifelong dedication to public service. It features a 10-foot bronze statue of the Queen as a young monarch, standing on a 14-foot base called a plinth. The memorial is expected to be finished in 2028.

The statue was designed by famous sculptor Martin Jennings. It will be made of bronze and shows the Queen early in her reign. She wears the robes of the Order of the Garter, a special honor. The design was inspired by a well-known painting by Pietro Annigoni.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.
persona1_adapted:
Mr. Jennings will also create a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving royal partner. The statue will show him at a similar age, wearing the uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. It will stand close to Queen Elizabeth's statue. This placement honors the support he gave her during her 70-year reign.

Other parts of the memorial were designed by Foster + Partners, working with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display includes small models, called maquettes, of the statues. It also has a scale model of the larger memorial park.

On April 21, the King and Queen, other Royal Family members, and the Prime Minister saw the designs at the Museum. This date marked the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth.

The permanent memorial will also include:
- A new bridge made of cast glass, inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara. Queen Elizabeth wore this tiara on her wedding day.
- A bust, or head-and-shoulders sculpture, of the Queen in her later years. Artist Karen Newman created it.
- The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for regenerating community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to submit their own memories of the late Queen.
persona1_adapted: The memorial park includes gardens for the Commonwealth and the nations of the United Kingdom. These gardens give people quiet spaces to relax and think. The memorial was created with help from the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee. They chose the design after a competition last year. Besides the park, the committee also suggested two other projects. One is a new charity to improve community spaces across the UK. The other is an online Digital Memorial where people can share their own memories of the late Queen.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
persona1_adapted:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt. Learn about their hopes for the afterlife.  
This tour looks at human remains, painted coffins, amulets (small objects worn for protection), shabtis (small figures that served the dead), and tomb models showing daily life. You will also see wall paintings and papyri (ancient paper made from reeds).  
These objects help us understand what tombs, human remains, and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptians.  
The tour explores how the dead were prepared for burial. This includes mummification (preserving the body) and filling the tomb with items for an eternal, restful life. It also shows how magic was used to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife.  
Funerary remains also reveal how ancient Egyptians lived.  
Please note: This tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum treats these remains with care, respect, and dignity. Learn more about our rules for keeping, showing, and studying human remains.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.
persona1_adapted:
Discover the amazing history of ancient Egypt. Explore one of the most famous rooms in the Museum before it opens to the public.

In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see huge statues of kings and gods. You will also find large tomb buildings and ancient carvings on tomb walls. These objects cover 3,000 years of history. They include the giant bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. On this special guided tour, learn why large sculptures were important in ancient Egyptian temples and tombs.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.
persona1_adapted: The British Museum opened in 1759, just six years after it was founded in 1753. It tells the story of human culture through many different collections. This room honors some of the collectors who helped shape the Museum over 400 years. It also highlights people and groups who continue to shape its future today. The rooms next door explore other parts of collecting. Room 1, called Enlightenment, shows how the early Museum gathered and sorted objects and knowledge. Room 2a, the Waddesdon Bequest, displays a collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces. These are artworks from the 1300s to 1700s. Baron Ferdinand Rothschild, a member of Parliament, left this collection to the Museum when he died in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
persona1_adapted:
The British Museum raised £3.5 million to keep the Tudor Heart Pendant on public display for years to come. In December 2019, a person using a metal detector in Warwickshire found this rare jewel buried in the ground. It had been resting there for centuries. The pendant is linked to King Henry VIII and his first wife, Katherine of Aragon.

This pendant is unlike any other object in the British Museum or anywhere else in the UK. It is a highly skilled piece of jewelry with designs made from enamel. The pendant hangs from a 75-link gold chain. A clasp shaped like a hand coming out of a cloud holds it in place.

Very few objects survive that tell the story of Henry’s life with Katherine, who was a devoted Catholic. Their marriage ended in 1533 when Henry had it annulled. Their union lasted 24 years—the longest of all Henry’s marriages—and it shaped the first 30 years of his rule.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.
persona1_adapted:
In the 1700s, the Museum's collections were split into two groups: "Natural and Artificial Rarities." Natural rarities were things found in nature, like plants and rocks. Artificial rarities were objects made by people.

At first, only a few rooms in the Museum held manmade objects. Most of the space was filled with natural specimens from Sir Hans Sloane's collection. His library also included 265 volumes of pressed plants, called a herbarium.

In 1735, a Swedish scientist named Carl Linnaeus changed how scientists named and organized plants, animals, and other natural objects. His student, Daniel Solander, worked as a curator at the Museum. Solander used Linnaeus's system to organize Sloane's collections. He also used it for the natural history specimens he collected with Sir Joseph Banks on Captain Cook's first voyage.

Many of these original specimens are still in the exhibition today, along with a number of fossils. By 1880, the Museum had so many natural history specimens that they needed their own building. That's why the Natural History Museum was built in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
persona1_adapted:
Antiquaries were people who studied the past. They collected old books, handwritten papers, drawings, and objects from earlier times. They also traveled around the country to look at ruins. Their goal was to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its even earlier history.

Learning about medieval times was fairly easy. Many objects from that period had survived. These included religious items, armor, books, and buildings.

But antiquaries also started to study Roman and even older sites, like Stonehenge, in a more scientific and organized way. They made maps and surveys of these places. This helped them learn more about the earliest people in Britain.

This new knowledge, combined with the study of rock layers (called strata), led archaeologists to question the widely accepted date of the world's creation. That date was 4004 BC, which had been calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona1_adapted:
In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures arrived at the British Museum. He had gathered these works during his Grand Tours in Italy. They joined other collections of prints, drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases, and ancient objects. These came from fellow art lovers like Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight.

These men studied each other's collections to understand how art had "progressed." They believed art started with simple beginnings in early civilizations. They thought it reached its peak in the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This Greek art became the standard for judging all other art. In late 1700s Britain, this led to a revival of classical styles in buildings and decorative objects.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona1_adapted: Everything collected in the 1700s had to be sorted and organized so people could understand and use it more easily. King George III’s library was a gift to the nation from his son, George IV. It was kept in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery. (Today, the library is at the British Library.) The coins and medals were among the first items from the library to arrive at the Museum. They were arranged by country and ruler to help bring history to life. George III was also very interested in the navy, navigation, and science. He collected tools and working models. Sir William Hamilton organized his vases from the area near Naples by painting style. He tried to figure out their dates. Later, he published them in books with beautiful pictures. In the early 1700s, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious manmade objects from around the world. Some of these objects came from enslaved people in Jamaica. These items were listed and displayed in many ways—by type, material, date, or purpose.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona1_adapted:
During the Enlightenment, people became very curious about ancient writing. They wanted to understand Egyptian hieroglyphs—picture symbols used in ancient Egypt—and cuneiform, a wedge-shaped writing from the Middle East. These writings appeared in lands mentioned in the Bible. Scholars also studied early forms of Sanskrit, an old language from India.

In the 1800s, experts "cracked the codes" of these ancient scripts. This opened up thousands of years of history. At the same time, other researchers decoded runes—letters used by early Germanic peoples—and other old writing systems. This helped reveal more about our own past.

During this time, many ancient objects came into the British Museum. They were collected from private collectors, diplomats, and through digging at ancient sites.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona1_adapted: For a long time, many Christians were taught to distrust superstitions, idols, rituals, and magic. This led to widespread ignorance about other religions. During the Enlightenment, scholars tried to change that. They studied both ancient and modern religions they came across. They looked at rituals and religious groups in different cultures. They collected religious objects, sorted gods into families, and examined each god's traits and roles. Their goal was to find similarities that could explain how the world's religions began. Much of this work happened during the age of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona1_adapted:
During the time of colonialism and global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers, and diplomats collected objects from different cultures. These objects included both ceremonial items and everyday tools. People thought these items showed the customs and ways of life of people around the world.

Collectors gathered these objects and wrote books with pictures about the lives and cultures of people from a European point of view. The British Museum holds items from Sir Hans Sloane's collection. It also has objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks. They traveled to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands.

Many of these objects came from people who worked for the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company. These companies became wealthy by using enslaved workers and by taking advantage of people and natural resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
persona1_adapted:
Alexander the Great led his empire from 336 to 323 BC. During that time, he conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire. He also founded more than 70 cities. His empire stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.

The Hellenistic period began after Alexander died in 323 BC. It lasted until 31 BC, when Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony were defeated at Actium. After Alexander's death, his empire was split into kingdoms. His generals ruled these kingdoms. The major royal families paid for large building projects. They also supported art and culture.

Room 22 displays many objects from this time. One is a huge carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos. That temple was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Another object is the Demeter statue from Knidos in southwest Turkey. There are also Greek bronzes. Smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, and gem cutters. Coroplasts—makers of clay or terracotta figurines—also created detailed works.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition celebrates the art and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together amazing objects. You will see feathered cloaks worn by chiefs, finely carved statues of gods, powerful weapons made with shark teeth, and bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across the ocean. Their journey marked a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. This exhibition honors over 200 years since that royal visit. It explores the deep and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition celebrates the art and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together amazing objects. You will see feathered cloaks worn by chiefs, finely carved statues of gods, and powerful weapons made with shark teeth. You will also see bold modern works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across the ocean. Their journey would change the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. This exhibition marks over 200 years since that royal visit. It explores the deep and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.
persona1_adapted:
This fashion show features modern clothing from Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa, also known as the Pacific Ocean). The show was organized in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia.

The collection was designed by Māori creator Lyall Hakaraia. He worked with respected experts from across the Pacific. These experts are called *tohunga* (specialists). They build and grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They see themselves as guides. Through their daily work, strong intentions, and vision, they try to be 'good ancestors.' They are creating strong futures for *mokopuna* (grandchildren) and generations to come.

The show will be a gathering point for the Pacific diaspora community. They will open a *VĀ* (a portal) to their ancestors. The show is a gift to those ancestors who remain at the British Museum. These ancestors are present in the Pacific collections. The show aims to 'warm' them as they rest. During the show, the group will sing, chant, and offer prayers. They do this with *Aroha* (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.
persona1_adapted: One side of the gallery shows China’s history from 5000 BC to today. You’ll see famous Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain and delicate handscrolls. There are also grand Tang dynasty tomb figurines and modern artworks. The displays highlight the variety of Chinese art and objects, including paintings, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer, and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.
persona1_adapted: This side of the gallery shows South Asia's many histories. It is organized by time and by region, from early humans to today. Key objects include seals from the Indus civilization. There are also beautiful sculptures of Shiva from south India. One of the finest statues of the goddess Tara, from Sri Lanka, is here too. You can see detailed paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors. These are displayed next to 20th-century paintings, including works by Rabindranath Tagore, who won the Nobel Prize.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.
persona1_adapted:
China is one of the world's oldest civilizations. Today, it covers a huge area about the size of Europe. One out of every four people on Earth lives there. From very early times, China created a unique culture. It made beautifully crafted objects in large numbers.

Room 33 looks at 7,000 years of China's history. It explores writing, rituals, beliefs, war, and international trade. The room also covers South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to today. Trade, exchange, migration, and political power are some of the topics.

South Asia includes India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau, and Sri Lanka. Many ancient and modern languages are spoken there, like Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. Religions such as Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, Sikhism, and Islam have inspired amazing buildings, sculptures, paintings, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms
persona1_adapted:
The museum shows Africa's rich culture and history in several places. You can see it in Room 25, The Sainsbury Galleries, and other galleries too. Join our volunteer-led tours. These tours were made with help from community members. Each guide picks objects they find special, from ancient times to today. Then they share their own personal tour on one of these topics:

- Out of Africa: stories about migration, materials, and myths
- The Igbo worldview: old objects from the past at the British Museum
- African metal objects: tradition, skill, and trade
- Ancient objects from African kingdoms

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.
persona1_adapted:
**Step into spring and the month of May with this creative play session for kids ages five and under.**

You will meet May Queens, Green Men, and woodland creatures. This session is child-led and open-ended. That means your child decides what to do and how to explore.

Stop and smell some floral scents. Draw and scribble with different art materials. Make crowns and masks to wear for spring celebrations! We will work at your child's pace. Together, we will explore textures, colors, and scents using a variety of materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
persona1_adapted:
Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704 to 681 BC. He was famous for his building projects. His Southwest Palace at Nineveh had rooms and courtyards decorated with detailed carved stone panels. Many of these panels are now on display in Room 9.

The panels show different scenes. One scene shows workers moving huge stone sculptures of human-headed winged bulls, called *lamassu*. These sculptures weigh up to 30 tons. They were meant for the main entrances to the palace.

These images give us a look at how people in ancient times cut and moved stone. They also show how much Sennacherib cared about his building projects. Other panels in the room show the king's military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.
persona1_adapted:
These galleries are spread across three rooms. They show what life was like during the time of the Assyrian Empire. You can see stone carvings of lion hunts, the attack on the city of Lachish, and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.

In Room 10a, visitors are greeted by two giant statues. They have the bodies of bulls, wings, and human heads. These figures were meant to be supernatural guards. They stood watch at the palace of King Sargon II. Their job was to keep all kinds of evil from entering.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona1_adapted: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was a sport only for kings. It showed the king's role as a protector and fighter for his people. The carved stone pictures in Room 10a tell the story of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (who ruled from 668 to 631 BC). These artworks were made for his palace in Nineveh, which is in modern-day northern Iraq. The hunting scenes are full of tension and look very real. They are some of the finest examples of Assyrian art. The pictures show the lions being set free, the chase that follows, and the final kill.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona1_adapted:
Lachish was one of the main cities in the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern part of the ancient Near East. In 701 BC, the Assyrian King Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC) captured the city. This happened because Lachish refused to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. The Bible also mentions this siege.

In Room 10b, you can see many stone reliefs (carved pictures) that show the capture of Lachish. There are also objects and weapons used during the siege. A special clay prism with an Assyrian account of the campaign is on display too.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.
persona1_adapted: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq) were built for the Assyrian King Sargon II, who ruled from 721 to 705 BC. Huge statues of human-headed winged bulls once guarded the palace entrances. These statues were meant to protect the palace, along with magical spirits. Two of these impressive statues are now in Room 10c. You can also see carvings there of the king, the crown prince, royal courtiers, and hunting scenes. Inscriptions in the gallery come from a similar winged bull. That bull was from the palace of King Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh. The inscriptions were badly burned when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona1_adapted:
Rooms 57–59 show objects from a region that was home to many ancient peoples. These objects date from around 8000 BC, when the first farmers lived there, to 539 BC, when the Neo-Babylonian Empire fell. The items on display help tell the story of major historical events.

The objects show how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant (an area that includes modern-day Israel, Palestine, and Jordan) lasted for thousands of years. They also highlight that both the Israelites and the Phoenicians had local roots in this region. The display compares the Canaanite culture with that of people from central inland Syria, including the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.
persona1_adapted: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu were key land bridges between Europe and Asia. These regions are now part of modern Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwest Iran. On display, you can see Early Bronze Age crafts, like a silver bull and a cup. Records from Middle Bronze Age merchants show trade between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewelry and small statues come from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu include winged bulls and griffins, which were used to decorate furniture.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition looked back more than 2,000 years. It explored the origins of sacred art in Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism. That art began with the powerful nature spirits of ancient India. The show also traced how this art spread beyond the Indian subcontinent.

It was one of the first major exhibitions in the world to study early devotional art from India. It did so from a multi-faith, modern, and global viewpoint. The exhibition highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar images of gods and enlightened teachers from these world religions. It also showed how these images were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition is colorful, multi-sensory, and full of atmosphere. It was created with help from an advisory group of Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains who practice these religions today. These living traditions and their sacred art are now a key part of daily life for nearly two billion people around the world, including in the UK. Important objects borrowed from our community partners helped tell this modern story.

CuratorialText: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition featured over 180 objects. They included sculptures, paintings, drawings, and manuscripts. These items came from the British Museum's South Asian collection. Many other museums in the UK and around the world also lent pieces. The show focused on provenance. This means it looked at each object's full story. It traced how each item was made and how it ended up in a museum.

CuratorialText: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona1_adapted: This exhibition tells the ancient stories behind three living traditions. It begins with symbolic footprints that came before images of the Buddha in human form. It also includes cosmic serpents in Hindu art. Nature spirits attend Jain enlightened teachers. These artworks bring these old stories to life.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition is the first on Hiroshige at the British Museum. It is also the first show on the artist in London in over 25 years. The display offers a stunning look at a country about to change forever.

Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) was born during an unsettled time in Japan's history. He became one of the country's most talented, productive, and popular artists. As Japan faced the outside world moving in, Hiroshige's calm art connected with people at every level of society. It gave them comfort.

Hiroshige captured many sides of life in his Japan. He showed fashionable people and busy city views. He also painted remote landscapes and scenes from nature. His beautiful bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature. His landscapes reflect the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige sometimes showed his world as it really was. But often, he showed it the way he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona1_adapted: Hiroshige had amazing technical skills as a colorist and draftsman. He felt sympathy for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his time, he came from a samurai family. But he crossed social boundaries to focus on showing popular customs. His work was also affordable. Besides his famous landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that anyone could buy.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition shows prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum. It also includes a major gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a top US collector of Hiroshige's work. Other important loans are part of the show too.

The exhibition explores Hiroshige's amazing body of work. It also looks at his global legacy. That legacy stretches from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) to artists like Vincent van Gogh and modern artists such as Julian Opie.

CuratorialText: A stone figure of 'Hara-Pārvatī' (Śiva seated with Pārvatī on his knee). Also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti'. Accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi) and surrounded by many flying and standing attendants (musicians, garland-bearers), there is also a row of ritual equipment: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp and a bell.
persona1_adapted:
This stone carving shows the Hindu gods Shiva and Parvati. Shiva is seated with Parvati on his knee. This pose is also called "Umamahesvaramurti."

A lion and a bull named Nandi stand nearby. Many attendants surround the gods. Some are flying, and some are standing. They are musicians and garland-bearers.

A row of ritual items appears below. It includes two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp, and a bell.

CuratorialText: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
persona1_adapted: These are a pair of bride's slippers, called *tarkasin*. They are made from red-dyed leather. The slippers are embroidered with silk thread and decorated with metal studs. The tongue of each slipper is cut in the shape of the *khamsa*, which means "five" in Arabic. This shape is also known as the "Hand of Fatima."

CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona1_adapted:
This statue shows the head and upper body of Ramses II. It is made of pink and gray granite. It was one of a pair of statues placed in front of the door of the Ramesseum, a temple built for the king.
