The king wears a royal headdress called a *nemes* head-cloth. He also wears a circlet of uraei—a band of cobras, a symbol of royal power. About half of that circlet is now missing.

The sculptor used the two colors of the stone on purpose. The pink and gray help separate the king’s face from his body. The back pillar of the statue has rows of hieroglyphs carved into it. These symbols give the king’s name and titles. They also include part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra.

In 1817, people noticed traces of color on the statue. It may have been painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText: Head from monumental red granite statue of Amenhotep III(?).
persona1_adapted: This head is from a very large statue. It was made of red granite. The statue probably showed the pharaoh Amenhotep III.

CuratorialText: Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held a large number of priestly offices. Polychrome painted and gilded face, wig, collar and pectoral, winged scarab across breast, body inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities, the rest of the surface is unelaborated. Interior of lid decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. Interior of base also decorated.
persona1_adapted:
This is the base and lid of the inner coffin of a man named Hornedjitef. He was the son of Nekhthorheb and held many important jobs as a priest. The coffin is made of wood and shaped like a human body.

The face, wig, and collar are painted in bright colors and covered with gold. A winged beetle, called a scarab, is shown across the chest. The body is covered with rows of painted hieroglyphs (picture writing). Gods stand on either side of these rows. The rest of the surface is plain.

The inside of the lid shows a figure of the sky goddess Nut. Around her are images of stars and groups of stars called constellations. The inside of the base also has decorations.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.
persona1_adapted:
This head is made of brass, a type of metal. It was created using a special method called "lost wax casting." In this process, a model is first made in wax, then covered in clay. When heated, the wax melts away, leaving a mold. Liquid metal is then poured in to take its shape.

The head is slightly smaller than a real human head. It is made in a naturalistic style, meaning it looks realistic. The head wears a complex headdress that looks like a crown.

The crown has three main layers. The top layer has four flat, round beads. Above them is a red-painted tube-shaped bead and a tassel. The middle layer has a row of vertical rectangle beads, also with tassels. The bottom layer has a row of rosettes painted in red.

Around the forehead, the crown has a curved section made of small tube-shaped beads. These are edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The center of this cover has eighteen vertical lines carved into it, suggesting braided hair or plaitwork. Traces of black paint remain. The bottom and sides of the neck cover have a row of red-painted rosettes.

A crest rises from the front of the crown. It has a cone-shaped roundel with a central bump, surrounded by seven rings that probably represent beads. Behind the roundel, a braided element rises and ends in a pointed oval tip. Both parts have traces of black paint.

The face is slightly long and has vertical carved lines called striations. Two lines of holes run between the earlobes. One line goes through the angle of the neck and jaw. The other crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip.

The eyes are almond-shaped and small compared to the head. The brow ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not carved with lines. There are grooves around the front of the neck that represent skin creases. Holes are pierced through the neck at the front and both sides. There is a large, irregular hole on the right side of the jaw.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.
persona1_adapted:
**Goddess**

This is a standing figure of a female goddess named Tārā. It was made from solid bronze and then covered with a thin layer of gold. She has a tall, tube-shaped hairstyle called a *jatamukuta*. A round medallion holds it in place, and on each side of the medallion are *makaras*—mythical water creatures. The medallion probably had precious stones in it, just like the eyes did.

Her earlobes are long and stretched. She wears a tight cloth around her lower body, tied at the hips. Her upper body is bare. Her right hand is in a pose called *varadamudrā*, which means the gesture of giving. Her left hand is in *katakahastamudrā*, where the tips of her fingers touch her thumb to form a ring, as if she is holding a flower. The two middle fingers of her right hand are missing, and some toes are missing from both feet.

CuratorialText:
The Virgin and child: cartoon; the Virgin half-length, Christ standing with his arms around his mother
Black chalk and/or charcoal, touches of white chalk, pricked and indented for transfer, on two conjoined sheets; framed
persona1_adapted:
**The Virgin and Child: A Drawing**

This is a drawing of the Virgin Mary and the baby Jesus. Mary is shown from the waist up. Jesus stands with his arms around his mother.

The artist used black chalk and charcoal to make the drawing. He added small touches of white chalk for highlights. Tiny holes were pricked along the outlines of the figures. This was a technique to transfer the design onto another surface. The drawing is on two sheets of paper joined together. It is now displayed in a frame.

CuratorialText: Marble torso of Nike, originally winged, wearing a sleeveless chiton; probably part of an akroterion.
persona1_adapted: This marble sculpture shows the upper body of Nike, the Greek goddess of victory. She originally had wings. She wears a sleeveless tunic called a chiton. This piece was probably part of a larger roof decoration, known as an akroterion.

CuratorialText:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.
The small fragment originates from the standing, rectangular snapped off corner palmetto above a volute acroterion. One both sides of the middle leave, which protrudes from the corner, three lightly curved and hollow shaped leaves are visible and whose edges are accompanied by a bar. The round endings were emphasised on the outer side by tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment remains of a curved ovolo moulding of the volute is preserved. The upper and back side show a small pentagonal block with a 45^ (degrees) outstanding, discs-like tunnel. Leaves with a convex back site seem to grow out of the tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels two claws are visible with bird legs on each the edges
On the darts on the left side traces of red paint was found on the uppermost and the third. Traces of red and blue paint.
The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface, however, the underside is fractured.
persona1_adapted:
This is a fragment of a marble corner decoration shaped like a palm leaf, with a bird’s foot.

The piece comes from a larger stone ornament that once stood on the corner of a roof. It was part of a carved plant-like shape called a palmetto, which sat above a spiral scroll (a volute acroterion).

On both sides of the center leaf, which sticks out from the corner, you can see three gently curved, hollow-shaped leaves. A raised line runs along the edges of these leaves. The rounded tips of the leaves were emphasized on the outside with small bumps called tubercles. Dart-shaped carvings appear between the leaves.

On the left side of the fragment, part of a curved molding (an ovolo) from the scroll is still preserved. The top and back of the piece show a small five-sided block with a disc-like tunnel sticking out at a 45-degree angle. Leaves with a curved back seem to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnel, two claws are visible, each with bird legs along the edges.

On the dart-shaped carvings on the left side, traces of red paint were found on the topmost and third darts. There are also traces of red and blue paint.

The light, crystalline marble is fairly smooth on the surface, but the underside is broken and rough.

CuratorialText: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded; wearing floriated crown, tunic, mantle and brooch; seated upon high-backed chair holding sheathed sword; chair back: two horizontal compartments separated by zigzag band; upper filled with interlaced arcading of round arches; lower filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments; chair sides decorated with interlaced patterns.
persona1_adapted:
This chess piece is made from walrus ivory. It shows a king with a beard. He wears a crown decorated with leaf shapes. He also has a tunic, a cloak, and a brooch. The king sits on a high-backed chair. He holds a sword in its case.

The back of the chair has two horizontal sections. A zigzag band separates them. The top section has a pattern of interlaced round arches. The bottom section has matching leaf-like decorations. The sides of the chair are decorated with interlaced patterns.

CuratorialText: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona1_adapted: This is a small Egyptian decoration made from baked clay, called terracotta. It is shaped like a palm leaf and was once the top corner of a miniature altar. The altar was made using a mold, probably in two pieces, but maybe more. The object has a red coating, called a slip, on the inside and outside. There are also signs of burning inside. The clay is brown Nile silt with a light-brown center. It contains a lot of fine gold mica and some white specks.

CuratorialText: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona1_adapted:
This cloth is called *adire eleko*. It is a type of indigo-dyed fabric. The dye was applied using a resist technique, where parts of the cloth are protected from the dye to create patterns.

The cloth is made from factory-made cotton. Two panels of this cotton were sewn together by machine. Each panel is divided into a grid of four squares by seven squares.

The patterns were painted by hand. Two of the squares show the pillars of Mapo Hall, a famous building in Ibadan, Nigeria. Between these pillars are four shapes that look like pestles (tools used for grinding) or spoons.

CuratorialText:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks to see Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery, with image of a creature behind the ticket, with a bat's wing, claw and bone rib cage. 1888
Wood-engraving and letterpress
persona1_adapted: A ticket from 1888 lets A. W. Franks see Falero's painting "Nightmare" at the Gainsborough Gallery. The ticket shows a creature with a bat's wing, a claw, and a rib cage made of bone. It was made using wood-engraving (a print carved into wood) and letterpress (a printing method using raised letters).

CuratorialText:
Ticket to see Frith's painting 'The Railway Station', with central image of a steam train arriving at the station with figures; empty space at the top to add name. 1865
Lithograph
persona1_adapted: This ticket lets you see William Powell Frith's painting *The Railway Station*. The main image shows a steam train arriving at a station. People are waiting on the platform. There is empty space at the top of the ticket for your name. This ticket was made in 1865. It is a lithograph, which is a type of print made by drawing on stone.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photographs of, left to right, Klimt painting, the Leopold Museum and Shiele painting; inscription in black above: multicolour print on white background.
Inscription, in black print on white background.
persona1_adapted: This is a paper admission ticket. It has photographs on it. From left to right, you can see a painting by Klimt, the Leopold Museum, and a painting by Schiele. Above the photos, there is black text printed on a white background. The rest of the ticket has more black text printed on a white background.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photograph, view of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul showing columns and arched roof; inscription to right in black: multicolour print on pink and white background.
Unidentified historical painting showing forces gathered, tree to left: multicolour print on white background.
persona1_adapted:
**Paper admission ticket.**  
This is a ticket made of paper. It lets someone enter a place.

**Photograph of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul.**  
The photo shows tall columns and a curved, arched roof. On the right side, there is black writing. The image is printed in many colors on a pink and white background.

**Unidentified historical painting of gathered forces.**  
This painting shows a group of soldiers or fighters gathered together. A tree is on the left side. The image is printed in many colors on a white background.

CuratorialText:
View of the interior of a tent with visitors gathered before Copley's huge history painting (Guildhall Art Gallery), elaborately framed with pillars and swags, including two young men at left flirting with young women, and two older men at right seated with papers, figures at a desk behind at left; facsimile of the admission ticket to the exhibition, from an engraving by Bartolozzi. c.1850s-60s
Photomechincal reproduction
persona1_adapted: This picture shows the inside of a tent. Visitors are gathered in front of a huge history painting by Copley. The painting has an elaborate frame with pillars and fabric decorations (swags). On the left, two young men are flirting with young women. On the right, two older men sit with papers. Behind them on the left, there are figures at a desk. The image also includes a copy of the admission ticket for the exhibition. This ticket comes from an engraving by Bartolozzi. The picture was made in the 1850s or 1860s. It is a photomechanical reproduction, meaning it was printed using a photographic process.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona1_adapted: This ticket was for a show put on by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left side, you can see the painter Richard Wilson. He is taking a painting to a pawn shop. On the right side is the writer Daniel Defoe. He has the manuscript for *Robinson Crusoe* in his pocket. Defoe is leaving the office of his publisher, Edmund Curll, with a sad look on his face. His worried wife and daughter meet him. In the front of the picture, there are books and a painter's palette with brushes. This artwork was made in 1851, based on a design by Ward. The artist used a mix of methods: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It was printed on a special paper called chine collé.

Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé

On the right side, the writer Daniel Defoe is leaving a publisher's office. He has a sad look on his face. A copy of his book *Robinson Crusoe* sticks out of his pocket. His worried wife and daughter meet him outside. Books and a painter's palette with brushes lie on the ground in front. This artwork was made in 1851. It was created using a mix of methods: etching, aquatint, and engraving. The print is on a special paper called chine collé.

CuratorialText:
Ticket to a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum, with signature by James Beck; am emblem on the top left with the letters VR and a crown above. 1862
Woodcut and letterpress
persona1_adapted: This is a ticket for a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum. It has a signature by James Beck. In the top left corner, there is an emblem with the letters "VR" and a crown above them. The ticket is from 1862. It was made using a woodcut and letterpress.

CuratorialText:
Advertisement for "Aspinall's Enamel"; Princess Alexandra, full length, seated to right, painting a stool with Aspinall's Enamel; illustration to "The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper" (3 December, 1887).
Wood engraving
persona1_adapted: This advertisement shows Princess Alexandra sitting in a chair. She is painting a stool with Aspinall's Enamel. The picture was printed in a newspaper called "The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper" on December 3, 1887. The artist made this image using a wood engraving. That means they carved the picture into a block of wood to print it.

CuratorialText: Large glazed and framed oil painting on canvas depicting a swirling abstract composition dominated by crimson on a dark background. The central bright design has areas of thickly applied paint while in the black background the contracting of the paint surface has produced a textured effect.
persona1_adapted: This large oil painting on canvas is framed and covered with glass. It shows a swirling, abstract design. The main colors are bright red against a dark background. In the center, the paint is very thick. In the black areas, the paint has shrunk as it dried, creating a bumpy texture.

CuratorialText: Painting. Oil painting on canvas. The adoration of the Śālagrāma is shown in the middle of the picture. To the left, is Balakṛṣṇa holding the butter ball. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne which suggests an aniconic reverence to Viṣṇu, also suggested by the large conch positioned centrally in the foreground. Other ritual objects typically used in 'puja' can be seen too: a copper water-pourer and a bell.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. In the center, people are worshipping a sacred stone called a Śālagrāma. On the left, you can see Balakṛṣṇa holding a ball of butter. On the right, a woman holds a parasol over a throne in the middle. This suggests a form of respect for the god Viṣṇu, who is not shown directly. A large conch shell in the front also points to Viṣṇu. You can also see other items used in 'puja' (a Hindu worship ritual): a copper water-pourer and a bell.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; oil painting on canvas in a carved and gilded wood frame. Lady Layard is seated on the balcony of Ca Capello, that Layards' house in Venice, overlooking the Grand Canal. She wears a closely-fitting belted red jacket and a black skirt with an enormously long train; she holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony balustrade. Open French doors set with glass panels give onto an interior with potted plants; in the distance is a view across the Grand Canal.
persona1_adapted:
This is a portrait of Lady Layard, the wife of Henry Layard. It is an oil painting on canvas, set in a carved and gold-covered wooden frame.

In the painting, Lady Layard sits on the balcony of Ca Capello, the Layards' house in Venice. The balcony looks out over the Grand Canal. She wears a tight red jacket with a belt and a long black skirt. The skirt has a very long train that trails behind her. She holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony railing.

Behind her, open French doors with glass panels lead inside the house. You can see potted plants in the room. In the distance, there is a view across the Grand Canal.

CuratorialText: Painting in mixed media (?oil pencil) of a bearded head and shoulders of a male figure. This is one from the series 'Telingana Faces'. The figure wears a square amulet around his neck. Signed and dated by the artist. Mounted
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of a man’s head and shoulders. He has a beard. The artist used mixed media, which means a mix of materials like paint and pencil. This work is part of a series called “Telingana Faces.” The man wears a square amulet, a small charm, around his neck. The artist signed and dated the piece. It is mounted on a backing.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Voltaire (1694-1778), T.Q.L., standing, body nearly in profile to r., head turned half r., and eyes looking full, wearing dark green coat trimmed with gold lace, and holding book in his l. hand.
persona1_adapted: This portrait shows Voltaire (1694–1778). He stands with his body turned slightly to the right. His head faces half-right, and his eyes look straight at the viewer. He wears a dark green coat trimmed with gold lace. In his left hand, he holds a book.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk a Dakota Sans Arc Chief, sitting. Wearing three feathers in his hair, ear and neck -ornaments, decorated shirt with feathers on upper sleeves and blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk.
Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting of a man sitting. He is Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk. He was a chief of the Dakota Sans Arc people. He wears three feathers in his hair. He also has ornaments on his ears and neck. His shirt is decorated with feathers on the upper sleeves. A blanket is wrapped around him. He holds a pipe-tomahawk, which is a tool that combines a smoking pipe and a small axe.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398-1468), H.L., turning half-r., the eyes looking full, wearing a cap, holding a wood block with the letters of the alphabet A-I.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted: This portrait shows Johann Gutenberg, who lived from about 1398 to 1468. He is turned halfway to the right, but his eyes look straight at us. He wears a cap and holds a wooden block. The block has the letters A through I carved into it. The artist painted this picture using oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria (1566-1633), Infanta of Spain, half-length turned slightly to left, wearing white dress trimmed with red rosettes etc., large ruff, and jewelled headdress; after Frans Pourbus II
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria. She was a Spanish princess who lived from 1566 to 1633. In the portrait, she is shown from the waist up, turned slightly to the left. She wears a white dress decorated with red rosettes, a large ruff (a stiff, frilly collar), and a jeweled headdress. This artwork is a copy made after the original by the artist Frans Pourbus II. It is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Augustus II ('The Strong') (1670-1733), King of Poland and Elector of Saxony, three-quarter length, standing, body turned half-right, head half-left, wearing armour and ermine-trimmed cloak, and holding sceptre.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted:
This is a painting of Augustus II, also known as "The Strong." He was the King of Poland and the Elector of Saxony. He lived from 1670 to 1733.

In the portrait, he is shown from the chest up. His body faces slightly to the right, but his head turns a bit to the left. He is standing and wearing armor. Over his armor, he has a cloak trimmed with ermine, which is a type of fur. He is holding a royal scepter.

This artwork is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait said to be of Mary Tudor (1516-58) as Princess; Head and shoulders, 3/4 face, eyes looking full. Wearing ruff, earring, embroidered dress, ?chain, headdress.
Oil painting on canvas
persona1_adapted: This portrait is believed to show Mary Tudor (1516–58) when she was a princess. It shows her head and shoulders, with her body turned slightly to the side. Her eyes look straight at the viewer. She wears a ruff, an earring, an embroidered dress, and a headdress. She may also wear a chain. The artist painted this work with oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Jacques (aka Jean, John, James) Rousseau (1630-93), Oval, head and shoulders turned half-r., eyes looking full, wearing dark coat and lace cravat. Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. It shows a man named Jacques Rousseau, who lived from 1630 to 1693. He is also known as Jean, John, or James. The portrait is oval-shaped. It shows his head and shoulders, turned slightly to the right. His eyes look straight at the viewer. He wears a dark coat and a lace cravat, which is a type of necktie.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Dr Robert G W Anderson. Head, profile, facing to sitter's right
Oil painting on panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio at Talfourd Road, Peckham. Superimposed on the window is a section of the Sloane astrolabe. The tie is a version of the Great Court ties sold at the British Museum around the end of 2000, to mark the opening of the Great Court.
persona1_adapted:
This is a portrait of Dr. Robert G. W. Anderson. It shows his head in profile, facing to the right.

The artist painted it with oil on a wooden panel. Dr. Anderson is looking out of a window in the artist’s studio on Talfourd Road in Peckham. A section of the Sloane astrolabe is placed over the window. An astrolabe is an old tool used to measure the position of stars and planets.

Dr. Anderson’s tie is a special version of the Great Court ties. These ties were sold at the British Museum around the end of the year 2000 to celebrate the opening of the Great Court.

CuratorialText: Oil painting on canvas stretched and glued to back of stained wooden frame. Painting shows scene of an imaginary beach side village with huts and trees in the foreground, ocean and sky with full moon behind. Painting is signed.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. The canvas is stretched and glued to the back of a stained wooden frame. The painting shows a make-believe beach village. In the front, there are huts and trees. Behind them, you can see the ocean and sky. A full moon shines in the sky. The artist signed the painting.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; commercial sign-board made of plywood, used to advertise barbers' shop hairstyles. Six individual heads are depicted in profile, each numbered and displaying a different haircut. The heads face in different directions and are painted in bold black outlines on a white background; each head has a blue collar.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on a plywood sign. The sign was used to advertise haircuts at a barbershop. It shows six heads in profile, meaning we see them from the side. Each head has a different haircut and is numbered. The heads face different directions. They are painted with bold black outlines on a white background. Each head also has a blue collar.

CuratorialText: A large oil painting on canvas depicting the slaying of the dragon by St George who is the central figure of the painting, mounted on a white horse and carrying a circular shield in one hand, a spear in the other. The dragon, with striped skin and small spotted wings, lies on its back beneath the horse's hooves, together with a little blue vanquished devil, his face in profile. To the right of the painting Biruwit is depicted in the branches of a tree; to the left stand a group of soldiers armed with spears and shields.
persona1_adapted:
This large oil painting on canvas shows Saint George killing a dragon. George is the main figure in the painting. He sits on a white horse. In one hand, he holds a round shield. In the other, he holds a spear.

The dragon lies on its back under the horse's hooves. It has striped skin and small spotted wings. A little blue devil lies next to the dragon, with his face shown from the side. He has been defeated.

On the right side of the painting, a figure named Biruwit sits in the branches of a tree. On the left side, a group of soldiers stands. They carry spears and shields.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; wooden frame painted brown; matte of white mesh; at centre is miniature oil painting on white background with brown matte around it; painting shows a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. Written on back: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985"
persona1_adapted: This artwork is an oil painting in a brown wooden frame. A white mesh matte surrounds the center. In the middle is a tiny oil painting on a white background. It shows a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. On the back, there is writing: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985."

CuratorialText:
Painting; pouches made from the skin of small animals; used for carrying tobacco, smoking barks and weeds. plate 13.
Oil on cardboard
persona1_adapted: This painting shows pouches made from the skin of small animals. People used them to carry tobacco, smoking barks, and weeds. The artist painted it with oil on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Painting; three Javanese men; wearing head-dress, shirt and sarong. Man at the front has a kris or sword, secured to his back with a waist wrap. House and trees are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows three Javanese men. They wear head-dresses, shirts, and sarongs (a type of long cloth wrapped around the waist). The man in front has a kris, or traditional sword, secured to his back with a waist wrap. A house and trees are in the background. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait a Javanese woman; wearing head, neck, arm ornament and designed costume. People, trees and two thatched buildings are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a full-length portrait of a Javanese woman. She wears decorated headwear, necklaces, arm ornaments, and a patterned costume. In the background, you can see people, trees, and two thatched buildings. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait of a Javanese man holding a spear; wearing a helmet, striped shirt and leggings and sarong with waist wrap. He carries a kris either side of his waist wrap and a sword hanging on his left side. Trees and buildings, one with a large flag are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This is a full-length painting of a Javanese man. He holds a spear. He wears a helmet, a striped shirt, leggings, and a sarong with a waist wrap. He carries a kris—a type of traditional dagger—on each side of his waist wrap. A sword hangs on his left side. In the background, there are trees and buildings. One building has a large flag. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr; was principal Chief of the Sacs & Fox Nation; wearing headdress with feathers, ear-rings, necklaces, one made of bear claws, shirt and a decorated blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk and fan made of feathers.
Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of Wunagisa, also called Moses Keokuk, Jr. He was the principal chief of the Sac and Fox Nation. In the portrait, he wears a feathered headdress, earrings, and necklaces. One necklace is made of bear claws. He also wears a shirt and a decorated blanket wrapped around him. He holds a pipe-tomahawk and a fan made of feathers. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; from a collection of seventeen. An Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, Victoria; wearing head-band and possum skin cloak.
Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This painting is part of a set of seventeen works. It shows an Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, Victoria. She wears a headband and a cloak made from possum skin. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of a Native North American Chief, name unknown; wearing two feathers in his hair, neck ornament, decorated frill shirt, blanket wrap and holding a Pipe-tomahawk and tobacco pouch.
Oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This is a painting of a Native North American chief. We do not know his name. He wears two feathers in his hair and a neck ornament. His shirt has a decorated frill, and he is wrapped in a blanket. He holds a pipe-tomahawk and a tobacco pouch. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; side view of a Javanese soldier holding a kris and circular shield; wearing head-dress, top garment and serong, with a kris-sheath secure to his back with his waist wrap. People, trees and the top of a building are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a side view of a Javanese soldier. He holds a kris, which is a traditional dagger with a wavy blade, and a round shield. The soldier wears a headdress, a top garment, and a cloth called a serong. He also has a sheath for his kris attached to his back with his waist wrap. In the background, you can see people, trees, and the top of a building. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; five decorated pipe bowls featuring men, bears, and a woman; below is a decorated tomahawk pipe. plate 8.
Oil on cardboard
persona1_adapted: This painting shows five decorated pipe bowls. They feature carvings of men, bears, and a woman. Below them is a decorated tomahawk pipe. This artwork is plate 8. It was made with oil paint on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Framed painting; portrait of Sina Kavaliku, seated on the ground, wearing a western-style dress, her hair in plaits and a flower in her hair. Tonga. 1951.
Oil on canvas.
persona1_adapted: This is a framed painting of a woman named Sina Kavaliku. She is sitting on the ground. She wears a Western-style dress. Her hair is in braids, and she has a flower in her hair. The painting was made in Tonga in 1951. The artist used oil paint on canvas.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona1_adapted:
This is a small pottery container called an alabastron. It was made in ancient Corinth. The potter shaped it on a wheel. The body is round and sits on a curved base. The mouth is small with a slanted rim. A small handle sticks out from the top.

The artist used fine, light-colored clay and a thin coating called slip. The painted decoration turned black when fired. The artist also added purple paint on the animals. Details of the animals were scratched into the clay.

The main band of decoration shows two griffins. A griffin is a mythical creature with the head and wings of an eagle and the body of a lion. A tree-like design separates the two griffins. A lion fills the space between them. Small shapes fill the empty areas around the animals.

Above the main scene is a band of flower-like designs. Below are simpler patterns made of dots and S-shaped lines. The bottom of the vase has simple lines like rays. The lip has tongue-shaped designs.

CuratorialText: IWM holds approximately 11 million photographs of modern conflict from the First World War to present day.
persona1_adapted: IWM has about 11 million photos. These pictures show modern wars, from World War I to today.

CuratorialText: The collection covers the activities of British and Commonwealth forces and international perspectives. Photographs from official, press, and private perspectives such as soldiers, nurses and civilians who participated in or witnessed conflict reveal the human impact of war. The collection includes notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt and Tim Hetherington.
persona1_adapted: This collection shows the activities of British and Commonwealth forces, as well as views from around the world. The photos come from official sources, the press, and private individuals. Soldiers, nurses, and civilians who took part in or saw conflict took these pictures. Their images reveal how war affects people. The collection includes famous photographers like Olive Edis, Bill Brandt, and Tim Hetherington.

CuratorialText: The photographs can be viewed alongside original documents which help us understand how and why these photographs were taken.
persona1_adapted: The photographs are displayed with original documents. These documents help us learn how and why the photos were taken.

CuratorialText:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges and Insignia
An important and international collection of flags and uniforms, the latter representing all of the armed services and institutions of not only Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but Allies as well as former enemies along with their associated badges and insignia.
persona1_adapted:
**Uniforms, Flags, Badges, and Insignia**

This is a large and important collection from around the world. It includes flags and uniforms from many different countries. The uniforms come from all branches of the military and other official groups. They represent not only Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but also allies and former enemies. Each uniform has its own badges and insignia, which are special symbols that show rank or membership.

CuratorialText:
Mementoes and Miscellanea
A diverse collection containing objects of personal significance, including escape and evasion items, prisoner of war and civilian internee items, trench art, battlefield souvenirs, commemorative objects, foodstuffs, currency, tobacciana, toys and games, Kindertransport and Holocaust related items, home-front and peace-time items.
persona1_adapted:
**Mementoes and Miscellanea**

This collection holds many different kinds of objects. Each one has a personal story. You will find items used to escape or avoid capture. There are things made by prisoners of war and civilians held in camps. You can see "trench art"—objects soldiers made from war materials. There are also souvenirs from battlefields and items made to remember people or events. The collection includes food, money, and smoking items. Toys and games are here too. Some objects relate to the Kindertransport, which saved children from the Holocaust. Others show life on the home front or during peacetime.

CuratorialText:
Orders, Medals and Decorations
An important collection comprising orders, medal and decorations issued to service personnel and civilians.
persona1_adapted:
**Orders, Medals, and Decorations**

This is a major collection of awards given to both military members and civilians. It includes orders, medals, and decorations.

CuratorialText:
Equipment
An extensive collection of equipment used in twentieth and twenty-first-century warfare, including radios, cipher machines, radars, cameras, medical equipment and even pigeon baskets.
persona1_adapted:
**Equipment**

This section shows a wide range of tools used in wars from the 1900s to today. You'll find radios, code-making machines, radar systems, cameras, medical supplies, and even baskets for carrier pigeons.

CuratorialText:
Weapons and Ammunition
A major reference collection that includes firearms, edged weapons, trench clubs or concussive weapons, ordnance and munitions.
persona1_adapted:
**Weapons and Ammunition**

This is a large collection of reference items. It includes guns, bladed weapons, trench clubs (used for hitting), and other military supplies like bombs and bullets.

CuratorialText:
Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships
An internationally renowned collection including models, battle tanks, maritime equipment and aircraft components. Most of the collection of artillery and large military equipment is on show at IWM London or IWM Duxford.
persona1_adapted:
**Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships**

This collection is famous around the world. It includes models, battle tanks, ship equipment, and aircraft parts. Most of the artillery and large military equipment is on display at IWM London or IWM Duxford.

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.
persona1_adapted: IWM’s Sound Collection holds over 35,000 recordings about conflicts since 1914. It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world. Oral history means people tell their own stories out loud. The collection has more than 60,000 hours of material. It includes interviews in over 15 languages. Besides interviews, it also has speeches, sound effects, radio shows, and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of everyday people. These include soldiers, activists, factory workers, and journalists. It also features famous people like Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and singer Vera Lynn. Our unique collection of oral history interviews has helped with major academic research. It has also been used in bestselling books, films, TV shows, radio programs, and podcasts. You can listen to many thousands of these recordings on the IWM website. The site adds new recordings regularly.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
persona1_adapted:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over 100 years. From the very start, the museum knew that film was an important primary source—a firsthand record of events. This large collection shows the causes, course, and consequences of conflict from World War I to today. It is a valuable resource for researchers and filmmakers.

Our collection keeps growing. It now has over 25,000 hours of moving images. This includes a wide range of material: public and service information films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two large collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. A film digitisation programme—converting old film into digital files—gives free online access. Newly-digitised media is added as soon as it becomes available.

CuratorialText:
Private Papers
IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.
persona1_adapted:
**Private Papers**

IWM keeps more than 20,000 private paper collections. These are mostly unpublished diaries, letters, and memoirs. British and Commonwealth soldiers and nurses wrote them from 1914 onward. Civilians wrote them mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from people who survived or were victims of the Holocaust.

CuratorialText:
Foreign and Related Documents
We also look after a large collection of foreign documents, including the official British records of the major war crimes trials conducted at Nuremberg and Tokyo, together with related documents from the Second World War which mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.
persona1_adapted:
**Foreign and Related Documents**

We also take care of a large collection of foreign documents. This includes the official British records from the major war crimes trials held in Nuremberg and Tokyo. It also includes related documents from World War II. These mostly deal with the German and Japanese war efforts.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.
persona1_adapted: This collection is one of the most important displays of 20th-century British art anywhere in the world. It includes many great works from British government war art programs during World War I and World War II. These programs hired the best artists of the time, including leaders of the avant-garde—artists who tried new, bold styles. Some of these artists were Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent, and Sir William Orpen. IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive. This archive shows how the war art programs worked day to day. It also gives fascinating glimpses into the artists' experiences.

CuratorialText: Our Library collection consists of over 150,000 items covering twentieth and twenty-first century conflict involving British and Commonwealth countries. Along with material on military units and campaign histories, we have biographies, manuals, items relating to the economic, social, and cultural aspects of war, journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and e-resources.
persona1_adapted: Our library has more than 150,000 items. They cover wars from the 1900s to today that involved Britain and Commonwealth countries. We have materials about military groups and battle histories. You can also find biographies, manuals, and items about the economic, social, and cultural sides of war. There are journals, newspapers, short-lived printed items (ephemera), propaganda, and online resources.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona1_adapted:
**The Sinking of HMS Laurentic**

This painting shows the moment HMS Laurentic was sinking. Half of the ship is underwater, and its front end (the bow) sticks up into the air. In the front of the painting, a lifeboat filled with sailors glows in the dark, lit by a small light. Another lifeboat appears on the right, and tiny dots of light in the background show more lifeboats. On the left, you can see the top of the German submarine (called a U-boat) that attacked the ship.

HMS Laurentic started as a passenger ship for the P&O shipping company. In 1939, it was fitted with 6-inch guns and turned into an armed merchant cruiser—a warship that also carried cargo. This was the first ship the artist, Frank Worsley, served on. He stayed with it until it was sunk off the coast of Foreland, Ulster, by German U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer on the night of November 3, 1940.

Worsley painted the first version of this scene right after the attack, while on leave as a survivor. The War Artists' Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought that painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America as part of a traveling show of war art. But on the way, the ship carrying it was torpedoed and the painting was lost. So, at the WAAC's request, Worsley painted this replacement.

CuratorialText:
image: a canopy of camouflage nets suspended over a road that leads to a cluster of industrial buildings painted with a camouflage patters. A group of figures gather in the background.
History note
War Artists Advisory Committee purchase

A canopy of camouflage nets hangs over a road. The road leads to a group of industrial buildings painted with a camouflage pattern. In the background, a group of people gather.

**History Note:**
The War Artists Advisory Committee bought this artwork.

CuratorialText:
image: a coastal radar station with the radar equipment and masts in the centre background. In the foreground are several buildings with personnel in dark uniforms milling around outside. The sea is visible in the background to the left.

War Artists Advisory Committee purchase

CuratorialText: A rough for a furniture mural. whole: text is split between left, middle and right side, with separate images associated with each. image: (left) blueprint illustrating furniture with aircraft, tanks and ships in background. In the middle, different types of furniture are arranged over a house blueprint. Factories are in the background.

The text reads: "FROM WAR PRODUCTION THROUGH AUSTERITY TO BETTER TIMES."

CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.
persona1_adapted:
**The Artwork and Its Story**

This drawing shows a view down a tree-lined road. On one side, there is a high wall next to a railway cutting. The wall has graffiti that reads "Nuclear state = Police state." It also has an anarchy symbol and the words "One World." Behind the wall on the right is a large industrial building. Further right, there is a road junction with a road leading off to the left.

**The Artist's Experience**

In the summer of 1983, the artist Eric Rimmington discovered an old train station in Mildmay Grove. He started making ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. While working on the second drawing, he realized something important. The train line he was drawing was used several times a week to carry nuclear waste. The waste came from Sizewell nuclear power station and went to Sellafield, a nuclear fuel reprocessing plant.

Over the next six weeks, Rimmington could not stop thinking about this fact. He said it became mixed up with every mark he made in his drawings.

**The Historical Context**

Rimmington made these drawings during a time of strong anti-nuclear protests. People were protesting against both nuclear weapons and nuclear power plants.

In 1979, the British Government decided to allow American Cruise Missiles to be placed in the UK. This decision sparked many marches and protests. One famous protest was the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which started in 1981. It was at its peak in 1983, when the missiles finally arrived at the base in Berkshire.

Also in 1979, a serious accident happened at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA. This accident made people even more worried about the safety of nuclear power.

**The Issue Today**

Nuclear waste, and how it is transported and stored, is still a major concern. People continue to protest about it. In fact, in 2001, the Greater London Assembly ordered a report on the safety of nuclear waste trains. This shows that the issue remains important even today.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).
persona1_adapted:
**Object Description**  
Five kids sit around a table in a room lit by a big window. An older woman sits in the middle with the youngest child on her lap. A large pudding called "Spotted-Dick" sits on the table, with bowls and forks around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right, who holds out his bowl for a portion.

**Label**  
This is Mrs. Norris from Cookham. She is taking care of five children from London. These kids are part of a "war family" of seven children. By January 1941, they had been living with her for sixteen months.  

Mrs. Norris is an ideal foster parent. Many other homeowners like her also took in children during the war. Over a million kids were moved from dangerous areas in school groups. With so many children, finding homes for them mainly meant placing them with local families.  

Thanks to Mrs. Norris's kindness and care, city kids quickly got used to their new surroundings.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. Knight was fascinated by circus artistes and dancers, and she emphasises the balance and posture of her subject at work. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment.
persona1_adapted:
This painting shows a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a large machine called a lathe. She was known as "an outstanding factory worker." The War Artist's Advisory Committee wanted her to be painted.

The artist, Laura Knight, expected to paint a portrait in her studio. But the Ministry of Supply asked her to paint Loftus at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport, Wales. Loftus was making a part for a Bofors gun called a breech ring. This was the most skilled job in the factory. Normally, it took eight or nine years of training to learn.

Loftus was only 21 years old and had never worked in a factory before. Her ability to run the machine was a big win for publicity. However, some people think she was placed at that machine just for the painting.

Industrial machinery was new for Knight. But experts praised her for getting the technical details right. Just like Loftus, Knight was proving herself in a job that was usually done by men.

Knight loved painting circus performers and dancers. In this portrait, she focuses on Loftus’s balance and posture while she works.

CuratorialText:
image: a skeletal Death drives a bulldozer through the rubble, debris and detritus of war. Bricks, metal struts and tyres litter the ground and there is a flaming object on the right.
Label
This is a fantasy of utter destruction, as death rides in on a bulldozer. Only the flags stand tall, criss-crossing but ultimately adding to the confusion. The only architectural remains are bricks and girders. Michael Sandle is primarily a sculptor and has produced a number of public monuments. A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.
persona1_adapted:
**Image Description:**  
A skeleton representing Death drives a bulldozer through the wreckage of war. The ground is covered with bricks, metal beams, and tires. On the right, something is on fire.

**Label:**  
This picture shows a wild fantasy of total destruction. Death rides in on a bulldozer. Only flags stand tall, but they cross each other and add to the mess. The only building parts left are bricks and metal beams.  

Michael Sandle is mainly a sculptor. He has made several public monuments. A big theme in his work is war. This comes from his own early life. He lived through air raids in Plymouth and later served in the military. This print is one of six in a series called *The Driver*.

CuratorialText: image: a view through a few trees across the large field of crosses of the British military cemetery at Etaples on the French coast. The coastline is bordered by trees and a sailing boat is visible in the English Channel.
persona1_adapted: Through a few trees, you can see a huge field of crosses. This is the British military cemetery at Étaples on the coast of France. The coastline is lined with trees, and a sailing boat is visible in the English Channel.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.
persona1_adapted:
On October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—sailed near the coast of Turkey. They were heading to Leros, a Greek island held by British troops. On board the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, a high-ranking officer on his way to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships also carried soldiers, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 p.m., the *Eclipse* hit a mine (an underwater explosive) between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The ship blew up and sank in just two minutes. Besides its crew of about 200 men, the *Eclipse* was carrying another 200 soldiers. Only around 100 people survived. They were rescued by an RAF air-sea rescue boat from Leros and by boats from HMS *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the water.

General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He wrote about this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.

**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows a view across the River Spree in Berlin. The sun is rising over the city in the background. On the far bank, you can see fortifications and an observation post. A boat is tied up below them. On the near side, a British military policeman stands with a vehicle.

CuratorialText: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona1_adapted:
This drawing shows twisted metal beams of all sizes. It explores how bombs changed the shapes of buildings. The fast, jagged lines and forms make the explosion's violence clear.

Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol. She returned there during World War II after living in Paris for over 10 years as an artist. She never got an official job as a war artist. This was because of her left-wing political views and her abstract style. But with help from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she got a permit to draw the city's ruined buildings.

CuratorialText: image: In the foreground two sailors sit with their backs to the viewer on the deck of a ship. A third sailor stands behind them leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun protrudes out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead of them and clouds of smoke rising from the hills in the distance. The sky is dotted with barrage balloons. The artist has also made several other sketches of ships and sailors in the upper right corner of the image.
persona1_adapted: In the front of the picture, two sailors sit on a ship's deck with their backs to us. A third sailor stands behind them, leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun sticks out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead. They also see clouds of smoke rising from the hills far away. The sky is filled with barrage balloons—large balloons tied to ropes that help protect against enemy planes. In the upper right corner, the artist added several small sketches of ships and sailors.

CuratorialText:

Short notes from the time. On the back of the photo, it says: "Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941."

CuratorialText: image: Against a gloomy sunset and upon a grassy mound stands a smoking but unmanned French 75mm field gun, facing left. Below running the length of the composition lies the detritus of battle: a broken limber, four German corpses and two dead mules.

CuratorialText: Powder paint painting, hinged in corners onto a single sheet of white board. Bottom left hinge has previously been cut to allow access to object number. Neatly torn along top and right edges, rather than cut. Previously folded in half vertically.
