CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.
persona1_adapted: Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) is known as one of the greatest landscape painters ever. He knew Oxford very well. As a child, he made many drawings and watercolors of the city's streets, colleges, and pretty views. His famous oil painting of Oxford's High Street is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To celebrate the 250th anniversary of Turner's birth on April 23, 1775, explore this selection of his Oxford pictures from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room holds over 100 watercolors and drawings by Turner, plus many landscape etchings. You'll find lively studies of animals, birds, and fish, as well as beautiful views of places he visited in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona1_adapted:
Around 2000 BCE, the island of Crete was home to a group of people called the Minoans. They lived there long before the ancient Greeks. In 1900, an archaeologist named Sir Arthur Evans began digging at a site called Knossos on Crete. As he worked, he often linked the objects he found to old Cretan myths.

Evans named the large building at Knossos the "Palace of Minos." He chose this name after a mythical king of Crete. He also connected the building to the story of the Labyrinth. In the myth, King Minos ordered a clever maze called the Labyrinth to be built. Its purpose was to trap the Minotaur, a fierce creature with a bull's head and a man's body.

The hero Theseus, who was half-god, killed the Minotaur. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece. He became king and founded the city of Athens. The Cretan myths came from stories that Greek and Roman people living in Knossos told long after the Palace had been destroyed.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona1_adapted:
Landscape painting has a long history in East Asian art. But landscape scenes did not become a big part of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By then, Japanese people were going on more outdoor trips, like visiting temples or taking boat rides. There was a growing demand for souvenir pictures. In the competitive publishing world, companies were always looking for new products. Landscape prints were a fresh and exciting idea.

Technical changes also helped landscape prints become popular. A key factor was a new blue pigment called Prussian Blue, which came from overseas. Unlike older Japanese blues that faded quickly, this bright color gave artists more freedom. They could now show sky and water in new ways.

Here we show a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige. We also include selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.
persona1_adapted:
The museum's collection of ancient objects is famous around the world. It includes items from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, and the Minoan, Mycenaean, and Classical Greek cultures. It also covers Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, and Medieval and later Europe. The collection holds the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also has one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections of casts in the UK. Casts are copies made from molds of Greek and Roman sculptures.

CuratorialText: In our Eastern Art collections you will find sculpture, textiles, ceramics and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India and the Himalayas.
persona1_adapted: Our Eastern Art collection includes sculptures, textiles, ceramics, and paintings. These artworks come from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India, and the Himalayas.

CuratorialText: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the leading international coin cabinets with particular strengths in the fields of Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic and Chinese coinages. It also holds collections of paper money, tokens, jetons and commemorative art medals.
persona1_adapted: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the world’s top coin collections. It is especially strong in Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic, and Chinese coins. The room also holds collections of paper money, tokens, jetons (small coin-like tokens used for counting), and commemorative art medals.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.
persona1_adapted: The Western Art collections feature amazing European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to today. These include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolors, and prints. You'll also find English and European ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings, and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's print collection is exceptional. It holds about 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints. Many of these are available to see in our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.
persona1_adapted:
This painting started as an important early job. Turner was asked to make two watercolors. They would be turned into engravings for a yearly calendar called the *Oxford Almanack*. The calendar was published by the University Press.

The watercolors were so successful that Turner made ten of them between 1798 and 1804. Later, he painted this work and one other in oils. He did this for a local print-seller and frame-maker named James Wyatt.

Turner asked Wyatt for advice on the details of the buildings in the scene. He also added people to the painting. These included members of the university, clergy, and some women. Turner said he added the women "for the sake of color."

This painting is one of Turner's best-documented works. That is because Wyatt kept the letters Turner sent him. Those letters were sold after Wyatt died.

The original goal of the job was to make an engraving from the design. But Wyatt decided to buy an oil painting instead of a watercolor. It was half the size of Turner's usual canvases. The cost was 100 guineas.

The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. In the 1830s, Turner chose views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolors. He did not pick the High Street. He felt that in this painting, he had reached a level of technical skill he could not match again.

CuratorialText: Together with a St Francis (Christ Church, Oxford) and a Risen Christ (National Gallery of Wales), WA1850.13 and WA1850.13.1 come from a lost altar-piece by Granacci, painted around 1520. The four panels may have been placed in the pilasters. Granacci was a follower of Ghirlandaio who was particularly interested in the art of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.
persona1_adapted: These four paintings were once part of a larger altar-piece. An altar-piece is a work of art placed behind an altar in a church. The artist, Granacci, painted them around the year 1520. The panels may have been set into the pilasters, which are the flat columns on the sides of the altar. Granacci was a follower of the artist Ghirlandaio. He was also very interested in the work of two other artists: Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.

CuratorialText: These are pinnacle panels that once belonged to the same unknown altar-piece, which may have been crowned with a central pinnacle panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was Florentine, working in a late Gothic manner and an attribution to Bartolomeo di Fruosino has recently been suggested. A painter and miniaturist, Bartolomeo was a pupil of Agnolo Gaddi.
persona1_adapted: These are pointed panels that once belonged to the same unknown altar-piece. The top of the altar-piece may have had a central panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was from Florence, Italy, and worked in a late Gothic style. Experts recently suggested that the artist might be Bartolomeo di Fruosino. He was a painter and a miniaturist, which means he created small, detailed paintings. Bartolomeo was a student of Agnolo Gaddi.

CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.
persona1_adapted:
**The Hunt in the Forest: A Nighttime Adventure**

*The Hunt in the Forest* is a very original painting. It shows a nighttime scene and has a clever, well-planned design. In the past, hunting was a fancy activity for rich people, with its own special rules. But the idea of a hunt at night is playful. It might be more about symbolism—meaning a deeper idea—than about real life.

To create this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first drew a grid on the wooden panel. He placed a single vanishing point in the center. This point pulls our eyes deep into the scene. In painting the hunt, he added details like the hunters' spears, cut branches, logs, and water in the front. These elements help focus our attention on the space and on the men and animals moving away into the distance.

Gold specks in the tree leaves would have matched the bright colors of the figures against the dark forest. When lit by candlelight, the gold would have sparkled and seemed to come alive.

Paolo di Dono (1397–1475) was famous in his time as a master of perspective, animals, and landscapes. His nickname, Uccello (which means "Bird"), refers to his skill at painting nature. He was a versatile designer who also worked on mosaics and stained glass. He combined all these talents to create this masterpiece.

*The Hunt* is a type of painting called a *spalliera*. This means it was mounted at shoulder height. It could have been the backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden wall panels of a room. We don't know who first bought this painting, but it was clearly made for a Renaissance palace. It might have been in Urbino, where Uccello worked from 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: The portrait corresponds generally to a number of paintings and prints of Louix XIII of France on a prancing horse. From the type and handling of the paint, it seems to be either by or after a Flemish painter. The appearance of the king suggests a date of about 1622.
persona1_adapted: This portrait looks a lot like several paintings and prints that show King Louis XIII of France riding a horse that is prancing. Based on how the paint is used and applied, it was likely made by a Flemish painter, or by someone copying that painter's style. The way the king looks in the painting suggests it was made around 1622.

CuratorialText: The Magdalen is recognisable by her jar of ointment, a reference to her traditional identification with the woman who anointed the feet of Christ and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). Possibly cut from a larger composition, this small panel has been enlarged by being fitted into another and the figure extended. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 17th century in the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.
persona1_adapted: The Magdalen is easy to spot because she holds a jar of ointment. This refers to the woman who anointed Christ’s feet and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). This small painting may have been cut from a larger scene. Later, someone added extra wood to make it bigger and extended the figure. The artist likely worked in Parma in the early 1600s. He was part of the group around Bartolomeo Schedoni.

CuratorialText: The landscape is reminiscent of Éragny. It may have been painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, before Félix Pissarro left Éragny for Belgium. In: Jon Whiteley, Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum, (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum 2022)
persona1_adapted: This landscape looks like the area around Éragny. The artist may have painted it in the summer of 1893 or 1894. That was just before Félix Pissarro left Éragny to go to Belgium.

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.
persona1_adapted:
This painting is a free copy of a fresco. A fresco is a type of wall painting made on wet plaster. The original fresco is in Rome, in a room called the Loggia di Psiche. It was painted by Raphael and his workshop in 1518-19. The room shows scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche.

This copy was made in the late 1600s or early 1700s. It copies a part of the ceiling called a spandrel. A spandrel is the curved space between two arches. The original spandrel might have been painted by Raphael's main assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist changed the shape of the painting from a curve to a square.

CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
persona1_adapted:
Three objects in this still life also show up in other paintings from Renoir’s later years. The silver cup appears with a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted in Cagnes in 1910. It also appears in a similar painting without the knife, also from 1910. The bronze-colored child statue on the column was included in a small study dated around 1905 by the Daubervilles.

The double salt holder, made of faience (a type of glazed pottery), was produced in large numbers in the 1800s at Desvres in Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany. It appears in at least four other sketches. A blurry image of the same figure, seen from behind, floats in the upper right corner. This is probably a reflection in a mirror.

Renoir worked as a painter at a pottery factory early in his career. He seems to have owned several pieces of this popular type of faience. He used many different colored and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Although damaged, this portrait of an unknown woman is of fine quality and has affinities with the work of Andrea in the 1520s. Apart from a short stay in Rome c.1516, Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and in Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.
persona1_adapted: This portrait of an unknown woman is damaged, but it is still very well made. It shares similarities with the work of the artist Andrea in the 1520s. Andrea spent most of his career in the Italian cities of Siena and Florence. He did take one short trip to Rome around 1516. In Florence, he worked together with his brother, Raffaello Piccinelli.

CuratorialText: After 1739, Ruysch painted mainly small-scale paintings of bouquets and posies, inscribed with her age, as here. The arrangement of curving stems, full, rounded roses and a number of smaller flowers looks back to the work of Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has not been recovered
persona1_adapted: After 1739, Ruysch mostly painted small bouquets and posies. She often wrote her age on them, like in this painting. The curved stems, full roses, and smaller flowers are inspired by the artist Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has never been found.

CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona1_adapted:
This panel was once part of a larger artwork called an altarpiece. An altarpiece is a painting or sculpture placed behind a church altar. This one was made between 1433 and 1435 for a church in Florence, Italy. Today, its pieces are spread across different museums.

The center of the altarpiece showed the Virgin Mary, baby Jesus, and four angels. On each side were pairs of saints. Saints are holy people in Christianity. The saints included Benedict, Nicholas of Bari, John the Baptist, and an Evangelist (a writer of the Gospels), probably Saint Matthew.

The bottom part, called the predella, showed scenes from the life of Saint Nicholas. Five of these scenes are still known today. They include: his birth, pilgrims at his shrine, him bringing three youths back to life, him giving money to three sisters (shown as three gold balls, which became his symbol), and him calming a storm.

The panels probably had frames shaped like four-leaf clovers, called quatrefoils. The artist, Bicci, based this panel's design on an earlier altarpiece by Gentile da Fabriano from 1425.

CuratorialText: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind. He holds a terracotta figure of a saint, perhaps the Virgin Mary. The painting has been tentatively attributed to the circle of the de Critz family.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of a professional sculptor. He is in his studio, and some of his work sits on a shelf behind him. He holds a small clay figure of a saint. It might be the Virgin Mary. Experts think this painting was made by someone in the de Critz family’s workshop.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems bear dates of Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few identify the source from which the poem came or where the poem was composed. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.
persona1_adapted: These calligraphy pages were all written by the same person. Most of them include *kanshi*, which are poems written in Chinese. The pages are signed with different versions of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems have dates: Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880). A few others say where the poem came from or where it was written. If the calligraphy was made at the same time as the paintings, then this album would be an early work by Sobun. It was created before he started showing his art in national and international expositions.

CuratorialText: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.
persona1_adapted:
This painting started in 1857, when it was shown as *The Mother's Grave*. The first version, known from a drawing at the Ashmolean Museum, showed a boy grieving alone at his mother's grave.

The artist began the landscape in the summer of 1856. He painted it in an old churchyard in Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes changed the background. He also added the figure of the sister. The artist's wife, Tryphena, posed for this figure.

The small details in the painting make the sad feeling stronger. Spider webs, dew drops, dog roses, and dandelion seeds all remind us that life is short. The boy's loss is also shown by a lamb separated from its mother by the tomb.

CuratorialText: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) succeeded his father as Keeper of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace and further enriched the family collection of specimens of natural and man-made curiosity. He is shown here with his friend, Roger Friend, a brewer (for which Zythepsa is the Greek) from Lambeth, who may have contributed the shells to Tradescant's museum.
persona1_adapted: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) took over his father's job as Keeper of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace. He added many more items to the family's collection of natural and man-made wonders. This painting shows him with his friend, Roger Friend, a brewer from Lambeth. (The Greek word for brewing is "Zythepsa.") Friend may have given the shells that were part of Tradescant's museum.

CuratorialText: As one of the leading members of post-war generation of St Ives artists, Lanyon was primarily interested in the representation of landscape. This work epitomises the tension between abstraction and figuration in an otherwise carefully descriptive work.
persona1_adapted: Lanyon was a key artist in the group that worked in St Ives after World War II. He mainly focused on showing the landscape. This painting captures the push and pull between abstract and realistic styles, even though the work is otherwise carefully detailed.

CuratorialText: Bundi, Rajasthan, c.1760-70. Gouache with gold on paper. Attended by a drowsy maid, a young noble couple sleep in close embrace on a palace terrace, sheltered by a shamiana or awning. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie close to hand.
persona1_adapted: A young noble couple sleeps in a close embrace on a palace terrace. A drowsy maid attends to them. They are sheltered by a shamiana, which is a type of awning or canopy. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie nearby, ready for use. This painting is from Bundi, Rajasthan, made around 1760-70. It uses gouache, a type of watercolor paint, with gold on paper.

CuratorialText: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil. However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.
persona1_adapted:
This painting was once called *A Nun Playing a Piano* when it was sold in 1919 and again in 1936. That title was likely used because the woman in the painting wears a long veil. But there is no other reason to think she is a nun.

In fact, this is almost certainly a portrait of the artist’s daughter, Desirée-Marie Ribot, who was known as Louise. The artist often used her as a model. She was born in 1857, and her young appearance helps date the painting. Based on the style, it was likely made in the late 1860s or early 1870s. That was before the artist’s brushwork became thicker and more textured.

The dark background and the simple, everyday scene remind us of the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887). Bonvin was an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911). Both artists shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.

CuratorialText: The inscription on this painting reads ‘Wind from the west is blowing through layers of curtains on doors, the proud [chrysanthemum] are falling but their beauty remains. In fading moonlight I [stayed] with these flowers while they cooled, versing through this cool evening in autumn.’
persona1_adapted: The inscription on this painting says: "Wind from the west blows through layers of curtains on doors. The proud chrysanthemums are falling, but their beauty stays. In fading moonlight, I stayed with these flowers while they cooled. I wrote poems through this cool autumn evening."

CuratorialText: Sinnott is well known for his complex figurative compositions. He often deals with the human figure in a context. Although the same fluid brush-strokes can be seen in the background and in the figures, the two wrestling men can be distinguished by a lighter touch and a slight variation in the use of paint. The underpainting, in places, appears through the upper layers, accentuating the sense of speed in the painting process.
persona1_adapted: Sinnott is known for his detailed paintings of people. He often places the human figure in a setting. You can see the same smooth brushstrokes in both the background and the figures. But the two wrestling men stand out because of a lighter touch and a small difference in how the paint is used. In some spots, the first layer of paint shows through the top layers. This highlights how quickly the painting was made.

CuratorialText: The only securely documented painting by the artist is an altar-piece of 1447 now in the Museo Civico, Padua. A follower of Michele Giambono, Francesco may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Together with WA1850.10, this panel was once part of an unknown altar-piece possibly for a church in Padua or Venice. St Catherine, of royal birth, wears a crown and carries her martyr's palm; the wheel refers to earlier attempts at her martyrdom.
persona1_adapted: The only painting we can be sure was made by this artist is an altar-piece from 1447. It is now in the Museo Civico in Padua, Italy. Francesco was a follower of Michele Giambono. He may have worked with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. This panel, along with WA1850.10, was once part of an unknown altar-piece. It might have been made for a church in Padua or Venice. St. Catherine was born into a royal family. She wears a crown and carries a palm branch, which is a symbol of her martyrdom. The wheel in the painting refers to earlier attempts to kill her.

CuratorialText: The grief-stricken figures of the Virgin and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful devotional image; it may have been the right wing of a portable diptych, although no hinge-marks are now visible. The anonymous artist was very close to Jacopo di Cione (to whom WA1921.3 has also been attributed) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in mid 14th-century Florence
persona1_adapted: The sad faces of Mary and St. John the Evangelist make this small painting a strong image for prayer. It might have been the right side of a folding two-panel artwork called a diptych, but no hinge marks are left today. The unknown artist worked very closely with Jacopo di Cione (who is also thought to have made WA1921.3). This artist likely trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, who was the top artist in Florence during the mid-1300s.

CuratorialText: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
persona1_adapted: In 1573, the artist Veronese painted a famous version of the Last Supper for a dining hall in a monastery in Venice. The religious authorities, called the Inquisition, questioned him about it. They thought the painting was disrespectful, or blasphemous. To solve the problem, Veronese changed the title to *The Feast in the House of Levi*. This new subject allowed him to include many colorful details from a Bible story (Luke 6:27-30). Many copies of this large painting exist. The original is now in the Accademia museum in Venice. Our painting, WA1855.159, is a smaller, high-quality copy. It was made in the mid-1700s by a Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.

CuratorialText: A companion to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764-1815), WA1934.422. Both paintings were exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1800.
persona1_adapted: This painting is a companion to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764–1815), which is also in the museum's collection (WA1934.422). Both artworks were shown at the Royal Academy in 1800.

CuratorialText: Painted in 1886 at a time when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were working with the pointillist technique. The view, taken from a bend on the path leading from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte from Éragny to Bazincourt, includes the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny in the centre with the sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte runs along the line of trees that crosses the composition. In 1887 Lucien painted an almost identical view in watercolour and bodycolour. The buildings seen from this stretch of the path are now largely concealed by the trees. However, it is clear that in both works Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor and suppressed the farm buildings that lie in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more improvised style of the early 1880s.
persona1_adapted:
Camille Pissarro and his son Lucien both painted this scene in 1886. They were using a technique called pointillism. In pointillism, artists make a picture with many small dots of color.

The view shows a bend in the path from Pré du Chêne to Bazincourt. The path crosses the Epte River near the village of Éragny. In the center of the painting, you can see the spire of the Saint-Martin church in Éragny. On the right is a brick manor house from the 1500s. The Epte River runs along the line of trees that cuts across the painting.

In 1887, Lucien painted an almost identical view using watercolor and bodycolor (a type of thick paint). Today, the buildings seen from this part of the path are mostly hidden by trees. But we can tell that in both works, Lucien made the church and manor much bigger. He also left out the farm buildings that stand in front of the manor.

Lucien probably knew that his father had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884. Camille used a looser, more improvised style back then, typical of his early 1880s work.

CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.
persona1_adapted:
This painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in southern France. On the left, you can see its famous 1700s castle and garden terrace. The Petit Luberon mountains rise up in the background.

The artist, Paul Guigou, sat or stood on a sandbank to paint this scene. During the dry season, the river Durance splits into many channels between the villages of Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron. Sandbanks appear between these channels.

Like most of Guigou’s landscapes from Provence, the air is clear and the sunlight is strong. Other artists like Corot or Daubigny may have influenced his choice of a wide, panoramic view. Guigou seemed to prefer this type of scene. But his painting style is unique.

He painted the sky, sand, and river with thin layers of paint. Then he added life to the surface with small, thick dabs of paint and short strokes made with the tip of his brush. This was his special technique.

CuratorialText: This painting, which is a cosmic landscape with Daoist undertones, was produced in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003) who wished for something with a predominantly blue colour. The inscription reads ‘To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.’
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a cosmic landscape with ideas from Daoism, a Chinese philosophy about living in harmony with nature. It was made in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003), who asked for something mostly blue. The writing on the painting says, “To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.”

CuratorialText: One of Lucien's most extrovert works, painted in June 1925 and illustrating the broom in full flower at Le Brusq.
persona1_adapted: This is one of Lucien's most outgoing works. He painted it in June 1925. It shows the broom plant in full bloom at Le Brusq.

CuratorialText: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) was educated at Oriel College, Oxford, before entering the church. He was Headmaster of Winchester College between 1766 and 1793 and an accomplished poet. This portrait was painted and exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1777, the year in which Warton was also elected to 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, the Poet Laureate, whose portrait Reynolds also painted (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).
persona1_adapted: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) went to Oriel College, Oxford, before becoming a church leader. He was the head of Winchester College from 1766 to 1793 and also a skilled poet. This portrait was painted and shown at the Royal Academy in 1777. That same year, Warton was chosen to join 'The Club.' He was the brother of Thomas Warton, who was the Poet Laureate. The artist Reynolds also painted Thomas's portrait (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).

CuratorialText: The influence of Vuillard is strongly marked. There is a less finished version of the same composition on the back
persona1_adapted: Vuillard's influence is very clear. On the back of this painting, there is a less finished version of the same scene.

CuratorialText: Bollongier was one of the few painters of the first half of the 17th century in Haarlem who specialised in painting flowers. His work seems to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school but with an added naturalism in the arrangement of the flowers. The glass container in this painting was a favourite accessory. It appears first in a work of 1627 and for the last time in a flower piece of 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).
persona1_adapted: Bollongier was one of the few painters in Haarlem during the early 1600s who focused on painting flowers. His work was likely influenced by the Bosschaert school, a group of artists known for flower paintings. But Bollongier added a more natural look to how he arranged the flowers. The glass container shown in this painting was a favorite item for him. It first appears in a work from 1627 and last appears in a flower painting from 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).

CuratorialText: When Pharaoh ordered all new-born Hebrew sons to be killed, Moses's mother put him in an ark of bulrushes in the Nile. His sister stayed to watch while Pharaoh's daughter came to the river with her maidens and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). WA1950.169 is striking for its emotional intensity, with the focus on Moses's grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of the finding of Moses was a popular one, whereas this subject is far less common (Poussin earlier treated it c. 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, WA1950.169 was greatly admired by contemporaries for the artist's learned treatment of the subject, his skill in painting reflections and the beauty of the landscape. Pharoah's city is based on Pirro Ligorio's reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx accompanied by a river-god and cornucopia identifies the Nile. Different explanations, based on Poussin's antiquarian interests and wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the significance of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies are recorded in the 17th century; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side) and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella's niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.
persona1_adapted:
When Pharaoh ordered that all newborn Hebrew baby boys be killed, Moses’ mother placed him in a basket made of reeds and set it in the Nile River. His sister stayed nearby to watch. Pharaoh’s daughter came to the river with her servants, found the baby, and decided to keep him (Exodus 2:2-4).

This painting, WA1950.169, is striking because of its strong emotions. The focus is on Moses’ parents, who are filled with grief. The joyful scene of finding Moses was a popular subject for artists, but this sad moment is much rarer. (The artist Poussin had painted a similar scene around 1627.)

Painted in 1654 for a man named Jacques Stella, this work was greatly admired at the time. People praised the artist’s deep understanding of the story, his skill in painting reflections on water, and the beauty of the landscape. The city shown in the background is based on Pirro Ligorio’s drawings of famous buildings from ancient Rome.

A sphinx, along with a river-god and a cornucopia (a horn-shaped basket filled with food), helps identify the river as the Nile. Behind this group, a set of Pan pipes hangs from a tree. Scholars have offered different explanations for what these pipes mean, based on the artist’s interest in ancient objects and his wide range of visual references.

In the 1600s, two painted copies of this work were recorded. Other copies and variations exist, including a tapestry version of the right side. Three engraved versions were also made—the first by Stella’s niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

CuratorialText: The brother of Masaccio, Lo Scheggia was an eclectic artist who painted numerous works for domestic settings. This type of decorative, ceremonial subject is often found on cassone panels. Although the picture is much abraded, it has sufficient affinities with the mature work of Lo Scheggia to make the attribution convincing.
persona1_adapted: Lo Scheggia, the brother of Masaccio, was a versatile artist. He painted many works for people's homes. This kind of decorative, ceremonial scene is often found on cassone panels—these are decorated chests used for weddings or special events. Even though this painting is very worn, it still has enough similarities to Lo Scheggia's later work to make the attribution believable.

CuratorialText: Lemon studied in Rome and in Paris with Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He exhibited at the Royal Academy from 1880 and came into contact with Giovanni Costa and his circle of Etruscans in Italy. This slight sketch from nature probably dates from the last years of the 19th century.
persona1_adapted: Lemon studied art in Rome and Paris with a teacher named Carolus-Duran. But he spent his early years in California. Starting in 1880, he showed his work at the Royal Academy, a famous art museum in London. In Italy, he met Giovanni Costa and a group of artists called the Etruscans. This small sketch, drawn from real life, was probably made in the late 1890s.

CuratorialText: The subject, the flat and dry handling and the monumental quality of the figures show Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes. The painting has been dated to c.1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favourite themes.
persona1_adapted: The subject, the flat and dry painting style, and the large, statue-like figures show that Shannon was influenced by Puvis de Chavannes. This painting was made around 1910. It is a version of one of the artist's favorite themes.

CuratorialText: Views in and around the village of Berck in northern France appear in many of the artist's paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This view across the dunes is precisely dated. The unbroken horizon and solitary figure in the foreground emphasize the windswept emptiness of the site.
persona1_adapted: The artist painted many views of the village of Berck in northern France. He made these paintings from 1870 until he died in 1898. This painting shows a view across the sand dunes. It has a specific date. The flat horizon and the single person in the front of the scene highlight the windy, empty feeling of the place.

CuratorialText: Traditionally but wrongly said to be a portrait of Antonio Stradivari, A863a is a portrait of a musician or instrument-maker of the late 16th century and identifications of Claudio Monteverdi (1567-1643) and Gasparo da Salo (1540-1609) have been proposed.
persona1_adapted: People used to think this painting showed Antonio Stradivari, but that was a mistake. It actually shows a musician or instrument maker from the late 1500s. Some experts have suggested it might be Claudio Monteverdi (1567–1643) or Gasparo da Salo (1540–1609).

CuratorialText: During his later years, Calvert produced many small works in thin oil colours on paper, the restrained colours and muted harmonies evocative of a dream world. They are generally peopled with figures from Greek mythology. Painted on a pink ground.
persona1_adapted: In his later years, Calvert created many small paintings on paper. He used thin oil colors. The colors are soft and quiet, like a dream. These works often show figures from Greek myths. He painted them on a pink background.

CuratorialText: Traditionally attributed to Guido Reni as the Head of a Sibyl, WA1926.9 was recently identified as a copy after Cerrini. The same expressive head appears in his altar-piece of The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes painted for San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, Rome (consecrated in 1646) and now in the convent there.
persona1_adapted:
This painting was once thought to be by Guido Reni. It was called *Head of a Sibyl*. Recently, experts found that it is actually a copy. The original was made by a different artist named Cerrini.

The same expressive face appears in one of Cerrini’s altar-pieces. An altar-piece is a large painting placed behind an altar in a church. That painting shows The Holy Family with two saints. It was made for a church in Rome. The church was consecrated, or officially blessed, in 1646. The painting is still kept in the convent there today.

CuratorialText: One of Linnell's most important religious paintings, this was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union for the purposes of engraving in 1838. It was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely associated with the 'Ancients' at Shoreham. Samuel Palmer was the model for the disciple on the right. The source is Luke 24: 13-35.
persona1_adapted: One of John Linnell's most important religious paintings was shown at the Royal Academy in 1835. In 1838, it was sold to the Art Union so it could be turned into an engraving, a type of print made from a carved surface. The painting is based on sketches Linnell made in 1829. At that time, he was closely connected with a group of artists called the 'Ancients' in Shoreham. The disciple on the right side of the painting is modeled after Samuel Palmer, another artist. The story comes from the Bible, Luke 24: 13-35.

CuratorialText: Design is an impulse that unifies us across time and culture. This installation unifies two traditionally disparate collecting fields, fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts to better understand what underlies our motivations and capacity for design. Ensembles from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the exuberant remixing and inventive styling of one of the world’s most prominent fashion icons, while constellations of unique and culturally significant works of design, fashion, and textiles explore distinctive and resourceful forms of creative expression. A recent gallery highlight is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant ensembles from the collection of Boston-based entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a hotspot for lavish evening and wedding ensembles and that became a household name throughout the region, and a shorthand for glitz and glamor in the late 20th century. Cellucci generously donated 57 works of fashion and accessories to PEM in 2021, along with archival photographs and materials related to the legacy of her bridal shop.
persona1_adapted:
Design is a basic human urge that connects people across time and cultures. This exhibit brings together two areas that are usually kept separate: fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts. The goal is to help us understand what drives our desire to design and how we are able to do it.

Outfits from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection show the bold, playful mixing of styles from one of the world’s most famous fashion icons. At the same time, groups of unique and important works of design, fashion, and textiles explore creative and clever ways people express themselves.

A recent highlight in the gallery is the display of 38 bright and flashy outfits from the collection of Boston businesswoman and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Her store, Yolanda’s, was located in Waltham, Massachusetts. It became famous for fancy evening wear and wedding dresses. In the late 1900s, the store was a household name in the region and a symbol of glamour and excitement.

In 2021, Cellucci generously donated 57 fashion pieces and accessories to PEM, along with old photographs and materials that tell the story of her bridal shop.

CuratorialText: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. Beginning her career in Boston in 1863, she traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking international, racial and gender barriers. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way,” Lewis recalled in 1878. “That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.” Following her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy endured in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely been underrecognized. Some of her great masterpieces were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, naturalistic stone sculptures feature abolitionists and social reformers, engage with Indigenous identity and give new life to religious and mythological figures.
persona1_adapted:
Edmonia Lewis was born in Greenbush, New York, in 1844. She became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) heritage to gain worldwide fame. She started her career in Boston in 1863. In 1866, she moved to Rome to join the top American sculptors of her time. Along the way, she broke through barriers of race, gender, and nationality.

In 1878, Lewis said, “Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way. That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.”

After she died in 1907, Lewis’s legacy lived on in Black communities. Still, her role in American sculpture was largely overlooked. Some of her greatest works were rediscovered decades later. Others remain missing today. Her stone sculptures are vivid and naturalistic. They show abolitionists and social reformers, explore Indigenous identity, and give new life to religious and mythological figures.

CuratorialText: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest spans nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle, and makes up one of the world’s last remaining stretches of true wilderness. Discover the biodiversity and global importance of our northernmost forests through first-person stories, commissioned objects, interactive experiences and exquisite photography and videography. The boreal forest is critical to the health of our entire planet. It helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the world’s largest land biome, is a critical water reservoir and holds two to three times as much carbon as tropical forests.
persona1_adapted:
The boreal forest is a huge wilderness that stretches across the top of the world, just below the Arctic Circle. It is home to 3.7 million people, 85 animal species, 32,000 insect species, and 2 billion migratory birds. This forest is one of the last truly wild places left on Earth.

Through first-person stories, specially made objects, hands-on activities, and stunning photos and videos, you can explore the rich variety of life in these northern forests and learn why they matter to the whole planet.

The boreal forest is critical to Earth’s health. It helps control the global climate and is the largest land-based ecosystem on the planet. It also stores a huge amount of fresh water and holds two to three times more carbon than tropical forests do.

CuratorialText: Salem’s connections to the Declaration of Independence center around Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and disseminating the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition coincides with the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution as well as the 400th anniversary of the European settlement of Salem. Works on view in the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery include two of the earliest broadside editions of the Declaration of Independence alongside Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets and broadsides that showcase the nation’s founding values of democracy: freedom, liberty and equality.
persona1_adapted: Salem’s link to the Declaration of Independence comes through Ezekiel Russell, a local printer. In 1776, he helped publish and spread the Declaration. This exhibition marks the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution. It also marks 400 years since Europeans first settled in Salem. The show is in the small James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery. It features two of the earliest printed versions of the Declaration. You’ll also see handwritten papers, newspapers, pamphlets, and posters from the Revolutionary time. These items show the nation’s founding ideas: freedom, liberty, and equality.

CuratorialText: These films honor the lesser-known stories of Salem and offer a behind-the-scenes look at what makes this city so remarkable. Produced over the course of more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera and many other filmmakers for presentation at the Salem Film Fest, these short films span topics from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime Salem residents, these stories might spark nostalgia; for new residents and tourists, they illuminate Salem’s vibrant communities, bygone businesses and traditions that local filmmakers have long worked to record and preserve. Beyond the Broom showcases the vibrant community of Salem, one with an identity that extends beyond its witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which commemorates the quadricentennial of the city’s settlement while honoring the preexisting Indigenous community. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and ensure a vibrant and sustainable city for generations to come.
persona1_adapted:
These films tell the lesser-known stories of Salem. They also show what makes this city so special. Filmmakers Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera, and many others worked on them for over seven years. The films were made for the Salem Film Fest. They cover topics from breakfast at Red's to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows.

For people who have lived in Salem a long time, these stories may bring back memories. For new residents and tourists, they reveal Salem’s lively communities, old businesses, and traditions. Local filmmakers have worked hard to record and save these pieces of history.

"Beyond the Broom" shows the vibrant community of Salem. It has an identity that goes beyond its famous witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration called Salem 400+. It marks 400 years since the city was settled, while also honoring the Indigenous people who lived here first. The goal is to bring the Salem community together and help create a lively, sustainable city for future generations.

CuratorialText: Explore several hundred fascinating objects inside the historic hall, representing a sampling of the many collections that were displayed in the museum during its earliest years. This installation gives voice to early collectors, trading partners and museum visitors about these objects — with some accounts extracted from records in the museum’s vast archive — and shares perspectives from a range of people living today. These captivating stories and works that reflect local and global history, as well as the impact of maritime trade, recenter East India Marine Hall as the heart of the museum. From its inception, this space and the objects in it have elicited empathy, curiosity and wonder and transported visitors to places around the world. This project has been made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support is provided by Mr. Ulf B. Heide, Tim and Joanie Ingraham, Alex Ingraham, and Sam Ingraham. Generous support has been provided by Haven Trust, Mr. and Mrs. James B. Hawkes, Mr. and Mrs. Howard B. Hodgson Jr., Angus and Leslie Littlejohn, Chip and Susan Robie, Mr. and Mrs. Stuart W. Pratt, Katie Huddleson and Alan Kraning, Edward A. Studzinski and Anne G. Studzinski, the Salem Marine Society, and Henry Birdseye Weil and Ann Uppington. Additional support has been provided by The Lee and Juliet Folger Fund, Jonathan B. Loring, Walter C. Meibaum III, Jean Verbridge, and Connie and Stan Grayson.
persona1_adapted:
Step inside this historic hall and explore hundreds of fascinating objects. They show a sample of the many collections the museum displayed in its earliest years.

This exhibit gives a voice to early collectors, trading partners, and museum visitors. Some of their stories come from records in the museum’s huge archive. It also shares views from a range of people living today.

These captivating stories and objects reflect local and global history. They also show the impact of maritime trade (trade by sea). Together, they put East India Marine Hall back at the heart of the museum.

From the very beginning, this space and the objects in it have sparked empathy, curiosity, and wonder. They have transported visitors to places around the world.

This project was made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support comes from Mr. Ulf B. Heide, Tim and Joanie Ingraham, Alex Ingraham, and Sam Ingraham. Generous support has been provided by Haven Trust, Mr. and Mrs. James B. Hawkes, Mr. and Mrs. Howard B. Hodgson Jr., Angus and Leslie Littlejohn, Chip and Susan Robie, Mr. and Mrs. Stuart W. Pratt, Katie Huddleson and Alan Kraning, Edward A. Studzinski and Anne G. Studzinski, the Salem Marine Society, and Henry Birdseye Weil and Ann Uppington. Additional support has been provided by The Lee and Juliet Folger Fund, Jonathan B. Loring, Walter C. Meibaum III, Jean Verbridge, and Connie and Stan Grayson.

CuratorialText: Renowned for its expansive holdings of decorative arts and paintings, PEM’s American art collection spans four centuries of creative expression in the United States. Rooted in our historic holdings that celebrate the rich and diverse artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, the collection has continued to grow to embrace a broader representation of the art of this country. The artists represented in this Seamans gallery use a variety of materials — wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber and more — to convey their creative ambitions. Many of the artistic practices and decorative inspirations behind these works have long histories, with their origins traceable to Europe, Asia and the Americas. These examples of formal perfection, domestic utility and luxury, and innovative twists on cultural traditions tell a larger story of our global connectedness. Together, these works tell compelling stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between the people of the region, the country and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
PEM’s American art collection is famous for its large number of decorative arts and paintings. It covers four centuries of creative work in the United States. The collection began with historic pieces that celebrate the rich and diverse art and culture of Essex County, Massachusetts. Over time, it has grown to include a wider range of American art.

The artists in this Seamans gallery use many different materials—wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber, and more—to show their creative ideas. Many of the art methods and design inspirations behind these works have long histories. Their origins can be traced back to Europe, Asia, and the Americas.

These objects show formal perfection, everyday use, luxury, and new twists on cultural traditions. Together, they tell a bigger story of how we are all connected around the world. They also share powerful stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between people in this region, the country, and the wider world. This gallery is located in the Seamans Gallery.

CuratorialText: Persian and Turkish architecture, textiles and miniature paintings inspire the precise, stylized floral forms that compose Anila Quayyum Agha’s sculptural chamber of light and shadow. This luminous installation located in the Wheatland Family gallery provides an opportunity to contemplate the differences and commonalities that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the US, Agha is acutely attuned to the social codes that inform the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her effort to create a sense of how women can reclaim and safely open up private space to welcome others. Located in the Wheatland Family Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
Anila Quayyum Agha’s artwork is a room filled with light and shadow. It is inspired by the detailed flower patterns found in Persian and Turkish art, such as architecture, textiles, and tiny paintings. These patterns are very precise and stylized, meaning they are simplified and decorative rather than realistic.

This glowing installation is in the Wheatland Family Gallery. It invites you to think about the differences and things we share in our lives and relationships.

Agha was born in Lahore, Pakistan, and now lives in the United States. She understands the unwritten rules that shape the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She says this artwork is her way of showing how women can take back and safely open up private space to welcome others.

CuratorialText:
Merchants across the globe went to great lengths to acquire these spectacular commodities. Now known as Asian export art, these objects connected societies and created a complex global economy that continues to shape our world to this day.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection, foremost in the world, explores cross-cultural exchange as a catalyst for creativity and celebrates the interplay of commerce and creative expression. The gallery features more than 200 works of art made in diverse media by artists in China, Japan, and South Asia. These transcultural objects demonstrate the beauty and ingenuity that can be created through blending artistic traditions, materials, and technologies. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the focus of intensive trade activity between Asia and the rest of the world. This installation in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also addresses the uncomfortable truth that many of these works of art were originally purchased with profits derived from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, millions of Indian and Chinese lives were devastated by opium, a foreshadowing of today’s opioid crisis.
persona1_adapted:
Merchants around the world worked very hard to get these amazing goods. Today we call them Asian export art. These objects connected different societies and helped create a complex global economy. That economy still affects our world today.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection is the best in the world. It shows how people from different cultures shared ideas and made new kinds of art. The collection also celebrates how business and creativity worked together. The gallery has over 200 works of art. Artists from China, Japan, and South Asia made them using many different materials. These objects show the beauty and skill that come from mixing different art styles, materials, and techniques.

Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the main goods traded between Asia and the rest of the world. This display in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also talks about a hard truth. Many of these works of art were bought with money from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, millions of people in India and China suffered because of opium. This was an early sign of the opioid crisis we face today.

CuratorialText: This exhibition explores America as a place where disparate viewpoints are brought together: America as both longed for and greatly revered. Previously on view in the context of PEM's 2019 Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle exhibition, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal presents a solo exhibition of Collins’ art as an immersive experience. Explore 100 different versions of the popular patriotic anthem “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” rewritten between the 18th and 20th centuries in support of various American causes. Versions of the lyrics are bound together chronologically in Collins’ artist book, where she burns away the musical notes with a laser — chronicling an idea of America through the history of one song’s varied verses. A range of these powerful voices and expressions are heard throughout the gallery in a sound piece, what Collins calls, “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The gallery is wallpapered with a newly commissioned work of flocked flower silhouettes. Each flower contains a meaning as the artist uses floriography, the symbolic language of flowers, to relay messages of hope, longing and loss to further illustrate the evolution and complexity of American experiences. In addition to the artwork on view, a study center offers deeper dives into Collins' artistic approaches and historical sources. Through interactives, PEM collection objects and media components, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal inspires us to activate our own civic mindset. Located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibition looks at America as a place where different viewpoints come together. America is both a place people dream of and a place they deeply respect.

The show is called *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal*. It was first shown as part of PEM’s 2019 exhibition *Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle*. Now, it is a solo exhibition of Collins’ art. You can walk into the gallery and feel like you are inside the artwork.

You will find 100 different versions of the patriotic song “My Country ‘Tis of Thee.” People rewrote the lyrics between the 1700s and 1900s to support many different American causes. Collins put these versions together in a book. She used a laser to burn away the musical notes. This shows how the idea of America has changed over time through one song’s many verses.

In the gallery, you can also hear a sound piece. It plays many of these voices and expressions. Collins calls it “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.”

The walls are covered with a new artwork made of flocked flower shapes. Each flower has a special meaning. Collins uses floriography, which is the language of flowers. She sends messages of hope, longing, and loss. This helps show how American experiences have grown and changed over time.

There is also a study center in the gallery. You can learn more about Collins’ artistic methods and the history she used. Through hands-on activities, objects from PEM’s collection, and media, *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* encourages you to think about your own role as a citizen.

This exhibition is located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.

CuratorialText: Encompassing everyday objects and the fine arts, the museum’s Japanese collection is distinguished for its range, from paintings and sculpture to decorative arts and costumes and textiles. Spanning Hokkaido in the North to Okinawa in the South, and prehistory to the present, these objects illuminate the varied artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. Toward the end of the 19th century, Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a precursor to PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to travelers. Morse was highly influential in encouraging American interest in Japanese art and culture, and helped create this remarkable Japanese collection.
persona1_adapted:
The museum’s Japanese collection includes everyday objects and fine art. It is known for its wide range, from paintings and sculptures to decorative arts, costumes, and textiles. These objects come from all over Japan, from Hokkaido in the north to Okinawa in the south. They span from ancient times to the present day. Together, they show the rich artistic and cultural traditions of Japan.

In the late 1800s, Edward Sylvester Morse became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to travelers. He was the director of the Peabody Academy of Science, which was a forerunner to PEM. Morse helped spark American interest in Japanese art and culture. He also played a key role in building this remarkable collection.

CuratorialText: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to today, PEM’s exceptional collection explores art, culture, and life in Korea during a time of significant transition and global connection. PEM’s early Korean collection was primarily formed by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a predecessor organization to PEM), who proactively pursued Korean acquisitions during his tenure from 1880 to 1914. Among his most significant acquisitions from this period is a set of eight Korean musical instruments played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition. PEM’s connection with Korea was also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856-1914), the reformist scholar and politician who came to the U.S. as a member of the first Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and foundational works to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, presented alongside the museum’s renowned Korean textile collection, exquisite 19th-century paintings — including the Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan screen — and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.
persona1_adapted:
From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to today, PEM’s special collection shows us art, culture, and life in Korea during a time of big change and global connection.

PEM’s early Korean collection was mostly built by Edward Sylvester Morse. He was the director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a group that came before PEM). From 1880 to 1914, he actively looked for Korean objects to add to the museum. One of his most important finds was a set of eight Korean musical instruments. These instruments were played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition, a huge world’s fair in Chicago.

PEM’s link to Korea was also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856–1914). He was a reformist scholar and politician—someone who wanted to change and improve society. Yu came to the U.S. as part of the first Korean delegation and lived near Salem, Massachusetts, for almost two years. He and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea gave many important and meaningful works to the museum’s collection.

PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings these early pioneers’ stories to life. The gallery also shows the museum’s famous Korean textile collection, beautiful 19th-century paintings (including the *Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan* screen), and inspiring modern works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.

CuratorialText: PEM’s Maritime art collection, which is the finest in the country, frames the sea as an enduring source of opportunity as well as peril, a force that inspires creativity and innovation, and encourages engagement with the wider world. This installation located in the Byrne Family gallery offers a global perspective on our creative response to the sea, from a 17th-century brass Pakistani astrolabe to Valerie Hagerty’s monumental Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles (2012) and artworks that reflect Salem’s rich history of maritime trade and exploration. Immersive digital media amplify compelling stories that lay behind unassuming objects, like a calendar stick from 1803 whose carved notches record the long days that Rhode Island native, James Drown, spent shipwrecked and left for dead on Tristan da Cunha, a remote speck island in the South Atlantic.
persona1_adapted:
PEM’s maritime art collection is the best in the country. It shows the sea as both a source of opportunity and danger. The sea inspires creativity and new ideas, and it encourages people to connect with the wider world.

This exhibit in the Byrne Family gallery looks at how people around the world have responded to the sea. It includes a brass astrolabe from 1600s Pakistan. An astrolabe is an old tool used to measure the position of stars and help with navigation. It also features Valerie Hagerty’s large artwork *Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles* (2012). Other artworks reflect Salem’s rich history of sea trade and exploration.

Digital media bring to life the stories behind simple objects. For example, a calendar stick from 1803 has carved notches. These notches record the long days that James Drown, a man from Rhode Island, spent shipwrecked on Tristan da Cunha. That is a tiny, remote island in the South Atlantic where he was left for dead.

CuratorialText: Bringing two extraordinary collections of Native American and American art together for the first time in our institution's history, this long-term installation celebrates artistic achievements across time, space, and worldviews. Located in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery, the 250 artworks on display span in time from 10,000 years ago to the present, and demonstrate a range of voices, modes of expression, cultures, and media including: sculpture, paintings, textiles and costumes, furniture, decorative arts, works on paper, installations, video, and a re-envisioned period room. The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most iconic dramas in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem region to death. The new installation will be responsive to these urgent times by giving our public an opportunity to grapple with the links, continuities, and disjunctions of our complex histories in America in order to shape a more connected and empathetic present and future. By rethinking America’s histories, visitors are asked to consider how we might envision a better future together.
persona1_adapted:
