For the first time in our museum’s history, we are bringing together two major collections: Native American art and American art. This long-term display celebrates artistic achievements across time, space, and different ways of seeing the world.

The exhibition is in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and the Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery. It features 250 artworks that span from 10,000 years ago to today. These works show a wide range of voices, styles, cultures, and materials. You’ll see sculptures, paintings, textiles, costumes, furniture, decorative art, works on paper, video installations, and a reimagined period room (a room designed to look like a specific time in history).

The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most famous events in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem area to death.

This new installation responds to today’s urgent times. It gives visitors a chance to think about the connections, continuities, and breaks in America’s complex histories. The goal is to help shape a more connected and caring present and future. By rethinking America’s histories, visitors are asked to consider how we might imagine a better future together.

CuratorialText: These figures, from disparate cultures around the world and across time, embody power as both a fundamental social dynamic and a part of our innate wiring to respond to figures and faces. Curved niches encourage visitors to engage with each artwork, decluttering the field of view and focusing attention. Poetically concise labels heighten the emotional tenor of the experience. Located in the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.
persona1_adapted: These sculptures come from different cultures and time periods around the world. They show power as a basic part of how societies work. They also tap into our natural instinct to react to faces and figures. Curved display spaces help visitors focus on each artwork. They clear away visual clutter and draw your attention. Short, poetic labels add emotion to the experience. This gallery is named after Louise Du Pont Crowninshield.

CuratorialText: The exhibition starts at “A is for Always Indigenous” to acknowledge the Native communities who have lived for millennia on the land where the museum now sits. It ends with Z is for Zoology and coincides with the return to the galleries of a leatherback turtle specimen captured in 1885, a favorite of longtime visitors. Salem is a city with many stories of local, national and international significance. Alexander Graham Bell completed the first successful long-distance telephone call from Salem in 1877. Parker Brothers produced Monopoly here. And in 2013, President Obama signed legislation recognizing the city as the birthplace of the United States National Guard. Using selections from PEM’s vast collection, Salem Stories features more than 100 works, including paintings, sculpture, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, eyewitness accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A–Z structure creates an accessible and entertaining way to engage with Salem’s history, from past to present day. This is an evolving story, one that will continue to unfold throughout the life of the exhibition. Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.
persona1_adapted:
The exhibition begins with **"A is for Always Indigenous."** This section honors the Native communities who have lived on this land for thousands of years. The show ends with **"Z is for Zoology."** That part features a leatherback turtle specimen from 1885, a favorite of longtime visitors.

Salem is a city with many important stories. In 1877, Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful long-distance phone call from Salem. Parker Brothers created the game Monopoly here. In 2013, President Obama signed a law naming Salem the birthplace of the U.S. National Guard.

**Salem Stories** includes over 100 works from PEM’s collection. You’ll see paintings, sculptures, textiles, photographs, and natural history specimens. There are also manuscripts, posters, books, eyewitness accounts, and even a murder weapon.

The A–Z format makes Salem’s history easy and fun to explore. This story is still growing and will keep changing as the exhibition continues. You can find it in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.

CuratorialText: Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. Following independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India aimed to uncover a visual language that was uniquely Indian in inspiration to convey their experiences, struggles, ambitions and dreams. The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused primarily on the 19th century, the gallery considers India's long and complex history of foreign occupation, and its troubling impact on the representation of Indian people in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM, including unfired clay sculpture, mica paintings and Kalighat paintings, the gallery considers and questions the timeless tropes of India that persist even today.
persona1_adapted:
The Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery mixes myth with social and political history. It tells the story of building a nation and finding identity. The gallery shows works by India’s most famous artists from the 1900s.

After India gained independence from British rule in 1947, artists wanted to create a new visual language. They looked for inspiration that was truly Indian. They used this art to share their experiences, struggles, hopes, and dreams.

The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery displays objects from PEM’s large collection of historical Indian art. This gallery focuses mainly on the 1800s. It looks at India’s long history of foreign rule and how that affected the way Indian people were shown in art.

The gallery includes some of the earliest objects that came to PEM. These include unfired clay sculptures, paintings on mica (a shiny mineral), and Kalighat paintings (a style from Kolkata). The gallery asks us to think about the lasting images of India that still exist today.

CuratorialText: This diverse selection of contemporary glass represents the work of more than 40 of the most influential glass artists from the 20th century to today. Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York-based collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, this collection is remarkable for its range of forms, scale and techniques in glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift significantly expands PEM’s noted historic glass collection while celebrating the extraordinary aesthetic possibilities that arise from the combination of sand and fire.
persona1_adapted: This selection of modern glass art shows the work of over 40 important glass artists. These artists worked from the 1900s to today. The collection was given to PEM in 2022 by Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, who are collectors from New York. It stands out because of its wide range of shapes, sizes, and glass-making methods. The Pforzheimers’ gift makes PEM’s historic glass collection much bigger. It also celebrates the amazing beauty that can come from mixing sand and fire.

CuratorialText: The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, which investigates our place in nature through contemporary art, memorable objects and interactive experiences. Designed for visitors of all ages, the family-friendly center features installations, changing exhibitions, hands-on displays and art-making spaces. Grow your awareness of nature, try on new ways of being, and tap into your own creativity. Discover a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline modeled on a live tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move as different animals and embody local habitats. And create your own imaginary creature inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the globe. What creative sense will be sparked in you on your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and curator of The Pod, describes her idea of a pod and what you’ll encounter when you visit our new multisensory exhibition space. There is something for everyone here, ranging from fabulous, nature-inspired contemporary and historic artworks to stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children will discover the Wonder Zone which includes activity bins, a light table and picture books just for them
persona1_adapted:
The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center. This center helps us explore our place in nature through modern art, interesting objects, and hands-on activities. It is designed for visitors of all ages. The family-friendly space has art displays, changing exhibits, hands-on stations, and areas where you can make your own art.

You can grow your awareness of nature, try new ways of being, and tap into your own creativity. See a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline. She modeled it after a real tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move like different animals and show what local habitats feel like. You can also create your own imaginary creature. Get ideas from real animals and fantastical beasts from around the world.

What creative spark will you find on your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod, or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell is the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and the curator of The Pod. She shares her idea of a pod and what you’ll see in this new, multisensory exhibition space.

There is something for everyone here. You’ll find amazing nature-inspired art, both modern and historic. There are stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children can visit the Wonder Zone. It has activity bins, a light table, and picture books just for them.

CuratorialText: This extraordinary series of events between June 1692 and March 1693 led to the deaths of 25 innocent women, men and children who were wrongfully convicted of crimes. More than 300 years later, the personal tragedies and grievous wrongs that occurred still provoke us to reflect and reckon with the experiences of those involved. Learn the true story of this tragedy as told through the voices and with the possessions of those directly involved. This ongoing installation tells this story through court documents and authentic historic objects presented as tangible fragments directly tied to people in Salem and nearby communities in the late 17th century. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick — each illuminates an aspect of individuals who lived through Salem’s witch trials and serves as a reminder of the real people impacted by these harrowing events. The Salem Witch Trials 1692 is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.
persona1_adapted:
Between June 1692 and March 1693, a series of events led to the deaths of 25 innocent people. They were women, men, and children who were wrongly convicted of crimes. More than 300 years later, these personal tragedies still make us think about what happened. They also push us to understand the experiences of those involved.

This exhibit tells the true story of the Salem witch trials. It uses the voices and belongings of the people who lived through it. You will see court documents and real objects from the late 1600s. These items come from Salem and nearby towns. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, and a walking stick each show a part of someone’s life. They remind us that real people were affected by these terrible events.

*The Salem Witch Trials 1692* is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.

CuratorialText: The artist researched the museum’s vast and varied holdings, diving deep to find unexpected connections across time, cultures, and materials. Platacis turned her findings into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand with X-Acto blades. After applying the stencils to the walls, she and her assistant used a variety of spray-paint and graffiti techniques, including drop shadows, high contrast, and layering, to apply color to the walls and give dimensionality and life to her forms. Organic forms and curvilinear lines emerged as unifying design motifs that speak to the natural world. As you move deeper into these galleries, floral forms give way to enchanting fauna and larger-than-life presentations of familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’ labor-intensive contemporary approach to painting connects to the artistry and skill embedded within the historical objects in PEM’s collection, creating a fascinating interplay between artistic practice then and now. What discoveries await? Located in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.
persona1_adapted:
The artist explored the museum’s huge and varied collection. She looked deep to find surprising links between different times, cultures, and materials. Platacis turned what she found into 210 canvas stencils. She drew and cut each one by hand using an X-Acto knife.

After placing the stencils on the walls, she and her assistant used spray paint and graffiti methods. These included drop shadows, high contrast, and layering. These techniques added color and made the shapes look three-dimensional and alive.

Curving lines and organic shapes became the main design themes. They connect to the natural world. As you move deeper into these rooms, flower shapes turn into charming animals and larger-than-life versions of familiar and surprising objects.

Platacis’s hands-on, modern way of painting links to the skill and artistry found in the historical objects at PEM. This creates a fascinating connection between art-making then and now.

What discoveries await you? This exhibition is in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.

CuratorialText: As one of the 20th century’s most critically acclaimed and genre-blurring artists, Noguchi created a remarkable design legacy encompassing sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics and designs for public spaces such as gardens, playgrounds and plazas. Organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer" shines a light on the artist’s limitless creativity and social consciousness. Discover the evolution of Noguchi’s design work, particularly the way he created environments through theatrical set design, furniture and inclusive public spaces — all of which were intentionally designed to improve the way people lived. Many of his designs remain incredibly popular, such as the celebrated coffee table and Akari light sculptures that you may recognize in stores today. As Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” Noguchi also excelled at working collaboratively and forming close relationships with other creative professionals, particularly with modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. These fruitful creative relationships are also highlighted within the exhibition.
persona1_adapted:
Isamu Noguchi was one of the most famous and creative artists of the 1900s. He worked in many different styles and materials, making sculptures, lamps, furniture, and even designing public spaces like gardens, playgrounds, and plazas. This exhibition, organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, is called *Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer."* It shows how Noguchi’s endless creativity and concern for society shaped his work.

The exhibition explores how Noguchi’s designs changed over time. He created environments through theater sets, furniture, and public spaces that were meant to improve people’s lives. Many of his designs are still popular today, like the famous coffee table and the Akari light sculptures you might see in stores.

Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” He also worked closely with other artists, including dancer Martha Graham, architect R. Buckminster Fuller, and sculptor Constantin Brâncuși. These important collaborations are also featured in the exhibition.

CuratorialText: In the nearly two decades since PEM featured the crowd-pleasing exhibition Origami Now!, contemporary origami has continued to expand into exciting new styles of expression and invention. This multigenerational exhibition in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center highlights origami as not just an exciting art form, but also an important influence on new innovations in science, engineering, math and more. See works from leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top origami masters and a Massachusetts resident. In addition to showcasing masterworks of origami, PEM will highlight what is emerging in contemporary origami and design, ranging from incredibly complex artistic models and folding nano structures, to origami-inspired emergency shelters. Investigate origami-inspired breakthroughs in robotics, medicine and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials will give visitors of all ages hands-on opportunities to investigate and participate in the artistic process of origami.
persona1_adapted: It has been almost 20 years since PEM held the popular exhibition *Origami Now!* Since then, origami has grown into new and exciting styles of art and invention. This exhibition, in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, shows that origami is not just a fun art form. It is also an important influence on new ideas in science, engineering, math, and more. You will see works from top origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse. He is one of the world’s best origami masters and lives in Massachusetts. Along with masterpieces of origami, PEM will show what is new in modern origami and design. This includes very complex artistic models, tiny folded structures called nano structures, and even emergency shelters inspired by origami. You can explore how origami has led to breakthroughs in robotics, medicine, and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials give visitors of all ages hands-on chances to learn and try the art of origami themselves.

CuratorialText: View iconic contemporary artworks that reach back into millennia-old art practices. The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art is the largest exhibition of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America, representing the incredible breadth and depth of Contemporary Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition charts watershed moments in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a vivid history of creativity that predates the arrival of Europeans by many thousands of years. The exhibition features nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists, among them several undisputed masterworks from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these significant works have never before left the country. The Stars We Do Not See is curated especially to introduce North American audiences to customary forms and styles, such as the conceptual map paintings of the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes colloquially referred to as “dot paintings”), ochre on bark paintings from far Northern Australia and ambitious experimental weavings. The exhibition also presents powerful works in neon, video and photography that acknowledge and innovate upon tradition.
persona1_adapted:
**The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art**

This exhibition shows you famous modern artworks that connect to art practices thousands of years old. *The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art* is the largest show of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America. It displays the amazing range and depth of contemporary Indigenous Australian art.

The exhibition marks key turning points in Australian Indigenous art. It celebrates a rich history of creativity that began many thousands of years before Europeans arrived. You will see nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists. Several of these are major masterpieces from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these important works have never left Australia before.

*The Stars We Do Not See* was created especially to introduce North American audiences to traditional art forms and styles. These include the conceptual map paintings from the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes called “dot paintings”). You will also see ochre on bark paintings from far Northern Australia and bold experimental weavings. The exhibition also features powerful works in neon, video, and photography. These pieces honor tradition while also building on it in new ways.

CuratorialText: Come and experience the liveliness of a drinking party, the opulence of a royal wedding and poetic evocation of spring on a delicate dish. Discover plants and animals, myths and symbols and decipher the Chinese character for "Double Happiness." With more than 30 highlights from the museum's wide-ranging Chinese collection spanning 3,000 years, this exhibition celebrates China's artistic achievements crystallized in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies and celebrations. Located in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.
persona1_adapted: Come see a lively drinking party, a fancy royal wedding, and a poetic spring scene on a delicate dish. Find plants, animals, myths, and symbols. Learn what the Chinese character for "Double Happiness" means. This exhibit shows over 30 special objects from the museum's Chinese collection. These objects span 3,000 years. They celebrate China's art in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies, and celebrations. The exhibit is in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.

CuratorialText:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only Philadelphians painted by John Singleton Copley, the greatest artist in the American colonies prior to the Revolution. Mifflin was an ardent patriot and by the time this portrait was made, had established himself as a successful merchant; later he rose to the rank of major general in the Continental Army, and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States achieved independence. Sarah Morris, who married Thomas in 1767, was an accomplished, witty, and supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a portable loom, which may symbolize the couple's endorsement of the colonists' boycott of English goods.
Famous as one of Copley's finest works soon after it was painted, this portrait shows the artist at the height of his powers. Born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family, Copley was self-taught. He developed a highly finished style that rendered the features, costumes, and settings of his subjects with remarkable accuracy. Copley always kept a keen eye on his competition, and he may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin on a single canvas by his contemporary Charles Willson Peale, who had recently completed a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family that was admired for its portrayal of family unity and affection. Here, Copley depicts not only the features and costumes of his sitters with his famed skill, but creates an image of marriage as an affectionate, equal partnership--an innovative concept in American portraiture at the time.
persona1_adapted:
**Thomas Mifflin and His Wife Sarah**  
Thomas Mifflin (1744–1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?–1790), were the only people from Philadelphia painted by John Singleton Copley. Copley was the best artist in the American colonies before the Revolutionary War.  

Thomas was a strong supporter of American independence. By the time this portrait was made, he was already a successful merchant. Later, he became a major general in the Continental Army. After the United States won its freedom, he was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania.  

Sarah married Thomas in 1767. She was talented, witty, and a supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a small, portable loom. This may show that the couple supported the colonists' boycott of English goods.  

This portrait was famous as one of Copley’s best works soon after it was painted. It shows the artist at the peak of his skill. Copley was born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family. He taught himself to paint. He developed a very detailed style that showed people’s faces, clothes, and surroundings with amazing accuracy.  

Copley always watched his rivals closely. He may have been inspired to paint both Thomas and Sarah together by another artist, Charles Willson Peale. Peale had just finished a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family. That painting was praised for showing family unity and love.  

Here, Copley does more than just paint his subjects’ faces and clothes with his famous skill. He also creates an image of marriage as a loving, equal partnership. This was a new idea in American portrait painting at the time.

CuratorialText: Better known as a patriot during the American Revolution, Paul Revere was the most prolific and accomplished silversmith in late 1700s Boston. His shop produced domestic and ecclesiastical objects for clients belonging to the same civic, military, political, and religious organizations as Revere. The teapot’s inverted pear shape and engraved ornament of C-scrolls, foliage, and shells epitomizes the Rococo style of his early career. Equally renowned as an engraver of bookplates, trade cards, magazine illustrations, and political prints, Revere probably executed this decoration himself. John Singleton Copley’s famous 1768 portrait of Revere (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) depicts the silversmith with engraving tools, holding a teapot with the same low, domed cover and slightly pointed, pinecone finial as this example. Could they be one and the same?
persona1_adapted:
Paul Revere is best known as a patriot from the American Revolution. But he was also the most skilled and productive silversmith in Boston during the late 1700s. His shop made objects for homes and churches. His customers were people who belonged to the same clubs, military groups, and political organizations as Revere.

This teapot has an upside-down pear shape. Its engraved decorations include C-shaped curves, leaves, and shells. These details are perfect examples of the Rococo style, which Revere used early in his career.

Revere was also famous as an engraver. He made bookplates, business cards, magazine pictures, and political prints. He probably carved the decorations on this teapot himself.

A famous 1768 portrait of Revere by John Singleton Copley shows the silversmith holding engraving tools. In the painting, he holds a teapot with the same low, rounded lid and pointed pinecone-shaped top as this one. Could they be the same teapot?

CuratorialText: Human rights and social issues often figure in Pippin’s work, but this painting is particularly overt in its treatment of racism. Clouds hover over a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a burly white man holding a noose. The two menacing figures stand opposite the Statue of Liberty, here painted brown instead of green. At the center, Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a symbolic V for victory, segregating white and black machinists and servicemen. Pippin includes himself as one of the soldiers, wearing a World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.
persona1_adapted: Pippin often painted about human rights and social issues. This painting is very direct about racism. Dark clouds hang over a hooded Ku Klux Klan member and a large white man holding a noose. These two threatening figures stand across from the Statue of Liberty, which is painted brown instead of green. In the center, a figure called Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a V-for-victory symbol. This wedge separates white and Black workers and soldiers. Pippin painted himself as one of the soldiers. He wears a World War I uniform, and his wounded right arm hangs at his side.

CuratorialText: While the bed chambers of chic early Philadelphia houses often featured a high chest (for storing textiles; 1957-129-1) and dressing table (for cosmetics and hair accessories; 2012-59-1), this pairing represents the pinnacle of design, cabinetmaking, and carved ornament. The raised space underneath the high chest distinguishes it as a confection of North America since the British had abandoned that design by the 1730s. While most high chests and dressing tables fit within well-defined groups, the proportions and shaping of the rails and the legs of this pairing relate to only one other dressing table, making it difficult to identify the maker. The choice mahogany, with an even surface and swirling figure, signifies the status of the commission. And a highly skilled carver ornamented it profusely—most conspicuously with a narrative from Aesop’s fable of “The Fox and the Grapes” on the central bottom drawers. Aesop’s moralistic tales were the only fiction Quakers were allowed to read.
persona1_adapted:
In early Philadelphia, fancy bedrooms often had a tall chest for storing fabrics and a dressing table for makeup and hair items. This particular set is the best example of design, furniture-making, and carved decoration. The raised space under the tall chest shows it was made in North America. By the 1730s, British furniture makers had stopped using that design.

Most tall chests and dressing tables fit into clear categories. But the shape and curves of the rails and legs on this set match only one other dressing table. That makes it hard to know who made it. The high-quality mahogany wood has a smooth surface and a swirly pattern. This shows the person who ordered it was wealthy and important.

A very skilled carver added lots of decoration. The most noticeable part is on the bottom middle drawers. It shows a story from Aesop’s fable “The Fox and the Grapes.” Quakers were only allowed to read Aesop’s moral tales as fiction.

CuratorialText: Pennsylvania-made Kleiderschranken (clothes presses) functioned, as did the earlier models in northern Europe, to hold hanging clothes and store cherished textiles. This schrank is notable for its extraordinary decoration—bold architectural paneling, carving, and lettering that records the name of its owner, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, and the date it was made, 1779, only a year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. Rather than a lightwood inlay, the lettering and floral designs were made by pouring molten sulfur into channels carved in the walnut. Swiss and German designs inspired the decorative motifs, many of which are commonly found on other Pennsylvania German furniture as well as ceramics and fraktur, and helped to connect the owners and makers to their cultural roots. The wardrobe’s monumental size, bold composition, and profuse ornament demonstrate not only Huber’s aesthetic taste and wealth but also the skills and sensibilities of the Germanic artisans who made it.
persona1_adapted:
This clothes press, called a *Kleiderschrank* in German, was made in Pennsylvania. Like earlier versions from northern Europe, it held hanging clothes and stored treasured fabrics.

This *schrank* stands out because of its amazing decoration. It has bold, building-like panels, carvings, and lettering. The lettering records the owner’s name, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania. It also shows the year it was made: 1779, just one year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer.

The lettering and flower designs were not made with light-colored wood. Instead, the maker poured hot, melted sulfur into grooves carved into the walnut wood. The decorative patterns were inspired by Swiss and German designs. Many of these same patterns appear on other Pennsylvania German furniture, as well as on pottery and *fraktur* (decorative handwritten documents). These designs helped the owners and makers feel connected to their cultural roots.

The wardrobe’s huge size, bold look, and rich decoration show more than just Huber’s taste and wealth. They also show the skills and traditions of the German craftspeople who built it.

CuratorialText: Inscribed (translation): 1786 G. H. From the earth with sense the potter makes everything. Cadarina Raeder, her dish. German-speaking immigrants to Pennsylvania maintained the artistic traditions they brought from Europe, often retaining art forms and styles over several generations. This dish features decoration made using the centuries-old sgraffito technique, achieved by cutting through a slip coating to expose the red clay underneath. The verse in German around the border, extolling the skill of the potter, also shows an adherence to tradition: language provided an immediate means of holding onto ethnic identity. With such rich ornamentation, this dish was clearly not intended for use.
persona1_adapted:
This dish was made in 1786 by a potter named G. H. The potter shaped it from clay found in the earth. The words on it say: "Cadarina Raeder, her dish."

German-speaking immigrants who moved to Pennsylvania kept the art traditions they brought from Europe. They often passed down these art forms and styles for many generations.

The decoration on this dish uses an old technique called sgraffito (skrah-FEE-toh). To do this, the potter covered the dish with a thin layer of light-colored clay, called slip. Then, the potter cut through the slip to show the red clay underneath.

The verse around the border is written in German. It praises the potter's skill. Using the German language helped these immigrants hold onto their cultural identity.

Because of its rich decoration, this dish was clearly not meant to be used for eating. It was made to be admired.

CuratorialText: In 1795 the Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this illusionistic portrait to showcase his artistic skills. At the same time, he wished to represent the roles played by two of his sons at Peale’s Museum, the first American museum created to educate and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale, who crafted natural science displays, gestures at the top of the stairs as his older brother, Raphaelle, an artist with his palette in hand, strides upward just above a real step attached to the base of the canvas. The life-size portrait is known to have startled and delighted visitors, reputedly even George Washington, before the Peale Museum’s collection was dismantled and finally sold in 1854. Acquired by the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1945, the picture continues to engage and welcome visitors.
persona1_adapted: In 1795, Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this lifelike portrait to show off his skills. He also wanted to show the jobs of two of his sons at Peale’s Museum. That museum was the first in America built to teach and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale made displays about nature and science. He waves from the top of the stairs. His older brother, Raphaelle, is an artist holding a paint palette. He steps upward just above a real step attached to the bottom of the canvas. This life-size painting surprised and delighted visitors. People say even George Washington was amazed. The Peale Museum’s collection was broken up and finally sold in 1854. The Philadelphia Museum of Art bought this painting in 1945. It still welcomes and engages visitors today.

CuratorialText: A rare early representation of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States, and an outstanding example of Peale’s late naturalistic style, the picture is distinguished by the direct and sympathetic encounter between the artist and his subject and a skilled rendering of details. Yarrow’s knit cap suggests a kufi, a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to assert their religion or African identity, but Peale artfully employed its yellow band to highlight his sitter’s steady gaze with its glint of humor and wisdom. Seventy-seven years old when he created this portrait, Peale was seeking to record the traits that he believed supported a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale celebrated making wise choices to maintain good health and a positive attitude. He perceived Yarrow’s perseverance in the face of racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, industriousness, and sobriety.
persona1_adapted:
This painting is a rare early example of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States. It is also a great example of Charles Willson Peale’s later naturalistic style. "Naturalistic" means the artist tried to paint things as they really look.

The picture stands out because of the direct and kind connection between the artist and his subject. Peale also skillfully painted small details. For instance, Yarrow’s knit cap looks like a kufi. A kufi is a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to show their religion or African identity. Peale cleverly used the cap’s yellow band to draw attention to Yarrow’s steady gaze. That gaze shows a hint of humor and wisdom.

Peale was 77 years old when he made this portrait. He wanted to capture the qualities he believed helped people live a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale praised making smart choices to stay healthy and keep a positive attitude. He saw Yarrow’s strength in the face of racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, hard work, and self-control.

CuratorialText: This chair is part of the elegantly painted furniture Latrobe designed for the Walns' drawing room. Decorated in rich red, yellow, and gilt on a black ground and incorporating classical motifs and symbols, the set of drawing room furniture consisted of a pier table, Grecian sofas, a pair of card tables, window benches, and chairs. Its legs that curved inwards make the chair the earliest American example of a Klismos chair, a forerunner of the classical style that enjoyed widespread popularity in the late 1810s and 1820s. In addition to this chair from the Waln set, the Museum's collection includes six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.
persona1_adapted:
This chair is part of a set of painted furniture. Benjamin Latrobe designed it for the Waln family's drawing room. The set was decorated in rich red, yellow, and gold on a black background. It also used classical designs and symbols. The set included a pier table, Grecian sofas, two card tables, window benches, and chairs.

The chair's legs curve inward. This makes it the earliest American example of a Klismos chair. A Klismos chair is a type of chair with curved legs that comes from ancient Greek designs. This style became very popular in the late 1810s and 1820s.

The Museum's collection has more pieces from this set. Besides this chair, there are six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.

CuratorialText:
The Gross Clinic is recognized as one of the greatest American paintings ever made. The young and little-known Eakins created it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition, intending to showcase his talents as an artist and to honor the scientific achievements of his native Philadelphia.
Choosing the city’s world-famous surgeon and teacher Dr. Samuel Gross as his subject, Eakins sets the scene in Jefferson Medical College’s surgical amphitheater. Dr. Gross is shown leading a clinic of five doctors operating on the left thigh of a patient. At the same time Gross is demonstrating to students the relatively new surgical procedure he had developed to treat bone infections. In contrast to the recoiling woman to the left—traditionally identified as the patient’s mother—Gross embodies the confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Casting himself as a witness, Eakins can be seen seated to the right of the tunnel railing, sketching or writing.
persona1_adapted:
**The Gross Clinic** is known as one of the greatest American paintings ever made. A young and little-known artist named Thomas Eakins created it for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition. He wanted to show off his skills as an artist. He also wanted to honor the scientific achievements of his hometown, Philadelphia.

Eakins chose Dr. Samuel Gross as his subject. Dr. Gross was a world-famous surgeon and teacher. The scene takes place in a surgical amphitheater at Jefferson Medical College. Dr. Gross leads a team of five doctors who are operating on a patient’s left thigh. At the same time, he teaches students about a new surgery he created to treat bone infections.

On the left side of the painting, a woman pulls back in shock. She is often thought to be the patient’s mother. In contrast, Dr. Gross shows the confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Eakins places himself in the scene as a witness. You can see him seated to the right of the railing, sketching or writing.

CuratorialText: The dramatic rescue from a foundering ship shown here was made possible by a recent innovation in lifesaving technology, the breeches buoy. Secured firmly to ship and shore, the device permitted the transfer of stranded passengers to safety by means of a pulley that was hauled back and forth by crews at either end. Cropped down to its essentials, Homer's composition thrusts us into the midst of the action with massive waves rolling past, drenching the semiconscious woman and her anonymous savior. The Life Line was immediately recognized by critics as a major contribution to American art, portraying a heroic, contemporary subject with both painterly virtuosity and detailed observation.
persona1_adapted:
The dramatic rescue from a sinking ship shown here was made possible by a new lifesaving tool, the breeches buoy. This device was a life ring with canvas pants attached. It was tied firmly to both the ship and the shore. Crews on each end pulled a rope to move the buoy back and forth, carrying stranded passengers to safety.

Homer cropped the scene down to its most important parts. This pulls us right into the action. Huge waves roll past, soaking the half-conscious woman and her unknown rescuer.

Critics quickly praised *The Life Line* as a major work of American art. It shows a heroic, modern subject with both skillful painting and careful detail.

CuratorialText: Mary Cassatt’s flair for digging into an ordinary moment and distilling its emotions stands out in this portrayal of three individuals lulled by a carriage ride into silent reverie. Once titled simply Driving, the painting depicts an outing in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver of the carriage is Lydia Cassatt, sister of the artist, and the girl seated primly beside her is Odile Fèvre, niece of the painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is unidentified. Usually associated with domestic interiors and the private lives of women and children, here Cassatt explored an outdoor setting and a degree of autonomy enjoyed by upper-class women. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters enjoyed, particularly after 1879, when the family acquired a small carriage and Bichette, the horse partly depicted here
persona1_adapted:
Mary Cassatt had a special talent for capturing an ordinary moment and bringing out its feelings. This painting shows three people quietly lost in their own thoughts during a carriage ride. It was once simply called *Driving*.

The scene takes place in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver of the carriage is Lydia Cassatt, the artist’s sister. Sitting primly beside her is Odile Fèvre, the niece of painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is not named.

Cassatt is best known for painting indoor scenes of women and children in their private lives. But here, she explores an outdoor setting and shows the freedom that upper-class women could enjoy. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters loved, especially after 1879, when their family bought a small carriage and a horse named Bichette, who is partly shown in this painting.

CuratorialText: Tanner painted The Annunciation soon after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. The son of a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, Tanner specialized in religious subjects, and wanted to experience the people, culture, architecture, and light of the Holy Land. Influenced by what he saw, Tanner created an unconventional image of the moment when the angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will bear the Son of God. Mary is shown as an adolescent dressed in rumpled Middle Eastern peasant clothing, without a halo or other holy attributes. Gabriel appears only as a shaft of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon exhibition, after which it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1899, making it his first work to enter an American museum.
persona1_adapted:
Tanner painted *The Annunciation* right after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. His father was a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church. Because of this, Tanner focused on religious subjects. He wanted to see the people, culture, buildings, and light of the Holy Land for himself.

What he saw there influenced him. He created an unusual version of the moment when the angel Gabriel tells Mary she will give birth to the Son of God. In this painting, Mary is shown as a teenager. She wears wrinkled, simple Middle Eastern peasant clothes. She has no halo or other holy symbols. Gabriel appears only as a beam of light.

Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon, a major art exhibition. In 1899, the Philadelphia Museum of Art bought it. This was the first of Tanner’s works to enter an American museum.

CuratorialText: A father’s love radiates from this iridescent painting of sunlight and youth. Shaped by the realist tradition of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, Daniel Garber adopted Impressionist color and handling during a two-year sojourn in Europe. Returning in 1907, he settled into a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he harmoniously merged nature, art, and domestic life. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio renders her smock translucent, while the composition’s serene geometry captures the shimmer of an endless summer afternoon.
persona1_adapted: A father's love shines through this glowing painting of sunlight and youth. Daniel Garber learned from the realist style of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts. While living in Europe for two years, he picked up the bright colors and loose brushwork of Impressionism. When he returned in 1907, he moved to a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. There, he blended nature, art, and family life together. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio makes her smock look see-through. The calm, balanced design of the painting captures the feeling of a long, endless summer afternoon.

CuratorialText: Georgia O’Keeffe once remarked, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is an extraordinary example of her floral compositions, made of sweeping, broad waves of subtly blended hues. The white petals, highlighted in green, are arranged against a pink backdrop, and from each one emerges a bright yellow pistil. Many have interpreted O’Keeffe’s depictions of floral anatomy in relation to sexuality and gender, but the artist always resisted these interpretations, considering them too specific and limiting.
persona1_adapted: Georgia O’Keeffe once said, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is a great example of her flower art. It uses wide, flowing shapes made of gently blended colors. The white petals have green highlights and sit against a pink background. A bright yellow pistil, the flower’s central part, rises from each lily. Many people have linked O’Keeffe’s flower images to ideas about sexuality and gender. But the artist always rejected these views. She thought they were too narrow and limited.

CuratorialText: Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors are among his most important early sculptural work. Inspired by the angular shapes and intersecting lines of the Cubist movement, the artist incorporated the natural patterns and grains of assorted woods into his pieces to emphasize their strong geometric and organic forms. Born in Philadelphia in 1887, Esherick studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908–10, and discovered wood as a medium for artistic expression when he began to make carved frames for his canvases. By the mid-1920s he was fully committed to sculpture and extended his definition of the medium to the carving of furniture and architectural interiors. His work is often seen as a forerunner to the contemporary studio furniture movement.
persona1_adapted:
Wharton Esherick is known for his carved interiors. These are some of his most important early sculptures. He was inspired by Cubism, an art style that uses sharp angles and crossing lines. Esherick used the natural patterns and grains of different types of wood in his work. This helped him show strong geometric and organic shapes.

Esherick was born in Philadelphia in 1887. He studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908 to 1910. He first discovered wood as a way to make art when he started carving frames for his paintings. By the mid-1920s, he focused fully on sculpture. He expanded his idea of sculpture to include carving furniture and entire rooms. Many people see his work as an early example of the modern studio furniture movement.

CuratorialText: Andrew Wyeth shared the realist approach of many mid-twentieth-century painters of the American scene, although his process of simplification and synthesis often led to haunting, surrealist effects. His picture of his neighbor Karl Kuerner’s light-filled kitchen holds an air of something missing. Wyeth explained that the painting was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” The snarling log outside the window, like the knife on the table, connected in the artist’s mind to Kuerner’s wartime experience, his violent dog, and his strong hand on the entire farm. Said Wyeth, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”
persona1_adapted:
Andrew Wyeth was a realist painter. That means he painted things to look like they do in real life. He shared this style with many American artists from the mid-1900s. But Wyeth also simplified and combined elements in his work. This often gave his paintings a strange, dreamlike feeling.

This painting shows the bright kitchen of his neighbor, Karl Kuerner. The room feels like something is missing. Wyeth said the painting is about "the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed."

The twisted log outside the window and the knife on the table were important to Wyeth. They reminded him of Kuerner’s time as a soldier in a war. They also connected to Kuerner’s mean dog and his tight control over the farm. Wyeth explained, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”

CuratorialText: In November 1943 this painting was included in Jackson Pollock's stunning solo exhibition at Art of This Century, the innovative New York art gallery run by legendary art patron Peggy Guggenheim. As its title suggests, the work depicts a male figure, embodied in the black columnar form at right, with its mysterious arithmetic graffiti, and a curvy female figure at left, with marvelous catlike eyelashes. Standing on tiny triangular feet, both figures are surrounded by splashed and smeared oil paint that enhances the sense of energy inherent in the two vertical forms, while also foreshadowing the famous "drip" technique that Pollock would develop later that decade.
persona1_adapted:
In November 1943, this painting was part of Jackson Pollock's amazing solo show. The show was at Art of This Century, a New York art gallery run by famous art supporter Peggy Guggenheim.

As the title suggests, the painting shows a male figure. You can see him in the tall black shape on the right, which has strange, math-like marks. On the left is a curvy female figure with long, catlike eyelashes.

Both figures stand on tiny triangle-shaped feet. Around them are splashed and smeared oil paints. This makes the two vertical shapes feel full of energy. It also hints at the "drip" technique Pollock would become famous for later in the 1940s.

CuratorialText: Here you will find Benjamin Franklin as you’ve never seen him before. Philadelphia-born artist Roberto Lugo riffs on the traditional representations of Franklin found throughout the city—ones that celebrate him as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. You can see one such stately portrayal of Franklin directly behind you, in gallery 384. Lugo places Franklin within a constellation of songs, symbols, and rappers’ names—ones that shaped his childhood and adult life. The artist also includes his own self-portrait on the other side of the vase, creating an imagined dialogue between himself and Franklin around the themes of money, history, violence, and success.
persona1_adapted:
Here you will see Benjamin Franklin in a way you've never seen him before. The artist Roberto Lugo was born in Philadelphia. He plays with the usual images of Franklin found all over the city. Those images celebrate Franklin as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. You can see one of those formal portraits of Franklin right behind you, in gallery 384.

Lugo places Franklin inside a mix of songs, symbols, and rappers' names. These are things that shaped the artist's childhood and adult life. On the other side of the vase, Lugo includes his own self-portrait. This creates an imagined conversation between himself and Franklin. They talk about money, history, violence, and success.

CuratorialText: Florine Stettheimer set this scene like a stage, framed by a grand red staircase at left and a long red drape mimicking a theater curtain at right. The depicted customers of Bendel’s, the famous Manhattan department store, lack the decorum and manners expected of women in this period. Instead, they frantically dive for clearance items, change clothes, inspect garments, and examine their mirrored reflections. Stettheimer lets us see "behind the curtain" to observe these women during a moment of unabashed liberation. The action-packed canvas contains an astonishing thirty-two people and two dogs, including the Pekingese at the forefront who serves as a surrogate for the artist, as indicated by the "FS"-monogrammed sweater. Henri Bendel, the store’s queer owner, stands in the left foreground. Opposite is an androgynous salesperson, whose appearance and pose—slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and delicate hand gesture—align with how white gay men often presented themselves at the time.
persona1_adapted: Florine Stettheimer set this scene like a stage. A grand red staircase is on the left, and a long red drape on the right looks like a theater curtain. The customers in Bendel’s, a famous store in Manhattan, do not act with the good manners expected of women at that time. Instead, they dive for sale items, change clothes, check garments, and look at themselves in mirrors. Stettheimer lets us see "behind the curtain" to watch these women in a moment of total freedom. The busy painting shows thirty-two people and two dogs. One dog, a Pekingese in the front, stands in for the artist—it wears a sweater with the letters "FS." Henri Bendel, the store’s queer owner, stands in the left front. Across from him is a salesperson who looks androgynous. This person has slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and a delicate hand gesture. These features match how many white gay men presented themselves at that time.

CuratorialText: Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies identify them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) of Daoist lore, who live deep in the mountains searching for herbs. Here they show each other what they have gathered. The figure at the far left holds a lingzhi fungus (thought to bestow longevity if ingested) and a basket of plants is draped over her arm. The focus of the painting is on a large health-benefiting ginseng root displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other herbs depicted include leaves of banana and palm tree carried in the baskets on the women’s backs. The representation of Feathered Women exhibiting precious medicinal items functions as a special talisman, protecting the owner from illness.
persona1_adapted:
Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grass and leaves. Their hairy bodies mark them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) from Daoist stories. These women live deep in the mountains, searching for healing plants. In this painting, they show each other what they have found.

The figure on the far left holds a lingzhi fungus. People believed eating this fungus could give a long life. A basket of plants hangs from her arm.

The main focus of the painting is a large ginseng root. The central immortal holds it in her hand. Ginseng was thought to be very good for health.

Other herbs include banana and palm leaves, carried in baskets on the women’s backs.

The painting of Feathered Women showing precious medicinal items worked as a special charm. It was meant to protect the owner from sickness.

CuratorialText:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light in the hand that it feels almost weightless. Candles placed within it would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice designs, which required great skill to achieve due to the extremely thin clay. It was not until the early 1700s that artists developed this piercing technique.

Auspicious Decoration

The four flowers within the central medallions on the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—convey peace, unity, and longevity.
persona1_adapted:
This porcelain lantern feels almost weightless when you hold it. Candles inside would have glowed and flickered through the delicate lattice patterns. Those patterns were hard to make because the clay was so thin. Artists did not develop this piercing technique until the early 1700s.

Auspicious Decoration

On the six sides, four flowers appear inside the central medallions. The camellia, rose, peony, and lily stand for peace, unity, and long life.

CuratorialText:
Various deva, or guardian deities, are depicted on this fragment from the outer section of a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. Murals representing such figures, richly dressed in costumes and adorned with jewelry, are typical of northern Chinese temples built from the late Yuan (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasties (1368–1644), when the ruling elite embraced a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism. Although there is some loss of color, we can identify several of the deities:
(Far left) The guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith, Weituo (Skanda in Sanskrit), wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword destroys demons and Buddhism’s enemies.
(Center) The Heavenly King of the West brandishes a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and stupa/pagoda.
(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one frontal and two in profile—and multiple arms.
persona1_adapted:
This fragment comes from a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. It shows several **deva**, or guardian deities. These are protective spirits in Buddhism.

Murals with richly dressed figures wearing jewelry are common in northern Chinese temples. They were made from the late Yuan dynasty (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasty (1368–1644). During this time, the ruling elite followed a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism.

Some of the colors have faded, but we can still identify several deities:

- **Far left:** Weituo (Skanda in Sanskrit) is the guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith. He wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword destroys demons and enemies of Buddhism.

- **Center:** The Heavenly King of the West holds a snake. The Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and a **stupa** (a small pagoda-like structure).

- **Right:** The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one facing forward and two in profile—and multiple arms.

CuratorialText: The Zhihua monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple compounds in the Chinese capital of Beijing. Conceived on a grand scale, the compound was built in the early 1400s by the eunuch Wang Zhen. This ceiling comes from the second of five main halls on the monastery's central axis—the Hall of Great Wisdom. To give visitors a sense of the original context, the Museum installed the ceiling with architectural elements reconstructed from measured drawings made at the original site. In the center is a writhing imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, bracketing, and eight canted panels, each bearing a smaller dragon among clouds. Lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols are carved in the surrounding panels. The Chinese name for this central part of the ceiling is tianjing, or "well of heaven." While most of the original red lacquer is well preserved, much of the rich overlay of gold leaf has been lost. A series of model temples supported by cloud-decorated brackets encompass the central element; within these miniature structures are delicately carved sculptures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas. The remainder of the ceiling consists of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers that show the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (Lamaism). Each bears a character from a North Indian script (called Lantsha by the Tibetans) that symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs made in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).
persona1_adapted:
The Zhihua monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple complexes in Beijing, China. It was built in the early 1400s by a eunuch named Wang Zhen. This ceiling comes from the second of five main halls, called the Hall of Great Wisdom. To help visitors imagine the original setting, the Museum rebuilt parts of the ceiling using drawings made at the site.

In the center is a twisting imperial dragon surrounded by clouds. Around it are eight angled panels, each with a smaller dragon among clouds. The surrounding panels have carvings of lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist symbols. The Chinese name for this central part is *tianjing*, which means "well of heaven."

Most of the original red lacquer is still in good shape, but much of the gold leaf that once covered it has worn away. Around the center is a ring of model temples held up by cloud-shaped brackets. Inside these tiny temples are carved figures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas (enlightened beings).

The rest of the ceiling is made of square panels painted with lotus flowers. These flowers show the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (also called Lamaism). Each panel has a character from a North Indian script (known as Lantsha to Tibetans) that symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).

CuratorialText: Educator and collector Ernest Fenollosa installed this monumental painting by Kano Hōgai in his home in Tokyo, where it held pride of place. The artist’s highly naturalistic rendering of intertwined dragons reflects a fusion of East and West. While the subject of dragons dates back to ancient China, the three-dimensionality and foreshortening of the animals’ forms reflect Hōgai’s familiarity with the Western use of perspective.
persona1_adapted:
Ernest Fenollosa was both an educator and an art collector. He placed this huge painting by Kano Hōgai in his home in Tokyo. It held the most important spot in the house.

The artist painted two dragons twisting together in a very lifelike way. This style shows a mix of ideas from East and West. Dragons have been a subject in art since ancient China. But Hōgai’s dragons look three-dimensional and their bodies are shortened in a way that shows depth. This tells us he knew about the Western method of using perspective.

CuratorialText: Fifty-eight sixteenth-century granite architectural elements are presented within the museum gallery in an arrangement designed to resemble a South Indian temple hall (mandapam). In 1913 these pieces lay discarded at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai, where a Philadelphia couple purchased them while on their two-year-long honeymoon. But recent research supports that the majority were created for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There they had been part of a shrine to the goddess Lakshmi, wife of the Hindu deity Vishnu. This shrine had become dilapidated, and the temple authorities dismantled it by 1907, completing its replacement by 1923. Stone slabs between the lion brackets depict several scenes from the Ramayana, the epic tale of one of Vishnu's earthly incarnations. They once formed parts of a carved frieze showing the entire story. The life-size figures projecting from pillars are deities and mythological characters, including from the Ramayana. The many small images on the pillars include gods and heroes as well as humans (like an architect with his measuring stick).
persona1_adapted:
Here is the adapted text for an 8th-grade reader:

In the museum gallery, fifty-eight stone pieces from the 1500s are arranged to look like a South Indian temple hall (called a *mandapam*). In 1913, these pieces were left unused at a small temple in Madurai, India. A couple from Philadelphia bought them during their two-year honeymoon.

New research shows that most of these pieces were actually made for a much larger temple nearby, called Kudal Alagar. They had been part of a shrine to the goddess Lakshmi, who is the wife of the Hindu god Vishnu. That shrine had fallen into bad condition. The temple authorities took it apart by 1907 and finished replacing it by 1923.

Stone slabs between the lion-shaped brackets show scenes from the *Ramayana*. This is an epic story about one of Vishnu's earthly forms. These slabs were once part of a carved band that told the entire story. The life-size figures that stick out from the pillars are gods and characters from myths, including some from the *Ramayana*. The many small images on the pillars include gods, heroes, and even humans—like an architect holding his measuring stick.

CuratorialText: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape.  Over the past thirty years researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6500 craftsmen who enslaved people.  In fact, this fact is among the most common things we know about an identified craftsman.  Often anonymous, those who were enslaved by craftsmen were often at work on building sites, at the potters’ wheels, in cabinet shops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and at dozens of other trades.  Their skills are revealed in the objects that they helped to fashion.  Their identities are often lost to history. A line in a legal document, or a brief mention in an advertisement is often all we know about an enslaved craftsman – but careful reading of period documents by MESDA researchers has identified nearly 3000 enslaved craftsmen by name.  The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen looks at icons of southern decorative arts through the lens of research conducted by MESDA over more than thirty years.  It recovers the stories of the many named and nameless individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.
persona1_adapted: Enslaved Black and free white workers labored together across the South. Over the past thirty years, researchers at MESDA have found nearly 6,500 craftsmen who enslaved people. In fact, this is one of the most common facts we know about a craftsman from that time. Often, their names are unknown. Those enslaved by craftsmen worked at building sites, at potters' wheels, in cabinet shops, at iron foundries, behind silversmith counters, and in many other trades. Their skills show in the objects they helped make. But their identities are often lost to history. Sometimes, all we know about an enslaved craftsman is a line in a legal paper or a short mention in an ad. Still, by carefully reading old documents, MESDA researchers have found nearly 3,000 enslaved craftsmen by name. The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen looks at famous pieces of Southern decorative art. It uses research from MESDA that spans more than thirty years. The show brings back the stories of many named and unnamed people whose hidden work shaped the Southern landscape.

CuratorialText: North Carolina’s Piedmont region is known around the world for the manufacture of fine furniture.  However, long before the factories of Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point came into existence, the Piedmont was home to a diverse group of cabinetmakers who crafted distinctive regional furniture. By the middle of the eighteenth century, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began pouring into the North Carolina Piedmont in search of inexpensive yet fertile land.  By 1800 about 250,000 Scotch-Irish, English, and German settlers called the region home.  Cabinetmakers immediately utilized the abundant walnut, cherry, pine and poplar trees to produce well-made, stylish furniture for their homes.  In most cases, these craftsmen combined national trends with local idiosyncratic preferences to produce regional furniture with a distinctive style and appearance. This exhibit is drawn from MESDA’s extensive collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture made between 1780 and 1860.
persona1_adapted:
The Piedmont region of North Carolina is famous for making fine furniture. But before the big factories in Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point existed, the area was home to many different cabinetmakers. These craftspeople made furniture with a unique local style.

In the mid-1700s, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia started moving to the North Carolina Piedmont. They came looking for cheap, good land. By 1800, about 250,000 settlers—including Scotch-Irish, English, and German people—lived there.

Cabinetmakers used the area’s plentiful walnut, cherry, pine, and poplar trees. They built well-made, stylish furniture for their homes. Often, these craftsmen mixed national trends with local tastes. This created furniture with a one-of-a-kind look.

This exhibit comes from MESDA’s large collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture. The pieces were made between 1780 and 1860.

CuratorialText: Masterfully decorated slipware, sculptural bottles, refined creamware, and faience are but part of the rich artistic legacy of North Carolina’s first earthenware potters. During the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent introduced a variety of Old World ceramics traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical standpoint, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their Middle Atlantic and New England contemporaries. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest of materials into vessels of practical utility, astonishing beauty, and deep cultural significance. This exhibit is the first major survey of this work an the context in which it was created.
persona1_adapted:
North Carolina’s first potters made many kinds of pottery. They created decorated slipware, sculptural bottles, refined creamware, and faience. These are just part of their rich artistic legacy.

In the last half of the 1700s, European-trained artisans brought Old World pottery traditions to the Carolina backcountry. Their work was often better than that of potters in the Middle Atlantic and New England regions. These North Carolina craftsmen turned simple materials into useful vessels. Their pots were also beautiful and had deep cultural meaning.

This exhibit is the first major survey of this pottery and the world in which it was made.

CuratorialText: Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities and from the moment of arrival the earliest settlers documented their landscape with a variety of detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Salem was founded in 1766 as a congregation town with the Church overseeing all activities—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all land and leased it to individuals who owned only the improvements. Salem was a bustling trade center with a mill, pottery, tannery, brewery, and store. But the earliest landscapes emphasize the centrality of the Church. They depict the town’s conscious design around a central square, the heart of the community. The square was home to the Gemein Haus (and later Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal dwellings for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. Gardens and fields appear nestled in and around the most prominent buildings in early views, suggesting the town’s growing success both agriculturally and commercially. As the nineteenth century progressed, the residents lobbied for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-hold system in favor of private land ownership. Manufacturing enterprises grew, and the railroad’s arrival in 1873 set the stage for tremendous growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely intertwined with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries flourished, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.
persona1_adapted:
Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities. From the start, the first settlers recorded their landscape with detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Salem was founded in 1766 as a congregation town. The Church controlled all activities—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all the land and leased it to individuals. People owned only the buildings and improvements they made on the land.

Salem was a busy trade center. It had a mill, pottery workshop, tannery (where animal hides were turned into leather), brewery, and store. But the earliest landscapes focus on the Church’s importance. They show the town was carefully designed around a central square, the heart of the community. The square held the Gemein Haus (later called Home Church), where religious services took place. It also had large communal homes for Single Brothers and Single Sisters, schools for boys and girls, and the store.

Gardens and fields appear tucked in and around the most important buildings in early drawings. This suggests the town was growing successfully in both farming and business. As the 1800s went on, residents asked for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the nearby town of Winston. In 1856, it ended the lease system and allowed people to own land privately.

Manufacturing businesses grew. The railroad arrived in 1873, setting the stage for huge growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely linked with the town of Winston. Textile and tobacco industries thrived. By 1900, the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.

CuratorialText:
James River Valley pottery has been the subject of archaeological and collector interest for over half a century.  The James River and its tributaries were home to more than a dozen potteries before the Civil War.  Potters hailing from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland and England, created a rich  hybrid tradition with forms and decorations adapted from their regional styles and wrought in local clays dug from Kaolin to Claymount.

Transcending their status as utilitarian objects, these pots, many exhibited here for the first time, provide a remarkable record and a unique glimpse into a significant and long-lived concentration of pottery manufacturing in eastern Virginia.  Viewed collectively, they present a bold decorative aesthetic rivaling long-recognized and celebrated schools of production seen elsewhere in the commonwealth and the Nation.
Today, many of these important historic sites are threatened by development.  Fortunately, archaeologists and decorative arts scholars are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the important legacy of the James River Potteries before they are lost forever.  At the end of 2013 the Chipstone Foundation journal Ceramics in America will publish an extensive article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries based on this ongoing research.
This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, Marshall
persona1_adapted:
For over 50 years, people have studied and collected pottery from the James River Valley. Before the Civil War, more than a dozen potteries were located along the James River and its smaller streams. Potters came from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland, and England. They brought their own regional styles and created a rich mix of forms and decorations. They made their pots from local clays, dug from places like Kaolin to Claymount.

These pots are more than just useful objects. Many are shown here for the first time. They give us a remarkable record and a unique look at a large, long-lasting pottery industry in eastern Virginia. When seen together, they show a bold decorative style. This style matches the famous pottery schools found elsewhere in Virginia and the United States.

Today, many of these important historic sites are at risk from new development. Luckily, archaeologists and decorative arts experts are working ahead of the bulldozers. They are documenting and studying the important legacy of the James River Potteries before it is lost forever.
