At the end of 2013, the Chipstone Foundation journal *Ceramics in America* will publish a long article about these stoneware potteries, based on this ongoing research.

This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, and Marshall.

CuratorialText:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A multi-national female pastelist. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 18th and early 19th centuries.

This exhibition features objects created by these craftspeople whose origins spanned continents and oceans. As much as possible, it connects those objects with the words of their makers through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities forged in new homes, responses to changing political climates, and the creative blending of traditions into something new.
persona1_adapted:
An Irish schoolteacher. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A female pastel artist from several countries. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. These people all share one thing: they were “lately from” somewhere else. They came to the American South in the 1700s and early 1800s.

This exhibition shows objects made by these craftspeople. Their origins spanned continents and oceans. Where possible, it connects those objects with the makers’ own words. Those words come from letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities built in new homes, responses to changing politics, and the creative mixing of traditions into something new.

CuratorialText: Early Southerners drank a lot — nearly twenty gallons of alcohol each — every year. They drank when they woke up in the morning, as they ate and worked during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank was as important as what they drank. From imported Madeira sipped at mahogany tables to local whisky out of redware jugs, this exhibit explores the decorative arts that defined the early Southern drinker.
persona1_adapted: Early Southerners drank a lot—almost twenty gallons of alcohol per person each year. They drank in the morning, during meals, while working, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank mattered just as much as what they drank. This exhibit looks at the decorative objects that shaped the way early Southerners drank. It shows everything from imported Madeira wine, sipped at fancy mahogany tables, to local whiskey poured from redware jugs.

CuratorialText: this exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 18th and early 19th centuries to explore how maps were made, used, and did work in the early American South. Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections as well as major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. The exhibit includes two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger; a rare first-state copy of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s General Map of the Middle British Colonies produced at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.
persona1_adapted:
This exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 1700s and early 1800s. It explores how maps were made, used, and did important work in the early American South.

The show is called *Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South*. It includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections. It also features major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province.

You’ll see two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger. There’s a rare first-state copy of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia. Two copies of Lewis Evans’s *General Map of the Middle British Colonies* are on display. These were printed at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia. The exhibit also includes surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.

CuratorialText: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the distinctive combination of people “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across the present-day state of Alabama, Indigenous people including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw, practiced ceramic traditions brought by their ancestors into the region over 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a market for these craftspeople, who produced hybrid wares that meshed traditional techniques and designs that appealed to these foreign consumers. The geography of Alabama influenced how and where diverse potters and people came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north across the middle of the state. Most of the 19th century potters north of the mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was the site of early interactions between French, Spanish and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled ceramists. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.
persona1_adapted:
Alabama’s pottery traditions show how different groups of people came together in this region. This exhibition looks at the human hands that made, held, and kept these objects.

For over 10,000 years, Indigenous people like the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw have lived in what is now Alabama. They brought pottery-making skills passed down from their ancestors.

When European colonists and American settlers arrived, they created both danger and new chances for these craftspeople. The potters made new kinds of wares that mixed their own traditional methods with designs that would appeal to these foreign buyers.

Alabama’s geography played a big role in how potters from different places came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs through the middle of the state, from south to north. Most potters who worked north of these mountains in the 1800s came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, most potters came from Georgia or South Carolina.

The Gulf Coast was an early meeting place for French, Spanish, and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether immigrants came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic Ocean, they brought enslaved people of African descent with them. Some of these enslaved people were skilled ceramists—experts in making pottery. Together, all these different hands shaped the clay of the American South.

CuratorialText:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) exhibits some of the furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South.  From fine art to folk art, these objects tell the stories of the diverse craftsmen and consumers who called the South home for more than four centuries.

The early South is usually divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry.  The rivers, roads, and natural resources of these regions defined their economic and settlement patterns.  Each was home to a unique blend of ethnicities and religions: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed, and free and enslaved peoples of African descent.  The dynamic interactions between and among these distinct groups flavored the decorative arts of the early South.
persona1_adapted:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery is part of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA). It shows furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South. These objects range from fine art to folk art. They tell the stories of the many craftsmen and buyers who lived in the South for over 400 years.

The early South is usually split into three main cultural areas: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry. Rivers, roads, and natural resources in these areas shaped how people settled and built their economies. Each area had a unique mix of ethnic groups and religions. These included Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed Christians, and free and enslaved people of African descent. The lively interactions between these different groups influenced the decorative arts of the early South.

CuratorialText:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery is home to the most comprehensive collection of antebellum pottery made in Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among the decorative arts, pottery is the one media that is most intimately connected to place.  The southern pottery in this gallery reflects the interplay between maker, consumer, culture, and geography.  It is a tangible reflection of how potters responded to specific cultural needs to craft vessels out of local materials that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on exhibit in the Mariner Gallery.  The first is through a series of virtual galleries curated by the noted ceramics scholar Robert Hunter, Editor of Ceramics in America that explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through the lens of southern pottery.  You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by media: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware.  Within these pages you are also able to filter the objects by maker, place, and time
persona1_adapted:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery holds the largest collection of pottery made in the South before the Civil War. This pottery comes from Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among decorative arts, pottery is the type most closely linked to a specific place. The southern pottery in this gallery shows how makers, buyers, culture, and geography all work together. It is a physical example of how potters met local needs. They used local materials to create vessels that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery gives you two ways to explore the southern ceramics on display. First, you can view virtual galleries curated by Robert Hunter, a well-known ceramics expert and editor of *Ceramics in America*. These galleries explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—through southern pottery. Second, you can browse the objects by type: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware. On these pages, you can also filter the objects by maker, place, and time.

CuratorialText: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection is home to rare and important works about the early American South. The collection represents a lifetime of connoisseurship for Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and cousin of MESDA founder Frank L. Horton. What began as an collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually expanded to include important texts from all seven of MESDA’s southern states. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries – from 1585 to 1865 – the Gray collection is a perfect complement to the MESDA and Old Salem collections.  Books and manuscripts from the collection are exhibited in the MESDA galleries and are available to researchers.  A selection of highlights are shown below.
persona1_adapted:
The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection holds rare and important works about the early American South. Tom Gray built this collection over his lifetime. He was a director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) and a cousin of MESDA’s founder, Frank L. Horton.

At first, Tom focused on books from his home state of North Carolina. Over time, the collection grew to include important texts from all seven southern states that MESDA covers. The books and manuscripts span four centuries, from 1585 to 1865. This makes the Gray collection a perfect match for the MESDA and Old Salem collections.

Some books and manuscripts from the collection are on display in the MESDA galleries. Researchers can also use them. Below is a selection of highlights.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection includes architectural woodwork collected from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia.  This collection was originally assembled by Frank L. Horton to serve as backdrops for period room installations.  Today, in addition to providing scale and context for our study galleries, MESDA’s architectural collections are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right that that can tell stories of style, craftsmanship, migration, ethnicity, and race.
persona1_adapted: MESDA’s collection has woodwork from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia. Frank L. Horton first gathered these pieces to use as backgrounds for rooms set up to look like historic spaces. Today, these woodworks do more than just give size and setting to our study galleries. Experts also study them as important Southern objects. They can tell stories about style, skill, movement of people, ethnic background, and race.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts are home to one of country’s most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related objects.  Longrifles were some of the most complicated objects crafted in the early American South.  Artistic masterworks, they required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts.  The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making is kept alive today by the talented craftsmen of the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.
persona1_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts hold one of the country's most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related items. Longrifles were some of the most complex objects made in the early American South. These artistic masterpieces required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and mechanical arts. The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making continues today. Talented craftsmen at the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop keep it alive. The shop is located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an exceptional collection of Southern bedcoverings including quilts and coverlets.  The collection also has a significant number of textiles made and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens has a special collection of Southern bedcoverings. These include quilts and coverlets, which are woven bed covers. The collection also holds many textiles made and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of rare books and manuscripts prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  Many of these books and manuscripts are part of the Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection.
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds a large collection of rare books and handwritten documents. These items tell the story of the people and places of the early American South. Many of these books and documents are part of the Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection.

CuratorialText: MESDA is home to an extensive collection of southern brass, copper, and pewter.
persona1_adapted: MESDA has a large collection of brass, copper, and pewter objects from the American South.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, and The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Collection are home to the most comprehensive collection of Southern ceramics in existence.
persona1_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, along with its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and the Mariner Southern Ceramics Collection, holds the largest and most complete collection of Southern ceramics in the world.

CuratorialText: MESDA is home to a collection of clocks, surveyors compasses, and sundials that span all seven of our constituent states.
persona1_adapted: MESDA has a collection of clocks, surveyors' compasses, and sundials. These objects come from all seven states in our region.

CuratorialText: German speaking communities throughout the South created richly illuminated documents celebrating births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.
persona1_adapted: German-speaking communities in the South made beautifully decorated documents. These papers celebrated births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.

CuratorialText: For over forty years the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected many of the finest examples of southern-made furniture.  These objects demonstrate how the early South evolved, inform us about the diverse peoples who came here, and illuminate the artisans who were integral to the process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers utilized their materials at hand, both native and imported, to create furniture that speaks to us about their heterogeneous backgrounds and the multifaceted aspects of their everyday lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic amalgamation of art, culture, and history that forms the basis for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.
persona1_adapted:
For over forty years, the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected some of the best examples of furniture made in the South. These objects show how the early South changed over time. They also tell us about the many different people who came to this region. And they help us learn about the skilled workers who were a key part of that story.

These furniture makers came from many places, like England, Scotland, Ireland, Germany, Africa, or France. They used the materials they had nearby, as well as materials brought from other countries. The furniture they made gives us a glimpse into their different backgrounds and the many sides of their daily lives. These craftspeople helped create a rich mix of art, culture, and history. That mix is the foundation of MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection of iron represents the work of furnaces and blacksmiths across all seven southern states.
persona1_adapted: MESDA's iron collection shows the work of furnaces and blacksmiths from all seven southern states.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to one of the most important collections of southern of maps.  On the surface maps appear scientific and authoritative, useful devices for governmental officials and colonial expansionists to claim ownership of land and exert power and authority over competing nationalities.  Below the surface, however, maps often provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens has one of the most important collections of southern maps. On the surface, maps look scientific and trustworthy. Government officials and colonial settlers used them to claim land and show power over other nations. But beneath the surface, maps can reveal a lot about the social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Music is an important part of Southern culture.  In Salem, North Carolina, music was intrgal to Moravian worship and daily life.  Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s and brass bands played regularly as part of religious services and to announce births and deaths in the community.  Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection of musical instruments and related materials reflects the important role played by music in the community.  Other objects in the collection reflect musical craftsmanship elsewhere in the American South.
persona1_adapted: Music is a big part of life in the American South. In Salem, North Carolina, music was central to Moravian worship and everyday activities. By the 1780s, Salem had its own orchestra. Brass bands played often during religious services. They also played to announce births and deaths in the community. The Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection includes musical instruments and related items. These objects show how important music was to the community. Other items in the collection highlight musical craftsmanship from other parts of the American South.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has led the way in researching and collecting work by Southern artisans, allowing us to better understand the rich cultural complexity of artistic production in the South. While it is true that with only a handful of significant urban centers, fewer painters settled and remained in the South, the painting collection at MESDA ably demonstrates the diversity of training and styles with works by European-trained and native-born artists living in urban and rural settings
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) is a leader in studying and collecting art made by Southern craftspeople. This helps us learn more about the rich and complex culture behind art in the South. It is true that only a few big cities existed in the South back then. Because of this, fewer painters moved there and stayed. Still, MESDA's painting collection shows a wide range of training and styles. It includes works by artists trained in Europe and artists born in America, both from cities and the countryside.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  These views documented English perceptions of America and served as vehicles to convey a host of attitudes and values and provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona1_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens has a large collection of prints. These prints show the people and places of the early American South. They recorded how the English saw America. The prints also carried many different ideas and beliefs. They give us a deep look into the social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Pastel portrait of Frances L’Escott. Half-length pastel portrait of young woman with body turned slightly to the right and head slightly to the left. She has auburn hair, brown eyes, with loosely fitted ecru and blue gown. The original wood frame retains its original spruce back with the following inscriptions: “Henrietta Johnston Fecit/ South Carolina Ano 1716.” “I/ Frances Lescott/ Married Peter Villep—.”
persona1_adapted:
This is a pastel portrait of Frances L’Escott. It shows her from the waist up. Her body is turned a little to the right, and her head is turned a little to the left. She has auburn hair and brown eyes. She wears a loose-fitting gown that is ecru (a light beige color) and blue.

The portrait is in its original wood frame. The back of the frame is made of spruce wood. There are two inscriptions written on it. One says: “Henrietta Johnston Fecit/ South Carolina Ano 1716.” This tells us the artist’s name and the year it was made. The other says: “I/ Frances Lescott/ Married Peter Villep—.” This tells us the subject’s name and that she married someone named Peter Villep—.

CuratorialText: Portrait of man, bust length, seated in a red upholstered chair, body half-left, eyes and head forward, brown hair, blue-gray eyes, ruddy complexion, wearing a black coat, yellow vest and white stock and tie, brass buttons. The background is a greenish-gray.
persona1_adapted: This is a portrait of a man. He sits in a red chair. His body is turned slightly to the left, but he looks straight ahead. He has brown hair and blue-gray eyes. His skin has a reddish tone. He wears a black coat, a yellow vest, and a white necktie with brass buttons. The background is a greenish-gray color.

CuratorialText: Portrait of man in brown coat and yellow vest with white stock. He has blue-gray eyes and dark hair. He is a half-length figure, facing half left, posed with hand in his vest. He stands in front of a column and what must be a balustraded porch with green trees and a greenish ground in the distance.
persona1_adapted: This man is shown from the chest up. He faces slightly to the left, with one hand tucked into his vest. He wears a brown coat, a yellow vest, and a white neck cloth. His eyes are blue-gray, and his hair is dark. Behind him, a tall column and a porch with a railing frame the scene. In the distance, green trees and a grassy landscape are visible.

CuratorialText: Portrait of a woman seated in a Chippendale type chair, half-length, facing forward and dressed with a white high crowned cap, gray dress and white neck piece with a black ribbon and jewel at her throat. She has brown eyes, dark hair. She holds a reddish-brown book in her right hand and is seated at a column base to the right, with red drapery and tassel, and with a balustraded rail and trees in the background to the left.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a woman sitting in a fancy wooden chair. We see her from the waist up. She faces forward and wears a tall white cap, a gray dress, and a white collar. A black ribbon with a jewel sits at her neck. She has brown eyes and dark hair. In her right hand, she holds a reddish-brown book. She sits next to a column base on the right side of the painting. Behind her on the right, there is red fabric with a tassel. On the left side, you can see a railing and trees in the background.

CuratorialText: Full length portrait of a man in blue uniform with red trim at lapel and cuffs, white vest and breeches with black boots trimmed with gold at the top, white epaulets and neck piece, and black tircorner hat decorated with silver cord. He is posed with sword in right upraised hand and a dagger-like knife in the left, facing half-left, with a tree at either side to frame a fight between American troops and Indians in the background. The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red facings, belly boxes, and linen overalls. The retreating Indians to the left rear of the painting appear with topknots or scalp locks, and wear battle dress of breechcloths and leggings.
persona1_adapted:
This is a full-length painting of a man in a blue uniform. The uniform has red trim on the lapel and cuffs. He wears a white vest and white breeches (short pants). His black boots have gold trim at the top. He also has white epaulets (shoulder decorations) and a white neck piece. On his head is a black three-cornered hat decorated with silver cord.

The man is posed with a sword raised in his right hand and a dagger-like knife in his left. He faces half-left. On each side of him is a tree. These trees frame a battle scene in the background between American troops and Native Americans.

The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red trim, belly boxes (small pouches worn on the belt), and linen overalls. The Native Americans are shown retreating to the left rear of the painting. They have topknots or scalp locks and wear battle dress made of breechcloths and leggings.

CuratorialText: Charcoal drawing of the profile of Joseph Lewis, Jr., facing left on pink paper. The drawing shows a gentleman with light hair tied at the back with dark ribbon who is wearing a dark high coat with a white stock. The painting is mounted in the original gold frame with black and gold painted glass.
persona1_adapted: This is a charcoal drawing of Joseph Lewis, Jr. He is shown in profile, facing left, on pink paper. The man has light hair tied back with a dark ribbon. He wears a dark, high-collared coat and a white stock, which is a type of neckcloth. The artwork is displayed in its original gold frame. The frame has black and gold painted glass.

CuratorialText: Watercolor on paper of a plantation with an inscription at the base of the painting. The house is a white frame two-story central hall structure with its chimneys on the rear elevation. A fence runs in front of the house with a bridge to the right in the background, indicating an inlet of water. An outbuilding stands behind some trees standing behind the bridge. Several large trees are located in the left foreground, along with some moss, grass and other foliage. A path leads to the house, and two men, one mounted upon a horse, the other standing below him are talking to one another. A body of water is in the background.
persona1_adapted:
This is a watercolor painting on paper. It shows a plantation. There is writing at the bottom of the painting.

The main house is white and made of wood. It has two floors and a central hallway. The chimneys are on the back of the house. A fence runs across the front.

In the background on the right, there is a bridge. It crosses an inlet, which is a narrow body of water. Behind the bridge and some trees, there is a small outbuilding.

In the left foreground, there are several large trees. You can also see moss, grass, and other plants. A path leads up to the house.

Two men are talking to each other. One man is sitting on a horse. The other man is standing next to him. A large body of water is in the background.

CuratorialText: Charcoal drawing, profile of man, facing right, on faded pink paper. The gentleman wears the uniform of an officer, a dark coat with light facings with buttons at the outside of long buttonholes on the lapel and collar, and epaulets of gold bullion. A black inner collar is over a white stock. The man’s hair is graying and is tied in a knot at the back with a black ribbon.
persona1_adapted: A charcoal drawing on faded pink paper shows a man in profile, facing right. He wears an officer’s uniform: a dark coat with light trim and buttons along the lapel and collar. Gold bullion epaulets sit on his shoulders. A black inner collar rises above a white neckcloth. His graying hair is tied in a knot at the back with a black ribbon.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a gentleman with dark eyes, dark complexion and a shaven head, who is facing half-right with hands folded resting on a table . His right hand holds a quill pen and the left is holding a document with a name written thereon. He wears a white loose collar shirt with deep red robe and turban of the same red material, and sits in a chair upholstered in a red-brown fabric with brass tacks. The background is a dark green drapery to the left and books on the right.
persona1_adapted:
This is an oil painting on canvas. It shows a gentleman with dark eyes and dark skin. His head is shaved. He faces slightly to the right. His hands are folded and resting on a table. In his right hand, he holds a quill pen. His left hand holds a document with a name written on it.

He wears a white, loose-collared shirt. Over it, he has a deep red robe and a matching red turban. He sits in a chair covered in red-brown fabric. The chair has brass tacks. Behind him, the background has dark green curtains on the left and books on the right.

CuratorialText: Bust portrait, oil on canvas, of woman wearing a pearl necklace and blue dress with lace trim, in mahogany architectural frame with cut-out corners.
persona1_adapted: This painting is a portrait of a woman. She wears a blue dress with lace trim and a pearl necklace. The artist used oil paint on canvas. The frame is made of mahogany wood. It has cut-out corners and looks like part of a building.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Richard Sprigg of Strawberry Hill, Maryland. Young man facing forward, his body is slightly to the right, his left hand in his vest, wearing a dark beige coat and light silk vest, with white stock. He has a ruddy complexion, dark eyes, brown hair. The background varies from greenish-gray to the lower right to very dark gray in upper left and has lunettes in the corners.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas of Richard Sprigg from Strawberry Hill, Maryland. The young man faces forward, with his body turned slightly to the right. His left hand rests inside his vest. He wears a dark beige coat and a light silk vest, along with a white stock around his neck. He has a ruddy, or reddish, complexion, dark eyes, and brown hair. The background changes from greenish-gray on the lower right to very dark gray on the upper left. The corners of the painting have lunettes, which are small, half-moon shaped spaces.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman facing half-right. The subject has dark hair combed upward to expose her left ear, and pearls in her hair that hang down over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk cross-bodice dress tied with a dark red sash. The pinned-up sleeves of the garment revealing a light under-garment. She is posed with her left arm upon a table, the right at her hip, and foliage painted in the background.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. It shows a woman facing slightly to the right. Her dark hair is combed up to show her left ear. Pearls in her hair hang down over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk dress with a cross-style bodice, tied with a dark red sash. The sleeves are pinned up, revealing a light-colored undergarment. She rests her left arm on a table and places her right hand on her hip. Foliage is painted in the background.

CuratorialText: A watercolor “en grisaille” of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, the second Clerk of Court of Hanover County. It passed to his son George William Pollard in 1840 and remained in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed the watercolor.
persona1_adapted: This is a watercolor painting of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The artist painted it using only shades of gray, a style called "grisaille." The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II. He was the second Clerk of Court for Hanover County. In 1840, the house went to his son, George William Pollard. The Pollard family kept the home until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard. Harry was an amateur artist. He drew and signed this watercolor.

CuratorialText: Painting, done in oil on mahogany panel, of a young man facing half-right. The gentleman has dark hair and side-burns, and is dressed in a white shirt, tie and vest, and a black coat supporting a high collar. There is an inscription on the back of the panel.
persona1_adapted: This painting is an oil portrait on a mahogany wood panel. It shows a young man facing slightly to the right. He has dark hair and sideburns. He wears a white shirt, tie, and vest. Over these, he has a black coat with a high collar. An inscription is written on the back of the panel.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas painting of a man facing half-left. The work is a half-length portrait. The subject has gray hair, dark gray eyes, wears a dark brown coat with a high turned-down collar, a yellow vest, and white stock at the neck.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. It shows a man facing half-left. The portrait shows him from the chest up. He has gray hair and dark gray eyes. He wears a dark brown coat with a high, turned-down collar. Underneath, he has a yellow vest and a white neckcloth.

CuratorialText: This painting depicts a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). It was a scene that the artist returned to regularly. Another version of this composition is in the collection of the Greenville County Museum of Art.
persona1_adapted: This painting shows a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage. The Hermitage was the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). The artist painted this scene many times. Another version of this painting is at the Greenville County Museum of Art.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Charles Town showing the city waterfront as seen from across the Cooper River. A large sailing ship is in the left foreground, the opposite shore in foreground with various figures along the shore.
persona1_adapted: This painting is an oil-on-canvas view of Charles Town. It shows the city's waterfront from across the Cooper River. In the left foreground, a large sailing ship is visible. The opposite shore is in the foreground, with several figures along the water's edge.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, waist-length portrait of a lady wearing a gray satin dress with gray stomacher in front and white lace at neck and sleeves, her hair is pulled tightly back, a loop of pearls in it on the left side of the portrait. Her right hand holds a pink rose.
persona1_adapted: This is an oil painting on canvas. It shows a woman from the waist up. She wears a gray satin dress. The front of the dress has a matching panel called a stomacher. White lace trims her neck and sleeves. Her hair is pulled back tightly. On the left side of the painting, a loop of pearls sits in her hair. Her right hand holds a pink rose.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of a man facing half-left and seated in a green painted chair. The man has a high foreheard, dark hair, dark blue eyes, ruddy complexion, and is wearing a dark coat mounted with gray buttons, a white stock, and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin is on his neck cloth. The painting is signed in the lower left corner.
persona1_adapted: A man sits in a green chair, facing slightly to the left. He has a high forehead, dark hair, and dark blue eyes. His skin is ruddy, or reddish in color. He wears a dark coat with gray buttons, a white neckcloth, and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin decorates his neckcloth. The artist signed the painting in the lower left corner.
